topics.title: X-Ray Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** X-Ray Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**X-rays** are a form of **electromagnetic radiation** with wavelengths ranging from approximately **0.01 nm to 10 nm** (frequencies of $3 \times 10^{16}$ Hz to $3 \times 10^{19}$ Hz), possessing sufficient photon energy to penetrate soft tissues of the human body. They are produced when high-velocity electrons, accelerated by a high potential difference (typically **30 kVp to 150 kVp** in diagnostic radiology), strike a metallic target within an evacuated glass envelope known as an **X-ray tube**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "X-rays are ionizing electromagnetic radiations produced by the sudden deceleration of high-speed electrons (bremsstrahlung) or by the transition of inner-shell atomic electrons (characteristic radiation) when accelerated electrons bombard a high atomic number (Z) target material in a high-vacuum diode."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the top of a tall slide in a playground and slide down — you reach the bottom at high speed. Now imagine a wall of sand at the bottom. When you hit the sand:
- Some of your kinetic energy is converted into **heat** (the sand warms up)
- A tiny fraction of your energy is converted into **sound and light** (a "braking flash")

This is exactly what happens inside an X-ray tube:
- **Electrons = children sliding down** (accelerated from cathode to anode)
- **High voltage (kVp) = height of the slide** (more height = more speed = more energy on impact)
- **Tungsten target anode = the sand wall** (where the electrons "brake" suddenly)
- **X-ray photon = the brief flash of light** released during the sudden stopping

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for BME Students:** Only about **1%** of the electron beam's kinetic energy is converted to X-rays. The remaining **~99%** becomes heat — which is why real X-ray tubes require sophisticated **rotating anodes** and **active cooling systems**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Range | Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Electron charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| Diagnostic kVp range | $V$ | **30 – 150** | kV |
| Filament current | $I_f$ | 3 – 5 | A |
| Tube current | $mA$ | 100 – 1000 | mA |
| Tungsten atomic number | $Z$ | **74** | — |
| Tungsten melting point | — | **3422 °C** | °C |
| X-ray wavelength range | $\lambda$ | $0.01 - 10$ | nm |

### 1.4 Historical Context (KTU Frequently Asked)

X-rays were discovered by **Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen** on **November 8, 1895** while experimenting with **Crookes tubes** (early cathode ray tubes) at the University of Würzburg, Germany. He received the first **Nobel Prize in Physics (1901)** for this discovery. He named them "X-rays" because their nature was unknown at the time (the "X" denoting the unknown mathematical variable).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The X-Ray Tube — Modern Design (Coolidge Tube, 1913)

The modern diagnostic X-ray tube is a **high-vacuum diode** consisting of two principal electrodes sealed inside a glass or metal-ceramic envelope:

#### A. The Cathode (Negative Electrode)
The cathode is the **electron source** and consists of three key components:

1. **Filament**: A coiled wire of **tungsten** (W, Z=74) of diameter ~0.2 mm, typically 1–2 cm long. Tungsten is chosen because of its:
   - Very **high melting point (3422 °C)**
   - Low vapor pressure at high temperatures
   - High mechanical strength
   - Excellent thermionic emission properties

2. **Focusing Cup**: A molybdenum or nickel cup-shaped structure that surrounds the filament. Its role is to **electrostatically focus** the diverging electron beam into a narrow, well-defined rectangular patch on the anode — this determines the **focal spot size**.

3. **Filament Transformer (Step-Down)**: Provides low-voltage, high-current AC (typically 3–5 A at 10 V) to heat the filament to incandescence (~2500 K) for thermionic emission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermionic Emission:** The process by which electrons are liberated from a heated metal surface. The current density follows the **Richardson-Dushman equation**:
> $$J = A_R T^2 e^{-W/kT}$$
> where $W$ is the work function, $T$ is the absolute temperature, and $A_R$ is Richardson's constant.

#### B. The Anode (Positive Electrode)
The anode is the **target** where high-energy electrons are abruptly decelerated, producing X-rays. Two types exist:

- **Stationary Anode**: Tungsten target embedded in a copper block. Used in dental and low-power applications.
- **Rotating Anode**: A tungsten-rhenium (W-Re) alloy disc mounted on a rotor, spinning at **3000–10,000 RPM**. The rotation distributes the heat over a larger area, allowing higher tube currents. Used in diagnostic radiology.

The anode has a **target angle** (typically 7°–20°) which, combined with the line-focus principle, produces an **effective focal spot** much smaller than the **actual focal spot**.

#### C. The High-Vacuum Envelope
The interior is evacuated to ~$10^{-6}$ mmHg to:
- Prevent electron collisions with air molecules (which would scatter and slow the electrons)
- Prevent filament oxidation/burning
- Maintain a stable high-voltage environment without electrical breakdown

### 2.2 Mechanism of X-Ray Production — Two Physical Processes

#### Process 1: Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation) — Continuous Spectrum

When a high-speed electron passes close to the **nucleus** of a target atom, the strong Coulomb attraction from the positively charged nucleus **decelerates and deflects** the electron. This sudden change in velocity causes the electron to lose kinetic energy, which is emitted as an **X-ray photon**.

**Key characteristics:**
- Produces a **continuous spectrum** of X-ray wavelengths
- The maximum photon energy corresponds to the **complete conversion** of the electron's kinetic energy into one photon (rare event)
- Governed by the **Duane-Hunt Limit** (see formulas below)
- Output intensity $\propto Z \times kVp^2$ (depends on target material Z and tube voltage squared)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bremsstrahlung = "Braking Radiation"** (German: *bremsen* = to brake, *strahlung* = radiation). This produces about **70–90%** of the diagnostic X-ray beam.

#### Process 2: Characteristic Radiation — Discrete Spectrum

When an incoming high-speed electron **ejects an inner-shell electron** (typically from the K-shell) of the target atom, the vacancy is filled by an electron from a higher shell (L or M shell). The energy difference between the shells is released as an **X-ray photon** with a discrete, well-defined wavelength — unique to the target material.

**Cascading process:**
1. K-shell electron ejected → K-shell vacancy created
2. L-shell electron drops to fill K-shell → emits **Kα X-ray** ($K_\alpha$)
3. M-shell electron drops to fill L-shell vacancy → emits **L X-ray**
4. Cascading continues until the atom returns to ground state

**Key characteristics:**
- Produces a **line spectrum** (discrete wavelengths)
- Wavelengths are **characteristic of the target element** (Moseley's Law)
- Requires the bombarding electron energy to **exceed the binding energy** of the K-shell electron (called the **K-edge**)
- K-series photons have higher energy (shorter wavelength) than L-series

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moseley's Law (1913):** $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$, where $\nu$ is the X-ray frequency, $Z$ is the atomic number, and $a, b$ are constants. This was the experimental basis for arranging the periodic table.

### 2.3 Factors Affecting X-Ray Production

| Factor | Effect on X-ray Quantity (Intensity) | Effect on X-ray Quality (Energy/Penetration) |
|--------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **kVp ↑** | $\uparrow$ (proportional to kVp²) | $\uparrow$ (higher max energy) |
| **mA ↑** | $\uparrow$ (linearly proportional) | No effect |
| **Time (s) ↑** | $\uparrow$ (linearly proportional) | No effect |
| **Target Z ↑** | $\uparrow$ (more bremsstrahlung) | No effect |
| **Added filtration ↑** | $\downarrow$ (absorbs low-energy photons) | $\uparrow$ (harder beam) |
| **Distance ↑** | $\downarrow$ (inverse square law) | No effect |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$ | Duane-Hunt minimum wavelength | m |
| 2 | $\lambda_{min}(\text{nm}) = \dfrac{1240}{V(\text{V})}$ | Practical form (1240 constant) | nm |
| 3 | $E_{max} = eV$ | Maximum photon energy | J (or keV) |
| 4 | $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | J |
| 5 | $\eta \approx \dfrac{Z \times kVp}{1000} \times 0.1\,\%$ | X-ray production efficiency | % |
| 6 | $I \propto kVp^2 \cdot mA \cdot t \cdot Z$ | X-ray beam intensity (relative) | — |
| 7 | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$ | Inverse square law | — |
| 8 | $H = V \times I \times t - X_{energy}$ | Heat units (HU) in anode | J |
| 9 | $J = A_R T^2 e^{-W/kT}$ | Richardson-Dushman thermionic emission | A/m² |
| 10 | $n = \dfrac{V_0}{V}$ | Step-up ratio of HVT | — |
| 11 | $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$ | Moseley's Law | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Duane-Hunt Shortcut:** At $V = 100$ kV, $\lambda_{min} = 0.0124$ nm. Memorize the **1240 constant** — KTU problems almost always use this simplified form.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The understanding of X-ray production is fundamental to:
- **Diagnostic Radiology**: Optimizing kVp, mA, and exposure time for different body parts (chest, abdomen, extremities)
- **CT Scanners**: Rotating anode tubes delivering high instantaneous power
- **Industrial Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**: Detecting cracks in welds, aircraft components
- **Security Screening**: Airport baggage inspection
- **Radiotherapy**: Megavoltage X-ray beams (6 MV, 18 MV) from linear accelerators for cancer treatment
- **Material Analysis**: X-ray fluorescence (XRF) spectroscopy

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Duane-Hunt Law (Minimum Wavelength)

The **Duane-Hunt Law** establishes the theoretical lower limit of X-ray wavelength produced by an X-ray tube operating at peak voltage $V$.

**Given:**
- An electron of charge $e$ accelerated through a potential difference $V$
- The electron gains kinetic energy $E_k = eV$
- Upon maximum braking, all kinetic energy converts to a single photon

**Step 1 — Energy gained by the electron:**

When an electron (charge $e$) falls through a potential difference $V$, the work done on it by the electric field equals its kinetic energy at impact.

$$E_k = eV$$

**Step 2 — Maximum photon energy scenario:**

The maximum photon energy is produced when the **entire** kinetic energy of a single electron is converted into **one X-ray photon** (the rarest, most energetic event). By conservation of energy:

$$E_{photon,max} = E_k = eV$$

**Step 3 — Apply Planck's relation:**

The energy of a photon is related to its frequency by $E = hf$, and wavelength by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$. Therefore:

$$E_{photon,max} = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{min}} = eV$$

**Step 4 — Solve for minimum wavelength:**

Rearranging for $\lambda_{min}$:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{hc}{eV}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical constants:**

Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot V}$$

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot V}$$

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \quad \text{(in meters)}$$

**Step 6 — Express in convenient units:**

If $V$ is in volts and $\lambda$ in nanometers:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{min}(\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{V(\text{volts})}}$$

This is the **Duane-Hunt Law** — the workhorse equation of KTU Module 1 numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Photon Energy at Given Wavelength

**Given:** An X-ray photon of wavelength $\lambda = 0.05$ nm produced in a tube operating at 50 kVp.

**Step 1 — Apply Planck-Einstein relation:**

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s})(3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s})}{0.05 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}}{0.05 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$0.05 \times 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{5 \times 10^{-11}} = 3.9756 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kiloelectron-volts (keV) for medical convention:**

$$E = \frac{3.9756 \times 10^{-15}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.4815 \times 10^{4} \text{ eV} \approx 24.82 \text{ keV}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 24.82 \text{ keV}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency of X-Ray Production

The **efficiency** $\eta$ of an X-ray tube is the ratio of useful X-ray energy emitted to the total electrical energy supplied to the tube.

**Step 1 — Energy input to the tube:**

Electrical power input $P_{in} = V \times I$, over time $t$:

$$E_{in} = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Energy emitted as X-rays:**

Empirical formula (from experimental data by Kramers and others):

$$E_{X-ray} = k \cdot Z \cdot I \cdot t \cdot V^2$$

where $k$ is a constant of proportionality and $Z$ is the atomic number of the target.

**Step 3 — Define efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{E_{X-ray}}{E_{in}} = \frac{k \cdot Z \cdot I \cdot t \cdot V^2}{V \cdot I \cdot t} = k \cdot Z \cdot V$$

**Step 4 — Apply empirical constant:**

For tungsten target ($Z = 74$), the empirical constant $k$ yields the practical rule:

$$\eta (\%) \approx \frac{Z \times kVp}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{\eta (\%) \approx \frac{74 \times kVp}{1000}}$$

**Numerical Check — At 100 kVp with Tungsten:**

$$\eta = \frac{74 \times 100}{1000} = 7.4\%$$

However, the **actual** efficiency is closer to **0.6–1%** because the empirical $k$ is smaller than 0.01. A more accurate practical approximation is:

$$\eta \approx 0.1 \times \frac{Z \times kVp}{1000}\%$$

This confirms that **>99% of input energy is wasted as heat** — justifying the need for rotating anodes and water/oil cooling in clinical tubes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **Problem:** An X-ray tube operates at 80 kVp and 200 mA. Calculate: (a) the minimum wavelength of X-rays produced, (b) the velocity of electrons striking the target, (c) the maximum photon energy, and (d) the expected production efficiency for a tungsten target.

#### (a) Minimum Wavelength

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1240}{V} = \frac{1240}{80{,}000} = 0.0155 \text{ nm}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{min} = 0.0155 \text{ nm} = 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}}$$

#### (b) Velocity of Electrons

Using the work-energy theorem: $\frac{1}{2}m_e v^2 = eV$, solve for $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (80{,}000)}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2.5632 \times 10^{-14}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2.8136 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 1.677 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.559c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At 80 kVp, the electron velocity is **~56% of the speed of light**! This is why **relativistic corrections** are sometimes applied in high-energy X-ray calculations. KTU typically ignores this in undergraduate problems.

#### (c) Maximum Photon Energy

$$E_{max} = eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(80{,}000) = 1.2816 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

Converting to keV:

$$E_{max} = \frac{1.2816 \times 10^{-14}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 80{,}000 \text{ eV} = 80 \text{ keV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{max} = 80 \text{ keV}}$$

#### (d) Production Efficiency

Using the practical formula:

$$\eta \approx \frac{Z \times kVp}{1000}\% = \frac{74 \times 80}{1000}\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 5.92\%}$$

(Actual measured efficiency is closer to **~0.8%** — the discrepancy is due to the empirical constant being much smaller than unity.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: X-Ray Tube Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step-Down Transformer] -->|3-5 V, 4 A| B[Filament Cathode]
    B -->|Thermionic Emission| C[Electron Cloud]
    D[Step-Up HVT] -->|30-150 kVp| E[Vacuum Tube Envelope]
    C -->|Accelerated Electrons| E
    E -->|Electron Beam Focus| F[Focusing Cup]
    F -->|Narrow Beam| G[Rotating Tungsten Anode]
    G -->|Sudden Deceleration| H[Bremsstrahlung X-Rays]
    G -->|K-shell Ionization| I[Characteristic X-Rays]
    H --> J[Composite X-Ray Beam]
    I --> J
    G -->|99% Heat| K[Anode Heat Sink]
    K -->|Cooling| L[Oil or Water Cooling System]
    J -->|Passes Through| M[Aluminum Filter]
    M -->|Hard Beam| N[Patient / Target]
    O[Stator Motor] -->|3000-10000 RPM| G
    P[Rectifier Circuit] -->|DC Pulsed| E
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: X-Ray Production Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Voltage Applied 30-150 kVp] --> B[Electron Acceleration]
    B --> C[Electron Strikes Target]
    C --> D{Type of Interaction}
    D -->|Near Nucleus| E[Bremsstrahlung]
    D -->|Inner Shell Collision| F[Characteristic Radiation]
    E --> G[Continuous Spectrum]
    F --> H[Discrete Line Spectrum]
    G --> I[Composite X-ray Output]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Filtration]
    J --> K[Diagnostic X-ray Beam]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: X-Ray Spectrum Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[X-Ray Output Spectrum] --> B[Continuous Component]
    A --> C[Discrete Component]
    B --> D[Bremsstrahlung Radiation]
    D --> E[Min Wavelength Cutoff at lambda_min]
    D --> F[Peak Intensity Region]
    D --> G[Long Wavelength Tail]
    C --> H[K-alpha Line]
    C --> I[K-beta Line]
    C --> J[L-series Lines]
    E --> K[Duane-Hunt Limit]
    F --> L[Depends on kVp]
    H --> M[Target Material Specific]
    I --> M
    J --> M
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: X-Ray Tube Components Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CATHODE_ASSEMBLY
        CA1[Filament Tungsten Coil] --> CA2[Focusing Cup Molybdenum]
        CA2 --> CA3[Stem Support]
    end
    subgraph ANODE_ASSEMBLY
        AA1[Tungsten-Rhenium Target Disc] --> AA2[Molybdenum Stem]
        AA2 --> AA3[Copper Rotor]
        AA3 --> AA4[Bearing Assembly]
    end
    subgraph HOUSING
        HO1[Glass/Metal Envelope] --> HO2[High Vacuum 10^-6 mmHg]
        HO2 --> HO3[HV Cable Connector]
    end
    CA3 --> HO1
    AA2 --> HO1
    HO3 -->|30-150 kV DC| CATHODE_ASSEMBLY
    HO3 -->|Grounded| ANODE_ASSEMBLY
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define X-rays. State any four properties of X-rays relevant to medical imaging.

**Model Answer:**

X-rays are a form of ionizing electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths in the range of **0.01 nm to 10 nm**, produced by the deceleration of high-velocity electrons in a high-Z target.

**Four properties of X-rays:**

1. **Penetrating power:** X-rays can penetrate matter that is opaque to visible light. Penetration depends on the energy of the X-ray photon and the atomic number and density of the material.

2. **Ionizing radiation:** X-rays possess sufficient energy to remove tightly bound electrons from atoms, producing ion pairs. This property is used in radiotherapy but also poses biological hazards.

3. **Fluorescence effect:** X-rays cause certain materials (e.g., zinc sulfide, calcium tungstate) to emit visible light. This is the basis of the **X-ray image intensifier** and traditional fluoroscopic screens.

4. **Photographic effect:** X-rays affect photographic film similar to visible light, forming a latent image upon exposure and development. This is the basis of **conventional radiography**.

5. (Bonus) **No mass, no charge:** X-rays are electrically neutral and have no rest mass — they travel in straight lines and are unaffected by electric or magnetic fields.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition = 1 mark, Four properties = 0.5 each = 2 marks. Total 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Differentiate between **Bremsstrahlung** and **Characteristic** X-ray production mechanisms.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation) | Characteristic Radiation |
|---------|------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Electron decelerated/deflected by the nuclear Coulomb field | Inner-shell electron ejected; outer electron fills the vacancy |
| **Spectrum** | Continuous (polychromatic) | Discrete line spectrum |
| **Energy** | Varies from 0 to $E_{max} = eV$ | Fixed, determined by shell energy difference |
| **Depends on** | kVp and target Z | Target material's atomic number |
| **Contribution** | ~70–90% of diagnostic X-ray beam | ~10–30% of diagnostic X-ray beam |
| **Discoverer** | Stokes / Sommerfeld (theory) | Barkla / Bragg (experimental) |
| **Threshold** | Any kVp produces some bremsstrahlung | Requires kVp > K-edge binding energy |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Coolidge X-ray tube**. Discuss the role of the **rotating anode** in modern diagnostic tubes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An X-ray tube is operated at 60 kVp and 100 mA. Calculate: (i) the minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted, (ii) the maximum photon energy in keV, and (iii) the production efficiency for a tungsten target ($Z = 74$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of Coolidge X-ray Tube**

The Coolidge tube (invented by **William D. Coolidge** in 1913 at General Electric) is a **high-vacuum thermionic diode** with the following components:

1. **Cathode assembly:**
   - **Tungsten filament** (length ~1.5 cm, diameter ~0.2 mm) heated by a low-voltage (3–5 V), high-current (3–5 A) AC supply from a step-down transformer
   - **Molybdenum focusing cup** biased negatively to focus the electron beam
   - Two filaments (small and large focal spot) are usually provided for different applications

2. **Anode assembly (rotating):**
   - A bevelled disc of **tungsten-rhenium (W-Re) alloy** (Z=74, melting point 3422 °C) embedded in a **molybdenum** disc, mounted on a copper rotor
   - Rotor spins at 3000–10000 RPM via an induction motor stator
   - The target angle is typically **7°–20°**

3. **Envelope:**
   - Borosilicate glass or metal-ceramic envelope evacuated to $\sim 10^{-6}$ mmHg
   - High-voltage cable enters through the cathode side

**Working principle:**
- Filament heated → **thermionic emission** releases electrons
- High voltage (30–150 kVp) applied between cathode (−) and anode (+) accelerates electrons
- Electrons strike the tungsten target → **bremsstrahlung** and **characteristic X-rays** are produced
- Heat is dissipated through the rotating anode to the surrounding oil/water cooling

**Role of the Rotating Anode:**
- The heat generated is distributed over the **entire circumference** of the rotating disc, not just one spot
- This allows the use of **much higher tube currents** (up to 1000 mA) for short exposures
- **Effective focal spot** can be made small (for sharp images) while the **actual focal spot** on the anode is large (for better heat dissipation) — the **line-focus principle**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Minimum Wavelength (Duane-Hunt Law):**

Using $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{1240}{V(\text{V})}$:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1240}{60{,}000} = 2.0667 \times 10^{-2} \text{ nm} = 0.0207 \text{ nm}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{min} = 0.0207 \text{ nm} = 2.07 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Photon Energy:**

$$E_{max} = eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(60{,}000) \text{ J}$$

$$E_{max} = 9.612 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

Converting to keV:

$$E_{max} = \frac{9.612 \times 10^{-15}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 60{,}000 \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{max} = 60 \text{ keV}}$$

**(iii) Production Efficiency:**

$$\eta \approx \frac{Z \times kVp}{1000}\% = \frac{74 \times 60}{1000}\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 4.44\%}$$

(This is the theoretical upper limit; practical efficiency is much lower at ~0.5–0.8% due to the empirical proportionality constant being smaller.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Duane-Hunt Law**. Derive the relationship between minimum X-ray wavelength and the operating tube voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diagnostic X-ray tube operates at 100 kVp with a tube current of 500 mA for 0.1 second. Calculate: (i) the total number of electrons striking the target, (ii) the minimum wavelength produced, and (iii) the total heat generated in the anode (assuming only 1% of input energy becomes X-rays). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Duane-Hunt Law — Explanation and Derivation**

**Statement:** *The minimum wavelength of X-rays produced by an X-ray tube is inversely proportional to the accelerating voltage applied across the tube.*

**Derivation:**

When an electron of mass $m_e$ and charge $e$ is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$, the work done on the electron by the electric field is converted to kinetic energy:

$$\frac{1}{2}m_e v^2 = eV$$

Upon striking the target, the electron may either:
- Be completely stopped → entire KE converts to a single photon (maximum energy event)
- Undergo multiple collisions → energy is shared between multiple photons and heat

The **maximum photon energy** corresponds to the case of a single photon carrying all the electron's KE:

$$E_{photon,max} = eV$$

By Planck's relation:

$$E_{photon,max} = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{min}}$$

Equating both expressions:

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_{min}} = eV$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{min} = \frac{hc}{eV}}$$

With numerical substitution, this yields the **engineering form**:

$$\lambda_{min}(\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{V(\text{V})}$$

**Significance:** The Duane-Hunt limit sets the **shortest possible wavelength** (highest photon energy) that can be produced. It is independent of the target material — only the operating voltage matters. This is verified experimentally by examining the **sharp cutoff** in the continuous X-ray spectrum on the short-wavelength side.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total number of electrons striking the target:**

Total charge that flows:
$$Q = I \times t = (500 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A})(0.1 \text{ s}) = 0.05 \text{ C}$$

Number of electrons:
$$n = \frac{Q}{e} = \frac{0.05}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 3.12 \times 10^{17} \text{ electrons}}$$

**(ii) Minimum wavelength:**

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1240}{100{,}000} = 0.0124 \text{ nm}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{min} = 0.0124 \text{ nm} = 1.24 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}}$$

**(iii) Total heat generated in the anode:**

Total input electrical energy:
$$E_{in} = V \times I \times t = (100 \times 10^3)(500 \times 10^{-3})(0.1) = 5000 \text{ J}$$

X-ray energy emitted (1% of input):
$$E_{X-ray} = 0.01 \times 5000 = 50 \text{ J}$$

Heat generated in anode (99% of input):
$$E_{heat} = 0.99 \times 5000 = 4950 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E_{heat} = 4950 \text{ J} \approx 4.95 \text{ kJ}}$$

This enormous heat load (equivalent to melting ~15 g of ice) in **0.1 second** demonstrates why **rotating anodes** and **active cooling** are essential in clinical X-ray tubes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in X-Ray Production Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Duane-Hunt:** Students often write $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{1240}{V}$ using $V$ in kV instead of **volts**. This gives a wrong answer by a factor of 1000. **Always convert kV → V before applying the formula.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert Joules to keV:** Photon energy problems often expect answers in **keV** for medical imaging. The conversion is: $1 \text{ keV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-16} \text{ J}$. Showing both units earns full marks.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Bremsstrahlung with Characteristic Radiation:** Bremsstrahlung = continuous spectrum (braking near nucleus); Characteristic = discrete spectrum (inner-shell transitions). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> 4. **Missing the "Why" in the Coolidge Tube Question:** Simply drawing the diagram is NOT enough. You must explain the **role of each component** (focusing cup, vacuum, rotating anode) to score full marks on the 7-mark question.
> 
> 5. **Neglecting the 99% Heat Issue:** Numerical problems on efficiency often expect you to **comment** on the practical implications (cooling, rotating anode, etc.). A bare numerical answer without discussion loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 6. **Moseley's Law Direction:** $\sqrt{\nu} \propto Z$ means **higher Z → higher frequency → shorter wavelength**. Students sometimes invert this relationship.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for X-Ray Production:**

- [ ] **Discoverer:** Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen, 8 November 1895 — Nobel Prize 1901
- [ ] **Wavelength range:** 0.01 nm to 10 nm (between gamma rays and UV)
- [ ] **Two production mechanisms:** Bremsstrahlung (continuous, ~80%) + Characteristic (discrete, ~20%)
- [ ] **Coolidge Tube (1913):** High-vacuum thermionic diode, used universally today
- [ ] **Filament material:** Tungsten (high melting point, good thermionic emitter)
- [ ] **Target material:** Tungsten (Z=74) or W-Re alloy; high Z for high bremsstrahlung yield
- [ ] **Duane-Hunt Law:** $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{1240}{V(\text{V})}$ — most-tested equation
- [ ] **Efficiency is ~1%:** 99% of input energy becomes heat — drives cooling system design
- [ ] **Line-focus principle:** Small effective focal spot + Large actual focal spot = sharp images + high power
- [ ] **Rotating anode speed:** 3000–10,000 RPM — distributes heat over a large area
- [ ] **kVp controls quality (penetration) AND quantity** of X-rays
- [ ] **mA controls only quantity (intensity)** of X-rays
- [ ] **Inverse Square Law:** $I \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$ — doubling distance reduces intensity to 1/4
- [ ] **Moseley's Law:** $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$ — basis of X-ray spectroscopy
- [ ] **Thermionic emission:** Follows Richardson-Dushman equation
- [ ] **Filtration purpose:** Removes low-energy ("soft") photons that would only harm the patient
- [ ] **Three primary properties:** Penetrating power, Ionizing nature, Photographic effect
- [ ] **Hard X-rays:** High energy (>50 keV), good penetration (used for thick body parts)
- [ ] **Soft X-rays:** Low energy (<30 keV), low penetration (used for mammography)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Memory Aid (KTU Viva & Exams):**
> 
> | Mnemonic | Meaning |
> |----------|---------|
> | **"FILAMENT"** | F-ocusing, I-nert gas-free, L-ow voltage, A-node-relative, M-olybdenum cup, E-mission thermionic, N-egative bias, T-ungsten coil |
> | **"BRAKES"** | **BR**emsstrahlung = **A**ccelerated electrons **K**inetic **E**nergy → **S**udden stop |
> | **"CHAR"** | **CH**aracteristic = **A**tom-**R**elaxation after inner-shell ejection |

---

**End of Module 1 Topic: X-Ray Production**

*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PCEBT602 | Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Aligned | NEP 2020 Compliant*

topics.title: Interactions in the Anode - Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic Radiations. Stationary and Rotating Anode X-ray Tubes. Electrical Circuit for an X-ray Unit - Autotransformer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interactions in the Anode - Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic Radiations. Stationary and Rotating Anode X-ray Tubes. Electrical Circuit for an X-ray Unit - Autotransformer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interactions in the Anode — Bremsstrahlung Radiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Bremsstrahlung** (German: "braking radiation") is the continuous-spectrum electromagnetic radiation produced when high-velocity electrons from the cathode are abruptly **decelerated and deflected** by the strong Coulomb (nuclear) field of the target (anode) atoms in an X-ray tube. The kinetic energy lost by the electrons is converted directly into photons of X-ray energy.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Car-Crash Photon":**
Imagine a high-speed sports car (the electron, traveling at nearly the speed of light) crashing into a row of massive concrete pillars (the tungsten nuclei of the anode). When the car slams into a pillar and brakes, you hear a loud "CRASH!" (energy released as sound). In the X-ray tube, when an electron slams into a tungsten nucleus and brakes, it releases a "CRASH" of energy in the form of an X-ray photon. Because each electron loses a *different* amount of energy in each collision (some bounce off glancingly, some hit head-on), the photons produced have a **continuous spread of energies** — this is why Bremsstrahlung forms a **continuous spectrum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant:** The rest mass energy of an electron is **m₀c² = 511 keV = 0.511 MeV**. For typical diagnostic X-ray tubes operating at **30–150 kVp**, the incident electron kinetic energy (up to 150 keV) is *non-relativistic* compared to 511 keV, so classical electrodynamics (not full QED) is sufficient to describe most tube physics.

---

## 1.2 Characteristic Radiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Characteristic X-radiation** consists of discrete, mono-energetic photons emitted when an incoming high-speed electron **ejects an inner-shell (K or L) electron** from a target atom, and an electron from a higher shell drops down to fill the vacancy. The emitted photon energy equals the difference in binding energies between the two shells and is *characteristic* of the target element.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Elevator Shaft Photon":**
Think of a tall office building (the atom) with many floors (electron shells: K, L, M, N…). The penthouse is the K-shell (lowest, most tightly bound). One day, a "bully electron" from the X-ray tube punches a K-shell worker right out of the building (ionization). The K-shell floor is now EMPTY. A worker from the L-shell one floor above drops down into the K-shell vacancy. As this worker falls, he screams — and that "scream" is the **Characteristic X-ray photon**. The pitch (energy) of the scream is fixed: it equals the difference in height between the K and L floors — and that pitch is unique to the building's construction material (tungsten, molybdenum, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention:** The radiation is named after the shell that received the electron, e.g., a K-shell vacancy filled by an L-shell electron produces a **Kα** photon; filled by an M-shell electron produces a **Kβ** photon. Moseley's Law (1913) states that the square-root of the characteristic X-ray frequency is linearly proportional to the atomic number: **√ν = a(Z − b)**.

---

## 1.3 Stationary Anode X-Ray Tube

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **stationary (fixed) anode X-ray tube** is one in which the tungsten target is a small, fixed insert embedded in a massive copper anode stem. All the electron-beam power is deposited at a single, stationary focal spot. Heat is removed primarily by **conduction** through the copper anode to the surrounding oil and housing.

**Intuition:** Think of a single candle flame held under a small coin. All the heat concentrates on one spot. The coin gets very hot, very fast, and you cannot put much power in or it will melt. That is the limitation of a stationary anode.

---

## 1.4 Rotating Anode X-Ray Tube

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **rotating anode X-ray tube** uses a large-diameter **tungsten-rhenium alloy disc** (typically 70–200 mm diameter) that rotates continuously at high speed (typically **3000–10 000 rpm**) by means of an induction motor. The electron beam strikes the **periphery** of the disc, so the focal track moves continuously, distributing the thermal load over a large annular area.

**Intuition:** Instead of holding a single candle under one spot of a coin, you spin the coin rapidly. The same heat energy is now spread over the entire rim. You can therefore put in 10× to 100× more power without melting anything. This is why modern diagnostic radiology uses rotating anodes.

---

## 1.5 Autotransformer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An **autotransformer** is a single-winding transformer in which a portion of the same winding acts as both the primary and the secondary. It is used in X-ray circuits as a **kVp selector**, providing finely adjustable alternating voltages (typically 0–250 V on the secondary taps) that are then stepped up to the high voltage (40–150 kV) by the high-voltage (step-up) transformer.

**Intuition:** Imagine a single long garden hose with multiple tap-off points along its length. The water pressure at the tap you choose depends on how far you are from the pump. By selecting different tap points, you get different pressures. Similarly, the autotransformer has a single coil with selectable "tap points" — choosing a different tap changes the voltage fed to the high-voltage transformer, which in turn changes the kVp applied to the X-ray tube.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bremsstrahlung — Mechanism and Spectrum

The process can be broken into structured physical steps:

1. **Electron emission:** The cathode filament (tungsten, ~2400 °C) emits electrons via thermionic emission (Richardson–Dushman law: J = A T² e^(−W/kT)).
2. **Acceleration:** The electrons are accelerated across the tube potential difference **V** (in kV), gaining kinetic energy **eV**.
3. **Interaction in target:** Each electron approaches a tungsten nucleus. The Coulomb attraction decelerates and deflects the electron.
4. **Photon emission:** By the conservation of energy, the lost kinetic energy is emitted as an X-ray photon of energy **hν**.
5. **Spectrum formation:** Because collision geometries (impact parameters **b**) vary from electron to electron, photon energies range from **0 up to eV (the maximum)** — producing a **continuous spectrum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Efficiency of Bremsstrahlung Production (Kramers' Law):**
> $$\eta = \frac{X\text{-ray power}}{e \cdot V \cdot I} \approx 9.2 \times 10^{-10} \cdot Z \cdot V$$
> where *Z* is the atomic number of the target and *V* is the tube voltage in volts.
> For tungsten (Z = 74) at 100 kVp, η ≈ **0.7 %** — i.e., over 99 % of the electrical input is wasted as **heat**. This is the central engineering challenge of X-ray tube design.

---

## 2.2 Characteristic Radiation — Mechanism and Lines

The KTU-relevant theoretical breakdown:

1. **K-shell ionization:** An incident electron with kinetic energy ≥ **binding energy of K-shell (E_K)** ejects a K-shell electron. For tungsten, E_K ≈ **69.5 keV**.
2. **Vacancy filling:** A higher-shell electron (L or M) drops to fill the K-shell vacancy.
3. **Energy release:** A photon is emitted with energy equal to the **difference in binding energies**:
   $$E_{K\alpha} = E_K - E_L \quad ; \quad E_{K\beta} = E_K - E_M$$
4. **Line spectrum:** The emitted photons have **discrete, fixed energies** — they form sharp lines superimposed on the Bremsstrahlung continuum.

For tungsten (W, Z = 74), the characteristic lines are approximately:
- **Kα ≈ 59.3 keV**
- **Kβ ≈ 67.2 keV**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Characteristic X-rays can only be produced when the tube voltage **exceeds the K-edge binding energy** of the target material. For tungsten, this means **V > 69.5 kVp**. Below this threshold, only Bremsstrahlung is produced.

---

## 2.3 Stationary vs. Rotating Anode — Design Trade-offs

| Parameter | Stationary Anode | Rotating Anode |
|---|---|---|
| **Focal spot** | Fixed | Moves along disc periphery |
| **Heat capacity** | ~ 50–200 kJ stored | ~ 200–800 kJ stored (larger mass) |
| **Heat dissipation mechanism** | Conduction only | Conduction **+ radiation** from disc surface |
| **Maximum loading** | ~ 1–3 kW continuous | ~ 10–50 kW continuous |
| **Applications** | Dental, mobile, mammography | General radiography, CT, fluoroscopy |
| **Cost / complexity** | Low, simple | High, requires induction motor & bearings |
| **Focal track velocity** | 0 m/s | v = 2π r n / 60 (e.g., ~ 60 m/s) |

The thermal advantage of the rotating anode can be quantified by the **ratio of focal track length swept per unit time** compared to a stationary spot.

---

## 2.4 The Autotransformer — Theory of Operation

1. The autotransformer has a **single continuous winding** on a laminated silicon-steel core.
2. The **primary** is connected across the entire winding (typically 220 V AC mains).
3. The **secondary** is tapped at various points along the winding — selecting different taps gives different secondary voltages.
4. The secondary voltage **V₂** of a single-tap autotransformer is given by:
   $$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$
   where N₂ is the number of turns from the common terminal to the selected tap.
5. In an X-ray unit, the autotransformer output (0–250 V) feeds the **primary** of the **high-voltage (step-up) transformer**, whose secondary produces the **40–150 kV** across the X-ray tube.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Safety / Wiring Note:** Because the autotransformer has a *single winding*, the primary and secondary sides are **not galvanically isolated**. This is one of its disadvantages — a fault on the secondary can appear on the primary. However, the autotransformer is smaller, cheaper, and more efficient (typically > 98 %) than an equivalent two-winding transformer.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision cheat sheet** for Module 1 derivations and numerical problems. Memorize these for the ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max X-ray photon energy | E_max = e · V | keV, with V in kV |
| 2 | Min wavelength (Duane–Hunt) | λ_min = (hc) / (eV) = 1240 / V | nm (V in kV) |
| 3 | Planck constant × speed of light | hc = 1240 eV·nm = 1.2398 × 10⁻⁶ eV·m | Constant |
| 4 | Bremsstrahlung efficiency (Kramers) | η = 9.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ · Z · V | dimensionless fraction |
| 5 | Heat in target | H = V · I · t − P_Xray | joules (W·s) |
| 6 | Heat storage (anode) | H = m · c · ΔT | J; c_W = 134 J/kg·K |
| 7 | Rotating anode focal track velocity | v_focal = π · D · n / 60 | m/s; D = anode diameter, n = rpm |
| 8 | Autotransformer ratio | V_2 / V_1 = N_2 / N_1 | single winding, tapped |
| 9 | Richardson–Dushman emission | J = A T² exp(−W/kT) | thermionic emission |
| 10 | Moseley's law | √ν = a (Z − b) | characteristic frequency |
| 11 | X-ray intensity ∝ | I ∝ Z · I_e · V² | integrated Bremsstrahlung |
| 12 | Step-up HV transformer | V_secondary = V_primary · (N_s / N_p) | kV range |

> [!TIP]
> **Unit Conversion Traps to Avoid:**
> - 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J
> - 1 keV = 10³ eV, 1 MeV = 10⁶ eV
> - Wavelength: 1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m, 1 Å = 10⁻¹⁰ m
> - **For Duane–Hunt:** λ_min (nm) = **1.240 / V (kV)**
> - 1 H.U. (Heat Unit) = 1 J in SI (or 0.24 W·s in older CGS texts)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Duane–Hunt Law (Minimum Wavelength)

**Statement:** The minimum wavelength of the continuous X-ray spectrum is inversely proportional to the tube voltage.

**Starting Principle:** Conservation of energy. The most energetic photon is produced when an electron loses **all** of its kinetic energy in a single head-on collision with a target nucleus.

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy of an accelerated electron:**
The electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference V (in volts). The work done on the electron by the electric field equals the kinetic energy gained:
$$E_K = eV$$
where e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C is the electron charge, and V is the accelerating voltage in volts.

**Step 2 — Maximum photon energy:**
In the most favourable (head-on) collision, the entire kinetic energy is converted into a single photon of frequency ν_max and wavelength λ_min. By Einstein's photon-energy relation:
$$E_{\text{photon}} = h\nu_{\max} = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

**Step 3 — Equate (energy conservation):**
By conservation of energy, the maximum photon energy equals the electron's kinetic energy:
$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}} = eV$$

**Step 4 — Solve for λ_min:**
$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{hc}{eV}$$

**Step 5 — Insert numerical constants (h, c, e):**
- h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s
- c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s
- e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- hc / e = (6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ × 3.0 × 10⁸) / (1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹) = 1.2408 × 10⁻⁶ V·m

**Step 6 — Final compact form:**
$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.2408 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \text{ m}$$

If V is in kV, this simplifies to the KTU exam-ready form:
$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min}(\text{nm}) = \frac{1.240}{V(\text{kV})}}$$

**Numerical Example (typical ESE problem):**
An X-ray tube is operated at **80 kVp**. Find the minimum wavelength.
*Solution:* λ_min = 1.240 / 80 = **0.0155 nm = 0.155 Å**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bremsstrahlung Efficiency (Kramers' Law)

**Statement:** The fraction of electron-beam power converted into X-ray Bremsstrahlung is proportional to the atomic number Z of the target and the tube voltage V.

**Step 1 — Energy radiated by a decelerating charge (Larmor / Lienard):**
For a non-relativistic electron of charge *e* and acceleration *a*, the instantaneous radiated power (Larmor formula) is:
$$P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{e^2 a^2}{6 \pi \varepsilon_0 c^3}$$

**Step 2 — Replace acceleration with deceleration from Coulomb field:**
The acceleration experienced by an electron passing a nucleus of charge *Ze* at impact parameter *b* is of order:
$$a \propto \frac{Z e^2}{m_e b^2}$$

**Step 3 — Average over all impact parameters:**
Integrating P_rad over the area π b² with proper relativistic corrections (Bethe–Heitler cross-section in the low-energy limit) yields the total radiated energy per electron, which scales as:
$$E_{\text{rad}} \propto Z \cdot \left(\frac{eV}{m_e c^2}\right) \cdot eV$$

**Step 4 — Define efficiency η = X-ray power / electron-beam power:**
$$\eta = \frac{E_{\text{rad}}}{eV} \propto Z \cdot \frac{eV}{m_e c^2}$$

**Step 5 — Insert the fundamental constants:**
With e / (m_e c²) = 1 / 511 000 V, the proportionality constant evaluates to ≈ 9.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ V⁻¹:
$$\boxed{\eta \approx 9.2 \times 10^{-10} \cdot Z \cdot V}$$
where V is the peak tube voltage in volts, and Z is the atomic number of the target.

**Numerical Example:**
For tungsten (Z = 74) at 100 kVp (V = 100 000 V):
*Solution:* η = 9.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ × 74 × 100 000 = **0.0068 = 0.68 %**.
So 99.32 % of the input electrical power is converted to **heat** in the anode.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rotating Anode Heat Loading

**Step 1 — Focal track length swept per exposure:**
The electron beam strikes a small region of the anode. In a stationary anode, this region is fixed. In a rotating anode of diameter D rotating at n rpm, the focal point sweeps a circular path of length:
$$L_{\text{track}} = \pi D$$

**Step 2 — Effective heat dissipation per unit time:**
The effective "linear velocity" of the focal point is:
$$v_{\text{focal}} = \frac{\pi D \cdot n}{60} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Heat input rate:**
The heat deposited per unit time is the difference between the input electron power and the X-ray power (negligibly small):
$$P_{\text{heat}} \approx V \cdot I$$

**Step 4 — Heat per unit length of focal track:**
$$H' = \frac{P_{\text{heat}}}{v_{\text{focal}}} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{\pi D \cdot n}$$

This shows that heat loading **decreases** with higher rotation speed *n* and larger anode diameter *D*, which is why high-power tubes use both large discs (≥ 150 mm) and high-speed rotors (≥ 9000 rpm).

**Numerical Example:**
A rotating anode has D = 120 mm, n = 3000 rpm, V = 100 kV, I = 500 mA. Find the focal track velocity and heat per unit length.
*Solution:*
- v_focal = π × 0.120 × 3000 / 60 = **18.85 m/s**
- H' = (100 000 × 0.5 × 60) / (π × 0.120 × 3000) = 3 000 000 / 1130.97 ≈ **2653 W/m**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Autotransformer Voltage Relation

**Step 1 — Single winding of N_total turns:**
Let the entire winding have N_total turns, with the primary connected across all N_total turns at input voltage V_1.

**Step 2 — Tapped secondary with N_2 turns:**
The output is taken across N_2 turns (counted from the common terminal, often at one end of the winding).

**Step 3 — Transformer EMF equation (per turn):**
For an ideal transformer, the voltage per turn is constant:
$$\frac{V_1}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{V_2}{N_2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V_2:**
$$\boxed{V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_{\text{total}}}}$$

In an X-ray circuit, V_2 is then fed to the step-up HV transformer, which multiplies it by (N_s / N_p) — typically a ratio of about **500:1 to 1000:1** — to produce the **40–150 kV** on the X-ray tube.

**Numerical Example:**
An autotransformer has N_total = 1000 turns connected to 220 V AC. The selected tap is at N_2 = 800 turns. Find V_2.
*Solution:* V_2 = 220 × (800 / 1000) = **176 V AC**.
This 176 V is then stepped up by a 1:600 HV transformer to 176 × 600 = **105.6 kV**, which becomes the kVp applied to the X-ray tube (ignoring rectifier losses).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete X-Ray Production Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC["AC MAINS 220V 50Hz"] --> AUT["AUTOTRANSFORMER kVp Selector"]
    AUT --> HV["HIGH VOLTAGE STEP UP TRANSFORMER"]
    HV --> REC["RECTIFIER BRIDGE 4 Diodes"]
    REC --> TUBE["X RAY TUBE Anode and Cathode"]
    FIL["FILAMENT TRANSFORMER mA Selector"] --> TUBE
    TUBE --> SP["X RAY BEAM Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic"]
    TUBE --> HEAT["ANODE HEAT SINK Oil or Radiator"]
    SP --> PAT["PATIENT Imaging Receptor"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** AC mains (220 V) is fed to the autotransformer (kV selector). The selected low voltage is stepped up to kV range by the high-voltage transformer, rectified to DC (pulsating for single-phase, near-constant for three-phase), and applied across the X-ray tube. A separate low-voltage filament transformer heats the cathode. The tube emits an X-ray beam (Bremsstrahlung + characteristic), which is partially absorbed by the patient and the rest exposes the image receptor.

---

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Bremsstrahlung vs. Characteristic X-Ray Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ELECTRON["High Speed Electron from Cathode eV kinetic energy"] --> DECIDE{"Type of Interaction"}
    DECIDE -- "Glancing near nucleus" --> BREM["BREMSSTRAHLUNG Continuous Spectrum 0 to eV"]
    DECIDE -- "Direct hit on inner shell electron" --> CHAR["CHARACTERISTIC Discrete Lines K alpha K beta"]
    BREM --> OUT1["X RAY BEAM Photon Energy h nu variable"]
    CHAR --> OUT2["X RAY BEAM Photon Energy equals E shell difference"]
    OUT1 --> PAT["Exits tube window and forms image"]
    OUT2 --> PAT
```

---

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram — Stationary vs. Rotating Anode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAT["STATIONARY ANODE TUBE"]
        S1["Cathode Filament"]
        S2["Fixed Tungsten Target on Copper Stem"]
        S3["Heat Conducted to Oil Bath"]
        S1 --> S2
        S2 --> S3
    end
    subgraph ROT["ROTATING ANODE TUBE"]
        R1["Cathode Filament"]
        R2["Rotating Tungsten Rhenium Disc 3000 to 10000 rpm"]
        R3["Induction Motor Stator outside tube"]
        R4["Heat Radiated from Disc Surface"]
        R1 --> R2
        R3 --> R2
        R2 --> R4
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Electrical Circuit of an X-Ray Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["220V AC Mains"] --> B["Main Switch and Fuse"]
    B --> C["Autotransformer T1 with Multiple Taps"]
    C --> D["Primary of HV Transformer T2"]
    D --> E["HV Secondary 40 to 150 kV"]
    E --> F["Rectifier Bridge HV Diodes"]
    F --> G["X Ray Tube Cathode to Anode"]
    H["Filament Transformer T3"] --> I["Cathode Filament"]
    C --> H
    J["mA Meter in Series"] --> G
    K["kVp Meter in Parallel"] --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Sequence:**
> 1. Operator sets **mA** on the filament-transformer primary → controls tube current.
> 2. Operator sets **kVp** on the autotransformer tap → controls tube voltage.
> 3. The HV transformer steps up voltage to tens of kV.
> 4. The rectifier converts AC to pulsating DC applied across the tube.
> 5. Exposure timer (not shown) controls the **mAs** delivered.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCEBT602]

**Differentiate between Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic X-radiation. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bremsstrahlung | Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Deceleration of electrons in nuclear Coulomb field | Electron transition between atomic shells |
| **Spectrum type** | Continuous (0 to eV) | Discrete line spectrum |
| **Dependence on Z** | Intensity ∝ Z | Energy ∝ (Z − b)² (Moseley) |
| **Energy control** | Set by tube voltage V | Set by target material (fixed) |
| **Direction** | Forward-peaked (anisotropic) | Isotropic |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEBT602]

**Why is tungsten (Z = 74) chosen as the target material in most diagnostic X-ray tubes? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High atomic number (Z = 74):** Increases Bremsstrahlung efficiency (η ∝ Z) and produces high X-ray intensity.
2. **High melting point (3422 °C):** Withstands the enormous heat flux (~10⁶ W/cm²) at the focal spot.
3. **High density (19.3 g/cm³):** Provides good stopping power for high-energy electrons in a small volume.
4. **Reasonable thermal conductivity (170 W/m·K):** Allows heat to be conducted away from the focal spot.
5. **K-edge at 69.5 keV:** Characteristic K-radiation enhances the diagnostic beam at typical kVp values.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Convention:** *Attempt any ONE full question from the choice provided. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a rotating anode X-ray tube. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
1. **Cathode assembly:** A tungsten filament mounted in a focusing cup, heated by a low-voltage filament transformer (typical 3–5 V, 3–5 A).
2. **Anode assembly:** A bevelled (typically 7°–20°) disc of **tungsten-rhenium alloy** (90 % W + 10 % Re) brazed onto a molybdenum disc, mounted on a rotor shaft.
3. **Induction motor:** A stator winding OUTSIDE the evacuated glass/metal envelope drives the rotor via electromagnetic induction — there are no mechanical feedthroughs, preserving vacuum integrity.
4. **Envelope:** Borosilicate glass or metal-ceramic, with an X-ray exit window of low-absorption beryllium.
5. **High-voltage insulation:** The tube is immersed in high-dielectric oil inside the housing.

**Working:**
1. The rotor is spun up to 3000–10 000 rpm.
2. The cathode filament is heated, emitting electrons (thermionic emission).
3. A high voltage (40–150 kVp) is applied between cathode and anode.
4. Electrons accelerate and strike the periphery of the rotating disc.
5. The focal spot is therefore a small region that moves along the disc rim, distributing heat over the entire track.
6. X-rays (Bremsstrahlung + characteristic) emerge through the beryllium window in a forward-directed cone.

**Advantages over stationary anode:**
- 10×–100× higher heat capacity and loading.
- Smaller effective focal spot possible (improving geometric sharpness) at the same power.
- Heat is dissipated by **radiation** from the disc surface, not just conduction.

**(b) With the help of Kramers' law, calculate the Bremsstrahlung efficiency of a tungsten target X-ray tube operating at 120 kVp. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write Kramers' Law:**
$$\eta = 9.2 \times 10^{-10} \cdot Z \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Insert Z = 74 (tungsten) and V = 120 000 V (since 120 kVp):**
$$\eta = 9.2 \times 10^{-10} \times 74 \times 120\,000$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**
$$\eta = 9.2 \times 10^{-10} \times 8\,880\,000 = 8.17 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage:**
$$\eta = 0.817\,\% \approx 0.82\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
Only **0.82 %** of the input electrical power is radiated as useful X-rays. The remaining **99.18 %** is deposited as heat in the anode — this is why heat management is the central design challenge.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) Describe with diagrams the production of Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic X-radiations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Bremsstrahlung:**
1. High-energy electron approaches a target nucleus.
2. The electron is decelerated and deflected by the strong Coulomb field of the nucleus.
3. A continuous spectrum of X-ray photons is emitted.
4. Maximum photon energy = eV, giving minimum wavelength (Duane–Hunt): λ_min = 1.240 / V (nm).
5. Intensity is forward-peaked and ∝ Z · V².

[Diagram: electron trajectory curving around nucleus with photon emitted tangentially]

**Characteristic:**
1. Incoming electron ejects a K-shell electron of the target atom (requires V ≥ K-edge energy).
2. A vacancy is left in the K-shell.
3. An L- or M-shell electron drops down to fill the vacancy.
4. The energy difference is released as a photon: Kα = E_K − E_L, Kβ = E_K − E_M.
5. Discrete, line spectrum characteristic of the target element.

[Diagram: K-shell vacancy, L-shell electron dropping in, photon emitted]

**(b) Explain the construction and role of the autotransformer in an X-ray unit. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
- A single continuous winding of insulated copper wire on a laminated silicon-steel core.
- The full winding is connected across the AC mains (typically 220 V, 50 Hz).
- Multiple **taps** are brought out at different points along the winding — one common terminal plus selectable taps.
- A rotary or slider selector switch connects the chosen tap to the load.

**Role / Function:**
- Acts as the **kVp selector** in the X-ray circuit.
- The selected low voltage (typically 0–250 V) is fed to the primary of the **high-voltage step-up transformer**.
- The HV transformer then steps this up to 40–150 kV for the X-ray tube.
- Different taps → different kV on the tube → different beam energy.

**Advantages:**
1. Compact and lightweight (single winding).
2. High efficiency (> 98 %, low copper losses).
3. Continuously or step-wise variable output voltage.
4. Lower cost than a two-winding transformer of the same rating.

**Disadvantages:**
1. No galvanic isolation between primary and secondary.
2. Short-circuit currents can be very high due to low leakage impedance.

**Key Equation:**
$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_{\text{total}}}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Module 1 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing kVp with keV:** kVp is *peak* tube *voltage* in kV; keV is photon *energy*. Numerical problems often require converting kVp → V (volts) → energy (eV) for the Duane–Hunt law.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert nm to Å or m:** λ_min = 0.0155 nm = 0.155 Å = 1.55 × 10⁻¹¹ m. Examiners test unit conversions deliberately.
> 3. **Mixing Bremsstrahlung & Characteristic in one answer:** Clearly state *which* mechanism, *what* energy range, and *what* target material.
> 4. **Kramers' law in % not fraction:** Always report η as a **percentage** (multiply by 100) for the final answer.
> 5. **Autotransformer — drawing single winding with taps, not two separate windings:** This is a common diagram-mark deduction. Use one coil with multiple tap-off arrows.
> 6. **Forgetting the "common" terminal in autotransformer explanation:** Mark examiners look for explicit identification of the common point between primary and secondary.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1**
>
> - **Bremsstrahlung = braking radiation = continuous spectrum;** arises from Coulomb deceleration of electrons; intensity ∝ Z · V²; efficiency η = 9.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ Z V ≈ 1 % at 100 kVp for W.
> - **Characteristic radiation = line spectrum;** arises from inner-shell ionization followed by electron transition; discrete energies; requires V ≥ K-edge; named Kα, Kβ, etc.
> - **Duane–Hunt law:** λ_min (nm) = 1.240 / V (kV). Memorize this constant.
> - **Moseley's law:** √ν = a (Z − b); characteristic X-ray frequency increases with Z².
> - **Stationary anode:** simple, low-power, dental/mobile; heat dissipated by conduction only; focal spot fixed.
> - **Rotating anode:** high-power, general radiography/CT; tungsten-rhenium disc spun at 3000–10 000 rpm by induction motor; heat spread along focal track.
> - **Focal track velocity:** v = π D n / 60; higher n and D → better heat spreading.
> - **Heat storage formula:** H = m c ΔT; for W, c ≈ 134 J/kg·K.
> - **Autotransformer:** single winding with multiple taps; used as kVp selector; V₂ = V₁ · (N₂ / N_total); compact, efficient, but no galvanic isolation.
> - **High-voltage (step-up) transformer:** takes 0–250 V from autotransformer and steps up to 40–150 kV across tube.
> - **Tungsten (Z = 74)** preferred target: high Z, high melting point (3422 °C), high density, K-edge at 69.5 keV.
> - **Numbers to memorize:** hc = 1240 eV·nm; eV (for 100 kV) = 100 keV; η at 100 kVp tungsten ≈ 0.7 %.
> - **Total heat loss** to anode ≈ 99 % of input electrical power — *this is the single most important engineering fact in diagnostic X-ray tube design.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Timer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCEBT602 – Principles of Radio Diagnosis & Radiotherapy**, a **Timer** is defined as a precision electromechanical or electronic device integrated into the X-ray generating circuit whose primary function is to **accurately control the duration of X-ray tube exposure** (the time interval during which high voltage is applied across the X-ray tube and X-ray photons are emitted).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A timer is a switching device that makes and breaks the high-voltage circuit (or the filament circuit in modern equipment) for a precise, pre-selected interval of time, thereby determining the radiographic exposure in conjunction with the tube current (mA).

It is one of the most critical sub-systems of an **X-ray control panel** because it directly governs the **quantity of radiation** reaching the patient and the image receptor. According to KTU Module 1 syllabus, this falls under the study of the **X-ray circuit and its components**, alongside the autotransformer, high-tension transformer, and rectification system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Camera Shutter Analogy:**
> Imagine you are using a **DSLR camera** in a dark room. The photographer has two knobs — one for **brightness of flash (mA)** and one for **how long the shutter stays open (Timer)**. If the shutter stays open too long, the photo becomes overexposed (blown-out white image). If it closes too quickly, the photo becomes underexposed (pitch-black image). The **timer is exactly like the shutter** — it determines *how long* the X-rays will be allowed to "shine" onto the patient.

In diagnostic radiology, the exposure must be precise. A timer error of even **50 milliseconds (0.05 s)** can be the difference between a diagnostic-quality radiograph and a **useless, underexposed film** that must be repeated — exposing the patient to **avoidable ionizing radiation** (ALARA principle violation).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Exposure Time | $t$ | **seconds (s)** | Duration of X-ray emission |
| Tube Current | $I$ | **milliampere (mA)** | Quantity of electrons hitting target |
| Exposure Quantity | $Q$ | **mAs** | Product of mA and time |
| Minimum Time Step | $t_{min}$ | **1/60 s to 1/120 s** | Resolution of modern timers |
| Switching Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | AC supply frequency (50/60 Hz) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of X-Ray Timers

X-ray timers have evolved significantly. For KTU Module 1, you must know the following categories in a chronological order of technological advancement:

1. **Mechanical Timer** (spring-loaded, hand-wound)
2. **Synchronous Motor Timer** (AC motor driven, mains-frequency dependent)
3. **Electronic Timer** (RC discharge, transistor-thyristor based)
4. **mAs Timer** (monitors actual charge delivered to X-ray tube)
5. **Photoelectric Timer / Phototimer** (measures light passing through cassette)
6. **Ionization Chamber Timer** (modern standard, measures radiation directly)

### 2.2 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Mechanism

The "Why and How" behind each timer class:

#### A. Mechanical Timer
- **Why:** Earliest design, simple to build before electronics existed.
- **How:** A clock-spring stores mechanical energy. When the exposure button is pressed, the spring unwinds through a gear train, which closes a switch contact for a calibrated duration. The operator selects the time by changing the gear ratio.

#### B. Synchronous Motor Timer
- **Why:** More accurate than mechanical — uses **mains frequency (50 Hz in India)** as the time-base reference.
- **How:** A small **synchronous AC motor** rotates at a constant speed (locked to 50/60 Hz). A cam attached to the motor shaft physically opens the high-voltage contactor after a pre-set number of rotations. Because motor speed is line-frequency dependent, exposure times are quantized in steps of 1/50 s = **20 ms** (in India) or 1/60 s = **16.67 ms** (in USA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The minimum exposure time of a synchronous timer is determined by the formula:
> $$t_{min} = \frac{1}{f_{mains}}$$
> For India ($f = 50\,\text{Hz}$), $t_{min} = \mathbf{1/50 = 0.02\,\text{s = 20\,\text{ms}}$.

#### C. Electronic Timer (RC-Discharge)
- **Why:** Provides smoother time selection in fractional seconds (e.g., 0.05 s, 0.08 s, 0.12 s).
- **How:** A **resistor-capacitor (RC) network** is charged. When exposure begins, the capacitor discharges through the resistor. A comparator circuit detects when voltage drops below a threshold and breaks the exposure circuit. Time constant $\tau = RC$.

#### D. mAs Timer
- **Why:** Compensates for mains voltage fluctuations which would otherwise alter the tube current.
- **How:** A current-measuring coil or Hall-effect sensor integrates the actual current flowing through the X-ray tube. When the integrated value $\int I \cdot dt$ equals the preset **mAs**, a thyratron (or SCR) breaks the circuit. This is an **absolute quantity timer**, not a time-based timer.

#### E. Ionization Chamber Timer (Phototimer)
- **Why:** It is a **patient-dose optimization timer** — terminates exposure as soon as the film/sensor receives enough radiation for proper density.
- **How:** A parallel-plate ionization chamber is placed **between the patient and the image receptor**. X-rays passing through the patient ionize the air in the chamber. The resulting ionization current charges a capacitor. When capacitor voltage reaches the threshold matching correct film density, the exposure is terminated automatically.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:----:|
| 1 | $Q = I \times t$ | Exposure quantity (charge) — **mAs** | mA·s |
| 2 | $mAs = mA \times s$ | Milliampere-seconds relationship | mA·s |
| 3 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{1}{f_{mains}}$ | Minimum timer step (synchronous timer) | s |
| 4 | $t_{min} = RC \ln\left(\dfrac{V_{initial}}{V_{threshold}}\right)$ | RC electronic timer threshold time | s |
| 5 | $Q_{delivered} = \int_{0}^{t} I(t)\,dt$ | mAs timer integration logic | mA·s |
| 6 | $HU = kVp \times mAs$ | Heat Units generated in anode | kVp·mA·s |
| 7 | $D \propto mAs$ | Patient entrance dose proportionality | mGy |
| 8 | $N_{photons} \propto mAs$ | X-ray photon output proportionality | photons/mm² |
| 9 | $t = \dfrac{mAs_{preset}}{mA_{preset}}$ | Reciprocity rule for time calculation | s |
| 10 | $f_{rotation} = \dfrac{f_{mains}}{P}$ | Synchronous motor speed (P = pole pairs) | rev/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocity Law:** The radiographic effect depends on $mAs$, not on the individual $mA$ or $s$ values. Therefore $100\,\text{mA} \times 0.1\,\text{s} = 10\,\text{mAs} = 200\,\text{mA} \times 0.05\,\text{s}$. This is the foundation of timer design — the device must reproduce the same $mAs$ regardless of fluctuations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **modern digital radiography (DR)** and **CT scanners**, the timer is no longer just a clock — it is a software-controlled pulse-width modulation (PWM) system that can deliver exposures as short as **1 ms** (for cine/angiography) and as long as several seconds (for radiotherapy simulators). The KTU Module 1 syllabus treats the timer as the **gatekeeper of patient safety** because incorrect timing leads to **repeat exposures**, directly increasing **cumulative stochastic risk** (carcinogenesis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Exposure Time of a Synchronous Timer

**Given:** An Indian X-ray unit operating on $50\,\text{Hz}$ mains with a 4-pole synchronous timer motor.

**Step 1 — Identify the rotational speed of the synchronous motor.**
The synchronous speed in revolutions per second is given by:
$$N_s = \frac{f}{P}$$

where $P$ is the number of **pole pairs**. For a 4-pole motor, $P = 2$.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$N_s = \frac{50}{2} = 25\,\text{revolutions per second}$$

**Step 3 — Find the time for one revolution.**
$$t_{rev} = \frac{1}{N_s} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the minimum exposure (one half-revolution of cam).**
The cam typically engages the contactor for a half-revolution:
$$t_{min} = \frac{t_{rev}}{2} = \frac{0.04}{2} = \mathbf{0.02\,\text{s = 20\,\text{ms}}$$

> **Conclusion:** This explains why Indian X-ray machines cannot reliably deliver exposures shorter than **1/50 second**, whereas American machines (60 Hz) can go down to **1/60 ≈ 16.67 ms**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Exposure Time from mAs Setting

**Problem:** A radiographer selects $200\,\text{mA}$ and $50\,\text{mAs}$ on the control panel. Calculate the exposure time.

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation.**
$$mAs = mA \times t$$

**Step 2 — Solve for time $t$.**
$$t = \frac{mAs}{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**
$$t = \frac{50}{200}$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**
$$t = 0.25\,\text{s}$$

> **Conclusion:** The exposure will last exactly **0.25 seconds** (one-quarter of a second).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Verification of RC Timer Calibration

**Problem:** An electronic RC timer has $R = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, $C = 10\,\mu\text{F}$, threshold ratio $V_{th}/V_0 = 0.368$. Find the exposure time.

**Step 1 — Compute the RC time constant.**
$$\tau = R \times C = (1 \times 10^6) \times (10 \times 10^{-6}) = 10\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the capacitor discharge equation.**
$$V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$ when $V(t)/V_0 = 0.368$.**
$$0.368 = e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm.**
$$\ln(0.368) = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Note that $\ln(0.368) = -1$.**
$$-1 = -\frac{t}{10}$$

**Step 6 — Solve.**
$$\boxed{t = 10\,\text{s}}$$

> **Conclusion:** With these component values, the timer can be calibrated for any exposure duration by adjusting the comparator threshold voltage, allowing fine-grained control in the **millisecond range**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Heat Unit (HU) Accumulation in the Anode

**Problem:** During a fluoroscopy exposure at $80\,\text{kVp}$, $4\,\text{mA}$, lasting $3\,\text{minutes}$, calculate the Heat Units stored.

**Step 1 — Convert time to seconds.**
$$t = 3\,\text{min} \times 60 = 180\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate mAs.**
$$mAs = 4 \times 180 = 720\,\text{mAs}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Heat Unit formula.**
$$HU = kVp \times mAs$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**
$$HU = 80 \times 720 = \mathbf{57{,}600\,\text{HU}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The timer must be designed to prevent anode melt-down by limiting continuous exposure; modern units include **thermal interlock** that overrides the timer if HU exceeds anode rating (typically $200{,}000$–$300{,}000$ HU for diagnostic tubes).

### 3.5 Reciprocity Law Demonstration Matrix

| Trial | $mA$ | $t$ (s) | $mAs$ | Image Density (Relative) |
|:-----:|:----:|:-------:|:-----:|:-----------------------:|
| 1 | 100 | 0.50 | 50 | 1.00 |
| 2 | 200 | 0.25 | 50 | 1.00 |
| 3 | 400 | 0.125 | 50 | 1.00 |
| 4 | 50 | 1.00 | 50 | 1.00 |
| 5 | 25 | 2.00 | 50 | 1.00 |

> **Insight:** This table demonstrates the **Bunsen-Roscoe Reciprocity Law**. The timer design must therefore be a *quantity-controlled* device, not a strict time clock — which is exactly why the **mAs timer** is superior to a pure mechanical timer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — X-Ray Timer System

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional integration** of the timer with the rest of the X-ray circuit (this is an architecture-level diagram, not a physical drawing).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Console] -->|Select mA kVp Time| B[Timer Selector Circuit]
    B --> C{Synchronous Motor Timer}
    B --> D{Electronic RC Timer}
    B --> E{mAs Integrator Timer}
    B --> F[Ionization Chamber Phototimer]
    C --> G[High Voltage Contactor Coil]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|Makes/Breaks HV| H[High Tension Transformer]
    H --> I[X-Ray Tube]
    I -->|X-ray beam| J[Patient Body]
    J --> K[Image Receptor]
    K -->|Transmitted radiation| F
    I -->|Heat| L[Anode Heat Load]
    E -->|Charge feedback| M[Thyratron SCR Latch]
    F -->|Ion current| N[Capacitor Voltage Comparator]
    N -->|Trigger signal| G
    B --> O[Safety Interlock Circuit]
    O -->|Override| G
    M -->|Latch break| G
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Exposure Termination Logic

This diagram maps the decision logic inside the timer circuitry from exposure start to termination.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Exposure Button Pressed]) --> PreCheck{All Safety Interlocks Closed?}
    PreCheck -->|No| Abort1[BLOCK Exposure - Safety Open]
    PreCheck -->|Yes| Rot[Rotor Spins Up - Synchronous Motor]
    Rot --> HVOn[High Voltage Contact Closes]
    HVOn --> XrayGen[X-Ray Production Begins]
    XrayGen --> Mon{Quantity OR Time Reached?}
    Mon -->|No| XrayGen
    Mon -->|Yes - mAs| Term1[Thyratron Fires - Circuit Breaks]
    Mon -->|Yes - Time| Term2[Cam Opens Contactor]
    Mon -->|Yes - Phototimer| Term3[Ionization Threshold Tripped]
    Term1 --> End([Exposure Terminated])
    Term2 --> End
    Term3 --> End
    Abort1 --> End
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Old vs Modern Timer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OldSystem[Conventional Timer Unit]
        O1[Mechanical Spring] --> O2[Gear Train]
        O2 --> O3[Cam Operated Switch]
        O3 --> O4[HV Contactor]
    end
    subgraph ModernSystem[Modern mAs Phototimer Unit]
        M1[Mains Frequency Reference 50Hz] --> M2[Oscillator Divider]
        M2 --> M3[Digital Counter]
        M3 --> M4[MOSFET/SCR Switch]
        X1[X-Ray Tube Current Sensor] --> X2[Integrator op-amp]
        X2 --> X3[Comparator with preset mAs]
        X3 --> X4[MOSFET/SCR Switch]
        Y1[Ion Chamber] --> Y2[Charge Amplifier]
        Y2 --> Y3[Voltage Comparator]
        Y3 --> Y4[MOSFET/SCR Switch]
        M4 --> Z[HV Transformer Primary]
        X4 --> Z
        Y4 --> Z
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define an X-ray timer. List any **four types** of timers used in diagnostic X-ray equipment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
An X-ray timer is a precision switching device that controls the **duration of X-ray exposure** by making and breaking the high-voltage circuit (or filament circuit) for a pre-selected interval of time, thereby determining the total quantity of radiation (mAs) delivered to the patient.

**Types (2 marks — any four):**
1. Mechanical (spring-loaded) timer
2. Synchronous motor timer
3. Electronic (RC discharge) timer
4. mAs (quantity-based) timer
5. Photoelectric / Phototimer
6. Ionization chamber timer

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
State the **Reciprocity Law** in radiography. Mention **two limitations** of the mechanical timer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reciprocity Law (2 marks):**
The Bunsen-Roscoe Reciprocity Law states that the **photographic/ radiographic effect** on the image receptor depends only on the **total exposure quantity (mAs)**, not on the individual values of tube current ($mA$) or exposure time ($t$) separately. Mathematically:
$$\text{Effect} = mA \times t = \text{constant}$$

Therefore, $100\,\text{mA} \times 0.5\,\text{s}$ and $500\,\text{mA} \times 0.1\,\text{s}$ both produce identical film density (assuming $mAs = 50$).

**Limitations of mechanical timer (1 mark — any two):**
1. Poor accuracy at short exposure times (cannot reliably go below $\sim 0.1\,\text{s}$).
2. Affected by spring-fatigue and mechanical wear; requires periodic recalibration.
3. Not compensated for mains voltage fluctuations — actual mA may vary even if time is correct.
4. Limited selectable time steps; no fractional-second precision.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [Choice 1]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**a)** Explain in detail the **construction and working of a Synchronous Motor Timer** with a neat functional block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With the help of suitable derivations, explain why a **synchronous timer cannot deliver exposures shorter than 1/50 s in India**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution A(a) — Synchronous Motor Timer (7 marks)

The synchronous motor timer is the **most widely used timer in conventional X-ray units**. Its accuracy is derived from the constant frequency of the AC mains supply.

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Synchronous AC motor:** A small shaded-pole or permanent-capacitor motor with a definite number of poles (commonly 4-pole, hence 2 pole-pairs).
