2. **Reduction gear train:** Connects the motor shaft to a calibrated cam shaft, allowing different time ranges.
3. **Cam and microswitch assembly:** The cam is shaped to engage and disengage a microswitch that controls the high-voltage contactor coil.
4. **Time selector dial:** Allows the radiographer to choose exposure time by varying the cam angle or gear ratio.
5. **Brake mechanism:** An electromagnetic brake stops the cam precisely at the end of exposure to prevent overrun.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. When the **exposure button** is pressed, the timer motor is energized and starts rotating at synchronous speed ($1500\,\text{rpm}$ for 4-pole, $50\,\text{Hz}$ supply).
2. Simultaneously, the high-voltage contactor closes, applying high voltage to the X-ray tube.
3. After the pre-selected number of motor rotations (or cam angle), the cam profile disengages the microswitch.
4. The microswitch breaks the contactor coil circuit, the high voltage collapses, and X-ray production ceases within milliseconds.
5. The electromagnetic brake stops the cam instantly to prevent timing errors.

**Functional block diagram (1 mark):**
Refer to Section 4.1 diagram — the `Synchronous Motor Timer` block feeds the `High Voltage Contactor Coil`.

#### Model Solution A(b) — Why 1/50 s is the minimum (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Fundamental physics of synchronous motors:**
A synchronous motor rotates at a speed strictly locked to the supply frequency. The relationship between frequency $f$, number of poles $p$, and synchronous speed $N_s$ (in RPM) is:
$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{p}$$

**Step 2 — For 50 Hz, 4-pole motor:**
$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\,\text{RPM}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to revolutions per second:**
$$N_s = \frac{1500}{60} = 25\,\text{rev/s}$$

**Step 4 — Time for one revolution:**
$$t_{rev} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04\,\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Cam typically engages microswitch for a half-revolution:**
$$t_{min} = \frac{t_{rev}}{2} = \frac{0.04}{2} = 0.02\,\text{s} = \frac{1}{50}\,\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (2 marks):**
Because the synchronous motor cannot rotate faster than its line-locked speed, the cam cannot actuate the microswitch in less than one half-revolution. Hence, the **shortest exposure achievable on Indian mains is 1/50 s = 20 ms**. To get shorter exposures (e.g., for pediatric or high-speed angiography work), **electronic or mAs timers** must be used, as they are not frequency-locked.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [Choice 2 — Alternative]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**a)** Describe the principle, construction, and working of an **Ionization Chamber Phototimer**. Mention **two advantages** over a synchronous timer. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An X-ray unit is set at $300\,\text{mA}$ and the radiographer needs $45\,\text{mAs}$. The mains frequency is $50\,\text{Hz}$. Calculate: **(i)** the exposure time in seconds, **(ii)** the number of revolutions the 4-pole synchronous motor will make during this exposure, and **(iii)** the Heat Units if the kVp is set at $70$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B(a) — Ionization Chamber Phototimer (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
The ionization chamber phototimer operates on the principle that **X-ray intensity after passing through the patient is proportional to the radiation reaching the image receptor**. A flat parallel-plate ionization chamber placed between the patient and the cassette produces a small ionization current proportional to the X-ray intensity. This current is integrated on a capacitor; when the integrated charge reaches a preset threshold (corresponding to correct film optical density), the exposure is automatically terminated.

**Construction (2 marks):**
1. **Parallel-plate ionization chamber:** A thin air-filled chamber (typically $3$–$5\,\text{cm}$ thick) divided into multiple cells to cover the cassette area uniformly.
2. **High-voltage bias electrode:** Polarizes the chamber (typically $\pm 400\,\text{V}$) so that ions are collected efficiently.
3. **Charge-sensitive amplifier:** Amplifies the tiny ionization current ($\sim 10^{-9}\,\text{A}$) into a measurable voltage.
4. **Comparator circuit with reference voltage:** Determines the threshold for termination.
5. **SCR / thyratron switching stage:** Breaks the high-voltage contactor when threshold is reached.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. The chamber is positioned just **above the cassette**, beneath the patient.
2. When X-ray exposure begins, radiation transmitted through the patient ionizes the air inside the chamber.
3. The resulting current charges a capacitor in the comparator circuit.
4. When capacitor voltage equals the preset reference (corresponding to correct film density), the SCR fires and breaks the high-voltage circuit.
5. Exposure ends within $\sim 1\,\text{ms}$ of the threshold being reached.

**Two advantages over synchronous timer (1 mark):**
1. **Automatic density control:** Compensates automatically for patient thickness — a thin patient gets a shorter exposure, a thick patient gets a longer one — **without radiographer intervention**.
2. **Eliminates repeat exposures:** Reduces patient dose by terminating exposure exactly when film is properly exposed.
3. (Bonus) Not dependent on mains frequency stability.

#### Model Solution B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $mA = 300$, $mAs = 45$, $f_{mains} = 50\,\text{Hz}$, 4-pole motor ($P = 2$ pole-pairs), $kVp = 70$.

**(i) Exposure time (2 marks):**
$$t = \frac{mAs}{mA} = \frac{45}{300} = \mathbf{0.15\,\text{s}}$$

**(ii) Number of revolutions of synchronous motor (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Synchronous speed in rev/s:
$$N_s = \frac{f}{P} = \frac{50}{2} = 25\,\text{rev/s}$$

Step 2 — Time for one revolution:
$$t_{rev} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04\,\text{s}$$

Step 3 — Number of revolutions in $0.15\,\text{s}$:
$$n = \frac{t}{t_{rev}} = \frac{0.15}{0.04} = \mathbf{3.75\,\text{revolutions}}$$

**(iii) Heat Units generated (2 marks):**
$$HU = kVp \times mAs = 70 \times 45 = \mathbf{3150\,\text{HU}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Timer Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Hz and RPM:** Students often write $N_s = 50/4 = 12.5$ (wrong) instead of using the formula $N_s = 120f/p$ or $f/P$ in rev/s. Memorize **both** versions.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds** in HU problems — this single mistake costs the entire 2 marks of that sub-part.
> 3. **Writing "1/50 s" without units** — always write $\mathbf{20\,\text{ms}}$ in brackets as a sanity check.
> 4. **Confusing the synchronous timer's cam mechanism:** the cam engages for a *half-revolution*, not a full revolution. Many students forget this and answer 1/25 s instead of 1/50 s.
> 5. **In Phototimer diagrams:** examiners specifically check whether you mentioned **parallel-plate ionization chamber + patient-to-cassette position**. Drawing it on the tube side is an automatic **−2 marks penalty**.
> 6. **In reciprocity law:** examiners want the *complete equation* $mAs = mA \times t$. Writing only words without formula = partial credit only.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — X-Ray Timer**

- ✅ Timer controls **duration** of exposure; mA controls **quantity** of electrons per second.
- ✅ Exposure $Q = mA \times t$, measured in **mAs**.
- ✅ **Synchronous motor timer** is frequency-locked; minimum time = $1/f_{mains}$ = **1/50 s in India**, **1/60 s in USA**.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed formula** (in rev/s): $N_s = f/P$ where $P$ = pole pairs.
- ✅ **Mechanical timer** = oldest, least accurate; uses clockwork spring.
- ✅ **Electronic timer** = uses RC discharge; can deliver ms-precision exposures.
- ✅ **mAs timer** = quantity-based; integrates actual tube current; immune to mains voltage drift.
- ✅ **Phototimer / Ionization chamber timer** = automatic density control; placed between patient and cassette.
- ✅ **Reciprocity Law** = image density depends only on $mAs$, not on $mA$ or $s$ separately.
- ✅ **Heat Units** $HU = kVp \times mAs$ — timer contributes indirectly via mAs.
- ✅ Modern DR/angiography uses **pulse-mode timer** with sub-millisecond resolution.
- ✅ In **radiotherapy simulators**, the timer is replaced or supplemented by **monitor unit (MU) counters** integrated into the linac (relevant for Module 4 onward).
- ✅ **Safety interlock** always overrides the timer — timer cannot fire if door is open or filter missing.
- ✅ **KTU favorite question type:** "Calculate exposure time given mA and mAs" — practice this *every* time you revise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HT Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** HT Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **High Tension (HT) Generator** is the heart of any diagnostic X-ray equipment. It is a specialized high-voltage power supply system designed to deliver a precisely controlled, high potential difference (typically **30 kVp to 150 kVp**) across the anode and cathode of an X-ray tube, along with a separate low-voltage AC/DC supply to heat the cathode filament. It transforms the available low-voltage mains supply (e.g., 230 V, 50 Hz single-phase AC) into the high voltages required to accelerate electrons in the X-ray tube.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The HT generator is not a single device but a *system* comprising a high-voltage step-up transformer, a low-voltage filament (step-down) transformer, a rectification circuit, a control console (containing kV selector, mA selector, and exposure timer), and high-voltage cables with cable compensation circuitry.

### Components of an HT Generator Circuit
1. **High-Voltage Transformer (Step-Up Transformer):** Steps the mains voltage up to the required kV (e.g., 230 V → 100,000 V).
2. **Filament Transformer (Step-Down Transformer):** Provides low voltage (typically 3–12 V) and high current (3–6 A) to heat the X-ray tube filament.
3. **Rectifier Circuit:** Converts AC voltage to a unidirectional (pulsating DC) voltage to prevent inverse voltage damage to the X-ray tube.
4. **High-Voltage Cables:** Special heavily insulated cables connect the generator to the X-ray tube housing.
5. **Control Console:** Houses kV selector, mA selector, exposure timer, and indicating meters.
6. **Exposure Timer:** Controls the duration of X-ray exposure (milliseconds to several seconds).
7. **Automatic Exposure Control (AEC):** Optional feedback circuit that terminates exposure when optimal film density is reached.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Mains Supply: **230 V, 50 Hz** (India standard) or **415 V, 50 Hz** three-phase
> - X-ray tube operating voltage: **30 kVp to 150 kVp** (diagnostic range)
> - Tube current: **10 mA to 1000 mA** (diagnostic range)
> - Filament heating voltage: **3 V to 12 V AC** at **3 A to 6 A**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an HT generator like a **pressure washer system**:
- The **mains electricity** is like the regular water tap (low pressure).
- The **step-up transformer** acts like the pump (boosts pressure to a very high level).
- The **rectifier** is like a one-way valve that allows water to flow in only one direction (so the nozzle doesn't get damaged by backflow).
- The **X-ray tube** is the nozzle that produces the high-energy stream (X-rays).
- The **filament transformer** is like a small lighter that preheats something (the cathode) so that the high-pressure stream can produce the desired effect.
- The **exposure timer** is the trigger that controls exactly how long the spray lasts.

Just as a pressure washer needs high pressure to clean tough dirt, an X-ray tube needs high voltage (thousands of volts) to accelerate electrons fast enough to produce X-rays when they slam into the anode.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the HT Generator

The HT generator performs **four critical functions** in sequence during an X-ray exposure:

### Step 1: Voltage Step-Up
- The mains 230 V (or 415 V three-phase) AC is fed into the primary winding of the **high-voltage transformer**.
- Based on the turns ratio, the secondary winding produces an AC voltage of the order of **30 kV to 150 kV**.
- The transformer operates on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction** governed by Faraday's law.

### Step 2: Voltage Rectification
- A simple AC high voltage cannot be applied directly to the X-ray tube because the inverse half-cycle would heat the anode excessively (anode becomes the electron emitter) — this is called the **"inverse voltage problem"**.
- **Rectifiers** (typically solid-state diodes like selenium or silicon) are used to convert AC to pulsating DC.
- The number of rectifiers used determines the rectification scheme.

### Step 3: Filament Heating
- Simultaneously, a low-voltage, high-current AC is supplied to the X-ray tube filament via the **filament transformer**.
- The heated filament (cathode) emits electrons via **thermionic emission**.
- The filament temperature is controlled by the mA selector (using a rheostat or electronic current regulator).

### Step 4: Exposure Control
- The **exposure timer** determines how long the high voltage is applied to the tube.
- Modern units use **solid-state electronic timers** with accuracy down to **1 millisecond**.
- After exposure, the high voltage is removed and the filament remains heated in standby mode.

## 2.2 Types of HT Generators (KTU High-Yield Classification)

### A. Based on Number of Phases

**(i) Single-Phase Generators**
- Operate on a single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz supply.
- Produces a pulsating kV waveform with significant ripple.
- The voltage across the X-ray tube drops to **zero twice per cycle** (every 10 ms at 50 Hz).
- Cheaper, smaller, and used in low-power mobile units.

**(ii) Three-Phase Generators**
- Operate on a three-phase 415 V, 50 Hz supply.
- Two subtypes:
  - **Three-Phase, 6-Pulse Generator:** Uses 6 rectifiers; ripple is approximately **13.4%**.
  - **Three-Phase, 12-Pulse Generator:** Uses 12 rectifiers; ripple is approximately **3.4%** — almost constant potential.
- The voltage never falls to zero, resulting in higher average photon energy and shorter exposure times.

### B. Based on Frequency

**(iii) High-Frequency (HF) Generators**
- Uses a rectifier to convert mains AC to DC, then an **inverter circuit** converts DC back to AC at very high frequency (typically **5 kHz to 100 kHz**).
- This high-frequency AC is then stepped up by a much smaller transformer and rectified.
- Output is near-constant potential (ripple < **1%**).
- **Advantages:** Compact, lightweight, efficient, accurate kV, lower cost, ideal for mobile C-arms and mammography.

### C. Based on Rectification

1. **Self-Rectification (No external rectifier):** Only possible with self-rectifying tubes. The X-ray tube itself acts as a rectifier. Limited to low-power dental units.
2. **Half-Wave Rectification:** One diode; conducts only on positive half-cycle. Half the power is wasted.
3. **Full-Wave Rectification (Bridge):** Four diodes; conducts both half-cycles. Used in most modern single-phase units.
4. **Constant Potential:** DC output with negligible ripple (HF and 12-pulse three-phase systems).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Transformer Turns Ratio | $\dfrac{V_s}{V_p} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | $V_p$ = primary voltage, $V_s$ = secondary voltage, $N$ = number of turns |
| Step-Up Voltage Gain | $V_s = V_p \times \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Boosts 230 V to ~100 kV |
| Power Equation (X-ray tube) | $P = V \times I$ | $P$ in watts, $V$ in volts, $I$ in amperes |
| Heat Capacity (Anode) | $HU = V_{peak} \times I \times t$ | $HU$ = Heat Units, $t$ = exposure time (s) |
| Voltage Ripple (%) | $\% \text{ Ripple} = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{V_{max}} \times 100$ | Lower ripple = closer to constant potential |
| Single-Phase Full-Wave Ripple | ~ **100%** | Voltage varies from 0 to $V_{max}$ |
| Three-Phase 6-Pulse Ripple | ~ **13.4%** | Voltage varies between $V_{max}$ and $0.866\,V_{max}$ |
| Three-Phase 12-Pulse Ripple | ~ **3.4%** | Nearly constant potential |
| High-Frequency Ripple | < **1%** | True constant potential |
| Bremsstrahlung Max Energy | $E_{max} = e \times V_{peak}$ | $E$ in keV, $V_{peak}$ in kVp |
| Exposure Output Relation | $X \propto V^{2} \cdot I \cdot t \cdot Z$ | $X$ = X-ray intensity, $Z$ = atomic number of target |
| Frequency of Three-Phase | $f_{ripple} = 6 \times f_{line}$ (6-pulse) | At 50 Hz → 300 Hz ripple |
| HF Operating Frequency | $f_{HF}$ = **5 kHz – 100 kHz** | Allows compact transformer core |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Diagnostic Radiology:** HT generators power X-ray tubes in radiography, fluoroscopy, mammography, and CT scanners.
- **Radiotherapy:** Linear accelerators (LINACs) use specialized pulsed high-voltage power supplies (pulse modulators) derived from HT generator principles to drive electron guns and klystrons/magnetrons.
- **Industrial Applications:** Non-destructive testing (NDT) of materials, baggage scanning at airports, and food sterilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the average photon energy matter?** In a single-phase full-wave generator, the average photon energy is approximately **63% of the peak kVp**, whereas in a constant potential generator, the average photon energy approaches **90–95% of the peak kVp**. This is why high-frequency generators produce diagnostically superior images at lower kVp settings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transformer Turns Ratio and Voltage Step-Up

**Statement:** A high-voltage transformer used in an X-ray unit has 230 V applied to its primary winding of 500 turns. Calculate the number of turns required in the secondary winding to produce a peak voltage of 100 kV.

### Given Data
- Primary voltage, $V_p = 230 \text{ V}$
- Number of primary turns, $N_p = 500$
- Required secondary (peak) voltage, $V_s = 100{,}000 \text{ V} = 100 \text{ kV}$

### Step 1: Recall the Transformer EMF Equation

For an ideal transformer, the ratio of voltages equals the ratio of turns (derived from Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction):

$$\frac{V_s}{V_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Known Values

$$\frac{100{,}000}{230} = \frac{N_s}{500}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $N_s$

$$N_s = 500 \times \frac{100{,}000}{230}$$

$$N_s = 500 \times 434.78$$

$$\boxed{N_s \approx 217{,}391 \text{ turns}}$$

### Step 4: Practical Interpretation

The secondary winding requires approximately **217,391 turns** to step up 230 V to 100 kV. This huge number of turns necessitates a very compact, well-insulated winding using enameled copper wire and oil-impregnated paper insulation. The entire assembly is housed in a grounded, oil-filled steel tank to prevent high-voltage arcing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Ripple in a Three-Phase 6-Pulse Generator

**Statement:** Prove that the ripple in a three-phase, 6-pulse full-wave rectified HT generator is approximately **13.4%**.

### Step 1: Define Ripple

Voltage ripple is defined as the variation of the output DC voltage around its average (DC) value:

$$\% \text{ Ripple} = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{V_{max}} \times 100$$

### Step 2: Analyze the 6-Pulse Waveform

In a three-phase 6-pulse rectifier, the output voltage consists of **6 pulses per cycle** of the line frequency. Each phase voltage is shifted by **120°** relative to the others. The instantaneous output voltage at any moment is the **maximum of the three phase voltages**.

For a phase voltage $V_p \sin(\theta)$, the **peak value** is $V_m$, and the **minimum value** (which occurs at the midpoint of the pulse) is:

$$V_{min} = V_m \cos(30°) = V_m \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$V_{min} = V_m \times 0.866$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Ripple

$$\% \text{ Ripple} = \frac{V_m - 0.866 \, V_m}{V_m} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Ripple} = (1 - 0.866) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Ripple} = 0.134 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Ripple} \approx 13.4\%}$$

### Step 4: Analytical Insight

This 13.4% ripple means the voltage never drops below 86.6% of its peak. Compared to single-phase full-wave rectification (where voltage drops to **0%** twice per cycle), the 6-pulse generator provides a much "stiffer" voltage source, leading to:
- Higher average X-ray beam intensity
- Better image contrast
- Shorter exposure times (reduced patient motion artifact)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Comparison of Average Photon Energy (Single-Phase vs Constant Potential)

**Statement:** Show that the average photon energy of a single-phase full-wave rectified X-ray beam is approximately **63%** of the peak kVp, while a constant potential (HF) generator produces close to **90–95%**.

### Step 1: Single-Phase Full-Wave Rectification

The voltage waveform is $V(\theta) = V_m \vert \sin(\theta) \vert$ for $0 \le \theta \le \pi$. The average voltage is:

$$V_{avg, SP} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta) \, d\theta$$

$$V_{avg, SP} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\theta) \right]_{0}^{\pi}$$

$$V_{avg, SP} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(0)) \right]$$

$$V_{avg, SP} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -(-1) + 1 \right] = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \times 2$$

$$V_{avg, SP} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \, V_m$$

$$\boxed{\text{Average energy} \approx 63.7\% \text{ of } V_m}$$

### Step 2: Constant Potential (HF) Generator

For an ideal constant potential source, $V(\theta) = V_m$ for the entire exposure. Therefore:

$$V_{avg, CP} = V_m$$

$$\boxed{\text{Average energy} = 100\% \text{ of } V_m}$$

In practice, HF generators have a small ripple (<1%), so the average is typically **95–99%** of peak kVp.

### Step 3: KTU Interpretation

This is why modern digital radiography systems (which are highly sensitive to low-energy photons) strongly prefer HF generators. The higher average photon energy reduces "soft" photon waste, lowers patient skin dose, and improves beam quality (narrower energy spectrum).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of HT Generator for Application

| Application | Required Generator Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Dental X-ray unit | Single-phase, self-rectified | Low mA, short exposure, portable |
| Mobile X-ray unit (ward) | High-Frequency (HF) | Lightweight, battery-compatible |
| General radiography (radiographic room) | Three-phase 6-pulse or HF | Higher power, faster exposures |
| Mammography | High-Frequency (HF) with Mo/Rh targets | Constant potential, low ripple essential for soft tissue contrast |
| CT Scanner | High-Frequency (HF) with high mA | Requires very high power and rapid kV switching |
| Angiography / Fluoroscopy (C-arm) | High-Frequency (HF) | Compact, precise kV control, supports pulsed fluoroscopy |
| Radiotherapy LINAC | Pulsed high-voltage modulator (specialized) | Megavoltage range, pulse formation network |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete HT Generator System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Mains["MAINS SUPPLY 230V 50HZ AC"] --> Filt[Input Filter and Line Compensator]
    Filt --> AutoT["AUTOTRANSFORMER kV Selector"]
    AutoT --> Pri["HV TRANSFORMER PRIMARY Winding 230V"]
    Pri --> Sec["HV TRANSFORMER SECONDARY 100kV to 150kV"]
    Sec --> Rect["RECTIFIER BRIDGE 4 Diodes Full Wave"]
    Rect --> HVCable["HV CABLE Insulated Oil Filled"]
    HVCable --> XTube["X RAY TUBE Anode and Cathode"]
    
    Filt --> FilPri["FILAMENT TRANSFORMER PRIMARY"]
    FilPri --> FilSec["FILAMENT TRANSFORMER SECONDARY 3V to 12V AC"]
    FilSec --> FilCable["FILAMENT CABLE"]
    FilCable --> Fil["X RAY TUBE FILAMENT Cathode"]
    
    Timer["EXPOSURE TIMER Solid State 1ms Resolution"] --> Relay["HIGH VOLTAGE RELAY / TRIAC Switch"]
    Relay --> Pri
    Relay --> FilPri
    
    Console["CONTROL CONSOLE kV mA Timer Display"] --> AutoT
    Console --> FilPri
    Console --> Timer
    Console --> kVmeter["kV METER Indicates Tube Voltage"]
    Console --> mAmeter["mA METER Indicates Tube Current"]
    
    CableComp["CABLE COMPENSATION NETWORK"] --> Rect
    CableComp --> HVCable
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Trace the path from **Mains → Autotransformer → HV Transformer → Rectifier → Cable → X-ray Tube**. The autotransformer allows fine adjustment of the primary voltage, which in turn controls the secondary high voltage (kV). The exposure timer controls when the high-voltage relay closes to permit current flow.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Three-Phase 6-Pulse vs High-Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP["SINGLE PHASE GENERATOR"]
        SP1[230V 50Hz] --> SP2[Step Up Transformer] --> SP3[Bridge Rectifier 4 Diodes] --> SP4[Pulsating DC Output Ripple 100%]
    end
    
    subgraph TP["THREE PHASE 6 PULSE GENERATOR"]
        TP1[415V 50Hz 3 Phase] --> TP2[3 Step Up Transformers] --> TP3[6 Rectifier Diodes] --> TP4[Pulsating DC Output Ripple 13.4%]
    end
    
    subgraph HF["HIGH FREQUENCY GENERATOR"]
        HF1[230V 50Hz] --> HF2[Rectifier to DC] --> HF3[Inverter 5 to 100 kHz] --> HF4[Compact HV Transformer] --> HF5[Final Rectifier] --> HF6[Near Constant DC Output Ripple under 1%]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Notice how the high-frequency generator "converts" the mains frequency up to a much higher value using an inverter stage. This allows the use of a **much smaller and lighter** transformer core, because the required core cross-sectional area is inversely proportional to frequency.

---

## 4.3 Voltage Waveform Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["kV WAVEFORM COMPARISON ACROSS X RAY TUBE"] --> B["Single Phase Self Rectified: Pulses only on positive half cycles"]
    A --> C["Single Phase Full Wave Rectified: Pulses both half cycles ripple 100%"]
    A --> D["Three Phase 6 Pulse: 6 pulses per cycle ripple 13.4%"]
    A --> E["Three Phase 12 Pulse: 12 pulses per cycle ripple 3.4%"]
    A --> F["High Frequency: Near constant DC ripple under 1%"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an HT Generator. List its main components.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
A High Tension (HT) Generator is a high-voltage power supply unit that converts low-voltage mains AC into the high DC voltage (30–150 kVp) required to operate an X-ray tube, and also supplies low-voltage high-current AC to heat the tube filament.

**Main Components:**
1. High-voltage step-up transformer
2. Filament (step-down) transformer
3. Rectifier circuit
4. High-voltage cables
5. Autotransformer (kV selector)
6. Exposure timer
7. Control console with kV, mA, and timer selectors
8. Meters (kV meter, mA meter)

---

### Q2. What is meant by "constant potential" in an HT generator? Name two generators that produce near-constant potential output.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
"Constant potential" refers to an HT generator output where the DC voltage applied across the X-ray tube remains nearly constant throughout the exposure, with negligible ripple (< 5%).

**Two examples of constant potential generators:**
1. **High-Frequency (HF) Generator** — ripple < 1%
2. **Three-Phase, 12-Pulse Generator** — ripple ≈ 3.4%

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1 — 14 Marks]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a single-phase full-wave rectified HT generator used in diagnostic X-ray units. Discuss its advantages and limitations. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (Block Diagram Description):**
The single-phase full-wave rectified HT generator consists of:

1. **Mains Supply:** 230 V, 50 Hz single-phase AC.
2. **Autotransformer:** A variable transformer with multiple tapped output points. The kV selector on the control console adjusts the tap position to feed a variable voltage (e.g., 100 V to 230 V) to the primary of the HV transformer.
3. **High-Voltage Step-Up Transformer:** Steps the voltage up to 30–150 kV at the secondary. The turns ratio is very high (~1:500).
4. **Rectifier Circuit:** A bridge of **four solid-state diodes** (silicon or selenium) connected in a Graetz bridge configuration. This converts the AC secondary voltage into a **pulsating DC** by allowing current to flow in only one direction during both half-cycles.
5. **Filament Transformer:** A small step-down transformer that supplies 3–12 V AC at 3–6 A to heat the X-ray tube filament.
6. **High-Voltage Cables:** Heavily insulated cables connect the rectifier output to the X-ray tube.
7. **Exposure Timer:** A solid-state electronic timer (1 ms to 6 s range) controls the duration of the exposure by switching the high-voltage relay.
8. **Meters:** kV meter and mA meter provide real-time feedback on operating parameters.

**Working:**
- The operator selects the desired kV, mA, and exposure time on the control console.
- The autotransformer is set to a tap corresponding to the required kV.
- When the exposure switch is pressed, the timer energizes the high-voltage relay.
- The HV transformer secondary produces high AC voltage, which is rectified by the bridge into pulsating DC.
- The DC voltage is applied across the X-ray tube through the HV cables.
- Simultaneously, the filament transformer heats the cathode filament to its emission temperature.
- Electrons emitted from the cathode are accelerated toward the anode by the high DC voltage, producing X-rays.
- After the preset exposure time, the relay opens, the HV is removed, and X-ray production ceases.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, inexpensive, and reliable.
- Suitable for low-to-moderate power applications.
- Compact size for mobile units.

**Limitations:**
- Voltage ripple is very high (~100%), so voltage drops to zero twice per cycle.
- Average photon energy is only ~63% of peak kVp.
- Longer exposure times required, increasing patient motion artifact.
- Higher patient skin dose due to inefficient beam quality.

---

**(b) Compare single-phase, three-phase, and high-frequency HT generators in terms of voltage ripple, transformer size, and clinical application. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Single-Phase Full-Wave | Three-Phase 6-Pulse | Three-Phase 12-Pulse | High-Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Supply | 230 V, 1-phase, 50 Hz | 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz | 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz | 230 V, 1-phase (converted) |
| Voltage Ripple | ~100% | ~13.4% | ~3.4% | < 1% |
| Avg. Photon Energy | ~63% of kVp | ~90% of kVp | ~95% of kVp | ~95–99% of kVp |
| Voltage never reaches zero | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Transformer Size | Large, heavy | Large, heavy | Large, heavy | **Small, compact** |
| Weight | Heavy | Heaviest | Heaviest | Lightest |
| Cost | Low | High | Very high | Moderate |
| Clinical Application | Dental, mobile, low-power | General radiography | Specialized heavy radiography | **Modern digital radiography, mammography, CT, C-arm** |
| Efficiency | Low | Medium-High | High | **Highest** |
| Exposure Time | Longest | Short | Shortest | **Shortest** |

**Key Insight:** The high-frequency generator is the modern standard because it combines the **compactness** of single-phase units with the **near-constant potential** of three-phase 12-pulse units. By operating the transformer at high frequency (5–100 kHz), the required core cross-section is dramatically reduced (since $V \propto N \cdot A \cdot f$, higher $f$ means smaller $A$).

---

### Question B — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Module 1 — 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the need for rectification in an X-ray HT circuit. Describe full-wave bridge rectification with a suitable circuit diagram. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Need for Rectification:**
The secondary of the HV transformer produces a high-voltage **alternating current (AC)** waveform. If this AC is directly applied to a conventional X-ray tube, the following problems occur:
- During the **positive half-cycle** (cathode negative, anode positive), electrons flow normally from cathode to anode, producing X-rays.
- During the **negative half-cycle** (cathode positive, anode negative), the polarity reverses. The heated cathode (still emitting electrons) would emit electrons toward what is now the negative anode, and the **massive anode block would become overheated** due to electron bombardment.
- Additionally, the inverse voltage can cause **arcing and insulation breakdown** in the tube.
- The net effect is reduced tube life, increased anode heating, and inconsistent X-ray output.

**Rectification** converts the AC into a **unidirectional (DC) pulsating voltage** that always maintains the correct polarity: cathode negative, anode positive. This protects the tube and ensures consistent X-ray production.

**Full-Wave Bridge Rectification:**

**Circuit Description:**
A bridge of four diodes (D1, D2, D3, D4) is connected to the secondary of the HV transformer. The X-ray tube is connected between the two output terminals of the bridge.

- During the **positive half-cycle:** Current flows through D1 → tube (cathode to anode) → D3 → back to transformer. D2 and D4 are reverse-biased.
- During the **negative half-cycle:** Current flows through D2 → tube (cathode to anode) → D4 → back to transformer. D1 and D3 are reverse-biased.

**Result:** The X-ray tube receives current in the **same direction during both half-cycles**, producing pulsating DC. The frequency of pulsation is **double the line frequency** (100 Hz in India).

**Key Features of Bridge Rectification in X-ray Systems:**
- All four diodes must have peak inverse voltage (PIV) rating ≥ the peak tube voltage (typically ≥ 150 kV).
- Diodes are mounted in a special **rectifier assembly** inside the oil-filled HV tank.
- Modern units use **silicon high-voltage rectifier stacks**.

---

**(b) With neat diagrams, describe the construction and working of a high-frequency HT generator. List its advantages over conventional generators. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Stages:**

1. **Mains Rectifier:** The incoming 230 V, 50 Hz AC is first rectified by a full-wave bridge rectifier into DC.
2. **DC Link / Filter Capacitor:** A large capacitor smooths the rectified DC.
3. **Inverter (Oscillator):** A solid-state oscillator circuit (using IGBTs or MOSFETs) chops the DC back into AC at a **high frequency (5 kHz to 100 kHz)**. Typical operating frequency: **20–50 kHz**.
4. **Compact HV Transformer:** A small, lightweight step-up transformer steps up the high-frequency AC to the required kV. Because the frequency is high, the transformer core (ferrite) is much smaller — the cross-sectional area $A$ is inversely proportional to frequency $f$.
5. **High-Voltage Rectifier:** A secondary rectifier (often voltage multiplier) converts the high-frequency AC to DC.
6. **Filament Inverter and Transformer:** A separate high-frequency inverter supplies the filament transformer.
7. **Control and Feedback:** Closed-loop electronic control of kV and mA via primary voltage sensing and filament current sensing.

**Working:**
- The operator sets kV, mA, and time on the console.
- The mains rectifier produces DC, which is then inverted to high-frequency AC.
- The compact HV transformer steps this up to the required kV.
- Output is rectified to produce near-constant DC across the X-ray tube.
- The exposure timer controls the duration precisely.

**Advantages over Conventional Generators:**

1. **Compact and Lightweight:** The transformer core is ~10× smaller than 50 Hz counterparts. Ideal for mobile C-arms and portable units.
2. **Near-Constant Potential:** Ripple < 1%, producing nearly 100% average beam energy.
3. **High Efficiency:** Less power loss as heat; lower electricity consumption.
4. **Precise kV and mA Control:** Electronic feedback allows ±2% accuracy in kV and mA.
5. **Fast kV Switching:** Essential for CT scanners and digital radiography (DR).
6. **Lower Patient Dose:** Higher beam quality means less soft radiation absorbed in skin.
7. **Reduced Exposure Time:** Better image quality, reduced motion artifact.
8. **Suitable for Digital Detectors:** Modern flat-panel detectors require the stable, high-quality beam that HF generators provide.

**Disadvantages:**
- Complex electronic circuits require skilled service technicians.
- Higher initial cost than single-phase units (though lower than three-phase).
- Sensitive to power supply fluctuations (mitigated by line compensation circuits).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "kV" and "kVp":** Always use **kVp** (peak kilovoltage), not just kV, when describing X-ray tube voltage. kV is the average, kVp is the peak.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention "ripple":** Whenever discussing generator types, you **must** state the voltage ripple percentage. Examiners specifically look for "13.4%" for 6-pulse, "3.4%" for 12-pulse, and "<1%" for HF.
> 3. **Not stating the reason for rectification:** A common pitfall is writing "rectifiers convert AC to DC" without explaining *why* the X-ray tube needs DC (to prevent anode overheating from inverse voltage).
> 4. **Mixing up transformer types:** Students often confuse the HV step-up transformer with the filament step-down transformer. Clearly state: **HV = step-up (high V, low I)**, **Filament = step-down (low V, high I)**.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always write kV, mA, ms, Hz, %, etc. with numerical values.
> 6. **Not labeling block diagrams:** A block diagram without labeled components and signal flow arrows loses at least **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist
- **HT Generator** = System that provides high DC voltage + filament heating to X-ray tube.
- **Mains:** 230 V, 50 Hz (1-phase) | 415 V, 50 Hz (3-phase).
- **HV Transformer:** Step-up to 30–150 kVp; turns ratio can be ~1:500.
- **Filament Transformer:** Step-down to 3–12 V, 3–6 A.
- **Autotransformer:** Allows variable kV selection.
- **Rectification Needed:** To prevent inverse voltage damage to X-ray tube.
- **Single-Phase Full-Wave:** 4 diodes; ripple = 100%; avg. energy = 63.7% of kVp.
- **Three-Phase 6-Pulse:** 6 diodes; ripple = 13.4%.
- **Three-Phase 12-Pulse:** 12 diodes; ripple = 3.4%.
- **High-Frequency (HF):** Inverter-based; ripple < 1%; compact transformer; modern standard.
- **Self-Rectification:** Used only in dental units with self-rectifying tubes; no external diodes.
- **Cable Compensation:** Circuit that compensates for voltage drop in long HV cables (especially in mobile units).
- **Exposure Timer:** Electronic, 1 ms to several seconds; controls the high-voltage relay.
- **AEC (Automatic Exposure Control):** Uses phototimer/ionization chamber feedback to terminate exposure.
- **Formula for Ripple %:** $(V_{max} - V_{min}) / V_{max} \times 100$.
- **Single-phase full-wave output frequency:** 2 × line frequency = 100 Hz (in India).
- **Three-phase 6-pulse output frequency:** 6 × line frequency = 300 Hz.
- **HF output frequency:** 5 kHz to 100 kHz.
- **Bremsstrahlung photon energy:** $E_{keV} = V_{kVp}$.
- **Clinical Preference Order (Modern Era):** HF > 12-pulse > 6-pulse > single-phase.
- **KTU Buzzwords to Use in Answers:** "constant potential," "voltage ripple," "thermionic emission," "inverse voltage," "filament saturation," "cable capacitance," "closed-loop feedback."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on HT generators, always structure your answer as: **(i) Block diagram with labeled components → (ii) Working in numbered steps → (iii) Advantages/Limitations or Comparison Table → (iv) Clinical application.** This 4-part structure maps perfectly to KTU's 7+7 mark sub-part allocation and secures full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rectification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Rectification

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Principles of Radio Diagnosis and Radiotherapy (PCEBT602)**, **Rectification** is defined as the process of converting alternating current (AC) into direct current (DC) or unidirectional current, by suppressing or reversing the half-cycles of the AC waveform that are not useful for the operation of the X-ray tube or radiotherapy equipment.

In diagnostic radiology, rectification is essential because **X-ray tubes must be energized with unidirectional (one-way) current**. If alternating current were applied directly, the anode would alternately act as the cathode during the negative half-cycle, leading to:
- Electron emission from the anode (back-emission)
- Tube damage
- Production of X-rays only during one half-cycle at best (in self-rectification)
- Unstable tube operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Rectification is a fundamental pre-requisite concept in Module 1 because every X-ray generator—from basic dental units to sophisticated linear accelerators—depends on a specific rectification topology to deliver the correct high-voltage waveform to the X-ray tube.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of rectification like a **one-way water valve** attached to a pipe that pumps water forward and then backward alternately. The valve only allows water to flow in one direction (forward), stopping the backward flow. The result is a pulsating stream of water that always moves forward—even though the original pump pushes water both ways.

In an electrical sense:
- **AC supply** = A pump pushing electrons forward, then backward, 50 or 60 times a second.
- **Rectifier** = The one-way valve that only permits electrons to flow from cathode to anode of the X-ray tube.
- **Output** = Pulsating DC (a series of unidirectional pulses).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Ripple** | Residual AC component superimposed on DC output | Expressed as % ripple |
| **Ripple Frequency** | Number of pulsations per second of rectified output | Depends on type |
| **Rectification Efficiency** | Ratio of DC power delivered to AC power consumed | Varies by type |
| **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)** | Maximum reverse voltage a rectifier can withstand | Rated in kV |
| **Line Voltage (mains)** | Standard AC input frequency | **50 Hz** (India) / 60 Hz (US) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Concept:** In India, the standard AC mains frequency is **50 Hz**. A single-phase full-wave rectified waveform will therefore produce **100 pulsations per second** (2 × 50 Hz), which is critical for image quality and exposure consistency in radiography.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Need for Rectification in X-Ray Circuits

X-ray tubes are **diodes**—they conduct current only when the anode is positive with respect to the cathode. If raw AC is applied:
- During the **positive half-cycle**, the anode is positive → current flows → X-rays are produced.
- During the **negative half-cycle**, the anode is negative → the tube either blocks current OR, if voltage is high enough, **arcing/damage** occurs (especially in self-rectified tubes).

Hence, rectification is mandatory to:
1. **Protect the X-ray tube** from inverse voltage damage.
2. **Increase X-ray output efficiency** (more useful photons per exposure).
3. **Improve exposure consistency** by reducing ripple in the kV waveform.
4. **Enable higher tube currents** safely.

## 2.2 Types of Rectification (KTU Board Focus)

### A. Self-Rectification (Half-Wave Self-Rectification)

In this configuration, the X-ray tube itself acts as its own rectifier. The AC high-voltage transformer is connected directly across the X-ray tube terminals.

**Operating Principle:**
- **Positive half-cycle:** Transformer secondary polarity is such that anode is positive → current flows → X-rays produced.
- **Negative half-cycle:** Anode becomes negative → tube does not conduct → no X-rays → no current.
- The tube **blocks** the inverse voltage by virtue of its diode nature.

**Drawbacks:**
- Only **50%** of the AC cycle is utilized (theoretically 70.7% efficiency in terms of average voltage).
- Tube must withstand the full inverse voltage.
- Cannot be used at high mA for prolonged periods.
- Historically used in low-power dental and portable X-ray units.

### B. Half-Wave Rectification

A **separate rectifier tube (or solid-state diode)** is added in series with the X-ray tube. This rectifier blocks the negative half-cycle, ensuring the X-ray tube only sees the positive half-cycle.

**Circuit:** Transformer → Rectifier Tube → X-Ray Tube (all in series)

**Waveform:** The output across the X-ray tube consists of only the positive humps of the AC sine wave; the negative half is suppressed.

**Advantage over self-rectification:** The X-ray tube is protected from inverse voltage, allowing higher and more reliable operation.

### C. Full-Wave Rectification (Most Common in Modern Equipment)

Uses **four rectifier tubes (or solid-state diodes)** arranged in a **bridge configuration** (Graetz circuit). Both half-cycles of the AC input are utilized, but the polarity across the X-ray tube is always the same.

**Bridge Circuit Operation:**
- During the **positive half-cycle**, two diodes conduct (D1 and D3) and current flows through the X-ray tube in the correct direction.
- During the **negative half-cycle**, the other two diodes (D2 and D4) conduct, but current still flows through the X-ray tube in the **same direction**.

**Output:** A series of positive humps at **twice the supply frequency** (e.g., 100 Hz in India).

**Advantages:**
- Nearly **100% utilization** of both half-cycles.
- **Reduced ripple** → smoother DC output.
- Higher average kV → more consistent X-ray output.
- Higher efficiency—nearly 90% of peak voltage is delivered as average voltage.

### D. Three-Phase Rectification (Used in High-End Equipment)

Modern high-power X-ray generators and CT scanners use **three-phase** full-wave rectification. A three-phase transformer with six rectifiers provides an almost ripple-free DC output.

- **Three-Phase, Six-Pulse Rectification:** Ripple frequency = 6 × 50 Hz = **300 Hz**
- **Three-Phase, Twelve-Pulse Rectification:** Ripple frequency = 12 × 50 Hz = **600 Hz**
- **% Ripple:** < 4% (compared to 100% in self-rectification, 100% in half-wave, 48% in single-phase full-wave)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Symbol | Description | Unit |
|---|----------------|--------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2 V_{max}}{\pi}$ | Single-phase HW | Average voltage for half-wave | Volts |
| 2 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2 V_{max}}{\pi}$ | Single-phase FW | Average voltage for full-wave | Volts |
| 3 | $\text{Ripple Factor} = \dfrac{V_{rms(ac)}}{V_{dc}}$ | $\gamma$ | Quality of rectified output | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\gamma_{HW} = 1.21$ | Half-wave | Ripple factor | — |
| 5 | $\gamma_{FW} = 0.482$ | Full-wave | Ripple factor | — |
| 6 | $\text{\% Ripple} = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{V_{max}} \times 100$ | — | Practical ripple percentage | % |
| 7 | $f_{ripple} = n \times f_{supply}$ | $n$ = pulses per cycle | Ripple frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $\eta_{rectification} = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} \times 100$ | $\eta$ | Rectifier efficiency | % |
| 9 | $\eta_{HW} = 40.6\%$, $\eta_{FW} = 81.2\%$ | — | Standard efficiencies | % |
| 10 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS | Root mean square value | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` where it would conflict with Markdown table syntax. In the table above, percentage notation uses `\%` and ratio uses `\dfrac`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Diagnostic X-Ray Units:** Modern radiography rooms use single-phase full-wave rectification or three-phase rectification to deliver stable kV for consistent image density.
2. **CT Scanners:** High-power CT gantries demand nearly constant DC—hence 12-pulse three-phase rectification is standard.
3. **Linear Accelerators (LINAC):** The klystron/magnetron in LINACs requires extremely stable high-voltage DC, derived from sophisticated multi-phase rectifier stacks.
4. **Mammography:** Uses high-frequency inversion (a form of rectification combined with chopper circuits) for extremely low ripple—essential for detecting microcalcifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average DC Output Voltage of a Half-Wave Rectifier

Consider a sinusoidal AC voltage applied to a half-wave rectifier:

$$
v(t) = V_{max} \sin(\omega t)
$$

For a **half-wave rectifier**, the output is:

$$
v_{out}(t) = 
\begin{cases}
V_{max}\sin(\omega t), & 0 \le \omega t \le \pi \\
0, & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi
\end{cases}
$$

The **average DC output voltage** over one full cycle (period $T = 2\pi/\omega$) is defined as:

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_{out}(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 1.** Split the integral into the conduction and non-conduction intervals.

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\pi} V_{max}\sin(\theta)\, d\theta + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} 0 \, d\theta \right]
$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate the first integral. The integral of $\sin(\theta)$ is $-\cos(\theta)$.

$$
\int_{0}^{\pi} V_{max}\sin(\theta)\, d\theta = V_{max}\left[-\cos(\theta)\right]_{0}^{\pi}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the limits $\pi$ and $0$.

$$
= V_{max}\left[-\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(0))\right] = V_{max}\left[-(-1) - (-1)\right]
$$

**Step 4.** Simplify the result.

$$
= V_{max}\left[1 + 1\right] = 2 V_{max}
$$

**Step 5.** Substitute back into the average expression.

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left(2 V_{max}\right) = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\pi}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{V_{dc(\text{half-wave})} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\pi} \approx 0.318 \, V_{max}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Average DC Output Voltage of a Full-Wave Rectifier

For a full-wave rectifier, both half-cycles contribute. Using the absolute value of the sine function:

$$
v_{out}(t) = V_{max} \vert \sin(\omega t) \vert
$$

The average is computed over one half-period (because the function repeats every $\pi$):

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_{max}\sin(\theta)\, d\theta
$$

**Step 1.** Pull out the constant.

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin(\theta)\, d\theta
$$

**Step 2.** Integrate.

$$
\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = \left[-\cos(\theta)\right]_{0}^{\pi} = -\cos(\pi) + \cos(0) = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute.

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\pi} \times 2 = \dfrac{2 V_{max}}{\pi}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{V_{dc(\text{full-wave})} = \dfrac{2 V_{max}}{\pi} \approx 0.636 \, V_{max}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A full-wave rectifier delivers **twice the average DC voltage** of a half-wave rectifier using the same transformer secondary voltage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ripple Factor of a Full-Wave Rectifier

The **ripple factor** is defined as:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{V_{rms(ac)}}{V_{dc}}
$$

where $V_{rms(ac)}$ is the RMS value of the AC component of the rectified output.

**Step 1.** RMS of the full-wave rectified output.

$$
V_{rms(total)} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} \left(V_{max}\sin\theta\right)^2 d\theta}
$$

**Step 2.** Use the identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2}$.

$$
V_{rms(total)}^2 = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} \dfrac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2}\, d\theta
$$

**Step 3.** Integrate. The cosine term vanishes over a full period.

$$
V_{rms(total)}^2 = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{2\pi}\left[\theta - \dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{2}\right]_{0}^{\pi} = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{2\pi}(\pi) = \dfrac{V_{max}^2}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{rms(total)} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 4.** AC component of the output.

$$
V_{rms(ac)} = \sqrt{V_{rms(total)}^2 - V_{dc}^2}
$$

**Step 5.** Substitute the known values.

$$
V_{rms(ac)} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{max}^2}{2} - \left(\dfrac{2V_{max}}{\pi}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 6.** Simplify using $V_{dc} = 2V_{max}/\pi$.

$$
V_{rms(ac)} = V_{max}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{4}{\pi^2}}
$$

**Step 7.** Numerical evaluation.

$$
\dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5000, \quad \dfrac{4}{\pi^2} = 0.4053
$$

$$
V_{rms(ac)} = V_{max}\sqrt{0.0947} = 0.3078 \, V_{max}
$$

**Step 8.** Divide by $V_{dc}$.

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{0.3078 \, V_{max}}{0.6366 \, V_{max}} = 0.483
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\gamma_{\text{full-wave}} \approx 0.482 \quad (\text{i.e., } 48.2\% \text{ ripple})}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Self-Rectification | Half-Wave | Full-Wave (1-Phase) | 3-Phase, 6-Pulse | 3-Phase, 12-Pulse |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------|---------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Number of Rectifier Tubes | 0 | 1 | 4 | 6 | 12 |
| Pulses per Cycle | 1 | 1 | 2 | 6 | 12 |
| Ripple Frequency @ 50 Hz | 50 Hz | 50 Hz | 100 Hz | 300 Hz | 600 Hz |
| % Ripple (theoretical) | 100% | 100% | 48.2% | 13.4% | 3.4% |
| Rectification Efficiency | — | 40.6% | 81.2% | 96.8% | 99.0% |
| Transformer Utilization Factor | Low | Low | High | Higher | Highest |
| Typical Application | Dental units, portables | Older portables | General radiography | Fluoroscopy, mobile | CT, angiography |
| Average kV delivered | $V_{max}/\pi$ | $V_{max}/\pi$ | $2V_{max}/\pi$ | $3V_{max}/\pi$ | $\approx V_{max}$ |
