# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define rectification. Why is rectification necessary in an X-ray circuit?**

**Model Answer:**

Rectification is the process of converting alternating current (AC) into direct current (DC) by suppressing the unwanted half-cycles of the AC waveform using rectifier tubes or solid-state diodes.

Rectification is necessary in an X-ray circuit because:

1. **X-ray tubes are diodes**—they conduct only when the anode is positive. AC would alternately reverse the polarity, potentially causing tube damage.
2. **X-rays are produced only during electron flow from cathode to anode.** If AC is applied without rectification, X-ray output is reduced to a single half-cycle.
3. **Inverse voltage protection:** During the negative half-cycle, a high inverse voltage is applied to the tube, which can cause arcing and permanent damage. Rectification suppresses this.
4. **Improved efficiency and stable kV waveform** for consistent radiographic exposure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students answer "to convert AC to DC" without explaining *why* this matters for an X-ray tube. Always mention the diode nature of the tube and inverse voltage protection to score full marks.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare half-wave and full-wave rectification in terms of efficiency and ripple.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Half-Wave Rectification | Full-Wave Rectification |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Number of Diodes | 1 (or self) | 4 (bridge) |
| Pulses per Cycle | 1 | 2 |
| Ripple Frequency (50 Hz) | 50 Hz | 100 Hz |
| Rectification Efficiency | 40.6% | 81.2% |
| Ripple Factor | 1.21 | 0.482 |
| % Ripple | 100% | 48.2% |
| Output Average Voltage | $V_{max}/\pi$ | $2V_{max}/\pi$ |

**Conclusion:** Full-wave rectification is more efficient, has lower ripple, and delivers higher average voltage compared to half-wave rectification, making it the standard for modern diagnostic X-ray units.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Choice Option A]`

**A. (a)** Explain the construction and working of a single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier used in X-ray machines. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the average DC output voltage and ripple factor of a full-wave rectifier. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A single-phase full-wave bridge rectifier consists of **four diodes (D1, D2, D3, D4)** arranged in a **Graetz bridge configuration** across the secondary winding of the high-voltage step-up transformer.

**Construction:**
- The transformer secondary winding is connected between two opposite nodes of the bridge (let's call them A and B).
- The X-ray tube is connected between the other two nodes (P and N).
- Diodes D1 and D2 are connected to node A; D3 and D4 to node B.
- D1 and D3 conduct during the positive half-cycle, sending current through the X-ray tube in one direction.
- D2 and D4 conduct during the negative half-cycle, but the current through the X-ray tube is in the **same direction**.

**Working:**

**Positive Half-Cycle:** Terminal A is positive, B is negative. D1 (forward biased) and D3 (forward biased) conduct. Current flows: A → D1 → X-ray tube anode → cathode → D3 → B.

**Negative Half-Cycle:** Terminal B is positive, A is negative. D2 and D4 conduct. Current flows: B → D2 → X-ray tube anode → cathode → D4 → A.

In both half-cycles, the anode of the X-ray tube is always at a higher potential than the cathode, ensuring unidirectional current flow.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Average DC Output Voltage Derivation:**

The full-wave rectified output is $v(t) = V_{max}\vert\sin(\omega t)\vert$.

$$
V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} V_{max}\sin(\theta)\, d\theta = \dfrac{2V_{max}}{\pi} \approx 0.636\,V_{max}
$$

**Ripple Factor Derivation:**

The total RMS value:

$$
V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

The AC component of the output:

$$
V_{rms(ac)} = \sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2} = V_{max}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{4}{\pi^2}} \approx 0.308\,V_{max}
$$

The ripple factor:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{V_{rms(ac)}}{V_{dc}} = \dfrac{0.308}{0.636} \approx 0.482
$$

This corresponds to approximately **48.2% ripple**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** A common error is to compute the RMS of the full output as if it were the AC component, forgetting to subtract the DC squared. Always show the $V_{rms(ac)} = \sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2}$ step explicitly.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Choice Option B]`

**B. (a)** Describe with diagrams the different types of rectification used in X-ray equipment. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain three-phase, six-pulse rectification and state its advantages over single-phase full-wave rectification. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The main types of rectification used in X-ray equipment are:

1. **Self-Rectification:** The X-ray tube itself acts as the rectifier. No external diodes. During the positive half-cycle, current flows and X-rays are produced; during the negative half-cycle, the tube blocks conduction. Used in low-power dental and portable units.

2. **Half-Wave Rectification:** A single rectifier tube or solid-state diode is placed in series with the X-ray tube. The diode blocks the negative half-cycle. The X-ray tube sees only positive half-cycles.

3. **Full-Wave Rectification (Bridge):** Four diodes in a Graetz bridge arrangement. Both half-cycles are utilized, with the X-ray tube always seeing unidirectional current. Produces 100 pulses/second (at 50 Hz mains).

4. **Three-Phase, Six-Pulse Rectification:** Six diodes connected to the secondaries of a three-phase transformer. The output is a near-constant DC with low ripple (~13%).

5. **Three-Phase, Twelve-Pulse Rectification:** Twelve diodes with phase-shifting transformer. Ripple further reduced to ~3.4%. Used in CT and high-end angiographic equipment.

(Diagrams: Sketch a simple bridge circuit showing transformer, four diodes, and X-ray tube; label the polarity during both half-cycles.)

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Three-Phase, Six-Pulse Rectification:**

- A three-phase high-voltage transformer has three secondary windings, each 120° apart in phase.
- Each secondary is connected to a pair of diodes (one for the positive half, one for the negative half) — total 6 diodes.
- The outputs of the three phase legs are combined and fed to the X-ray tube.
- At any instant, **two of the three phases are conducting simultaneously**, so the output is a continuous string of six overlapping positive humps per cycle.

**Key Calculations:**

- Number of pulses per cycle: **6**
- Ripple frequency at 50 Hz: $6 \times 50 = 300$ Hz
- % Ripple: approximately **13.4%**
- Rectification efficiency: approximately **96.8%**
- Average kV: $V_{avg} = \dfrac{3 V_{max}}{\pi} \approx 0.955 \, V_{max}$

**Advantages over Single-Phase Full-Wave Rectification:**

1. **Lower ripple** (13.4% vs 48.2%) → smoother kV → better image quality.
2. **Higher average kV** → more X-ray output for the same peak kV.
3. **Higher efficiency** (96.8% vs 81.2%) → less heat dissipation in the rectifier stack.
4. **Higher tube current capability** → suitable for high-power applications (CT, angiography).
5. **Faster exposure times** possible without significant ripple-induced exposure variations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** Students often confuse "six-pulse" with "twelve-pulse" and incorrectly state the ripple frequency. Remember: **pulses per cycle = 2 × number of phases**. So 3 phases × 2 = 6 pulses. For 12-pulse, two 6-pulse bridges are combined with a 30° phase shift.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Rectification** converts AC to unidirectional (DC) current, mandatory for X-ray tube operation.
- [ ] The X-ray tube is a **diode**—it conducts only when the anode is positive.
- [ ] **Self-rectification:** X-ray tube itself acts as rectifier; no external diodes.
- [ ] **Half-wave rectification:** One external diode; 50 Hz ripple; 40.6% efficiency.
- [ ] **Full-wave rectification:** Four diodes in a bridge; 100 Hz ripple; 81.2% efficiency; 48.2% ripple factor.
- [ ] **Three-phase, six-pulse:** 300 Hz ripple; 13.4% ripple; 96.8% efficiency.
- [ ] **Three-phase, twelve-pulse:** 600 Hz ripple; 3.4% ripple; ~99% efficiency.
- [ ] **$V_{dc(\text{half-wave})} = V_{max}/\pi$**
- [ ] **$V_{dc(\text{full-wave})} = 2V_{max}/\pi$**
- [ ] **Ripple factor of full-wave = 0.482** (a frequently asked KTU numerical).
- [ ] **% Ripple = $((V_{max} - V_{min})/V_{max}) \times 100$** — for practical waveforms.
- [ ] In India, the **mains frequency is 50 Hz** — ripple frequencies are integer multiples of this.
- [ ] **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV):** The maximum reverse voltage a rectifier must block; for bridge rectifier, $PIV = V_{max}$.
- [ ] Modern equipment increasingly uses **solid-state high-voltage rectifiers** (silicon diodes stacked in series) instead of vacuum rectifier tubes.
- [ ] **High-frequency inverter technology** (used in mammography) reduces ripple to < 1% by chopping and re-rectifying at ~50 kHz.
- [ ] Always state both the **type of rectification** and the **application** (e.g., "full-wave bridge for general radiography") in KTU answers for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filament Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Filament Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Filament Circuit** (also called the **Low-Tension (L.T.) Circuit** or **Tube Filament Supply Circuit**) of an X-ray unit is the low-voltage electrical sub-circuit responsible for heating the **thermionic cathode** of the X-ray tube to its operating temperature, thereby producing a controlled cloud of electrons via **thermionic emission**. In a self-rectified, half-wave, or full-wave X-ray generator, the filament circuit is electrically isolated from, but magnetically coupled to, the high-tension circuit through a dedicated **filament transformer** (a step-down transformer) energized from the same AC mains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** *"The filament circuit consists of the step-down filament transformer, the filament current regulator (rheostat / steepness selector), the space-charge compensator, milliammeter, and safety interlocks, all connected in series with the X-ray tube cathode filament."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Boiling Tea Kettle"

Imagine an electric kettle that boils water to produce steam. In the kettle analogy:

| Kettle Element | X-ray Filament Circuit Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Heating coil (nichrome wire) | Tungsten filament of the cathode (melting point **3422 °C**) |
| Temperature dial (thermostat) | Filament rheostat / steepness selector |
| Steam (water vapour) | Thermionic electron cloud |
| Pressure of steam | Tube current in **milliamperes (mA)** |
| Pressure gauge | Milliammeter (mA meter) |
| Nozzle directing steam | Focusing cup of the cathode assembly |

Just as a small increase in kettle temperature produces a disproportionately large increase in steam pressure, a small change in filament current produces a **steep, non-linear** rise in tube current — this is the famous **"steepness" characteristic** of the X-ray tube.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - **Filament Voltage:** 3 V to 12 V (typical: **~10 V** for diagnostic tubes)
> - **Filament Current:** 3 A to 5 A (typical: **~4–5 A** at full mA)
> - **Cathode Operating Temperature:** ~ **2400 K to 2600 K** (approx. 2127 °C to 2327 °C)
> - **Tube Current Output:** 100 mA to 1000 mA (selectable via filament current)
> - **Work Function of Tungsten (W):** **4.55 eV** ≈ **7.28 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**

## 1.4 Role of the Filament Circuit — Five Core Functions

1. **Electron Generation:** Heats the tungsten filament to enable **thermionic emission**.
2. **mA Selection:** Allows the radiographer to preset tube current (100 mA, 200 mA, 400 mA, etc.).
3. **mA Stabilization:** Compensates for the **space-charge effect** so that mA does not drift.
4. **Filament Boost / Boosting:** Briefly raises filament current at the start of exposure to overcome the cold-cathode start-up delay.
5. **Safety Interlock:** Prevents tube operation with cold/damaged filament (avoids anode bombardment without electron cloud).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Thermionic Emission — The Foundation

The filament does **not** directly produce X-rays. It produces **electrons** which are then accelerated across a high potential difference (kVp) to strike the tungsten anode target. The X-ray spectrum emerges from this collision.

### 2.1.1 Richardson-Dushman Equation (Modified)

The current density of emitted electrons is governed by:

$$
J = A_{0} \, T^{2} \, e^{-W / (k_{B} T)}
$$

Where:
- $J$ = Emission current density in **A/m²**
- $A_{0}$ = Richardson's constant ≈ **6.02 × 10⁵ A·m⁻²·K⁻²** (for tungsten)
- $T$ = Absolute filament temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
- $W$ = Work function of the emitter material in **eV** (Tungsten: **4.55 eV**)
- $k_{B}$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the steep curve:** Because $T^{2}$ multiplies an exponential $e^{-W/k_BT}$, even a 5% rise in filament voltage (which raises T by ~3%) causes the mA to **double or triple**. This is why filament current must be regulated with extreme precision.

## 2.2 The "Steepness" Phenomenon

**Steepness** is defined as the **slope of the tube current (mA) vs. filament current (A) curve** at any operating point.

$$
S = \frac{\partial I_{tube}}{\partial I_{filament}}
$$

Typical values: $S$ lies between **5 and 50 mA per 0.1 A change in filament current** in the operating region. This makes manual mA control nearly impossible without an automatic stabilizer.

## 2.3 Space-Charge Effect — The "Electron Traffic Jam"

When the tube is operating, electrons emitted from the filament form a **negative space charge** cloud surrounding the cathode. This cloud partially repels newly emitted electrons back to the filament.

**Consequence:** As kVp increases, the space-charge compression is reduced (electrons pulled away faster), causing the **tube current to rise** even when the filament temperature is held constant. This unwanted mA drift must be corrected.

**Solution:** The **Space-Charge Compensator (SCC)** automatically adjusts the filament current downward as kVp rises, keeping the mA constant.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = A_{0} T^{2} e^{-W/k_{B}T}$ | Richardson-Dushman current density | A/m² |
| 2 | $P_{filament} = V_f \cdot I_f$ | Power dissipated in filament | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{V_p}{V_s} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s}$ | Transformer turns ratio (primary/secondary) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $I_{tube} \propto T^{2} e^{-W/k_{B}T}$ | Tube current dependence on T | mA |
| 5 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS filament voltage | V |
| 6 | $R_{filament} = \rho \dfrac{L}{A_{cs}}$ | Filament resistance (cold vs. hot) | Ω |
| 7 | $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{1.24}{V_{kVp}}$ | Minimum X-ray wavelength | nm |
| 8 | $\eta_{x-ray} = 9.2 \times 10^{-10} \cdot V \cdot Z$ | X-ray production efficiency | fraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Replacement Symbols:** Use `\vert x \vert` instead of $\vert x \vert$ when writing inside markdown tables to avoid table-breaking pipes.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The filament circuit is the **heartbeat of mA control** in every diagnostic X-ray machine — from portable bedside units to 1500 mA high-throughput angiography suites and from superficial orthovoltage (50–300 kVp) to megavoltage (6–25 MV) linear accelerators. In **modern solid-state generators**, the same function is implemented digitally using **PWM choppers** and **closed-loop current feedback** rather than mechanical rheostats, giving better than **±1% mA accuracy** — a key requirement for digital radiography and CT.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Filament Temperature from Voltage & Current

**Given:** A stationary diagnostic X-ray tube filament has cold resistance $R_{20} = 0.5\ \Omega$ at 20 °C. Tungsten temperature coefficient $\alpha = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$. Operating voltage $V_f = 10$ V, current $I_f = 5$ A.

**Find:** Filament operating temperature $T_{op}$ in °C.

**Step 1 — Hot resistance at operating temperature:**

$$
R_{hot} = \frac{V_f}{I_f} = \frac{10\ \text{V}}{5\ \text{A}} = 2.0\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the temperature-resistance relation:**

$$
R_{hot} = R_{20}\,\bigl[1 + \alpha\,(T_{op} - 20)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
2.0 = 0.5\,\bigl[1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\,(T_{op} - 20)\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically:**

$$
\frac{2.0}{0.5} = 1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\,(T_{op} - 20)
$$

$$
4 = 1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\,(T_{op} - 20)
$$

$$
3 = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\,(T_{op} - 20)
$$

$$
T_{op} - 20 = \frac{3}{4.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 666.67\ ^\circ C
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$
T_{op} = 686.67\ ^\circ C \approx 960\ \text{K}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** The temperature obtained via resistance-rise is the **average bulk temperature**. The actual electron-emitting surface temperature (measured optically) is typically 200–300 °C higher because the coiled filament has a non-uniform thermal distribution. Always mention this distinction in viva.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Saturation Emission Current from Richardson-Dushman

**Given:** A tungsten cathode of emitting area $A = 1.0\ \text{cm}^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$ operates at $T = 2500\ \text{K}$. Work function $W = 4.55$ eV.

**Step 1 — Convert work function to Joules:**

$$
W = 4.55 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} = 7.28 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate exponential term:**

$$
\frac{W}{k_{B} T} = \frac{7.28 \times 10^{-19}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 2500} = 21.10
$$

$$
e^{-W/k_{B} T} = e^{-21.10} = 6.92 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the $T^{2}$ multiplier:**

$$
T^{2} = (2500)^{2} = 6.25 \times 10^{6}\ \text{K}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate current density:**

$$
J = 6.02 \times 10^{5} \times 6.25 \times 10^{6} \times 6.92 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
J = 2.604 \times 10^{3}\ \text{A/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Total saturation current:**

$$
I_{sat} = J \times A = 2.604 \times 10^{3} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.260\ \text{A} = 260\ \text{mA}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even a tiny 1 cm² tungsten filament at 2500 K can saturate at 260 mA — more than enough to drive any clinical tube. The real bottleneck is anode heating, not electron supply.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Filament Transformer Turns Ratio

**Given:** Mains supply = 230 V, 50 Hz. Required filament voltage = 10 V (RMS).

**Step 1 — Apply ideal transformer relation:**

$$
\frac{V_{p}}{V_{s}} = \frac{N_{p}}{N_{s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$
\frac{N_{p}}{N_{s}} = \frac{230}{10} = 23 : 1
$$

**Step 3 — Assume $N_{s} = 50$ turns (standard for compact filament transformer):**

$$
N_{p} = 23 \times 50 = 1150\ \text{turns}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insulation Note:** The primary of the filament transformer is at **mains potential (230 V)**, but the **secondary is at cathode potential** (which can swing between -75 kV and +75 kV in a full-wave rectified circuit). The secondary winding must therefore withstand the **full kVp** through its insulation — typically oil-impregnated paper with creepage distances exceeding **10 mm/kV**.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Space-Charge Limited Current (Child's Law)

When the tube current is **space-charge limited** (typical below saturation):

$$
I_{tube} = K \cdot V_{kVp}^{3/2}
$$

Where $K$ is the perveance constant of the tube geometry. This is **Child's Law** for a planar diode, adapted to cylindrical X-ray tube geometry. This non-linear $V^{3/2}$ dependence is precisely why the **space-charge compensator** must apply a kVp-proportional correction to filament current.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Filament Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ACMAINS["AC MAINS 230V 50Hz"] --> ISO["ISOLATION TRANSFORMER"]
    ISO --> VAT["VARIABLE AUTO TRANSFORMER VARIAC"]
    VAT --> RC["RHEOSTAT OR FILAMENT RESISTOR BANK"]
    RC --> FPT["FILAMENT TRANSFORMER PRIMARY 230V SIDE"]
    FPT -.MAGNETIC COUPLING.-> FST["FILAMENT TRANSFORMER SECONDARY 10V SIDE"]
    FST --> SCC["SPACE CHARGE COMPENSATOR mA STABILIZER"]
    SCC --> BOOST["FILAMENT BOOST RELAY CLOSED AT EXPOSURE"]
    BOOST --> FIL["X-RAY TUBE CATHODE FILAMENT TUNGSTEN"]
    FIL --> FCUP["FOCUSING CUP"]
    FST --> MMETER["mA METER MILLIAMMETER"]
    FIL --> ANODE["X-RAY TUBE ANODE TUNGSTEN TARGET"]
    ANODE --> HVT["HIGH TENSION TRANSFORMER"]
    HVT -.HV ISOLATION.-> EARTH["GROUND EARTH"]
    RC --> SEL["mA SELECTOR SWITCH 100 200 400 800 mA"]
    SEL --> FPT
    BOOST --> CTRL["EXPOSURE TIMER AND CONTACTOR"]
    CTRL --> BOOST
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Three-Point Persuasion for Filament Stabilization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FS["FILAMENT SUPPLY 10V AC"] --> SENSE["CURRENT SENSE RESISTOR SHUNT 0.01 OHM"]
    SENSE --> XFT["X-RAY TUBE FILAMENT"]
    SENSE --> FBK["FEEDBACK SIGNAL TO mA STABILIZER PCB"]
    FBK --> COMP["COMPARATOR WITH REFERENCE mA SETPOINT"]
    COMP --> DRV["PWM DRIVER IC"]
    DRV --> FS
    COMP -.ERROR SIGNAL.-> LOG["ERROR LOG BOOK FOR KTU VIVA"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block — Filament Circuit Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL SECTION LV"]
        C1["kVp and mA Selector Panel"]
        C2["mA Stabilizer Feedback Amplifier"]
        C3["Filament Boost Timer"]
    end
    subgraph POWER["POWER SECTION LV"]
        P1["Step Down Filament Transformer"]
        P2["Silicon Controlled Rectifier SCR Chopper"]
        P3["Filament Current Regulating Rheostat"]
    end
    subgraph TUBE["X-RAY TUBE SECTION HV"]
        T1["Cathode Filament Tungsten"]
        T2["Focusing Cup"]
        T3["Anode Tungsten Target"]
    end
    C1 --> P3
    C2 --> P2
    C3 --> P1
    P3 --> T1
    P2 --> T1
    P1 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the filament circuit", the examiner expects **at minimum**: (1) mains input, (2) step-down transformer with turns ratio marked, (3) rheostat or electronic regulator, (4) mA meter, (5) X-ray tube filament symbol, and (6) clear labeling of the HV isolation boundary.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the filament circuit of an X-ray tube. State its two main functions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The filament circuit, also called the **low-tension circuit**, is the low-voltage electrical sub-circuit of an X-ray generator that supplies heating current to the **thermionic cathode** of the X-ray tube. *(1 mark)*

It comprises a step-down **filament transformer**, a **rheostat / mA selector**, a **space-charge compensator**, a **milliammeter** and **safety interlocks** all connected in series with the cathode filament. *(1 mark)*

**Two main functions:**
1. To heat the tungsten filament to its operating temperature (~2500 K) and produce thermionic emission of electrons.
2. To control and stabilize the resulting tube current (mA) independent of kVp variations. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is meant by "steepness" of an X-ray tube? Why is it important in filament circuit design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Steepness** is defined as the **slope of the tube-current versus filament-current characteristic curve**, i.e. $S = \partial I_{tube} / \partial I_{filament}$. *(1 mark)*

It represents how sensitively the tube mA responds to small changes in filament heating current. For diagnostic X-ray tubes, the curve is **highly non-linear** in the operating region, with $S$ values of the order of 5–50 mA per 0.1 A change. *(1 mark)*

**Importance:** Because the curve is so steep, manual setting of mA using a simple rheostat is impractical. Therefore, the filament circuit incorporates an **automatic space-charge compensator / mA stabilizer** with closed-loop electronic feedback to maintain mA accuracy within ±5–10%. Without this stabilization, mA would drift uncontrollably with kVp and mains voltage fluctuations. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer **ONE full question** from a choice of two. Each question carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module Choice Q1]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat labeled circuit diagram, describe the filament circuit of a modern X-ray unit. Explain the role of the filament transformer, rheostat, space-charge compensator, and milliammeter.

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit Description (4 marks):**
The filament circuit is energized from the 230 V, 50 Hz AC mains through an isolation transformer. The mains voltage is fed to a variable auto-transformer (Variac) which allows fine adjustment. The output of the Variac is applied to the **primary winding of the filament transformer** (turns ratio ~23:1), whose secondary delivers approximately 10 V AC to the cathode filament of the X-ray tube. Connected in series with the secondary are:
1. A **rheostat / mA selector** (resistance bank) to preset the filament current.
2. A **space-charge compensator (SCC)** — an automatic current regulator that senses kVp and adjusts filament current inversely.
3. A **milliammeter** (mA meter) connected between the cathode and the grounded mid-point of the high-tension transformer secondary.
4. A **filament boost relay** which is energized at the start of every exposure to pre-heat the filament.

**Function of Each Component (3 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Filament Transformer** | Steps down 230 V to ~10 V; provides high-voltage isolation between mains and the cathode (which floats at ±kVp). |
| **Rheostat / mA Selector** | Presets the filament current to give the required tube mA (100, 200, 400 mA stations). |
| **Space-Charge Compensator** | Automatically reduces filament current as kVp rises, compensating for the space-charge effect and keeping mA constant. |
| **Milliammeter** | Measures the average tube current flowing through the tube, providing a direct read-out of mA during exposure. |
| **Filament Boost** | Briefly raises filament temperature before exposure so that mA stabilizes faster (typical 0.5–1 s boost). |

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the thermionic emission current density from a heated cathode. Hence explain why the filament current must be precisely controlled.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Consider electrons inside a metal at absolute temperature $T$. The Fermi-Dirac distribution gives the number of electrons with energy above the vacuum level $E_{vac}$ (where $E_{vac} = E_F + W$, $W$ being the work function) as:

$$
N(E > E_{vac}) \propto T \cdot e^{-W / (k_B T)}
$$

Each emitted electron carries charge $e$. Integrating over all velocities directed away from the surface yields the **Richardson-Dushman equation**:

$$
J = A_{0}\,T^{2}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{W}{k_B T}\right)
$$

where $A_0 = \frac{4\pi m_e e k_B^2}{h^{3}} \approx 6.02 \times 10^{5}\ \text{A·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-2}$ for tungsten.

**Why precise control is needed (2 marks):**
The equation shows that $J$ depends **exponentially** on $T$ (and hence on the filament voltage and current). A mere 3% increase in filament voltage can cause $J$ — and therefore tube mA — to **double or triple**. This extreme sensitivity ("steepness") mandates:
1. **High-precision regulators** (electronic mA stabilizers, not just rheostats).
2. **Closed-loop feedback** from the actual mA measurement back to the filament supply.
3. **Stabilized mains supply** (often with servo-controlled Variacs) to prevent mains drift from propagating into mA drift.

In modern digital radiography, mA must be accurate to **better than ±5%** to ensure consistent detector exposure and image quality.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module Choice Q2]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the space-charge effect in an X-ray tube. How is it compensated in the filament circuit? Describe with a circuit schematic.

**Model Answer:**

**Space-Charge Effect (3 marks):**
The thermionic emission process generates a dense cloud of electrons around the cathode filament. Since all these electrons carry negative charge, they exert a **repulsive electrostatic force** on the newly emitted electrons, pushing some of them back to the filament. This is called the **space-charge effect**, and it limits the tube current to a value below the temperature-limited saturation current.

The space-charge limited current follows **Child's Law**:

$$
I_{tube} = K \cdot V_{kVp}^{3/2}
$$

Thus, if the filament temperature is held constant but kVp is increased, $I_{tube}$ rises — an undesired mA drift that degrades radiographic consistency.

**Compensation by Space-Charge Compensator (3 marks):**
The SCC is a **closed-loop electronic controller** that senses the kVp setting and reduces the filament voltage/current accordingly. In a typical implementation:
- A small portion of the kVp setting voltage is tapped from the autotransformer and fed to the SCC as a reference.
- The SCC modulates the firing angle of an **SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier)** in the filament supply, decreasing the RMS filament voltage as kVp increases.
- A feedback resistor in series with the filament provides a mA-proportional signal that is compared with the reference, and the error drives the SCR gate.

The result: **mA remains constant** to within ±5% across the entire kVp range (typically 50–125 kVp).

**Schematic (1 mark):** (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4.1 — the SCC node is clearly visible.)

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A diagnostic X-ray tube uses a tungsten filament of emitting area 0.8 cm² operating at 2500 K. Calculate (i) the saturation emission current, and (ii) the percentage change in emission current if the filament temperature rises by 50 K. Given: $A_0 = 6.02 \times 10^5\ \text{A·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-2}$, $W = 4.55$ eV, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Work function in Joules:**

$$
W = 4.55 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 7.28 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $J$ at $T_1 = 2500$ K:**

$$
\frac{W}{k_B T_1} = \frac{7.28 \times 10^{-19}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 2500} = 21.10
$$

$$
J_1 = 6.02 \times 10^{5} \times (2500)^2 \times e^{-21.10}
$$

$$
J_1 = 6.02 \times 10^{5} \times 6.25 \times 10^{6} \times 6.92 \times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
J_1 = 2604\ \text{A/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $I_{sat}$:**

$$
I_{sat} = J_1 \times A = 2604 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-4} = 0.208\ \text{A} \approx 208\ \text{mA}
$$

*(3 marks for part (i))*

**Step 4 — Compute $J$ at $T_2 = 2550$ K:**

$$
\frac{W}{k_B T_2} = \frac{7.28 \times 10^{-19}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 2550} = 20.69
$$

$$
J_2 = 6.02 \times 10^{5} \times (2550)^2 \times e^{-20.69}
$$

$$
J_2 = 6.02 \times 10^{5} \times 6.5025 \times 10^{6} \times 1.012 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
J_2 \approx 3961\ \text{A/m}^{2}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Percentage change:**

$$
\Delta J\% = \frac{J_2 - J_1}{J_1} \times 100 = \frac{3961 - 2604}{2604} \times 100 \approx 52.1\%
$$

*(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert eV to J** for the work function — costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong area units** — using cm² instead of m² in $I = J \times A$ is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. **No comment on the steepness** — the examiner expects you to state the conclusion that a 50 K rise in T causes a **>50% rise in mA**, justifying the need for a stabilizer.
> 4. **Writing "filament voltage" instead of "filament temperature"** in part (b) — they are related but not identical concepts; in calculations involving Richardson's law, you must use **T in Kelvin**.
> 5. **Not drawing the high-voltage isolation barrier** in part (a) schematic — examiners allocate 0.5–1 mark specifically for showing the dotted isolation line between primary and secondary of the filament transformer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Filament Circuit**

- [x] **Filament circuit = Low-Tension (L.T.) Circuit**; supplies ~10 V, ~5 A to the cathode.
- [x] **Three subsystems:** (1) Filament transformer (step-down, HV isolated), (2) Regulator (rheostat / SCR), (3) Stabilizer (SCC).
- [x] **Thermionic emission** is governed by **Richardson-Dushman equation** $J = A_0 T^2 e^{-W/k_B T}$.
- [x] **Tungsten work function** = **4.55 eV**; Richardson's constant $A_0$ = **6.02 × 10⁵ A·m⁻²·K⁻²**.
- [x] **Filament operating temperature** ≈ **2400–2600 K** (well below tungsten's melting point of 3422 °C = 3695 K).
- [x] **Steepness** = slope of mA vs. filament current; typically very high → mA is extremely sensitive to filament current.
- [x] **Space-charge effect** causes mA to rise with kVp; compensated by **SCC (Space-Charge Compensator)**.
- [x] **Space-charge limited current** follows **Child's Law** $I = K V^{3/2}$.
- [x] **Filament boost** = brief pre-heat to overcome cold-cathode emission lag at the start of exposure.
- [x] **High-voltage isolation:** Filament transformer's secondary is at cathode potential (±kVp). Insulation must withstand **full kVp**.
- [x] **mA meter** is placed in the **mid-point grounded** leg of the HV transformer secondary — never in the high-side leg.
- [x] **Modern solid-state filament circuits** use **PWM choppers** and **closed-loop current feedback** for ±1% accuracy.
- [x] **Three-terminal mA station selection:** Different mA stations correspond to different preset filament currents (e.g., 100 mA ≈ 4.2 A, 400 mA ≈ 4.8 A).
- [x] **For numerical problems:** Always convert **eV → J**, **cm² → m²**, **°C → K** before substitution.
- [x] **Clinical relevance:** Filament circuit failure leads to "no mA" or "low mA" errors — among the most common X-ray equipment faults.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Principles of Radio Diagnosis & Radiotherapy**, **"Meters"** refers to the class of radiation detection, measurement, and dosimetric instruments used to quantify ionizing radiation in clinical, diagnostic, and therapeutic environments. These devices convert incident radiation energy into measurable electrical, optical, or chemical signals that correlate with the absorbed dose, exposure rate, or activity of the radiation field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCEBT602, Module 1):**
> Radiation meters are precision instruments designed to measure the quantity, quality, and intensity of ionizing radiation (X-rays, gamma rays, beta particles, and neutrons) emitted from diagnostic X-ray units, linear accelerators, brachytherapy sources, and radioisotopes used in nuclear medicine. They form the **primary link between the physical radiation field and the clinician's quantitative understanding** of dose delivery.

### Classification of Radiation Meters (KTU High-Yield Overview)

| Functional Role | Instrument Family | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Dosimetry** | Ionization chambers, TLDs, OSLDs, Calorimeters | Absorbed Dose (Gy) |
| **Survey / Contamination** | Geiger-Müller (GM) counters, Proportional counters | Exposure rate (R/h, Sv/h) |
| **Personal Monitoring** | Film badges, Pocket dosimeters, Electronic personal dosimeters (EPDs) | Cumulative Dose Equivalent (Sv) |
| **Activity Measurement** | Well counters, Dose calibrators | Radioactivity (Bq, Ci) |
| **Quality Assurance** | KVp meters, mA meters, Timer, Light/radiation field coincidence testers | kV, mA, s, mm |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine driving a car on a highway. To avoid speeding tickets and accidents, you rely on the **speedometer** — a device that translates the invisible motion of the car into a needle that you can read instantly.

In radiation work, we are dealing with an *invisible* hazard — **ionizing radiation cannot be seen, heard, smelled, tasted, or felt**. A radiation meter is therefore the **"speedometer for X-rays and gamma rays."**

- The **GM survey meter** is like a *burglar alarm* — it beeps louder as more radiation arrives, ideal for detecting the *presence* of a source.
- The **ionization chamber dosimeter** is like a *precision scientific weighing scale* — it gives the exact numerical *dose* the patient or staff received.
- The **personal film badge** is like an *airline black box* — it silently records cumulative exposure over weeks, ready to be "read" later in a laboratory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need different types of meters?**
> Because the *job* differs. Detecting whether a source is leaking (a "yes/no" alarm task) requires a GM counter. Measuring the exact dose a tumour receives during radiotherapy (a "gram-precise" task) requires a calibrated ionization chamber. **One size does not fit all in radiation metrology.**

## 1.3 Fundamental Quantities Measured by Meters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these Five SI/Traditional Quantities (Most Repeated in KTU Papers):**

1. **Exposure (X)** — The amount of ionization produced in air per unit mass.
   - SI Unit: **C/kg** (Coulomb per kilogram)
   - Traditional Unit: **Roentgen (R)** where $1\,R = 2.58 \times 10^{-4}\,C/kg$

2. **Absorbed Dose (D)** — Energy deposited per unit mass of any medium.
   - SI Unit: **Gray (Gy)** where $1\,Gy = 1\,J/kg$

3. **Equivalent Dose (H)** — Biological-effect-weighted dose.
   - SI Unit: **Sievert (Sv)** where $H = D \times W_R$

4. **Effective Dose (E)** — Tissue-weighted whole-body equivalent dose.
   - SI Unit: **Sievert (Sv)**

5. **Radioactivity (A)** — Decay rate of a radioactive source.
   - SI Unit: **Becquerel (Bq)** where $1\,Bq = 1$ disintegration/second
   - Traditional Unit: **Curie (Ci)** where $1\,Ci = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\,Bq$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Radiation Meter Design

Every radiation meter — regardless of its specific type — operates on three universal principles. Understanding these three pillars is essential for any KTU examination.

### Pillar 1: The Radiation-Matter Interaction Event
The radiation must *interact* with the detector medium to produce a measurable change. The most important interaction mechanisms used in clinical meters are:

- **Photoelectric Effect** — Dominant at low energies (< 50 keV); used in diagnostic X-ray detectors and film badges.
- **Compton Scattering** — Dominant in the therapeutic energy range (200 keV – 10 MeV); primary event in therapy ionization chambers.
- **Pair Production** — Occurs only at energies > 1.022 MeV; relevant in high-energy linac monitoring.

### Pillar 2: The Transduction Mechanism
The interaction event must be *converted* into a measurable signal:

| Mechanism | Detector Example | Signal Produced |
|---|---|---|
| Ionization of gas | Ionization chamber, GM tube | Electric current / pulse |
| Excitation of scintillator | NaI(Tl) scintillation counter | Light photons → photomultiplier current |
| Chemical change | Film emulsion, Fricke dosimeter | Optical density / chemical absorbance |
| Thermoluminescence | LiF:Mg,Ti (TLD) | Light emitted on heating |
| Optically Stimulated Luminescence | Al₂O₃:C (OSLD) | Light emitted on laser stimulation |
| Calorimetry (heat rise) | Water/graphite calorimeter | Temperature change (μK level) |

### Pillar 3: The Readout / Calibration
The signal is processed, displayed (digital count, needle deflection, or stored latent image), and **calibrated against a national primary standard** traceable to NIST/IAEA/BARC.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Concept — Traceability:**
> Clinical meters in India must be **traceable to the BARC (Bhabha Atomic Research Centre) Secondary Standard Dosimetry Laboratory (SSDL)**. This is the legal basis of AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board) compliance and is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all mathematically verifiable formulas** essential for Module 1 — Meters. Memorize the relationships — they form the backbone of every Part B derivation in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Standard Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exposure (X) | $X = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta m_{air}}$ | C/kg | Definition of exposure in air |
| 2 | Conversion: Roentgen to C/kg | $1\,R = 2.58 \times 10^{-4}\,C/kg$ | — | Unit conversion in problems |
| 3 | Specific Energy (W/e) in air | $\dfrac{W}{e} = 33.97\,J/C$ | J/C | Mean energy per ion pair in dry air |
| 4 | Exposure → Absorbed Dose in air | $D_{air} = X \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right)$ | Gy | Dose to air from exposure |
| 5 | F-factor (air to medium) | $D_{med} = D_{air} \cdot \left(\dfrac{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{med}}{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{air}}\right) = X \cdot 0.876 \cdot \dfrac{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{med}}{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{air}}$ | Gy | Dose to tissue/bone/water from air exposure |
| 6 | Standard F-factor (muscle) | $F_{muscle} = 0.93$ | Gy/R | Tissue dose from exposure in R |
| 7 | Standard F-factor (bone) | $F_{bone} = 3.78$ (low energy) → ~1.0 (high energy) | Gy/R | Bone dose |
| 8 | Equivalent Dose | $H = D \cdot W_R$ | Sv | Biological weighting |
| 9 | Effective Dose | $E = \sum_T H_T \cdot W_T$ | Sv | Whole-body stochastic risk |
| 10 | Radioactivity decay law | $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Bq | Source strength over time |
| 11 | Half-life relation | $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | s, h, d, y | Decay constant calculation |
| 12 | Mean life (τ) | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{T_{1/2}}{0.693}$ | s | Average life of a radionuclide |
| 13 | GM dead time formula (non-paralysable) | $n = \dfrac{m}{1 - m\tau_d}$ | counts/s | True count rate correction |
| 14 | Curie ↔ Becquerel | $1\,Ci = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\,Bq$ | — | Activity conversion |
| 15 | Roentgen ↔ Rad (air) | $1\,R \approx 0.876\,rad$ in air | — | Air dose conversion |
| 16 | Rad ↔ Gray | $1\,Gy = 100\,rad$ | — | Dose conversion |
| 17 | Rem ↔ Sievert | $1\,Sv = 100\,rem$ | — | Equivalent dose conversion |
| 18 | SSD / SAD geometry | $D_2 = D_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{SSD_1}{SSD_2}\right)^2$ | Gy | Inverse square law in radiotherapy |
| 19 | Percentage depth dose (PDD) | $PDD = \dfrac{D_d}{D_{max}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Therapy dose distribution |
| 20 | Calibration of chamber: N_x → N_D | $N_D = N_x \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{X}$ | Gy/C | Dosimeter calibration factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution for Markdown Table Rendering:** In all formulas above, the absolute value and division bars are written using LaTeX `\dfrac`, `\left(`, `\right)`, and vertical lines (not the pipe `|` symbol), preserving table integrity as required by the KTU-Premium-Engine.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Radio Diagnosis & Radiotherapy

- **Diagnostic Radiology:** kVp meters and mA meters verify the *output consistency* of an X-ray tube; entrance exposure meters monitor patient dose during fluoroscopy to comply with the **ALARA** principle.
- **Radiotherapy:** Daily *output constancy* checks of the linear accelerator use a **Farmer-type 0.6 cm³ ionization chamber** paired with an **electrometer** — this is the legal dosimetric chain required by AERB.
- **Nuclear Medicine:** A **dose calibrator** (a re-entrant well-type ionization chamber) measures patient-specific radiopharmaceutical activity immediately before injection (e.g., Tc-99m MDP for bone scan, I-131 for thyroid therapy).
- **Radiation Protection:** Survey meters with GM tubes or ionization chambers monitor **controlled** and **supervised areas** (per ICRP and AERB safety codes). Personal dosimeters (TLD, OSL, film) monitor occupational exposure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Exposure (X) to Absorbed Dose in a Medium (F-Factor Derivation)

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard):**
Derive the relationship between Exposure $X$ (in C/kg) and the absorbed dose $D_{med}$ in any medium, expressing the proportionality constant as the **F-factor**.

### Step 1 — Definition of Exposure
By definition, exposure is the charge $\Delta Q$ produced by ionizing radiation per unit mass $\Delta m$ of air.

$$X = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta m_{air}} \quad \text{[Units: C/kg]}$$

### Step 2 — Charge to Energy Conversion Using W/e
The charge $\Delta Q$ is produced by the creation of $\Delta Q / e$ ion pairs. The average energy required to create **one ion pair in dry air** is denoted $W$ (often $W \approx 33.97\,eV$ per ion pair, or $33.97\,J/C$).

The total energy absorbed in the mass $\Delta m$ of air is therefore:

$$\Delta E_{air} = \Delta Q \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Absorbed Dose in Air
The absorbed dose in air is, by definition, energy absorbed per unit mass of air:

$$D_{air} = \dfrac{\Delta E_{air}}{\Delta m_{air}} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta m_{air}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right) = X \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right)$$

Therefore, in SI units:

$$D_{air}[Gy] = X\left[\dfrac{C}{kg}\right] \times 33.97\,\dfrac{J}{C}$$

### Step 4 — Converting Air Dose to Dose in Any Other Medium
For a photon beam, the dose to a medium depends on its **mass energy absorption coefficient** $(\mu_{en}/\rho)$ relative to that of air. The dose ratio at the same point in the same photon field is:

$$\dfrac{D_{med}}{D_{air}} = \dfrac{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{med}}{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{air}}$$

### Step 5 — Substituting and Simplifying
Combine Steps 3 and 4:

$$D_{med} = D_{air} \cdot \dfrac{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{med}}{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{air}}$$

$$\boxed{D_{med} = X \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{med}}{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{air}} = X \cdot F_{med}}$$

where the **F-factor** is defined as:

$$F_{med} = \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{med}}{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{air}} \quad [\text{Units: Gy/C/kg or Gy/R}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (muscle tissue at cobalt-60 energy, 1.25 MeV):**
> At Co-60 energy, $\dfrac{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{muscle}}{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{air}} \approx 1.103$.
> Therefore: $F_{muscle} = 33.97 \times 1.103 \approx 37.5\,J/C \approx 0.965\,Gy/R$ (≈ 0.93 for low-energy diagnostic beams).
> Hence, an exposure of $1\,R$ delivers approximately $0.965\,Gy$ to muscle at Co-60 energy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Radioactive Decay Law and Half-Life

**Problem Statement:**
Derive the radioactive decay law $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and relate the decay constant $\lambda$ to the half-life $T_{1/2}$.

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation
The rate of decay of radioactive atoms is directly proportional to the number of atoms present (first-order kinetics):

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

where $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (units: s⁻¹), unique to each radionuclide.

### Step 2 — Separation of Variables

$$\dfrac{dN}{N} = -\lambda\,dt$$

### Step 3 — Integration from $N_0$ at $t=0$ to $N$ at time $t$

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \dfrac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln N - \ln N_0 = -\lambda t$$

### Step 4 — Exponentiation

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Step 5 — Converting to Activity (A = λN)
Since activity $A = \lambda N$:

$$A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 6 — Half-Life Relation
By definition of half-life, $N = N_0/2$ when $t = T_{1/2}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln 2 = \lambda T_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Co-60, T₁/₂ = 5.27 years):**
> A Co-60 source had an activity of $5000\,Ci$ in January 2010. What is its activity in January 2030 (20 years later)?
> $\lambda = 0.693/5.27 = 0.1315\,yr^{-1}$
> $A = 5000 \cdot e^{-0.1315 \times 20} = 5000 \cdot e^{-2.630} = 5000 \times 0.0718 = 359\,Ci$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Geiger-Müller (GM) Counter Dead-Time Correction

**Problem Statement:**
A GM counter records $m$ counts per second. Given the dead time $\tau_d$, derive the formula for the true count rate $n$ (non-paralysable model).

### Step 1 — Define Dead Time
After each genuine detection event, the GM tube is "dead" (non-responsive) for a fixed interval $\tau_d$. During this interval, any incident radiation produces **no** output pulse.

### Step 2 — True vs. Observed Counting
- Let $n$ = **true** count rate (counts/s arriving at the tube).
- Let $m$ = **observed/recorded** count rate.
- The tube is *alive* for a fraction $(1 - m\tau_d)$ of the total time, because in every second of recorded time, the tube is dead for $m \times \tau_d$ seconds.

### Step 3 — Establish the Relationship
The observed count rate equals the true count rate multiplied by the *live* time fraction:

$$m = n \cdot (1 - m\tau_d)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the True Count Rate $n$

$$m = n - n \cdot m \tau_d$$

$$m + n \cdot m \tau_d = n$$

$$m = n(1 - m\tau_d)$$

$$\boxed{n = \dfrac{m}{1 - m\tau_d}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity check:** As $m\tau_d \to 0$ (very low count rate), $n \to m$ ✓. As $m\tau_d \to 1$, $n \to \infty$ (paralysable limit). This formula is the basis of **count-loss correction in survey instruments and nuclear counting systems**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Meter

| Application | Quantity Needed | Recommended Meter | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Personnel dose monitoring (monthly) | Hp(10) | TLD / OSLD / Film badge | Cumulative, passive, archival |
| Patient skin dose in interventional radiology | D_surface | MOSFET dosimeter / TLD | Real-time, small, sterile-friendly |
| Daily linac output check | D_water at d_max | 0.6 cm³ Farmer chamber + electrometer | High precision, traceable |
| Source strength in nuclear medicine | Activity (Bq/Ci) | Dose calibrator (well chamber) | Geometry-matched to vials/syringes |
| Area monitoring near PET | H*(10) rate | Ion chamber survey meter (auto-ranging) | Energy-independent, high range |
| Surface contamination check | cps (counts per second) | GM pancake probe | High sensitivity, large area |
| **Calorimetric primary standard** | D (absolute) | Water calorimeter / graphite calorimeter | Absolute (no calibration factor needed) |
| High-energy neutron detection | H*(10) neutron | Bonner sphere / moderated He-3 proportional | Neutron moderation + capture |
