# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the X-ray spectrum. Name the two components of a diagnostic X-ray spectrum.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **X-ray spectrum** is the intensity distribution of X-ray photons emitted from an X-ray tube as a function of their energy (keV) or wavelength (Å/nm). It is a graphical plot of photon intensity versus energy.

The two components are:
1. **Continuous (Bremsstrahlung) spectrum** — produced by the rapid deceleration of high-speed electrons in the strong electric field of target nuclei. It forms a continuous bell-shaped curve.
2. **Characteristic (Line) spectrum** — produced when incident electrons eject inner-shell (K-shell) electrons of the target atom, and outer-shell electrons drop down to fill the vacancy, emitting photons of discrete, target-specific energies (Kα, Kβ, L-series).

---

### **Q2. State and explain the Duane–Hunt law.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** The minimum wavelength of X-rays emitted from an X-ray tube is inversely proportional to the applied tube voltage.

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24}{kV_{p}} \text{ nm} = \dfrac{12.4}{kV_{p}} \text{ Å}$$

**Explanation:** This follows from the principle of conservation of energy. The maximum photon energy is produced when an entire electron's kinetic energy ($eV$) is converted into a single photon. Since $E_{\max} = hc/\lambda_{\min}$, equating the two gives the Duane–Hunt law. For example, at 100 kVp, $\lambda_{\min} = 0.124$ Å.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**Explain in detail the various factors that influence the X-ray spectrum. Discuss how each factor affects the quality and quantity of the X-ray beam.**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks] — Discuss the electrical factors (kVp, mA, time, waveform).**

**Model Solution:**

The X-ray spectrum emitted by a diagnostic X-ray tube is shaped by six principal factors, four of which are electrical:

**(i) Tube Voltage (kVp) — The Most Influential Factor:**

The kilovoltage peak applied across the X-ray tube determines the maximum kinetic energy acquired by the bombarding electrons. By the Duane–Hunt law:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24}{kV_{p}} \text{ nm}$$

When kVp is increased:
- The **maximum photon energy** increases linearly with kVp.
- The **spectrum shifts rightward** — the peak intensity moves to higher energies.
- The **total X-ray output** increases approximately as $V^{2}$ (more accurately $V^{2.0}$ to $V^{2.5}$).
- The **penetrating power (quality)** of the beam increases — it becomes "harder."
- The **average energy** of the spectrum increases.

**(ii) Tube Current (mA):**

The mA controls the number of electrons crossing from cathode to anode per second. The X-ray intensity is directly proportional to mA:

$$I \propto mA \times Z \times V^{2}$$

However, mA does **not** alter the spectral shape, peak position, or maximum energy — it is purely a quantity parameter. Increasing mA simply lifts the entire spectrum vertically without shifting it.

**(iii) Exposure Time:**

Time and mA appear as the product **mAs** in the output equation. Doubling the exposure time at the same mA doubles the output. Time has no effect on beam quality.

**(iv) Voltage Waveform:**

Different generator types produce different voltage waveforms across the tube, which changes the effective average photon energy:
- **Single-phase full-wave rectified**: 100% ripple, lowest average energy.
- **Three-phase 6-pulse**: ~13% ripple, ~30% higher output than single-phase.
- **Three-phase 12-pulse**: ~4% ripple, ~50% higher output.
- **High-frequency / Constant potential**: <1% ripple, highest output, most uniform spectrum.

A constant potential generator produces a spectrum virtually identical to that predicted by theory, while single-phase produces a "weighted" spectrum biased toward lower energies.

#### **Part (b) [7 marks] — Discuss the physical factors (target Z, filtration, geometry).**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Target Material (Atomic Number Z):**

The atomic number of the target anode directly affects both the bremsstrahlung yield and the characteristic X-ray energies.

- **Bremsstrahlung efficiency** is proportional to Z: $I_{\text{brem}} \propto Z$.
- **Characteristic X-ray frequencies** follow **Moseley's law**:
$$\sqrt{\nu_{K\alpha}} = a(Z - b)$$
- Higher Z targets (e.g., W, Z = 74) produce **higher energy** characteristic X-rays (Kα at 59.3 keV) and a harder bremsstrahlung spectrum.
- For mammography, **Molybdenum (Z = 42, Kα at 17.5 keV)** and **Rhodium (Z = 45, Kα at 20.2 keV)** are preferred because their characteristic energies are ideally matched to the soft-tissue contrast requirements.

**(ii) Filtration (Inherent + Added):**

Filtration refers to the material placed in the path of the emerging X-ray beam to remove low-energy photons.

- **Inherent filtration (~0.5–1 mm Al equivalent)**: arises from the glass envelope, insulating oil, and beryllium window.
- **Added filtration (typically 2.5 mm Al in diagnostic radiology, or 0.5 mm Cu for higher kVp)**.

Effect on the spectrum:
- The **low-energy tail** is progressively removed — a phenomenon called **beam hardening**.
- The **average energy** of the beam shifts upward.
- The **HVT (Half-Value Layer)** increases, indicating improved beam quality.
- The **total quantity** of useful photons decreases slightly, but the **diagnostic quantity** increases substantially.
- **Patient skin dose is reduced** because low-energy photons that would have been absorbed superficially are filtered out.

**(iii) Target Angle and the Heel Effect:**

The anode is beveled at a small angle (typically 7°–20°) to define a small effective focal spot. This geometry produces a variation in X-ray intensity across the field, known as the **heel effect**:

$$I_{\theta} = I_{0} \cdot \cos^{n}(\theta)$$

The intensity is higher on the **cathode side** of the field (where X-rays pass through less target material) and lower on the **anode side**. The heel effect is exploited in mammography to compensate for varying tissue thickness across the breast but is generally a small effect in general radiography.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**With the help of a labeled spectrum diagram, derive the Duane–Hunt law. Discuss how the spectrum is modified by filtration and by changing the target material.**

#### **Part (a) [7 marks] — Derive the Duane–Hunt law and describe the spectrum.**

**Model Solution:**

**The X-ray Spectrum:**

The X-ray spectrum is a plot of X-ray intensity (vertical axis) versus photon energy in keV (horizontal axis). It consists of:
- A **continuous bell-shaped curve** (bremsstrahlung) with a sharp high-energy cutoff.
- **Discrete sharp peaks** (characteristic) superimposed on the curve, appearing only when kVp exceeds the K-shell binding energy of the target.

**Derivation of Duane–Hunt Law:**

Consider an electron of charge $e$ accelerated through a potential difference $V$. Its kinetic energy is:

$$E_{k} = eV$$

When this electron is suddenly decelerated and stopped at the target, conservation of energy demands that the *maximum* photon energy is produced when the **entire** kinetic energy is converted into a single X-ray photon:

$$E_{\text{photon, max}} = eV$$

Using the Planck relation $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$:

$$eV = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

Rearranging for $\lambda_{\min}$:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$$

Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \text{ meters}$$

When $V$ is expressed in kilovolts ($kV_{p}$) and $\lambda$ in nanometers:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24}{kV_{p}} \text{ nm} = \dfrac{12.4}{kV_{p}} \text{ Å}}$$

This proves the Duane–Hunt law: **the minimum wavelength is inversely proportional to the tube voltage.**

**Worked Numerical Example:**

For a tube operated at $kV_{p} = 80$ kV:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{12.4}{80} = 0.155 \text{ Å} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{\max} = 80 \text{ keV}$$

#### **Part (b) [7 marks] — Discuss the modifications due to filtration and target material.**

**Model Solution:**

**Effect of Filtration:**

When the X-ray beam passes through filtration material (Al, Cu, etc.), the low-energy photons are preferentially absorbed because the mass attenuation coefficient is much higher at low energies (photoelectric effect ∝ $\lambda^{3} Z^{3}$). The result is **beam hardening**:

- The **low-energy tail** of the spectrum is progressively cut off.
- The **peak of the spectrum shifts** to higher energies.
- The **average energy** (effective energy) of the beam increases.
- The **Half-Value Layer (HVL)** increases.
- **Patient skin dose** decreases because soft photons are removed before reaching the patient.
- The **spectrum area decreases slightly**, but the diagnostic portion is preserved.

A typical diagnostic beam uses 2.5 mm Al added filtration, raising the effective energy from approximately 1/3 of $E_{\max}$ (unfiltered) to 1/2 of $E_{\max}$ (filtered).

**Effect of Target Material:**

When the target material is changed, two changes occur in the spectrum:

**(1) Bremsstrahlung spectrum shifts:**

The bremsstrahlung intensity is proportional to $Z$, so a higher-Z target produces a more intense continuous spectrum. For example, switching from Molybdenum (Z = 42) to Tungsten (Z = 74) at the same kVp increases bremsstrahlung output by approximately 74/42 ≈ 1.76 times.

**(2) Characteristic peaks change in energy and position:**

The characteristic X-ray energies are governed by **Moseley's law**:

$$E_{K\alpha} = R_{\infty} h c (Z - 1)^{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{1^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{2^{2}} \right)$$

Numerically:
- **Tungsten (Z = 74):** $K\alpha_1$ at **59.3 keV**, $K\beta_1$ at **67.2 keV**.
- **Molybdenum (Z = 42):** $K\alpha_1$ at **17.5 keV**, $K\beta_1$ at **19.6 keV**.
- **Rhodium (Z = 45):** $K\alpha_1$ at **20.2 keV**, $K\beta_1$ at **22.7 keV**.

**Clinical application:** In mammography, Mo target with Mo filter produces nearly monoenergetic ~17.5–19.6 keV photons, ideal for soft-tissue contrast in breast imaging. Rh target with Rh filter is used for denser breasts. For general radiography, W target is universally used.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "quality" with "quantity":** Quality = beam energy / penetration (kVp dominates). Quantity = number of photons (mA, time dominate). Examiners specifically check this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the constants in the Duane–Hunt law:** Memorize the **1.24 nm** form for $kV_p$ in kV and the **12.4 Å** form for $kV_p$ in kV. Mixing up units = 0.5 mark penalty.
>
> 3. **Wrong Moseley's law constants:** The screening constant $b$ is **~1 for K-series** and **~7.4 for L-series**. Do not write a single constant for all series.
>
> 4. **Claiming mA changes the spectrum shape:** It does NOT. Only the *amplitude* changes. Examiners penalize this repeatedly.
>
> 5. **Confusing inherent and added filtration:** Inherent = glass + oil + beryllium window (built-in). Added = external Al/Cu placed by the radiographer.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Kramers' law derivation steps:** Examiner specifically awards 2 marks for the algebra of converting $dI/d\nu$ to $dI/d\lambda$.
>
> 7. **Missing units in the final answer:** Every spectrum problem must end with units (keV, nm, or Å). Missing units = up to 0.5 mark deduction.

---
