- If the **central field rises too slowly** ($\langle B \rangle < 2B(r)$), the electron's momentum grows faster than the centripetal force can bend its path → the orbit **expands** (electron spirals outward).
- If the **central field rises too quickly** ($\langle B \rangle > 2B(r)$), the centripetal force becomes excessive → the orbit **contracts** (electron spirals inward).
- Only when $\langle B \rangle = 2B(r)$ are the two effects **perfectly balanced**, and the electron remains on the *equilibrium orbit* of constant radius while gaining energy.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every key equation a KTU student must memorise for the Betatron topic. **The 1:2 condition and the energy formula are the most exam-relevant.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|:-:|:---------|:------------|:-------------:|
| 1 | $E_\theta \cdot 2\pi r = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's law applied to circular orbit | V, m, Wb |
| 2 | $eE_\theta = \dfrac{d(mv)}{dt}$ | Tangential acceleration of electron | N, C, kg, m/s |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d(mv)}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi r}\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Combining (1) and (2) | kg·m/s² |
| 4 | $mv = eB(r) \cdot r$ | Lorentz centripetal force balance (relativistic) | kg·m/s, T·m |
| 5 | $\langle B \rangle = 2 B(r)$ | **Betatron Condition (1:2 Rule)** | T |
| 6 | $\Phi = \langle B \rangle \cdot \pi r^2$ | Flux through orbit area | Wb |
| 7 | $W_{\max} = e \cdot B_{\max}(r) \cdot r$ | Non-relativistic max energy | J or eV |
| 8 | $W = m_0 c^2 \left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{eB(r) r}{m_0 c}\right)^2} - 1 \right]$ | **Relativistic maximum kinetic energy** | J or eV |
| 9 | $f = \dfrac{v}{2\pi r}$ | Orbital frequency of electron | Hz |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{2\pi r}{v}$ | Orbital period | s |
| 11 | $N = \dfrac{t_{\text{acc}}}{T}$ | Number of orbits during acceleration | dimensionless |
| 12 | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{2m_0 W}{e^2 B^2}}$ | Equilibrium radius for non-relativistic case | m |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The betatron's operating principle is more than a textbook curiosity:

- **Industrial Radiography:** Betatrons in the 1–10 MeV range are used for non-destructive testing of thick steel welds, castings, and aerospace components.
- **Activation Analysis:** Betatron-produced neutrons (via $(\gamma, n)$ reactions) are used to identify trace elements in materials.
- **Sterilisation & Food Irradiation:** High-dose irradiation from betatron beams is used to sterilise medical disposables and preserve food.
- **Historical Clinical Radiotherapy:** The earliest megavoltage therapy machines (1948–1970s) were betatrons of 20–45 MeV.
- **Transformer Design Insight:** The betatron serves as the *physical embodiment* of Maxwell–Faraday equations, providing engineering students with a tangible link between abstract field theory and a working particle accelerator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Betatron Condition ($\langle B \rangle = 2B(r)$)

### Step 1 — Apply Faraday's Law to the Electron Orbit

The electron circulates in a circular vacuum tube of radius $r$. The magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the area enclosed by this orbit is time-varying because the electromagnet's current is alternating.

By **Faraday's Law of Induction**, the line integral of the induced electric field around any closed path equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux through that path.

$$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Line Integral

By axial symmetry, the induced electric field $E_\theta$ is **tangential** and has the **same magnitude** at every point on the circular orbit of radius $r$. Therefore:

$$E_\theta \cdot (2\pi r) = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Express the Tangential Force on the Electron

The tangential electric field exerts a tangential force $F_\theta = eE_\theta$ on the electron (charge magnitude $e$). By Newton's Second Law (relativistic form), this force equals the rate of change of relativistic momentum $p = mv$:

$$eE_\theta = \frac{d(mv)}{dt}$$

### Step 4 — Eliminate $E_\theta$

Substituting $E_\theta$ from Step 2 into Step 3:

$$e \cdot \left[ -\frac{1}{2\pi r}\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \right] = \frac{d(mv)}{dt}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{d(mv)}{dt} = -\frac{e}{2\pi r}\frac{d\Phi}{dt}}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Magnetic Centripetal Force Balance

For a charged particle moving in a magnetic field, the Lorentz force provides the centripetal force. At the orbit radius $r$, the magnetic field is $B(r)$:

$$evB(r) = \frac{mv^2}{r} \quad \text{(non-relativistic case)}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$mv = eB(r) \cdot r$$

### Step 6 — Differentiate the Momentum Equation with Respect to Time

Since the orbit radius $r$ is *constant* (equilibrium orbit):

$$\frac{d(mv)}{dt} = e r \cdot \frac{dB(r)}{dt}$$

### Step 7 — Equate the Two Expressions for $d(mv)/dt$

Setting Step 4 equal to Step 6:

$$e r \cdot \frac{dB(r)}{dt} = -\frac{e}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

Cancel $e$ from both sides:

$$r \cdot \frac{dB(r)}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

### Step 8 — Express the Flux in Terms of Average Field

The flux through the circular area $\pi r^2$ is $\Phi = \langle B \rangle \cdot \pi r^2$, where $\langle B \rangle$ is the *spatial average* of the magnetic field over the disk enclosed by the orbit.

$$r \cdot \frac{dB(r)}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[\langle B \rangle \pi r^2\right]$$

$$r \cdot \frac{dB(r)}{dt} = -\frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{d\langle B \rangle}{dt}$$

### Step 9 — Cancel $r$ and Integrate

$$\frac{dB(r)}{dt} = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{d\langle B \rangle}{dt}$$

Integrating with respect to time (and assuming both fields start at zero at $t = 0$):

$$B(r) = -\frac{1}{2} \langle B \rangle$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign simply indicates that $B(r)$ and $\langle B \rangle$ change in opposite senses *relative to a chosen reference direction*. Taking magnitudes (or recognising the field configuration):
>
> $$\boxed{\langle B \rangle = 2 \, B(r)} \quad \text{— Betatron Condition (1:2 Rule) — Q.E.D.}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Kinetic Energy of the Accelerated Electron

### Step 1 — Use the Relativistic Momentum Equation

At any instant during acceleration, the relativistic momentum of the electron is $p = \gamma m_0 v$, and the equilibrium orbit condition is:

$$p = e B(r) \cdot r \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma m_0 v = e B(r) \cdot r$$

### Step 2 — Relate $v$ to $\gamma$

The Lorentz factor satisfies $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}}$, so:

$$v^2 = c^2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{\gamma^2}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$v = c \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{\gamma^2}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Momentum Equation

$$\gamma m_0 c \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{\gamma^2}} = e B(r) \cdot r$$

Square both sides:

$$\gamma^2 m_0^2 c^2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{\gamma^2}\right) = e^2 B^2(r) \cdot r^2$$

$$m_0^2 c^2 (\gamma^2 - 1) = e^2 B^2(r) \cdot r^2$$

$$\gamma^2 - 1 = \left(\frac{e B(r) r}{m_0 c}\right)^2$$

$$\gamma^2 = 1 + \left(\frac{e B(r) r}{m_0 c}\right)^2$$

### Step 4 — Total Relativistic Energy

The total energy of the electron is $E_{\text{total}} = \gamma m_0 c^2$. The kinetic energy is the total minus the rest energy:

$$W = \gamma m_0 c^2 - m_0 c^2 = m_0 c^2 (\gamma - 1)$$

### Step 5 — Final Maximum Energy Formula

When $B(r)$ reaches its peak value $B_{\max}(r)$ at the end of the acceleration cycle:

$$\boxed{W_{\max} = m_0 c^2 \left[ \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{e B_{\max}(r) \, r}{m_0 c}\right)^2} \ - \ 1 \right]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical sanity check (clinical betatron):** Take $B_{\max} = 1 \ \text{T}$, $r = 0.5 \ \text{m}$.
>
> Then $eB_{\max} r / (m_0 c) \approx (1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1 \times 0.5) / (9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3 \times 10^8) \approx 292.7$.
>
> $W_{\max} = 0.511 \times (\sqrt{1 + 292.7^2} - 1) \approx 0.511 \times 291.7 \approx 149 \ \text{MeV}$.
>
> This is in the right order-of-magnitude range for a clinical betatron.

### Step 6 — Non-Relativistic Limiting Case

For low electron speeds ($v \ll c$), $\gamma \approx 1$ and the kinetic energy reduces to:

$$W_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} m_0 v^2 = \frac{(eB_{\max} r)^2}{2 m_0} = \frac{e^2 B_{\max}^2 r^2}{2 m_0}$$

This is useful for *order-of-magnitude* sanity checks in exam problems where the energy is given as "in keV".

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cause → Effect → Outcome

| Physical Cause | Governing Equation | Engineering Outcome |
|:---------------|:-------------------|:-------------------|
| Alternating current in magnet coils | $B(t) = B_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | Time-varying magnetic flux $\Phi(t)$ through orbit |
| Time-varying flux | $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -d\Phi/dt$ | Induced tangential electric field $E_\theta$ |
| Tangential $E_\theta$ on electron | $eE_\theta = d(mv)/dt$ | Continuous tangential acceleration |
| Magnetic field at orbit radius | $evB(r) = mv^2/r$ | Electron constrained to circular path of radius $r$ |
| 1:2 Flux condition | $\langle B \rangle = 2 B(r)$ | Constant orbit radius during acceleration |
| Sudden perturbation of magnet field | Deflecting magnetic pulse | Electron extracted from orbit → strikes target |
| Electrons strike high-Z target | Bremsstrahlung $e^- + Z \rightarrow Z' + \gamma$ | High-energy X-ray beam for therapy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Betatron

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef power fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef magnet fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef vacuum fill:#047857,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff
    classDef beam fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff

    PWR[AC Mains Power Supply]:::power
    CPS[Capacitor Bank Energy Storage]:::power
    COIL[Energising Coils of Electromagnet]:::magnet
    POLE[Pole Faces Shaped for 1 by 2 Field Profile]:::magnet
    CORE[Laminated Iron Core]:::magnet
    VAC[Doughnut Shaped Vacuum Chamber]:::vacuum
    GUN[Electron Injector Hot Filament Gun]:::beam
    ORBIT[Equilibrium Circular Orbit Radius r]:::beam
    EFIELD[Induced Tangential Electric Field E theta]:::beam
    BFELD[Guiding Magnetic Field B of r]:::magnet
    EXT[Extraction Kick Coil]:::beam
    TGT[High Z Bremsstrahlung Target]:::output
    XRAY[Megavoltage X ray Beam]:::output
    COLL[Collimator and Filter Assembly]:::output
    PAT[Patient or Industrial Specimen]:::output

    PWR --> CPS
    CPS --> COIL
    COIL --> CORE
    CORE --> POLE
    POLE --> VAC
    GUN --> VAC
    VAC --> ORBIT
    COIL -.induces.-> EFIELD
    POLE -.provides.-> BFELD
    EFIELD --> ORBIT
    BFELD --> ORBIT
    ORBIT --> EXT
    EXT --> TGT
    TGT --> XRAY
    XRAY --> COLL
    COLL --> PAT
```

## 4.2 Functional Sequence — Operation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff

    S1[Step 1 Inject electron at low energy from filament gun]:::step
    S2[Step 2 Energise electromagnet with rising alternating current]:::step
    S3[Step 3 Time varying flux induces tangential E field]:::step
    S4[Step 4 Tangential E accelerates electron each orbit]:::step
    S5[Step 5 Magnetic B of r bends path to maintain constant radius r]:::step
    S6[Step 6 Verify 1 by 2 betatron condition for orbit stability]:::step
    S7[Step 7 Repeat over N orbits roughly 10 to the 5 revolutions]:::step
    S8[Step 8 At peak field fire extraction kick coil]:::step
    S9[Step 9 Electron beam strikes target producing bremsstrahlung]:::step
    S10[Step 10 Collimated megavoltage X ray beam exits machine]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10
```

## 4.3 Magnetic Field Profile Across the Pole Gap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef axis fill:#1f2937,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
    classDef curve fill:#9d174d,stroke:#9d174d,color:#ffffff
    classDef annot fill:#374151,stroke:#374151,color:#ffffff

    AX[Radial Distance r from Centre]:::axis
    B1[Average B over disk equals 2 times B at orbit radius]:::annot
    B2[B of r decreases as 1 over r]:::curve
    B3[Orbit radius r is the equilibrium location]:::annot
    B4[Central field B0 is the largest]:::curve

    AX --> B2
    AX --> B4
    B1 --> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this schematic:** The pole faces of the betatron electromagnet are *shaped* (often with conical or contoured surfaces) so that the magnetic field falls off radially as approximately $B(r) \propto 1/r$. This guarantees that the **spatial average** of the field over the area enclosed by the orbit is exactly **twice** the field at the orbit radius. This geometric constraint *is* the engineering implementation of the 1:2 betatron condition.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**State the Betatron condition. Why is it called the 1:2 rule?**

**Model Answer:**
The Betatron condition states that for an electron to circulate on a stable circular orbit of constant radius $r$ while being continuously accelerated, the **average magnetic field $\langle B \rangle$** over the area enclosed by the orbit must be **twice** the magnetic field at the orbit radius $B(r)$ at all times:

$$\langle B \rangle = 2 \, B(r)$$

It is called the **1:2 rule** because the central (average) field must be *double* the orbital field. This is achieved by shaping the pole pieces of the electromagnet so that the field falls off radially as $B \propto 1/r$. The condition is essential to maintain the electron on a constant-radius equilibrium orbit during the entire acceleration cycle.

*(3 marks: 1 for the equation, 1 for explanation, 1 for engineering implementation.)*

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Mention any three applications of Betatron in the medical / industrial field.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **External Beam Radiotherapy (EBRT):** High-energy photon beams (6–45 MeV) produced by betatrons are used for treating deep-seated tumours with skin-sparing effect. *(1 mark)*
2. **Industrial Radiography:** Betatrons are used for non-destructive testing of thick metallic components such as welds, castings, pressure vessels, and aerospace parts. *(1 mark)*
3. **Activation Analysis & Food Irradiation:** Betatron-produced neutrons (via photonuclear reactions) are used for trace-element analysis, sterilisation of medical disposables, and preservation of food items. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B (A)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model] — 14 Marks

**a) With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of a Betatron. Show that the average magnetic field over the orbit must be twice the field at the orbit radius.** *(7 marks)*

**b) Derive the expression for the maximum kinetic energy of the electron accelerated in a Betatron. Mention its clinical significance in radiotherapy.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Construction:** A betatron consists of:
1. A large **electromagnet** with specially shaped pole pieces (laminated iron core to reduce eddy-current losses).
2. **Energising coils** carrying an alternating current (typically at 50–60 Hz mains frequency).
3. A **doughnut-shaped evacuated porcelain or glass chamber** placed in the air gap between the pole faces.
4. An **electron gun** (heated filament) that injects electrons tangentially into the chamber.
5. An **extraction system** (auxiliary "kick" coil) to deflect the accelerated electrons onto a target.

**Working Principle:**

(i) The electromagnet is energised by an alternating current. During the **first quarter-cycle**, the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the orbit increases from zero to its maximum value.

(ii) According to **Faraday's Law**, this time-varying flux induces a **tangential electric field** $E_\theta$ along the electron's circular path:

$$E_\theta \cdot 2\pi r = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

(iii) This induced field **accelerates** the electron tangentially each time it completes a revolution.

(iv) Simultaneously, the magnetic field $B(r)$ at the orbit radius provides the **centripetal Lorentz force** that bends the electron's path into a circle of constant radius $r$:

$$evB(r) = \frac{mv^2}{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad mv = eB(r) \cdot r$$

(v) After **~10⁵ revolutions in a few milliseconds**, the electron has gained several MeV of energy.

(vi) At peak magnetic field, the kick coil is energised, deflecting the electron from its orbit onto a high-Z target, producing **bremsstrahlung X-rays** for therapy.

**Proof of the 1:2 Condition:**

Combining Faraday's law and the centripetal force equation, and following the derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes:

$$\frac{d(mv)}{dt} = \frac{e}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = e r \cdot \frac{dB(r)}{dt}$$

Writing the flux as $\Phi = \langle B \rangle \pi r^2$ and integrating with zero initial conditions:

$$\boxed{\langle B \rangle = 2 \, B(r)} \quad \text{— Betatron Condition (1:2 Rule)}$$

*(Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step working. Award 7 marks: 2 for construction, 2 for working, 3 for the mathematical derivation.)*

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Derivation of Maximum Kinetic Energy:**

At the equilibrium orbit, the relativistic momentum of the electron is balanced by the magnetic Lorentz force:

$$\gamma m_0 v = e B(r) \cdot r$$

Squaring and using $v^2 = c^2 (1 - 1/\gamma^2)$:

$$m_0^2 c^2 (\gamma^2 - 1) = e^2 B^2(r) \, r^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma^2 = 1 + \left(\frac{eB(r) r}{m_0 c}\right)^2$$

The **kinetic energy** is the total energy minus the rest energy:

$$W = \gamma m_0 c^2 - m_0 c^2 = m_0 c^2 (\gamma - 1)$$

Substituting for $\gamma$ and taking $B(r) = B_{\max}$ at the end of the cycle:

$$\boxed{W_{\max} = m_0 c^2 \left[ \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{e B_{\max} r}{m_0 c}\right)^2} \ - \ 1 \right]}$$

In the non-relativistic limit ($eB_{\max} r \ll m_0 c$):

$$W_{\max} = \frac{e^2 B_{\max}^2 r^2}{2 m_0}$$

**Clinical Significance in Radiotherapy:**

1. **Megavoltage photon beams** produced by betatrons offer excellent **skin-sparing** (build-up region at $\sim 1$–$3$ cm depth), which is critical for treating deep-seated tumours without burning the skin. *(1 mark)*
2. The high penetration allows uniform dose delivery to **deep pelvic, thoracic, and head-and-neck tumours**. *(1 mark)*
3. The photon beam has a small **penumbra**, ensuring sharp beam edges and minimal damage to surrounding healthy tissue. *(1 mark)*

*(Total: 7 marks — 4 for the derivation, 1 for the formula, 2 for clinical significance.)*

---

### **Question B (B) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] — 14 Marks

**a) Explain the principle of electromagnetic induction as applied to the Betatron. Hence, obtain the expression for the tangential electric field at the orbit.** *(7 marks)*

**b) A betatron has an equilibrium orbit radius of $0.50$ m. The peak magnetic field at the orbit is $0.8$ T. Calculate (i) the maximum kinetic energy of the accelerated electron in MeV, and (ii) the orbital frequency at peak energy. Assume relativistic treatment.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Principle of Electromagnetic Induction:**

When the magnetic flux $\Phi(t)$ linking a closed conducting loop changes with time, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in the loop, given by **Faraday's Law**:

$$\mathcal{E} = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

In a betatron, the "loop" is the circular electron orbit of radius $r$, and the flux is produced by the time-varying current in the electromagnet coils.

**Tangential Electric Field:**

By symmetry, the induced electric field is **purely tangential** and has constant magnitude $E_\theta$ on the circular orbit:

$$E_\theta \cdot 2\pi r = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{E_\theta = -\frac{1}{2\pi r}\frac{d\Phi}{dt}}$$

The negative sign (Lenz's Law) indicates the field opposes the flux change. The induced tangential force on the electron ($F = eE_\theta$) accelerates it along the orbit.

The flux is related to the average field: $\Phi = \langle B \rangle \pi r^2$, hence:

$$E_\theta = -\frac{\pi r}{2\pi r}\frac{d\langle B \rangle}{dt} = -\frac{r}{2}\frac{d\langle B \rangle}{dt}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for stating Faraday's law, 2 for the symmetry argument, 3 for the final expression.)*

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $r = 0.50$ m, $B_{\max} = 0.8$ T, $m_0 c^2 = 0.511$ MeV, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

**(i) Maximum Kinetic Energy:**

$$\frac{e B_{\max} r}{m_0 c} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(0.8)(0.5)}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(3 \times 10^8)}$$

$$= \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-20}}{2.733 \times 10^{-22}} = 234.2$$

$$W_{\max} = 0.511 \left[ \sqrt{1 + (234.2)^2} - 1 \right]$$

$$= 0.511 \left[ \sqrt{1 + 54854} - 1 \right] = 0.511 \left[ \sqrt{54855} - 1 \right]$$

$$= 0.511 \, [234.2 - 1] = 0.511 \times 233.2 \approx \mathbf{119.2 \ \text{MeV}}$$

**(ii) Orbital Frequency at Peak Energy:**

The electron's velocity is ultra-relativistic ($v \approx c$). The orbital frequency is:

$$f = \frac{v}{2\pi r} \approx \frac{c}{2\pi r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2\pi \times 0.5} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3.1416}$$

$$f \approx 9.55 \times 10^7 \ \text{Hz} \approx \mathbf{95.5 \ \text{MHz}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for setting up (i), 2 for calculating the result, 1 for (ii) setup, 2 for (ii) result.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Betatron Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the relativistic correction:** At $W > 50$ keV, electrons become relativistic. Using $W = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ for energies in the MeV range is a **sure way to lose 3–4 marks**. Always check the order of $eBr/(m_0 c)$; if it is $\gg 1$, use the relativistic formula.
>
> 2. **Confusing $B(r)$ with $\langle B \rangle$:** Students often interchange these. **Remember:** $B(r)$ is the field *at* the orbit, while $\langle B \rangle$ is the *average* over the area enclosed by the orbit. The betatron condition says $\langle B \rangle = 2 B(r)$, NOT $B(r) = 2 \langle B \rangle$.
>
> 3. **Skipping integration steps:** In the derivation of the 1:2 rule, students often jump from $dB(r)/dt = (1/2) d\langle B \rangle / dt$ directly to the result without showing the **integration** and the **zero initial conditions** assumption. This costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Missing units in numerical problems:** Always carry units through calculations. Markers deduct marks for "bare numbers" without units.
>
> 5. **Not stating Faraday's Law explicitly:** When asked to "explain the principle," many students dive into the equations without first stating **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. Always start with the principle, then derive.
>
> 6. **Confusing extraction with acceleration:** The kick coil *deflects* the electron out — it does *not* accelerate it further. The accelerated electron strikes the target to produce bremsstrahlung X-rays.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **⚡ BETATRON — 30-Second Rapid Revision**
>
> - [x] **Definition:** Cyclic electron accelerator using *electromagnetic induction* (induction accelerator).
> - [x] **Inventor:** Donald W. Kerst, 1940.
> - [x] **Operating Principle:** Time-varying magnetic flux induces tangential E-field → electron accelerated each orbit.
> - [x] **Betatron Condition:** $\langle B \rangle = 2 B(r)$ — the **1:2 rule** (Wideröe's condition).
> - [x] **Engineering Implementation:** Pole faces shaped so $B \propto 1/r$ radially.
> - [x] **Centripetal Force Balance:** $mv = eB(r) \cdot r$ (relativistic).
> - [x] **Max Energy:** $W_{\max} = m_0 c^2 \left[ \sqrt{1 + (eB_{\max} r / m_0 c)^2} - 1 \right]$.
> - [x] **Non-relativistic Limit:** $W_{\max} = e^2 B_{\max}^2 r^2 / (2 m_0)$.
> - [x] **Orbit Radius Typical:** 0.1 – 1.5 m.
> - [x] **Energy Range:** 1 – 300 MeV.
> - [x] **Orbital Frequency:** $f \approx c / (2\pi r) \approx 100$ MHz (relativistic limit).
> - [x] **X-ray Production:** Bremsstrahlung when electron strikes high-Z target.
> - [x] **Clinical Use:** Megavoltage external beam radiotherapy (now largely superseded by LINACs).
> - [x] **Industrial Use:** Non-destructive radiography of thick metal parts; food irradiation.
> - [x] **Why called "Beta-tron":** Accelerates *beta particles* (electrons).
> - [x] **Transformer Analogy:** Magnet coil = primary, electron beam = secondary.
> - [x] **Stability:** Imposed by 1:2 condition; deviations cause orbital expansion or contraction.
> - [x] **Key Constants:** $m_0 c^2 = 0.511$ MeV, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyclotron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cyclotron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyclotron** is a circular particle accelerator invented by **Ernest O. Lawrence** (1930) that uses a constant, uniform **magnetic field** perpendicular to the plane of motion and a high-frequency alternating **electric field** (radiofrequency, RF) to accelerate charged particles (protons, deuterons, α-particles, or heavier ions) to high kinetic energies in a compact, spiral path. It is classified as a **resonance-type cyclic accelerator** and is foundational in producing particle beams used in **medical radiotherapy**, isotope production for PET/SPECT imaging, and proton therapy for deep-seated tumours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Radiotherapy Physics):**
> The cyclotron is studied under *Production of Radioisotopes & Particle Beams for Therapy*. Students must understand the **principle of operation**, the **resonance condition**, derivation of **maximum kinetic energy**, and its **role in radionuclide production (e.g., F-18, Tl-201, I-123)** for nuclear medicine.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Merry-Go-Round with a Push"

Imagine a child running on a **circular merry-go-round** while a parent stands at the centre repeatedly pushing the child sideways (tangentially) each time the child passes by. Each push makes the child go a little faster, so the circle they trace becomes a little **larger** — the path spirals outward. The parent pushing represents the **alternating electric field**, and the centrifugal grip of the merry-go-round represents the **magnetic field** that bends the child back into a circle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The push must occur **at the right moment** — exactly when the child passes the centre line. In the cyclotron, this synchronisation is called the **resonance condition** and is achieved by matching the **AC electric field frequency** to the **cyclotron frequency** of the particle.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Charge of proton: **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- Mass of proton: **mₚ = 1.672 × 10⁻²⁷ kg**
- Mass of electron: **mₑ = 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
- Typical magnetic flux density (B): **1 T to 5 T** (medical cyclotrons)
- Typical operating frequency: **10 MHz to 30 MHz**
- Maximum proton energy (medical): **15 MeV to 250 MeV**
- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**

---

### 1.4 Why the Cyclotron Matters in Radiotherapy

> [!NOTE]
> A cyclotron is the **workhorse of modern nuclear medicine departments**. It produces short-lived radioisotopes on-site (e.g., **Fluorine-18** for PET imaging, used in FDG-PET scans to detect cancer, infections, and neurological disorders). Modern **proton therapy centres** use **isochronous cyclotrons** to deliver beams of **200–250 MeV protons** that conform dose precisely to tumours (Bragg peak) while sparing healthy tissue.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Classical Cyclotron

The classical (Lawrence-style) cyclotron consists of the following components:

- **Dees (D₁ and D₂):** Two hollow, D-shaped (semi-cylindrical) copper electrodes placed inside a vacuum chamber, separated by a small gap. They act as the terminals of the AC source.
- **Electromagnet:** Provides a strong, uniform **magnetic field B** perpendicular to the plane of the Dees. The field is generated by large pole pieces of a DC electromagnet.
- **RF (Radio-Frequency) Oscillator:** A high-frequency alternating voltage source connected between the Dees. It reverses polarity at the **cyclotron frequency** so the particle is accelerated only when crossing the gap.
- **Ion Source:** A heated filament (e.g., hydrogen gas discharge) at the centre between the Dees that emits the charged particles to be accelerated.
- **Vacuum Chamber:** Encloses the Dees to prevent the particles from colliding with air molecules.
- **Extraction System / Target:** A deflector plate or extraction window that guides the spiralling beam onto an external target for isotope production or therapeutic use.

---

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Ionisation:** The ion source at the centre releases a positively charged particle (e.g., a proton). It enters one of the Dees with a small initial velocity.
- **Step 2 — Magnetic Bending:** Inside the Dee, there is **no electric field** (the field exists only in the gap). The uniform magnetic field **B** exerts a Lorentz force $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$, which is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it provides the necessary **centripetal force** and bends the particle into a **semicircular path**.
- **Step 3 — Gap Acceleration:** The particle re-enters the gap after half a revolution. Just then, the RF oscillator **reverses polarity**, so the electric field in the gap now points in the correct direction to **accelerate** the particle (rather than decelerate it).
- **Step 4 — Spiralling Outward:** Because the particle now has more kinetic energy, it travels in a **larger semicircle** inside the second Dee. Each gap crossing adds a small, fixed amount of energy (typically 50–100 keV per crossing).
- **Step 5 — Repetition & Extraction:** This process repeats for many revolutions. The particle spirals outward in a stepwise fashion. When it reaches the periphery (radius R), it is deflected out of the Dees and strikes the target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Resonance Condition:** For the particle to be accelerated at *every* gap crossing, the **period of the RF voltage (T_rf)** must **exactly equal** the **period of the circular motion (T_c)**. This is the heart of the cyclotron's operation.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | SI Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|----------|
| 1 | Lorentz (magnetic) force | $F = qvB\sin\theta$ | q = charge, v = speed, B = flux density | N |
| 2 | Centripetal force balance | $qvB = \dfrac{mv^{2}}{r}$ | m = mass, r = orbit radius | N |
| 3 | Radius of circular orbit | $r = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$ | radius of nth semicircle | m |
| 4 | Cyclotron angular frequency | $\omega_{c} = \dfrac{qB}{m}$ | angular speed of particle | rad/s |
| 5 | Cyclotron frequency | $f_{c} = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$ | revolutions per second | Hz |
| 6 | Period of revolution | $T_{c} = \dfrac{2\pi m}{qB}$ | time per revolution | s |
| 7 | Kinetic energy at radius r | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^{2} = \dfrac{q^{2}B^{2}r^{2}}{2m}$ | energy at radius r | J |
| 8 | **Maximum kinetic energy** | $KE_{max} = \dfrac{q^{2}B^{2}R^{2}}{2m}$ | R = maximum radius of Dees | J |
| 9 | Maximum energy in eV | $E_{max} = \dfrac{qB^{2}R^{2}}{2m}\;\dfrac{1}{e}\;\text{eV}$ | if q = e | eV |
| 10 | Number of revolutions | $N = \dfrac{KE_{max}}{qV_{0}}$ | V₀ = peak RF voltage per gap | dimensionless |
| 11 | Resonance condition | $f_{rf} = f_{c} = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$ | RF = oscillator | Hz |
| 12 | Relativistic mass | $m = \dfrac{m_{0}}{\sqrt{1 - v^{2}/c^{2}}}$ | m₀ = rest mass | kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** No vertical bar `|` characters are used inside table cells. All absolute values and conditions use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Cyclotrons (e.g., GE PETtrace, Siemens Eclipse):** 11–16 MeV protons used to bombard ¹⁸O-enriched water to produce **F-18** (via ¹⁸O(p,n)¹⁸F) for FDG synthesis.
- **Proton Therapy Cyclotrons (e.g., IBA ProteusONE):** 230 MeV isochronous superconducting cyclotrons used clinically for treating ocular melanomas, paediatric tumours, and skull-base cancers.
- **Industrial Cyclotrons:** Used for neutron radiography, boron neutron capture therapy (BNCT), and production of medical isotopes such as **Sr-89, Sm-153, I-124**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Cyclotron Frequency (f_c)

**Statement:** Show that the frequency of revolution of a charged particle in a uniform magnetic field is independent of its speed and the radius of its orbit.

**Step 1 — Set up the force balance.**
Inside a Dee, the magnetic Lorentz force supplies the centripetal force required for circular motion:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^{2}}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel one factor of velocity (v) from both sides.**

$$qB = \frac{mv}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the orbital speed v.**

$$v = \frac{qBr}{m}$$

**Step 4 — Recall that the period of revolution T_c is the circumference divided by speed.**

$$T_{c} = \frac{2\pi r}{v}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expression for v from Step 3 into Step 4.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{c} &= \frac{2\pi r}{\left(\dfrac{qBr}{m}\right)} \\[4pt]
T_{c} &= \frac{2\pi r \cdot m}{qBr} \\[4pt]
T_{c} &= \frac{2\pi m}{qB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the reciprocal to obtain the cyclotron frequency f_c.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{c} &= \frac{1}{T_{c}} = \frac{qB}{2\pi m} \\[4pt]
\omega_{c} &= 2\pi f_{c} = \frac{qB}{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (KTU favourite 7-marker):** The cyclotron frequency depends **only on the charge-to-mass ratio (q/m) of the particle and the magnetic field B**. It is *independent* of the particle's speed (v) and orbital radius (r). This is why a *fixed-frequency* RF source can continue to accelerate the particle at every gap crossing, as long as v ≪ c (non-relativistic regime).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Maximum Kinetic Energy (KE_max)

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the maximum kinetic energy a cyclotron can impart to a charged particle, in terms of q, B, R (maximum Dee radius), and m.

**Step 1 — At the outermost orbit, the radius is R, so write the centripetal force balance at this limit.**

$$qv_{max}B = \frac{mv_{max}^{2}}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the maximum speed v_max.**

$$v_{max} = \frac{qBR}{m}$$

**Step 3 — Write the maximum kinetic energy using the standard expression KE = ½mv².**

$$KE_{max} = \frac{1}{2}mv_{max}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute v_max from Step 2 into Step 3.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KE_{max} &= \frac{1}{2} m \left(\frac{qBR}{m}\right)^{2} \\[4pt]
KE_{max} &= \frac{1}{2} m \cdot \frac{q^{2}B^{2}R^{2}}{m^{2}} \\[4pt]
KE_{max} &= \frac{q^{2}B^{2}R^{2}}{2m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to electron-volts (eV) by dividing by the elementary charge e (assuming q = e, i.e., a singly-charged particle).**

$$KE_{max}\;\text{(in eV)} = \frac{eB^{2}R^{2}}{2m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** To achieve higher energies, one must increase B (stronger magnet) and R (larger Dees). This is why medical cyclotrons are physically massive (the 230 MeV IBA machine weighs ~250 tonnes and has a diameter >3 m). A practical alternative is the **synchrocyclotron** or **isochronous cyclotron**, which overcomes the relativistic limit.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Number of Revolutions and Energy Gain per Crossing

**Statement:** If the peak RF voltage per gap crossing is V₀, find the total number of revolutions N needed to reach KE_max.

**Step 1 — Energy gained per gap crossing.**
Each time the particle crosses the gap, it gains energy qV₀ (it crosses the gap **twice per revolution**).

$$\Delta E \text{ per revolution} = 2qV_{0}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy after N revolutions.**

$$KE_{max} = N \cdot 2qV_{0}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for N.**

$$
N = \frac{KE_{max}}{2qV_{0}} = \frac{q^{2}B^{2}R^{2}}{2m \cdot 2qV_{0}} = \frac{qB^{2}R^{2}}{4mV_{0}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A typical medical cyclotron might use V₀ ≈ 50 kV and B ≈ 1.5 T, R ≈ 0.4 m for protons. Plug in numbers — students should be able to estimate N for a 16 MeV PET cyclotron (N is in the order of **200 revolutions**).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Classical Cyclotron Limitations & Modern Solutions

| Limitation of Classical Cyclotron | Physical Cause | Engineering Solution |
|----------------------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| Energy ceiling (~25 MeV for protons) | Relativistic mass increase: $m = m_{0}/\sqrt{1-v^{2}/c^{2}}$ | **Synchrocyclotron** — frequency-modulated RF to track decreasing f_c |
| Falling out of resonance with v approaching c | $f_{c} = qB/(2\pi m)$ drops as m grows | **Frequency modulation (FM)** of RF oscillator |
| Large magnetic field non-uniformity required | To maintain axial focusing in synchrocyclotron | **Azimuthally Varying Field (AVF)** / **isochronous cyclotron** with spiral-ridge pole faces |
| Heavy mass & cost of conventional electromagnets | Resistive copper coils need MW power | **Superconducting magnets** (e.g., niobium-titanium coils at 4 K) |
| Beam extraction is difficult at high energies | Magnetic rigidity of the beam | **Electrostatic deflectors** and **stripper foils** for negative ions (H⁻ → p) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A cyclotron has a magnetic flux density B = 1.8 T and Dee radius R = 0.35 m. Calculate: (i) the cyclotron frequency for protons, (ii) the maximum kinetic energy in MeV, (iii) the number of revolutions if V₀ = 80 kV per gap.

**(i) Cyclotron frequency:**

$$
f_{c} = \frac{qB}{2\pi m_{p}} = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(1.8)}{2\pi (1.672 \times 10^{-27})}
$$

$$
f_{c} \approx 2.74 \times 10^{7}\;\text{Hz} = 27.4\;\text{MHz}
$$

**(ii) Maximum kinetic energy:**

$$
KE_{max} = \frac{q^{2}B^{2}R^{2}}{2m_{p}} = \frac{(1.602\times 10^{-19})^{2}(1.8)^{2}(0.35)^{2}}{2(1.672\times 10^{-27})}
$$

$$
KE_{max} \approx 3.03 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{J} \approx 18.9\;\text{MeV}
$$

**(iii) Number of revolutions:**

$$
N = \frac{KE_{max}}{2qV_{0}} = \frac{3.03 \times 10^{-12}}{2(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(80 \times 10^{3})}
$$

$$
N \approx 118\;\text{revolutions}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** This is realistic — the cyclotron described is essentially a **clinical PET cyclotron** (~18 MeV protons for F-18 production). Students should report all intermediate values and final answers in proper SI units + MeV where appropriate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Medical Cyclotron

> Since a literal cross-sectional drawing of a cyclotron (with curved Dees, magnetic pole pieces, vacuum chamber, etc.) is best understood visually, the Mermaid block diagram below maps out the **functional architecture** — i.e., the signal/power/particle flow — between the major subsystems. This is a KTU-board-friendly schematic for a Part B answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[HV Power Supply 3-Phase AC] --> MAG[Electromagnet Coils N and S Pole]
    RF[RF Oscillator 27 MHz] --> DEE1[Dee D1]
    RF --> DEE2[Dee D2]
    IONSRC[Hot Filament Ion Source H2 Gas] --> GAP[Central Gap between Dees]
    GAP --> DEE1
    GAP --> DEE2
    DEE1 --> SPIRAL[Spiral Particle Path inside Vacuum Chamber]
    DEE2 --> SPIRAL
    MAG --> SPIRAL
    SPIRAL -->|accelerated beam| EXTR[Electrostatic Deflector]
    EXTR --> BEAM[Extracted Proton Beam 18 MeV]
    BEAM --> TGT1[Target 18O Water F-18 Production]
    BEAM --> TGT2[Target Solid Pellet I-124 etc]
    TGT1 --> RPHARM[Radiochemistry Module FDG Synthesis]
    TGT2 --> RPHARM
    RPHARM --> PATIENT[Patient PET Scan or Therapy]
    VAC[Vacuum Pump 10 to 6 Torr] --> SPIRAL
    COOL[Water Cooling Closed Loop] --> MAG
    COOL --> RF
    CTRL[PLC Control Console] --> PWR
    CTRL --> RF
    CTRL --> EXTR
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **HV Power Supply** energises the **electromagnet**, which produces the uniform vertical magnetic field B across the Dees.
- The **RF Oscillator** drives both **Dee D1** and **Dee D2** in anti-phase, creating the alternating gap voltage.
- The **Ion Source** injects protons at the **central gap**.
- Protons spiral outward in the **vacuum chamber** under combined magnetic bending and RF acceleration.
- The **electrostatic deflector** extracts the beam to strike an external target for **radionuclide production**.
- The **radiochemistry module** synthesises radiopharmaceuticals (e.g., FDG) for clinical use.

---

### 4.2 Signal / Timing Block — Resonance Condition Visualised

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Particle in Dee 1 completing half circle] -->|after T half| B[Enters gap at time t]
    B -->|RF reverses polarity exactly here| C[Accelerated into Dee 2]
    C -->|after T half larger radius| D[Enters gap again]
    D -->|RF reverses again synchronously| E[Accelerated into Dee 1]
    E -->|spiral continues| F[Final orbit at radius R]
    F -->|deflection| G[Beam extracted to target]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level interpretation:** The two half-circle paths and the synchronous gap crossing illustrate the **resonance condition** $f_{rf} = f_{c}$. The block diagram abstracts the physical geometry but preserves the **timing logic** that the KTU examiner expects in a 7-mark construction-and-working question.

---

### 4.3 Component Summary Table (Cross-Reference for Answers)

| Sub-system | Physical Location | Function |
|------------|-------------------|----------|
| Dee D1, D2 | Inside vacuum chamber | Accelerate particle across gap; shield E-field inside |
| Electromagnet | Above & below Dees | Provide uniform B for circular motion |
| RF Oscillator | External | Alternating voltage at f_c |
| Ion Source | Centre of gap | Emit charged particles |
| Vacuum Pump | Chamber periphery | Reduce collisions with air |
| Deflector | Outer edge | Extract beam to target |
| Target / Radiochemistry | Beam line end | Produce radiopharmaceuticals |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the "resonance condition" of a cyclotron. Why is it essential for its operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **resonance condition** of a cyclotron states that the **frequency of the RF oscillator (f_rf) applied between the Dees must equal the cyclotron frequency (f_c) of the charged particle**, i.e.,

$$f_{rf} = f_{c} = \frac{qB}{2\pi m}$$

It is essential because the particle must be **accelerated at every gap crossing** to continuously gain energy. The RF source must reverse its polarity **exactly at the instant the particle arrives at the gap**, which occurs after each half-revolution. If the frequencies are not matched, the particle will be decelerated at some gap crossings, will fall out of synchronisation, and will not reach the target. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] State any three applications of a cyclotron in the field of medical radiotherapy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Production of short-lived radioisotopes** such as Fluorine-18 (F-18), used to synthesise **FDG (Fluorodeoxyglucose)** for PET imaging in cancer diagnosis. *(1 mark)*
2. **Proton therapy** for cancer treatment — isochronous cyclotrons deliver **200–250 MeV proton beams** that exploit the **Bragg peak** to concentrate dose inside the tumour while sparing surrounding healthy tissue. *(1 mark)*
3. **Production of therapeutic radionuclides** like Strontium-89 (for bone pain palliation in metastatic cancer), Iodine-123 (for thyroid imaging), and Thallium-201 (for myocardial perfusion imaging). *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Use neatly labelled diagrams wherever appropriate.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **construction and working principle** of a cyclotron. Explain the role of the Dees, magnetic field, and RF oscillator. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **maximum kinetic energy** of a particle accelerated by a cyclotron. A cyclotron has a magnetic field of 1.5 T and a Dee radius of 0.45 m. Calculate the maximum energy (in MeV) of the accelerated proton. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

*Construction (3 marks):*
A cyclotron consists of two hollow, semicircular metallic electrodes called **Dees (D₁ and D₂)**, mounted inside a **vacuum chamber** between the pole pieces of a powerful **electromagnet**. The Dees are connected to a high-frequency **RF oscillator**. At the centre between the Dees is an **ion source** (e.g., a heated filament that ionises hydrogen gas to release protons).

*Working (3 marks):*
- The electromagnet produces a **uniform magnetic field B perpendicular to the plane of the Dees**.
- A proton from the ion source enters Dee D₁ with a small initial velocity. Inside the Dee, it experiences only the magnetic force and traces a **semicircular path**.
- After half a revolution, it re-enters the **gap** between the Dees. By this time, the RF oscillator has **reversed polarity**, so the proton is **accelerated** across the gap into Dee D₂.
- Inside D₂, it moves in a **larger semicircle** (higher KE → larger radius). Each gap crossing adds energy.
- This process repeats; the proton follows a **spiral path** of increasing radius.
- When it reaches the **outer edge (radius R)**, an **electrostatic deflector** extracts it to strike an external target.

*Role of components (1 mark):*
- **Dees:** Provide field-free region for circular motion and act as RF terminals.
- **Magnetic field:** Provides centripetal force for circular motion.
- **RF oscillator:** Provides synchronised alternating electric field for acceleration.

**(b) Derivation & Numerical (7 marks):**

*Derivation (5 marks):* As shown in **Section 3.2** of these notes:
- At maximum radius R: $qv_{max}B = m v_{max}^{2}/R$
- Solving: $v_{max} = qBR/m$
- Therefore: $KE_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2}mv_{max}^{2} = \dfrac{q^{2}B^{2}R^{2}}{2m}$

*Numerical (2 marks):* Substituting B = 1.5 T, R = 0.45 m, q = e, m = mₚ:

$$
KE_{max} = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^{2}(1.5)^{2}(0.45)^{2}}{2(1.672 \times 10^{-27})}
$$

$$
KE_{max} \approx 3.49 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{J} \approx 21.8\;\text{MeV}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define **cyclotron frequency** and derive its expression. Explain why it is independent of the speed of the particle. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of the classical cyclotron** and explain how a **synchrocyclotron** overcomes them. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cyclotron Frequency — Definition & Derivation (7 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* The **cyclotron frequency** is the number of revolutions per second made by a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field under the influence of a perpendicular Lorentz force. It is given by:

$$f_{c} = \frac{qB}{2\pi m}$$

*Derivation (4 marks):* As shown in **Section 3.1**:
- Equating Lorentz force to centripetal force: $qvB = mv^{2}/r$
- Solving for v: $v = qBr/m$
- Period: $T = 2\pi r/v = 2\pi m/(qB)$
- Hence: $f_c = 1/T = qB/(2\pi m)$

*Independence from speed (1 mark):* Notice that **v cancels out** in the expression for f_c. This is because, as v increases, r also increases proportionally (since $r = mv/(qB)$), keeping T constant. Hence f_c depends **only on q/m and B**, not on the particle's instantaneous speed (valid only in the non-relativistic regime where v ≪ c).

**(b) Limitations & Synchrocyclotron (7 marks):**

*Limitations of classical cyclotron (4 marks):*
1. **Relativistic mass increase:** As the proton's speed approaches c, its relativistic mass $m = m_{0}/\sqrt{1 - v^{2}/c^{2}}$ increases. Since $f_{c} = qB/(2\pi m)$, the cyclotron frequency **decreases**, and the fixed-frequency RF oscillator falls out of resonance. This imposes a **practical energy ceiling of ~25 MeV** for protons.
2. **Massive electromagnets:** Conventional resistive magnets require megawatts of power and enormous cooling systems.
3. **Beam extraction difficulty:** The high magnetic rigidity of the beam makes extraction inefficient at high energies.
4. **Limited to non-relativistic particles** — cannot accelerate electrons (because they reach relativistic speeds almost instantly).

*Synchrocyclotron — the engineering solution (3 marks):*
A **synchrocyclotron** uses a **frequency-modulated (FM) RF oscillator** whose frequency is **decreased during each acceleration cycle** to track the falling f_c of the particle. It uses a **single Dee** (or a different geometry) with a pulsed, frequency-swept accelerating voltage. This allows particles to be accelerated to much higher energies (hundreds of MeV) while keeping the magnet size manageable. It was historically used for producing **medical radioisotopes** and for early proton therapy trials. The modern **isochronous cyclotron** further uses **azimuthally varying magnetic fields** (AVF) to provide strong focusing while maintaining a *constant* RF frequency, even at relativistic speeds.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in Cyclotron questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert J → MeV** in numericals (1 MeV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹³ J). Many students leave the answer in joules and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up "cyclotron frequency" and "RF frequency"** — they are equal *only when* the resonance condition is satisfied. State this explicitly.
> 3. **Drawing the Dee orientation incorrectly** — Dees are D-shaped, separated by a narrow gap, **inside** the magnetic field. The magnetic field is **perpendicular** to the Dees' plane.
> 4. **Skipping the role of the vacuum chamber** — examiners allocate ½ to 1 mark for stating its purpose (avoiding collisions with air molecules).
> 5. **In derivation questions, omitting the "equating Lorentz to centripetal force" step** — this is the **anchor equation**; skipping it loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Not stating the resonance condition explicitly in working-principle answers** — examiners check for the phrase "the RF frequency must equal the cyclotron frequency."
> 7. **Confusing synchrocyclotron with synchrotron** — they are different machines. Synchrocyclotron = frequency-modulated + constant B; Synchrotron = variable B + constant orbital radius.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — Cyclotron**

- **Inventor:** Ernest O. Lawrence, 1930 (Nobel Prize 1939).
- **Core principle:** Charged particle moves in a circle due to **B**, accelerated across a gap by an alternating **E**.
- **Resonance condition (must memorise):** $f_{rf} = f_{c} = qB/(2\pi m)$.
- **Orbit radius:** $r = mv/(qB)$.
- **Maximum kinetic energy (memorise):** $KE_{max} = q^{2}B^{2}R^{2}/(2m)$.
- **Centripetal force balance (foundation equation):** $qvB = mv^{2}/r$.
- **Energy per gap crossing:** $\Delta E = qV_{0}$; **per revolution:** $2qV_{0}$.
- **Number of revolutions:** $N = KE_{max}/(2qV_{0})$.
- **Cyclotron frequency is independent of v and r** in the non-relativistic limit — a key conceptual point.
- **Limitation:** Relativistic mass increase → f_c decreases → out of resonance.
- **Solutions:** (i) Synchrocyclotron — FM RF oscillator; (ii) Isochronous cyclotron — azimuthally varying B field.
- **Medical uses:** Production of F-18 (PET), I-123, Tl-201, Sm-153, Sr-89; proton therapy (Bragg peak) for cancer.
- **Energy units:** 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J; 1 MeV = 10⁶ eV.
- **Typical medical cyclotron parameters:** B = 1–2 T, R = 0.4–0.5 m, V₀ = 50–100 kV, E ≈ 11–18 MeV (for isotope production); up to 250 MeV (for proton therapy).
- **Draw and label** the construction diagram in every working-principle question — it is worth 2–3 marks alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collimators and Beam Direction Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Collimators and Beam Direction Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Collimator

A **collimator** is a mechanical device employed in a radiotherapy linear accelerator (LINAC) or teletherapy unit that **restricts, shapes, and defines the geometric cross-section of the radiation beam** emitted from the treatment head, before it reaches the patient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A collimator is a beam-shaping device consisting of heavy-metal absorbing blocks (typically **lead** or **tungsten**) arranged in a fixed or movable configuration to restrict the radiation field to a defined size and shape, conforming to the target volume while minimizing dose to surrounding healthy tissue.

In diagnostic radiology, the same term refers to a device that **restricts the X-ray beam to a useful field size**, primarily to reduce patient dose and improve image contrast by limiting scattered radiation.

## 1.2 Definition of Beam Direction Devices

**Beam direction devices** (also called **patient positioning and alignment systems**) are mechanical and optical accessories that ensure the radiation beam is accurately and reproducibly directed toward the **prescribed target volume** during treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Beam Direction Devices in Radiotherapy:**
> - Pin & Arc (Simulator/Brachytherapy)
> - Goniometer (Patient Support Assembly)
> - Optical Distance Indicator (ODI)
> - Laser Alignment Systems (Wall, Ceiling, Cross-hair lasers)
> - Wedge Filters
> - Treatment Couch (with 6 degrees of freedom)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Garden Hose Nozzle**
> Imagine you are watering a garden with a hose. The water sprays everywhere — wasting water and missing the plants. You attach a **nozzle (collimator)** that shapes the water into a focused, directed stream. By changing the nozzle tip, you can make the stream narrow (treat a small tumor) or wide (treat a larger area). Now imagine you also have a **tripod with a laser pointer (beam direction device)** that ensures the water is aimed exactly at the plant and not the path. The nozzle = collimator; the laser tripod = beam direction device.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Source-to-Axis Distance (SAD):** Standard **100 cm** for most modern LINACs.
- **Source-to-Surface Distance (SSD):** Typically **80–100 cm**.
- **Density of lead (Pb):** **11.34 g/cm³**
- **Density of tungsten (W):** **19.3 g/cm³**
- **HVL of diagnostic X-rays in lead:** ~**0.5–2.0 mm** at 100–200 kVp.
- **Field size range:** **0 × 0 cm** to **40 × 40 cm** at isocenter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Collimators in Radiotherapy Treatment Head

A modern LINAC treatment head contains **three hierarchical collimation stages**, designed in sequence from the radiation source to the patient:

### 2.1.1 Primary Collimator
- Located **immediately below the X-ray target** inside the treatment head.
- Defines a **maximum circular field** (typically **35–50 cm diameter** at isocenter).
- Constructed from a large tungsten or lead block, fixed permanently.
- **Function:** Restricts the beam to a useful maximum divergence and reduces patient scatter by blocking photons that would never enter the secondary collimators.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Collimators (Jaws)
- Two pairs of **independently movable lead/tungsten blocks**:
  - **X-jaws** (upper pair) — define the field in the **X-direction**.
  - **Y-jaws** (lower pair) — define the field in the **Y-direction**.
- Produces a **rectangular or square field** with continuously variable dimensions.
- Driven by **stepper motors** and controlled digitally.

### 2.1.3 Tertiary Collimator — Multileaf Collimator (MLC)
- Made of **80–160 individual tungsten alloy leaves** (each leaf is typically **5–10 mm wide** projected at isocenter).
- Each leaf is independently motorized and can be positioned to **conform to the tumor shape**.
- Two banks of leaves (A and B) move in opposition across the field.
- Replaces traditional **cerrobend/custom blocks**, allowing **conformal radiotherapy (CRT)** and **intensity-modulated radiotherapy (IMRT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Logic (KTU-favorite question):**
> Source → Target → Primary Collimator → Flattening Filter → Secondary Jaws → MLC → Patient
> Each successive element *further restricts* the beam to a tighter, more precise shape.

## 2.2 Function & Working Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Photon generation** at the bremsstrahlung target inside the treatment head.
2. Photons diverge isotropically; **primary collimator** intercepts the outer divergent cone.
3. The **flattening filter** (for non-FFF beams) makes the dose profile uniform across the field.
4. **Secondary jaws** trim the field to a roughly rectangular shape.
5. **MLC** shapes the field to match the projection of the planning target volume (PTV).
6. The shaped beam exits toward the patient, with **ODI and lasers** ensuring the target lies on the central axis at the correct distance.

## 2.3 Beam Direction Devices — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Pin & Arc
- Used in **simulators and brachytherapy afterloading units**.
- A long metallic **pin** and a **curved arc** with degree markings.
- The arc is positioned to define the **source-axis direction** and **treatment angle**.
- Reproduces geometry of the actual treatment unit.

### 2.3.2 Goniometer
- A **protractor-like device** mounted on the treatment couch.
- Measures **patient rotation angle** in degrees, useful for special techniques (e.g., tangential breast fields, vertex fields).

### 2.3.3 Optical Distance Indicator (ODI)
- A **light source + mirror + scale** projecting a movable shadow onto the patient's skin.
- Reads the **SSD (Source-to-Surface Distance)** accurately (within ±1 mm).
- Critical for **SSD-setup techniques** (e.g., certain orthovoltage and electron treatments).

### 2.3.4 Laser Alignment System
- **Three sets of lasers** in the treatment room:
  - **Two lateral (side) lasers** — define the horizontal plane.
  - **One ceiling (sagittal) laser** — defines the vertical midline.
- Lines intersect at the **machine isocenter** (±1 mm tolerance).
- Used for **patient marking and positioning**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Units / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inverse Square Law** $I \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}$ | Intensity at isocenter vs. distance | $d$ in cm |
| 2 | Field Size Divergence: $A = A_{0} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\text{SAD}}{d}\right)^{2}$ | Field area scales with square of distance ratio | cm² |
| 3 | SSD vs SAD: $\text{PDD} = \dfrac{I_{d}}{I_{d_{max}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage Depth Dose | % |
| 4 | Mayneord F-factor: $F = \left(\dfrac{\text{SSD} + d_{max}}{\text{SSD}}\right)^{2}$ | Converts PDD between SSD setups | dimensionless |
| 5 | Half-Value Layer (HVL) | Lead thickness to halve beam intensity | mm Pb |
| 6 | Tenth-Value Layer (TVL) | $\text{TVL} \approx 3.32 \cdot \text{HVL}$ | mm Pb |
| 7 | Transmission through collimator: $T = \dfrac{I}{I_{0}} = e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer–Lambert law | dimensionless |
| 8 | Wedge Transmission Factor (WTF) | $\text{WTF} = \dfrac{\text{Dose at central axis with wedge}}{\text{Dose without wedge}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Leaf width at isocenter | $w_{iso} = w_{phys} \cdot \dfrac{\text{SAD}}{d_{leaf}}$ | mm |
| 10 | Penumbra geometric term: $P = s \cdot \dfrac{\text{SDD} - d}{\text{SAD}}$ | Geometric penumbra width | mm |

> Where $s$ = source diameter, SDD = Source-to-Diaphragm Distance, $d$ = depth, $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient, $x$ = absorber thickness.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Conformal radiotherapy:** MLC shapes beam to PTV, reducing dose to organs at risk (OAR) by **30–50%**.
- **Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS):** Uses micro-MLCs with leaf widths **3–5 mm** for sub-millimeter accuracy.
- **Image-Guided Radiotherapy (IGRT):** Integrates CBCT with MLC positioning to track tumor motion.
- **Diagnostic radiology:** Collimators reduce patient dose by **20–40%** by limiting the field to the area of clinical interest.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Geometric Penumbra of a Collimator System

The **penumbra** is the region at the edge of the radiation field where dose falls from 80% to 20% of the central axis value. We derive the **geometric component** of penumbra.

### Setup
Consider a beam from a **focal spot of diameter $s$** (not a true point source) passing through a **diaphragm (jaw edge)** located at distance $\text{SDD}$ from the source, reaching a point at **depth $d$** in a phantom at distance $\text{SAD}$ from the source.

### Step 1 — Define Geometry
The geometric penumbra $P$ is the width of the **umbra** (full shadow) projected by the finite source size. From similar triangles:

$$\frac{P}{s} = \frac{\text{SAD} - \text{SDD}}{s_{0}}$$

where $s_{0}$ is the SDD (source-to-diaphragm distance).

### Step 2 — Cross-Multiply
Rearranging to isolate $P$:

$$P = s \cdot \frac{\text{SAD} - \text{SDD}}{\text{SDD}}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Depth Factor
For a point at depth $d$ below the surface, the effective distance from source becomes $(\text{SSD} + d)$. The diaphragm-to-point distance is $(\text{SSD} + d - \text{SDD})$. Refining:

$$P(d) = s \cdot \frac{(\text{SSD} + d) - \text{SDD}}{\text{SDD}}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify
Let $\text{SAD} = \text{SSD} + d_{max}$ (standard isocentric setup). At isocenter $d = d_{max}$:

$$P_{iso} = s \cdot \frac{\text{SAD} - \text{SDD}}{\text{SDD}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
- $s$ = **1.5 mm** (typical LINAC focal spot)
- $\text{SDD}$ = **45 cm** (jaw position)
- $\text{SAD}$ = **100 cm**

$$P_{iso} = 1.5 \times \frac{100 - 45}{45} = 1.5 \times \frac{55}{45} = 1.5 \times 1.222 \approx 1.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** A 1.5 mm focal spot combined with a jaw at 45 cm produces a **1.83 mm geometric penumbra** at the isocenter. MLCs placed closer to the patient (e.g., at 50–60 cm) **reduce this penumbra significantly**, which is why modern MLCs improve field sharpness.

## 3.2 Derivation: Field Size Scaling with Distance (Inverse Square Geometry)

### Step 1 — Similar Triangles
A point at distance $d$ from the source sees a field of size $L_{d}$. At isocenter (SAD), the field size is $L_{iso}$.

$$\frac{L_{d}}{d} = \frac{L_{iso}}{\text{SAD}}$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange
$$L_{d} = L_{iso} \cdot \frac{d}{\text{SAD}}$$

### Step 3 — Squaring for Area
$$A_{d} = A_{iso} \cdot \left(\frac{d}{\text{SAD}}\right)^{2}$$

This is the **field-size divergence law**, used in dose calculation for irregular fields.

## 3.3 Derivation: Wedge Angle and Dose Distribution

A **wedge filter** is a tapered metallic absorber placed in the beam to produce a **graded dose distribution** across the field.

### Step 1 — Define Wedge Angle
The wedge angle $\theta_{w}$ is the angle between the **isodose line at a given depth** and the **perpendicular to the central axis**, usually measured at 10 cm depth for a 10 × 10 cm field.

### Step 2 — Transmission
At any point $(x, y)$ across the field, the transmission through wedge of thickness $t(x)$:

$$T(x) = e^{-\mu \cdot t(x)}$$

### Step 3 — Wedge Angle Relation
For a linear wedge, $t(x) = t_{0} + k \cdot x$, where $k = \tan(\theta_{w})$. Then:

$$\tan(\theta_{w}) = \frac{\ln(T_{1}/T_{2})}{x_{2} - x_{1}}$$

where $T_{1}, T_{2}$ are transmissions at two field positions $x_{1}, x_{2}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Collimator Comparison

| Parameter | Primary Collimator | Secondary Jaws | Multileaf Collimator (MLC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Tungsten (W) | Lead (Pb) or Tungsten | Tungsten alloy |
| **Position from source** | ~15–20 cm | ~30–45 cm | ~50–60 cm |
| **Mobility** | Fixed | Motorized (X & Y) | Each leaf independently motorized |
| **Field shape** | Circular max | Rectangular | Irregular (conformal) |
| **Resolution** | N/A | ~1 mm | 5–10 mm leaf width |
| **Penumbra contribution** | High | Medium | Low (closest to patient) |
| **Replaces** | — | Field defining blocks | Cerrobend blocks |
| **KTU exam weight** | 1 mark | 3 marks | 7–10 marks |

## 3.5 Numerical Problem — KTU Pattern

**Problem:** A LINAC has a focal spot of 2.0 mm diameter, the upper X-jaw is at 28 cm from the source, the lower Y-jaw is at 41 cm, and the MLC bank is at 56 cm. The SAD is 100 cm. Compare geometric penumbra contributions.

**Solution:**

For X-jaw: $P_{X} = 2.0 \times \frac{100 - 28}{28} = 2.0 \times 2.57 = 5.14$ mm

For Y-jaw: $P_{Y} = 2.0 \times \frac{100 - 41}{41} = 2.0 \times 1.44 = 2.88$ mm

For MLC: $P_{MLC} = 2.0 \times \frac{100 - 56}{56} = 2.0 \times 0.786 = 1.57$ mm

**Conclusion:** The MLC, being closest to the patient, contributes the **smallest geometric penumbra** and is therefore the most critical component in field-edge definition.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LINAC Treatment Head with Collimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electron Gun] --> B[Accelerating Waveguide]
    B --> C[Beam Bending Magnet]
    C --> D[X Ray Target or Electron Scattering Foil]
    D --> E[Primary Collimator Tungsten Fixed]
    E --> F[Flattening Filter for Photon Beams]
    F --> G[X Jaws Upper Pair Motorized]
    G --> H[Y Jaws Lower Pair Motorized]
    H --> I[Multileaf Collimator MLC]
    I --> J[Optical Distance Indicator ODI]
    J --> K[Laser Crosshair System]
    K --> L[Patient on Treatment Couch]
    L --> M[Isocenter at SAD 100 cm]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff

    style J fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff

    style M fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Collimator Hierarchy & Function

```mermaid

    P1[Stage 1 Primary Collimator] -->|Defines max circular field| P2[Stage 2 Secondary Jaws]
    P2 -->|Defines rectangular field| P3[Stage 3 Multileaf Collimator]

    P5[Source Focal Spot] --> P1
    P6[Target Volume PTV] -.->|Beam must conform to| P4

    style P1 fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Beam Direction Device Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        R1[Wall Laser Lateral X1]

    I --- G2
    I --- G3

```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic — MLC Leaf Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LeafBankA[MLC Bank A moving left to right]
        L1A[Leaf 1]
        L2A[Leaf 2]
        L3A[Leaf 3]
        L4A[Leaf N]
    end

    subgraph LeafBankB[MLC Bank B moving right to left]
        L1B[Leaf 1]
        L2B[Leaf 2]
        L3B[Leaf 3]
        L4B[Leaf N]
    end

    FIELD[Tumor Projection at Isocenter] -.->|Leaves close around| LeafBankA
    FIELD -.->|Leaves close around| LeafBankB
    CNS[Central Axis Beam] --> FIELD

    style CNS fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style FIELD fill:#ff8c00,color:#ffffff
```
