<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a collimator. List the different types of collimators used in a linear accelerator.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A collimator is a device used in a LINAC to **restrict and shape the radiation beam** to a defined geometric field, ensuring the beam conforms to the target volume while minimizing dose to healthy tissue.

**Types of collimators in a LINAC treatment head:**
1. **Primary Collimator** – fixed tungsten block defining maximum circular field.
2. **Secondary Collimator (Jaws)** – two pairs of movable blocks (X and Y) for rectangular fields.
3. **Multileaf Collimator (MLC)** – 80–160 individually motorized leaves for conformal shaping.

**(3 marks — full distribution)**

---

### Q2. What is the function of an Optical Distance Indicator (ODI) in radiotherapy?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

The ODI is a **beam direction device** mounted on the treatment head gantry. It projects a **movable shadow line** onto the patient's skin, allowing the radiographer to read the **Source-to-Surface Distance (SSD)** directly. It ensures reproducible patient setup, particularly for SSD-based treatment techniques, and operates typically within a tolerance of **±1 mm**. The ODI is calibrated at the standard SSD of **100 cm**.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Describe in detail the design, construction, and function of a Multileaf Collimator (MLC). List its advantages over custom cerrobend blocks. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Design & Construction:**
- A modern MLC consists of **two opposing banks** (A and B) of individually driven tungsten-alloy leaves.
- Each leaf is typically **5–10 mm wide** projected at isocenter, with a height of **6–8 cm**.
- The leaves move in a **tongue-and-groove** arrangement to minimize inter-leaf leakage.
- Leaves are driven by **DC servo motors or stepper motors** with **optical/encoder feedback** to achieve positioning accuracy of **±0.5 mm**.
- Total leaf count: typically **80–120** (40–60 per bank).

**Function:**
- Each leaf is independently positioned to create an **irregular, tumor-conformal field** at isocenter.
- Replaces conventional **cerrobend (low-melting-point alloy) blocks** that were hand-crafted for each patient.
- Enables **conformal radiotherapy (CRT)**, **IMRT (Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy)**, and **VMAT (Volumetric Modulated Arc Therapy)**.

**Advantages over Cerrobend Blocks:**
1. **No manual fabrication** — saves therapist time and eliminates risk of error.
2. **Dynamic delivery** — leaves move during treatment (dynamic MLC / sliding window IMRT).
3. **Smaller penumbra** at field edges due to closer proximity to patient.
4. **Reduced neutron and photon leakage** through better-defined geometry.
5. **Improved workflow** — fully digital, integrated with TPS (Treatment Planning System).
6. **Reproducibility** — digitally recorded positions, no block storage issues.

**(7 marks — well-distributed)**

#### (b) Explain the role and working of laser alignment systems in a radiotherapy treatment room. Why are three lasers used instead of one? (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Laser Systems:**
Laser alignment systems in a treatment room ensure the patient is positioned such that the **tumor isocenter coincides with the machine isocenter** (±1 mm tolerance). They are **passive beam direction devices** that provide fixed reference planes in the room coordinate system.

**Working Principle:**
- Each laser emits a thin, focused red (typically 635 nm) beam.
- The beam is **mounted on the wall or ceiling** of the treatment room and aligned to pass through the **mechanical isocenter** of the LINAC.
- The **lateral lasers** define a **horizontal plane** (transverse axis), and the **sagittal (ceiling) laser** defines the **midline plane**.
- Crosshairs of the three lasers intersect exactly at the **isocenter**.
- Patient skin is **tattooed** at these intersection points during simulation, and re-aligned daily.

**Why Three Lasers (not one)?**
A single laser could only define a **line** or a **plane**, not a 3D point. Three orthogonal lasers define:
1. **Two lateral lasers** → the **left-right** and **anterior-posterior** planes.
2. **One ceiling laser** → the **superior-inferior** axis.
Their intersection uniquely locates a **point in 3D space** — the isocenter.

**KTU bonus:** Modern systems use **five lasers** (two sagittal, two coronal, one axial) for enhanced accuracy in **SRS and SRT** treatments.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) With a neat diagram, describe the construction of a LINAC treatment head and explain the function of each collimation stage. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic.)*

**Construction of the LINAC Treatment Head (from source downward):**
1. **X-ray Target** — high-Z (tungsten) bremsstrahlung target, water-cooled.
2. **Primary Collimator** — fixed tungsten block; defines the maximum 35–50 cm circular field at isocenter.
3. **Flattening Filter** — conical aluminum/steel filter that flattens the forward-peaked bremsstrahlung spectrum.
4. **Secondary Collimator Jaws** — two pairs of motorized lead/tungsten blocks; X-jaws (upper) and Y-jaws (lower).
5. **Multileaf Collimator (MLC)** — tertiary collimator, 80–160 tungsten leaves, individually motorized.
6. **Reticle / Crosshair** — optical beam defining central axis.
7. **ODI** — measures SSD.
8. **Accessory Mount** — for wedges, blocks, electron applicators.

**Functions of Each Collimation Stage:**
- **Primary:** Crude restriction of divergent beam.
- **Secondary:** Rectangular field definition.
- **Tertiary (MLC):** Conformal shaping to PTV.
- **Cumulative Effect:** Field size is reduced progressively while **penumbra is minimized** at each stage.

**(7 marks)**

#### (b) Derive the expression for the geometric penumbra of a collimator and explain how it depends on source size, source-to-diaphragm distance, and source-to-skin distance. (7 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

*Derivation:*

Consider a finite-extended source of diameter $s$. Photons emerge from the extremities of the source and pass the edge of a diaphragm at distance $\text{SDD}$ from the source. They reach the surface at distance $\text{SSD}$, and a deeper point at distance $(\text{SSD} + d)$.

By similar triangles, the width of the geometric umbra (penumbra region) at the surface:

$$P_{surface} = s \cdot \frac{\text{SSD} - \text{SDD}}{\text{SDD}}$$

At a point at depth $d$ (where SAD = SSD + $d_{max}$ isocenter setup):

$$P(d) = s \cdot \frac{(\text{SSD} + d) - \text{SDD}}{\text{SDD}}$$

**Dependencies:**
1. **Source size $s$:** Larger focal spot → larger penumbra. Therefore, LINACs use small focal spots (~1–2 mm).
2. **Source-to-Diaphragm Distance (SDD):** As SDD increases, the collimator moves further from the source → **penumbra decreases**.
3. **Source-to-Skin Distance (SSD):** As SSD increases, the surface moves away from the diaphragm → **penumbra increases** (counter-intuitive but correct).
4. **Depth $d$:** As depth increases → **penumbra increases**.

**Engineering Implication:** To minimize penumbra, place the **final collimating element (MLC) as close to the patient as possible**, with a **small source size**, and use **larger SSD/SAD** setups.

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid**
> 1. **Confusing Primary and Secondary Collimator order** — Primary is *always* closest to the target. Reversing this costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Writing "collimator produces radiation"** — Wrong. The collimator *shapes* radiation emitted from the source.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in penumbra derivations (must be in **mm**).
> 4. **Using "MM" for Multiple Myeloma in an MLC question** — MLC here means Multileaf Collimator.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram label** in 7-mark questions — Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing SSD with SAD** in penumbra formulas — a classic KTU trap.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Collimators and Beam Direction Devices**
> - A **collimator** shapes the radiation beam; a **beam direction device** aligns the beam to the patient.
> - **Primary collimator** = fixed tungsten; first stage; defines max circular field.
> - **Secondary collimator** = jaws (X and Y); produces rectangular field.
> - **Tertiary collimator** = MLC; produces conformal field; **closest to patient = smallest penumbra**.
> - **MLC** has **80–160 leaves**, each **5–10 mm wide at isocenter**, individually motorized.
> - **Pin & Arc** = mechanical angle reference; **Goniometer** = couch rotation scale.
> - **ODI** reads SSD on patient skin (typical accuracy **±1 mm**).
> - **Three lasers** (2 lateral + 1 sagittal) intersect at **isocenter** to define 3D point.
> - **Geometric penumbra formula:** $P = s \cdot \dfrac{(\text{SSD} + d) - \text{SDD}}{\text{SDD}}$.
> - Penumbra depends on: source size $s$ (directly), SDD (inversely), SSD (directly), depth (directly).
> - **Wedge filters** produce graded dose distributions; **wedge angle** is measured at 10 cm depth for 10 × 10 cm field.
> - **Standard SAD** = **100 cm**; **standard SSD** = **80–100 cm**.
> - **Inverse square law** governs beam divergence; field area scales with $d^{2}$.
> - **Cerrobend blocks** have been replaced by **MLC** in modern LINACs.
> - **IMRT and VMAT** are enabled primarily by dynamic MLC motion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gamma Ray Units - Cobalt Machines. Radiation Sources (Radionuclides) for Brachytherapy. Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy - Multileaf Collimator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gamma Ray Units - Cobalt Machines. Radiation Sources (Radionuclides) for Brachytherapy. Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy - Multileaf Collimator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cobalt-60 Teletherapy Unit (Gamma Ray Unit)

### Formal Definition
A **Cobalt-60 Teletherapy Unit** (also called a **Gamma Knife** in some contexts, or more commonly a **Cobalt Unit / Theratron**) is a megavoltage external beam radiotherapy device that uses a sealed radioactive source of **Cobalt-60 (⁶⁰Co)** to produce a beam of gamma rays of average energy **1.25 MeV** for the treatment of deep-seated tumours. The unit follows the principles of *teletherapy* — where the radiation source is placed at a distance (typically **80 cm SSD – Source to Surface Distance**) from the patient.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cobalt-60 is the workhorse radionuclide of teletherapy. It decays by **β⁻ emission to Nickel-60 (⁶⁰Ni)**, which then emits two gamma photons (**1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV**), giving a weighted mean energy of **1.25 MeV**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a powerful **flashlight** placed far away from a wall. The light spreads out (diverges) and becomes dimmer as it travels. The Cobalt-60 unit behaves exactly like this — but instead of visible light, it emits high-energy gamma rays. By collimating (focusing) these rays through heavy lead blocks, the beam is shaped to "burn" only the tumour while sparing healthy tissue. The source is mounted on a **rotating gantry**, much like a satellite dish, allowing the beam to be aimed from any angle around the patient.

---

## 1.2 Radiation Sources (Radionuclides) for Brachytherapy

### Formal Definition
**Brachytherapy** (from Greek *brachys* = "short distance") is a form of radiotherapy where sealed radioactive sources are placed **inside or immediately adjacent to** the tumour. The radionuclides used emit photons (gamma or characteristic X-rays) of suitable energy and half-life, encapsulated in a metallic seed, wire, or needle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Radionuclides for Brachytherapy:**
> - **⁶⁰Co** — average energy 1.25 MeV (older HDR/LDR)
> - **¹³⁷Cs** — 0.662 MeV (gynecological intracavitary)
> - **¹⁹²Ir** — 0.380 MeV average (modern HDR afterloading)
> - **¹²⁵I** — 27–35 keV (permanent prostate/eye implants)
> - **¹⁰³Pd** — 21 keV (permanent prostate seeds)
> - **¹⁹⁸Au** — 412 keV (historical grain implants)

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of brachytherapy as **placing a small heater directly inside the room you want to warm**, rather than blowing hot air from outside (which is teletherapy). The radiation "heat" is delivered right where it is needed — the tumour — minimizing damage to the surrounding "walls" (normal tissues).

---

## 1.3 Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy (IMRT) and the Multileaf Collimator (MLC)

### Formal Definition
**Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy (IMRT)** is an advanced form of conformal radiotherapy in which the intensity of the radiation beam is **non-uniformly modulated** across its cross-section, allowing concave dose distributions that closely conform to the irregular three-dimensional shape of the target while avoiding organs at risk (OARs). This is achieved primarily through a **Multileaf Collimator (MLC)** — a device consisting of many (typically **40–120**) individually motor-driven tungsten leaves that dynamically shape the beam aperture during rotation of the gantry.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine watering an oddly-shaped garden with a **standard garden hose** — water falls everywhere, including on the pavement. An **MLC** is like a **smart sprinkler head with 80 individually controlled nozzles**, each one opening and closing in milliseconds so that water falls **only on the plants**, not on the walkways. The "intensity map" of the beam is built up by these rapid leaf movements from many beam angles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard physical constants used in this module:**
> - Half-life of ⁶⁰Co: **T½ = 5.26 years**
> - Half-life of ¹³⁷Cs: **T½ = 30.17 years**
> - Half-life of ¹⁹²Ir: **T½ = 73.83 days**
> - Half-life of ¹²⁵I: **T½ = 59.4 days**
> - Standard SSD for Co-60: **80 cm**
> - Standard SSD for linac: **100 cm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cobalt-60 Unit: Construction & Working

### Components of a Cobalt Unit
1. **Radioactive Source**: A cylindrical pellet (≈ 1–2 cm diameter × 1–2 cm height) of metallic ⁶⁰Co doubly encapsulated in stainless steel.
2. **Source Housing (Head)**: A lead-filled steel casing with a defined **Source to Surface Distance (SSD) of 80 cm**.
3. **Primary Collimator**: Fixed lead diaphragm that defines the maximum field size.
4. **Secondary Collimators**: Two pairs of adjustable lead jaws (X and Y) for square/rectangular fields.
5. **Penumbra Trimmer**: A thin lead ring near the patient to reduce the geometric penumbra.
6. **Gantry & Collimator Stand**: Allows 360° rotation and angulation.
7. **Treatment Couch**: Supports the patient.
8. **Source Drive Mechanism**: Pneumatic or electric "ON/OFF" — source moves to "BEAM ON" position via a steel cable; in the safe position, it is shielded by tungsten.

### Decay Scheme of ⁶⁰Co
- ⁶⁰Co (Z = 27) → ⁶⁰Ni* (Z = 28) + β⁻ + ν̄ₑ (Q = 0.318 MeV)
- ⁶⁰Ni* → ⁶⁰Ni + γ (1.1732 MeV) + γ (1.3325 MeV)
- **Average γ-energy = 1.25 MeV**
- **Specific Gamma Ray Constant (Γ) for ⁶⁰Co = 0.32 R·cm²/(mCi·h)** or in SI: **0.354 µGy·m²/(GBq·h)**

### Why Cobalt-60? (Engineering Advantages)
- **Long half-life (5.26 years)** — source needs replacement only every 5–7 years.
- **High energy (1.25 MeV)** — gives skin-sparing effect (build-up region at d_max ≈ 0.5 cm).
- **Simple, reliable** — no high-voltage electricity, no cooling system (unlike linac).
- **Lower cost** — ideal for low-resource settings (per IEAE recommendations).
- **Stable output** — predictable decay, no warm-up issues.

### Disadvantages
- Source decay → output drops ~1.1% per month (must recalculate treatment time).
- Penumbra is large (~1.5 cm) → poor dose conformity.
- Risk of source leakage, radioactive contamination.
- Not suitable for very small or very deep targets with sharp dose gradients.

---

## 2.2 Brachytherapy: Physical & Clinical Principles

### Classification of Brachytherapy
| Modality | Application | Typical Source |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Surface / Mould** | Skin lesions | ⁶⁰Co, ¹⁹⁸Au, ⁹⁰Sr |
| **Interstitial** | Prostate, breast, head-neck | ¹²⁵I, ¹⁹²Ir wires/seeds, ¹⁰³Pd |
| **Intracavitary** | Cervix, uterus, vagina | ¹³⁷Cs, ¹⁹²Ir, ⁶⁰Co |
| **Intraluminal** | Esophagus, bronchus | ¹⁹²Ir |
| **Intravascular** | Coronary arteries | ⁹⁰Sr / ⁹⁰Y |

### Dose Rate Categories
- **LDR (Low Dose Rate):** 0.4–2 Gy/h (¹²⁵I, ¹⁰³Pd permanent seeds)
- **MDR (Medium Dose Rate):** 2–12 Gy/h
- **HDR (High Dose Rate):** > 12 Gy/h (¹⁹²Ir in modern afterloaders)

### Key Physical Properties of an Ideal Brachytherapy Source
1. **Half-life** long enough for clinical use (days to years).
2. **Energy** sufficient to treat the target (40 keV – 1.5 MeV).
3. **Specific activity** high (small physical size).
4. **Afterloading compatibility** (modern requirement).
5. **Biocompatibility** of encapsulation.
6. **Tissue equivalence** of materials.

### Dosimetry Systems
- **Manchester System (Paterson-Parker)** — defines rules for planar and volume implants using radium.
- **Paris System** — used for linear ¹⁹²Ir wire implants, defines basal dose, reference dose.
- **AAPM TG-43 Formalism** — modern 1D / 2D dose calculation around cylindrical sources.

---

## 2.3 IMRT & Multileaf Collimator (MLC)

### Why IMRT is Needed
Conventional 3D-CRT (Conformal RT) shapes the beam to the *silhouette* of the target, but it cannot modulate intensity *within* a field. Many tumours (especially head-neck, prostate, concave targets) require *concave* dose distributions — sparing a critical OAR that curves into the field.

### Types of IMRT Delivery
- **Static (Step-and-Shoot) MLC** — beam is on only between leaf positions.
- **Dynamic (Sliding Window) MLC** — leaves move continuously during irradiation.
- **Volumetric Modulated Arc Therapy (VMAT)** — gantry rotates while MLC shape and dose rate vary continuously.
- **Tomotherapy** — a fan-beam from a linac mounted on a CT-style ring.

### MLC Anatomy
- **Material:** Tungsten alloy (ρ ≈ 17–18.5 g/cm³), height ≈ 5–7.5 cm.
- **Number of leaves:** 40 to 120 pairs (typical 60 pairs at 1 cm width or 120 pairs at 0.5 cm).
- **Leaf width at isocentre:** 5 mm or 10 mm (for stereotactic: 2.5–3 mm).
- **Leaf travel:** typically 15 cm across the central axis.
- **Leaf speed:** up to 3 cm/s.
- **Leaf transmission:** < 2% (inter-leaf leakage) and < 0.5% (intra-leaf).
- **Maximum field size:** typically 40 × 40 cm.

### Inverse Planning
The IMRT process uses an **inverse planning algorithm** (e.g., gradient descent, simulated annealing) where the planner specifies:
- **Target dose** (e.g., 70 Gy to PTV)
- **OAR constraints** (e.g., max 50 Gy to spinal cord)
- **Number of beams / arcs**

The optimizer solves for the **fluence map** of each beam, which is then decomposed into **leaf sequences** (MLC motions).

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay (general) | Bq / Ci |
| 2 | $A(t) = A_0 \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{t/T_{1/2}}$ | Decay by half-life | Bq / Ci |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ | Decay constant | s⁻¹ or day⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\dot{D} = \Gamma \cdot \dfrac{A}{d^2}$ | Exposure / Dose rate from point source (inverse square law) | Gy/h or R/h |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{D}{\dot{D}}$ | Treatment time | seconds/minutes |
| 6 | $T_{new} = T_{old} \cdot 2^{\Delta t / T_{1/2}}$ | Decay correction factor | – |
| 7 | $\text{OAR} = 2^{\Delta t / T_{1/2}}$ | Output Adjustment Ratio (Co-60) | – |
| 8 | $PDD = \dfrac{D_d}{D_{d_{max}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage Depth Dose | % |
| 9 | $SAD = 100 \, \text{cm (linac)}; 80 \, \text{cm (Co-60)}$ | Source-to-Axis Distance | cm |
| 10 | $S_{k} = \Gamma_{AK} \cdot A_k$ | Reference Air Kerma Rate (TG-43) | µGy·m²/h |
| 11 | $\dot{D}(r,\theta) = S_k \cdot \Lambda \cdot \dfrac{G(r,\theta)}{G(r_0,\theta_0)} \cdot g(r) \cdot F(r,\theta)$ | AAPM TG-43 brachytherapy dose rate | Gy/h |
| 12 | $\Lambda = \dfrac{\dot{D}(r_0,\theta_0)}{S_k}$ | Dose rate constant | cGy·h⁻¹·U⁻¹ |
| 13 | $FT = (d_2/d_1)^2$ | Tissue Maximum Ratio (TMR) inverse square correction | – |
| 14 | $\dot{K}_x = 1.602 \times 10^{-8} \cdot \Gamma_{\delta} \cdot A_x$ | Air-kerma rate from exposure constant | Gy/h |
| 15 | $\sum_i w_i \cdot M_i = 1$ | Inverse planning objective normalization | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - $\Gamma$ = specific gamma ray constant
> - $A$ = activity
> - $d$ = distance from source
> - $T_{1/2}$ = half-life
> - $\lambda$ = decay constant
> - $S_k$ = air-kerma strength
> - $G(r,\theta)$ = geometry factor
> - $g(r)$ = radial dose function
> - $F(r,\theta)$ = anisotropy function
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Decay of ⁶⁰Co Activity and Treatment Time

**Problem Statement:** A Cobalt-60 unit has an initial activity of **5000 Ci** when calibrated on **1-Jan-2020**. Calculate the activity on **1-July-2022** and the required treatment time to deliver **200 cGy** to a tumour at **d = 80 cm SSD** with a **field size of 10 × 10 cm**. Use the specific gamma-ray constant Γ = **0.32 R·cm²/(mCi·h)** and assume a tissue-phantom ratio giving **1 R ≈ 0.96 cGy**.

### Step 1 — Time elapsed
$$
\Delta t = 2.5 \text{ years} = 2.5 \times 365.25 \times 24 = 21915 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 2 — Decay constant
$$
\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.693}{5.26 \text{ years}} = 0.1317 \text{ /year}
$$

### Step 3 — Activity on target date
$$
A = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} = 5000 \times e^{-0.1317 \times 2.5}
$$

$$
A = 5000 \times e^{-0.3293} = 5000 \times 0.7194 = 3597 \text{ Ci}
$$

### Step 4 — Exposure rate at 80 cm (inverse square law)
$$
\dot{X} = \Gamma \cdot \dfrac{A}{d^2}
$$

Converting: A = 3597 Ci = 3.597 × 10⁶ mCi, d = 80 cm.

$$
\dot{X} = 0.32 \times \dfrac{3.597 \times 10^6}{80^2} = 0.32 \times \dfrac{3.597 \times 10^6}{6400}
$$

$$
\dot{X} = 0.32 \times 562.0 = 179.8 \text{ R/h}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert exposure rate to dose rate
$$
\dot{D} = 179.8 \times 0.96 = 172.6 \text{ cGy/h} = 2.877 \text{ cGy/min}
$$

### Step 6 — Treatment time
$$
T = \dfrac{D}{\dot{D}} = \dfrac{200}{2.877} = 69.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This shows the clinical cost of source decay — a 28% drop in activity extends treatment time by ~40% in cGy/min terms. This is why Co-60 sources are replaced every 5–7 years (when output drops to ~50% of original).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output Adjustment Ratio (OAR) for Cobalt-60

**Statement:** Derive the formula connecting a previously calculated treatment time with the new treatment time after a time interval.

### Derivation
Let $A_0$ be the calibration activity and $A$ the present activity.

$$
\dfrac{T_{new}}{T_{old}} = \dfrac{\dot{D}_{old}}{\dot{D}_{new}} = \dfrac{A_0}{A} = \dfrac{1}{(1/2)^{\Delta t / T_{1/2}}} = 2^{\Delta t / T_{1/2}}
$$

### Compact Result
$$
\boxed{T_{new} = T_{old} \cdot 2^{\Delta t / T_{1/2}}}
$$

### Numerical Example
For $\Delta t = 1$ month = 30.4 days, $T_{1/2} = 5.26$ years = 1920 days:
$$
\text{OAR} = 2^{30.4/1920} = 2^{0.01583} = 1.0110
$$

So treatment time increases by **1.1% per month** — a key teaching point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Square Law & PDD Relationship for Co-60

For a beam at SSD = 80 cm, the dose at depth d in a phantom can be written:

$$
D(d) = D_{d_{max}} \cdot PDD(d) \cdot \dfrac{(SSD + d_{max})^2}{(SSD + d)^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{BSF}
$$

**Where BSF** = Back-Scatter Factor (B factor) for the given field size.

For Co-60, the typical BSF at 10 × 10 cm² is **1.036**.

The "Mayneord F-factor" generalises this for any SSD:
$$
F = \left( \dfrac{SSD_2 + d_{max}}{SSD_1 + d_{max}} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{SSD_1 + d}{SSD_2 + d} \right)^2
$$

This is required whenever treatment is moved from SSD = 80 cm (Co-60) to a different SSD.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — AAPM TG-43 Brachytherapy Dose Calculation

The 2D formalism for dose rate at a point $(r, \theta)$ from a cylindrical source:

$$
\dot{D}(r, \theta) = S_k \cdot \Lambda \cdot \dfrac{G(r,\theta)}{G(r_0,\theta_0)} \cdot g(r) \cdot F(r,\theta)
$$

### Step-by-step
- **$S_k$** = Air-kerma strength of the source (in U, where 1 U = 1 µGy·m²·h⁻¹).
- **$\Lambda$** = Dose rate constant at reference point $(r_0 = 1 \text{ cm}, \theta_0 = 90°)$.
- **$G(r,\theta)$** = Geometry factor = $r^{-2}$ for a line source (ignoring self-absorption).
- **$g(r)$** = Radial dose function (accounts for scatter and attenuation in water).
- **$F(r,\theta)$** = 2D anisotropy function (accounts for dose variation with polar angle).

### Numerical Example (¹²⁵I seed, model 6711)
- $S_k$ = 0.5 U (typical)
- $\Lambda$ = 0.88 cGy·h⁻¹·U⁻¹
- $r$ = 1 cm, $\theta$ = 90°
- $G(r, \theta) / G(1, 90°)$ = 1
- $g(1)$ = 1.0
- $F(1, 90°)$ = 1.0

$$
\dot{D} = 0.5 \times 0.88 \times 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 0.44 \text{ cGy/h}
$$

This is consistent with LDR definition (0.4–2 Gy/h).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — MLC Effective Attenuation

For a tungsten leaf of thickness $t$ (in cm) with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ (in cm⁻¹):

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu t}
$$

Typical values: $\mu_{W} \approx 0.975 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at 6 MV photon beam (effective energy ~2 MeV), $t = 5$ cm.

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-0.975 \times 5} = I_0 \cdot e^{-4.875} = 7.6 \times 10^{-3} \, I_0
$$

So leaf transmission is **~0.76%** — consistent with the < 2% engineering spec.

The **inter-leaf leakage** (between adjacent leaves) is typically 2–4% due to the tongue-and-groove design.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Modality Characteristics

| Parameter | Cobalt-60 | Linac (6 MV) | IMRT/VMAT | HDR ¹⁹²Ir |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|-----------|-----------|
| Energy | 1.25 MeV γ | 6 MV photon | 6 MV (modulated) | 0.38 MeV γ avg |
| d_max | 0.5 cm | 1.5 cm | 1.5 cm | – |
| Penumbra | 1.0–1.5 cm | 0.5 cm | 0.3–0.5 cm | – |
| Skin sparing | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | None (surface) |
| Output stability | Decays 1.1%/mo | ± 2% | ± 2% | Decays 1.0%/day |
| Field conformity | Conformal | 3D-CRT | Highly conformal | Brachy |
| Source replacement | 5–7 yr | N/A | N/A | 3–4 months |
| Cost | Low | High | Very High | High |
| Maintenance | Simple | Complex | Very complex | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cobalt-60 Teletherapy Unit — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Decay of Co60 source in head] --> B[Gamma photons 1.25 MeV emitted]
    B --> C[Primary Collimator fixed lead]
    C --> D[Secondary Collimators X and Y jaws]
    D --> E[Penumbra Trimmer ring]
    E --> F[Patient SSD 80 cm]
    F --> G[Tumour in body at depth d]
    G --> H[Dosimeter Ion chamber or TLD]
    H --> I[Output R per hour or Gy per min]
    I --> J[Treatment Computer Time calculation]
    J --> K[Patient Couch positioning system]
    K --> L[Beam ON Source moves up via cable]
    L --> M[BEAM OFF Source returns to safe position]
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style L fill:#cce5ff
    style M fill:#ccffcc
```

> **Functional description:** The cobalt source is housed in the upper head. When "Beam ON" is triggered, a steel cable pneumatically/electrically lifts the source into the "exposed" position; the beam exits the collimator assembly and reaches the patient at the isocentre. When "Beam OFF" is pressed, the source drops back into the tungsten shield, ensuring safety.

---

## 4.2 Brachytherapy Workflow — Afterloader Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Activity Ir192 source in shielded safe] --> B[Pneumatic stepper motor drives source]
    B --> C[Indexer chain selects channel 1 to N]
    C --> D[Transfer tube guides source]
    D --> E[Applicator in patient uterus prostate etc]
    E --> F[Dwell positions programmed in TPS]
    F --> G[Dwell time at each position]
    G --> H[Source retracts automatically on completion]
    H --> I[Treatment summary and patient discharge]
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style F fill:#ffffcc
    style H fill:#ccffcc
```

> **Engineering note:** In modern HDR afterloaders (e.g., Varian GammaMed, Elekta Flexisource), the source is **never** manually handled. It travels through a thin steel cable in transfer tubes from the safe to the applicator and back, with sub-millimetre positional accuracy (0.5 mm).

---

## 4.3 IMRT and Multileaf Collimator — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CT Simulation of patient] --> B[Target OAR contouring by oncologist]
    B --> C[Dose prescription 70 Gy to PTV]
    C --> D[Inverse Planning Optimization engine]
    D --> E[Fluence map for each beam angle]
    E --> F[Leaf Sequencing Algorithm]
    F --> G[MLC file leaf positions vs control points]
    G --> H[Linac MLC drives stepper motors per leaf]
    H --> I[Gantry rotates or fixed beam positions]
    I --> J[Beam modulated in real time]
    J --> K[Dose delivered to target with OAR sparing]
    K --> L[EPID or log file records delivered fluence]
    L --> M[Dose reconstruction and QA check]
    style D fill:#cce5ff
    style F fill:#ffffcc
    style H fill:#ffcccc
    style M fill:#ccffcc
```

---

## 4.4 MLC Mechanical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MLC Carriage Assembly] --> B[Bank A 60 leaves left side]
    A --> C[Bank B 60 leaves right side]
    B --> D[Each leaf has motor and encoder]
    C --> E[Each leaf has motor and encoder]
    D --> F[Tungsten leaf 5 to 7.5 cm thick]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Tongue and groove interlocking design]
    G --> H[Leaf tip focused to avoid penumbra divergence]
    H --> I[Field aperture defined at isocentre]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style F fill:#ffcccc
    style I fill:#ccffcc
```

> **Engineering insight:** The **tongue-and-groove** design (a 0.05 mm interlock) prevents inter-leaf leakage of radiation. The leaf tip is **rounded/triple-focused** (divergent on two axes) so that the leaf edge matches the divergent beam geometry, minimising penumbra.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six essential physical properties required for a radionuclide to be used in brachytherapy.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Suitable half-life** (days to a few years) — long enough for clinical workflow but short enough not to be hazardous after treatment.
2. **Appropriate photon energy** (typically 20 keV – 1.5 MeV) — for adequate penetration without excessive shielding requirements.
3. **High specific activity** — so that the source can be made physically small.
4. **Stable daughter nuclide** — no further radioactive decay products.
5. **Biocompatible encapsulation** — leak-proof stainless steel or titanium capsule.
6. **Availability in suitable physical form** — seeds, wires, needles, ribbons, or as HDR afterloading pellets.
7. **Manufacturable to high purity** with consistent dosimetric properties.
8. **Low neutron yield** (for neutron-emitting sources) for safety.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Explain the "penumbra" of a Cobalt-60 beam and the methods used to reduce it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **penumbra** is the region at the edge of the radiation field where the dose falls off rapidly from ~80–20% of the central axis value. For Co-60, geometric penumbra is significant (1.0–1.5 cm) because of the source's finite size (1–2 cm diameter).
**Causes:**
- **Geometric penumbra** — finite source size and source-to-diaphragm distance.
- **Transmission penumbra** — leakage through the finite thickness of the collimator edges.
- **Scatter penumbra** — secondary electrons and scatter from the collimator and patient.

**Methods to reduce:**
1. Use a **penumbra trimmer** — a thin lead ring placed near the patient.
2. Place the diaphragm **as far as possible from the source** (long SSD).
3. Use **focused multileaf collimators** with rounded leaf tips (modern linacs).
4. Increase the **collimator thickness** to reduce transmission.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a Cobalt-60 teletherapy unit with a neat block diagram. Discuss the head assembly, source housing, collimation system, and safety mechanisms. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A Cobalt-60 source had an activity of **4000 Ci** when calibrated on **1-Jan-2020**. A patient requires **250 cGy** at the tumour located at **d = 80 cm SSD**, with a **10 × 10 cm²** field. Compute the activity on **1-July-2023** and the corresponding treatment time. Use Γ = 0.32 R·cm²/(mCi·h) and f-factor = 0.96 cGy/R. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)

1. **Source (1 mark):** ⁶⁰Co pellets of cylindrical geometry, doubly encapsulated in stainless steel; activity 200–500 TBq (5000–15000 Ci) for clinical units.

2. **Source Housing (Head) (1.5 marks):** Spherical or cylindrical lead/tungsten-filled steel head with SSD = 80 cm. Contains source in shielded "safe" position.

3. **Collimation System (2 marks):**
   - **Primary collimator** — fixed lead cone defining max field (35°).
   - **Secondary collimators** — two pairs of adjustable lead jaws (X, Y) for square/rectangular fields up to 35 × 35 cm at isocentre.
   - **Penumbra trimmer** — additional lead ring closer to the patient.

4. **Gantry & Stand (1 mark):** 360° rotation with mechanical isocentric accuracy ± 2 mm; collimator can also rotate ± 90°.

5. **Source Drive & Safety (1 mark):** Pneumatic / electric system; source ON/OFF with indicator lights; backup manual retraction; backup timer terminates beam if dose limit reached.

6. **Treatment Couch (0.5 marks):** Motorised, indexed, 4–6 degrees of freedom for patient positioning.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $A_0$ = 4000 Ci on 1-Jan-2020
- $\Delta t$ = 3.5 years (1-Jan-2020 to 1-July-2023)
- $D$ = 250 cGy
- $d$ = 80 cm
- $\Gamma$ = 0.32 R·cm²/(mCi·h)
- $f$ = 0.96 cGy/R

**Step 1 — Decay constant (1 mark):**
$$
\lambda = \dfrac{0.693}{5.26} = 0.1317 \text{ /year}
$$

**Step 2 — Activity on 1-July-2023 (2 marks):**
$$
A = 4000 \times e^{-0.1317 \times 3.5} = 4000 \times e^{-0.4610}
$$

$$
A = 4000 \times 0.6305 = 2522 \text{ Ci}
$$

**Step 3 — Exposure rate at 80 cm (2 marks):**
Converting: A = 2522 Ci = 2.522 × 10⁶ mCi
$$
\dot{X} = 0.32 \times \dfrac{2.522 \times 10^6}{80^2} = 0.32 \times \dfrac{2.522 \times 10^6}{6400}
$$

$$
\dot{X} = 0.32 \times 394.1 = 126.1 \text{ R/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Dose rate (1 mark):**
$$
\dot{D} = 126.1 \times 0.96 = 121.0 \text{ cGy/h} = 2.017 \text{ cGy/min}
$$

**Step 5 — Treatment time (1 mark):**
$$
T = \dfrac{250}{2.017} = 123.9 \text{ min} \approx 124 \text{ min} \approx 2 \text{ h } 4 \text{ min}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to convert Ci to mCi when using $\Gamma$ in R·cm²/(mCi·h). One of the most common errors is to use $\Gamma$ with Ci and forget the 1000× factor, leading to a 1000× smaller exposure rate. Always check units: **1 Ci = 1000 mCi**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**(a)** What is Intensity-Modulated Radiotherapy (IMRT)? With a neat block diagram, describe the components and working of a Multileaf Collimator (MLC). Discuss the differences between step-and-shoot and sliding-window IMRT delivery. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Enumerate the common radionuclides used in brachytherapy. Compare the physical properties (half-life, energy, dose rate, clinical use) of **¹²⁵I, ¹⁹²Ir, and ¹³⁷Cs** in a tabular form. Discuss the TG-43 formalism for dose calculation around a brachytherapy source. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — IMRT & MLC (7 marks)

1. **Definition of IMRT (1 mark):** IMRT is an advanced conformal radiotherapy technique where the beam intensity is varied across the field using an MLC, allowing the dose distribution to wrap tightly around concave or irregular targets while sparing OARs.

2. **MLC Construction (3 marks):**
   - **Material & Density:** Tungsten alloy (ρ ≈ 17.5 g/cm³), thickness 5–7.5 cm.
   - **Number of leaves:** 40 to 120 pairs.
   - **Leaf width at isocentre:** 5 mm or 10 mm.
   - **Each leaf** has a dedicated stepper motor and optical encoder for sub-mm positioning.
   - **Tongue-and-groove** design to minimise inter-leaf leakage.
   - **Focused leaf tips** (divergent in both X and Y) to match beam geometry.
   - **Leaf speed** up to 3 cm/s.
   - **Maximum field size:** 40 × 40 cm.

3. **Step-and-Shoot vs Sliding-Window (2 marks):**

| Feature | Step-and-Shoot (SMLC) | Sliding-Window (DMLC) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Beam state | Off between segments | On throughout delivery |
| Leaf motion | Discrete positions | Continuous motion |
| Delivery time | Longer | Shorter |
| Verification | Easier (per segment) | Harder (log files) |
| Inverse planning | 3–9 segments/beam | Many control points |
| Use case | IMRT, small targets | IMRT, VMAT, large fields |

4. **Inverse Planning Output (1 mark):** The optimizer produces a **fluence map** which is decomposed into **leaf sequences**; the linac control system drives each leaf per **control point** to deliver the planned intensity.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Brachytherapy Radionuclides (7 marks)

1. **List of radionuclides (1 mark):** ⁶⁰Co, ¹³⁷Cs, ¹⁹²Ir, ¹²⁵I, ¹⁰³Pd, ¹⁹⁸Au, ⁹⁰Sr, ⁹⁰Y.

2. **Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Property | ¹²⁵I | ¹⁹²Ir | ¹³⁷Cs |
|----------|------|--------|---------|
| Half-life | 59.4 days | 73.83 days | 30.17 years |
| Energy (avg) | 28.5 keV | 0.38 MeV | 0.662 MeV |
| Form | Seeds (0.8 mm dia) | Wire/seed/HDR pellet | Tubes/needles |
| Dose rate | LDR | HDR | LDR |
| Clinical use | Permanent prostate, eye | Temporary, all sites | Gynae intracavitary |
| Half-value layer | 0.025 mm Pb | 3 mm Pb | 6.5 mm Pb |
| Shielding needs | Minimal | High (HDR) | Very high |
| Source replacement | Disposable | Every 3–4 months | Every 5–10 years |

3. **TG-43 Formalism (3 marks):**
The AAPM Task Group 43 (1995, updated 2004 and 2017) provides a standardised dose calculation around brachytherapy sources:

$$
\dot{D}(r,\theta) = S_k \cdot \Lambda \cdot \dfrac{G(r,\theta)}{G(r_0,\theta_0)} \cdot g(r) \cdot F(r,\theta)
$$

- **$S_k$** = Air-kerma strength (in U = µGy·m²·h⁻¹).
- **$\Lambda$** = Dose rate constant; depends on source design.
- **$G(r,\theta)$** = Geometry function = $1/r^2$ for line source; $(1/r^2)$ in general.
- **$g(r)$** = Radial dose function; accounts for attenuation and scatter in water.
- **$F(r,\theta)$** = 2D anisotropy function; accounts for dose variation with polar angle.

Reference point is at $(r_0 = 1 \text{ cm}, \theta_0 = 90°)$ perpendicular to the source axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In TG-43, students commonly forget that the reference point is at **1 cm distance on the perpendicular bisector** of the source (not on the long axis). Also, $\Lambda$ has units of **cGy·h⁻¹·U⁻¹**, NOT Gy/h. Many candidates lose marks by not stating the units explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Cobalt-60 Unit
- ☑ Average γ energy = **1.25 MeV** (1.17 + 1.33 MeV)
- ☑ Half-life = **5.26 years**
- ☑ SSD = **80 cm**, d_max ≈ **0.5 cm** (skin-sparing)
- ☑ Output drops ~**1.1% per month** (~13% per year)
- ☑ Specific gamma constant Γ = **0.32 R·cm²/(mCi·h)**
- ☑ Penumbra large: **1.0–1.5 cm** → use penumbra trimmer
- ☑ Source replaced every **5–7 years** (when output ~50%)

### Brachytherapy Sources
- ☑ **Brachy = short distance**; **Tele = far distance**
- ☑ **¹³⁷Cs**: LDR gyne (1.04 Ci tubes)
- ☑ **¹⁹²Ir**: HDR afterloader (modern)
- ☑ **¹²⁵I**: permanent prostate seeds, low energy (~28 keV)
- ☑ **TG-43**: standardised formalism, $S_k$ in U, $\Lambda$ in cGy·h⁻¹·U⁻¹
- ☑ **Manchester & Paris systems** are older planning systems for radium/Ir wires

### IMRT & MLC
- ☑ **IMRT** = intensity-modulated beam → concave dose distributions
- ☑ **MLC** = 40–120 tungsten leaves, 5–7.5 cm thick
- ☑ **Step-and-shoot** (SMLC) vs **Sliding-window** (DMLC) vs **VMAT**
- ☑ **Inverse planning** → optimizer produces fluence maps
- ☑ **Leaf transmission** < 2%, **inter-leaf leakage** < 4%
- ☑ **Tongue-and-groove** design reduces inter-leaf leakage
- ☑ **Inverse square law** $1/d^2$ governs dose fall-off
- ☑ **OAR** = $2^{\Delta t / T_{1/2}}$ for output correction

### Universal Useful Formulas
- ☑ $A = A_0 (1/2)^{t/T_{1/2}}$
- ☑ $T = D/\dot{D}$
- ☑ $\dot{X} = \Gamma A / d^2$
- ☑ $\lambda = 0.693 / T_{1/2}$
- ☑ TG-43: $\dot{D}(r,\theta) = S_k \cdot \Lambda \cdot G \cdot g \cdot F$

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** Always write units at every step of a numerical answer — examiners award step marks for **units + magnitude + direction of change** (increase/decrease). Never skip showing the conversion between Ci ↔ mCi ↔ Bq ↔ GBq.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stereotactic Techniques. Gamma Knife
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stereotactic Techniques. Gamma Knife

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stereotactic Techniques — Formal Definition

**Stereotactic techniques** refer to a class of minimally invasive neurosurgical and radiotherapeutic procedures that use a **three-dimensional (3D) coordinate system** to precisely localize and target small, well-defined intracranial (and increasingly extracranial) lesions. The Greek roots *stereo* (solid/3D) + *taxis* (arrangement/positioning) literally mean "touching in three dimensions."

In the context of radiotherapy, stereotactic techniques are classified into:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)** — A single, high-dose, highly focused radiation treatment delivered in **one session** (or a few sessions) with sub-millimeter accuracy. No surgical incision is made. The term was coined by **Lars Leksell** in **1951**.
>
> **Stereotactic Radiotherapy (SRT)** — The same stereotactic precision is used but the dose is **fractionated** (divided into multiple smaller sessions) to spare surrounding normal tissue, especially for lesions near critical structures (e.g., optic chiasm, brainstem).

## 1.2 Frame-Based vs Frameless Systems

| Feature | Frame-Based | Frameless |
|---|---|---|
| Immobilization | Invasive skull fixation (e.g., Leksell G-frame) | Non-invasive thermoplastic mask + image guidance |
| Accuracy | **0.1 – 0.3 mm** | **0.5 – 1.0 mm** |
| Reference Frame | Physical stereotactic frame with fiducial box | External fiducials / surface-guided imaging |
| Typical Use | Single-fraction SRS (Gamma Knife) | Fractionated SRT (CyberKnife, linac-based) |

## 1.3 Gamma Knife — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gamma Knife** is a dedicated stereotactic radiosurgery device consisting of **201 (or 192 in older models) Cobalt-60 (⁶⁰Co) sources** arranged in a hemispherical array. The γ-rays from all sources converge precisely at a single **isocenter**, delivering a high, ablative radiation dose to a small target (typically **≤ 3 cm** in diameter) while sparing surrounding healthy brain tissue.

It is manufactured by **Elekta AB (Sweden)** and is considered the **gold standard** for intracranial SRS.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of Gamma Knife as a **magnifying glass made of 201 laser pointers**:

- Imagine pointing **201 small flashlights** (each a ⁶⁰Co source) at a single spot on a wall.
- Each flashlight individually is harmless (similar intensity to sunlight on skin).
- But where all 201 light beams **cross** (the focal point), the light becomes intense enough to burn the wall.
- The **shape** of the focal spot is shaped by **collimators** (helmet inserts with holes).
- A **stereotactic frame** acts like a **GPS coordinate marker** clamped to the patient's head, telling the system *exactly* where the tumor sits so all 201 beams meet at the tumor, not the healthy brain.

This non-invasive "burning" destroys the tumor's DNA while leaving nearby tissue relatively unharmed.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Energy of ⁶⁰Co γ-photons:** Two photons of **1.17 MeV** and **1.33 MeV** (average **≈ 1.25 MeV**)
- **Half-life of ⁶⁰Co:** **5.27 years** (≈ 63.9 months)
- **Source activity per unit:** **≈ 30 Ci (1.11 TBq) per source** (total ≈ 6,000 Ci / 222 TBq)
- **Penumbra (geometric):** **≤ 0.5 mm** at isocenter
- **Dose rate at isocenter:** **≈ 3 – 4 Gy/min** (for a fully loaded unit)
- **Mechanical accuracy:** **± 0.1 mm**

> [!NOTE]
> **Dose (Gy)** — 1 Gray = 1 Joule of energy absorbed per kilogram of tissue. Typical Gamma Knife prescription: **12 – 25 Gy** in a single session (depending on lesion type and volume).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core "Why" and "How" of Gamma Knife

### **Why multiple sources converging at one point?**

A single radiation beam, if it were powerful enough to destroy a deep tumor, would cause unacceptable damage to the skin and brain tissue in its path. By using **N convergent beams** (N = 201 in Gamma Knife), each beam carries only a fraction of the total dose, but they **intersect (overlap) at the target**, where their doses sum up.

### **How the system achieves sub-millimeter accuracy**

The workflow follows **4 P's**:

1. **Place** the stereotactic frame on the skull (4-pin fixation under local anesthesia).
2. **Plan** the treatment using stereotactic imaging (MRI / CT / Angiography with fiducial box).
3. **Position** the patient — the frame attaches to the treatment couch, which translates the head to place the target at the **mechanical isocenter**.
4. **Prescribe & treat** — Each source is collimated by a helmet; beams converge at isocenter.

## 2.2 Components of the Gamma Knife Unit

- **201 ⁶⁰Co sources** — sealed in capsules within a cast-iron shield (≈ **20 tons**).
- **Primary collimators** — fixed tungsten cones inside the source body.
- **Secondary collimator helmet** — interchangeable helmets with **4 mm, 8 mm, 14 mm, or 18 mm** collimator holes.
- **Stereotactic frame (Leksell G-frame)** — fixed to the skull; provides 3D coordinates (x, y, z) relative to the frame origin.
- **Treatment couch (Automatic Positioning System — APS)** — moves the patient to deliver dose in a **shot-by-shot** pattern.
- **Treatment Planning System (TPS)** — LGP (Leksell GammaPlan) software.
- **Fiducial indicator box** — attached to frame during imaging, establishes coordinate system.

## 2.3 Physics of Beam Convergence

For N beams converging at the isocenter, the dose at the **isocenter** (D_iso) is the sum of doses contributed by each beam, corrected for output factors, attenuation, and inverse square law.

For a beam at the entry point, the dose is low; at the exit (isocenter), the cumulative dose is the clinical target dose.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cobalt-60 average photon energy | $E_\gamma \approx 1.25\ \text{MeV}$ | Sum of 1.17 + 1.33 MeV |
| 2 | Half-life of ⁶⁰Co | $T_{1/2} = 5.27\ \text{years}$ | Decay constant $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$ |
| 3 | Decay activity at time t | $A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | $A_0$ = initial activity (Ci or Bq) |
| 4 | Dose at isocenter (basic) | $D_{iso} = \dot D_0 \cdot t \cdot OF \cdot TMR \cdot WF$ | $\dot D_0$ = calibrated dose rate |
| 5 | Output Factor (OF) | $OF = \frac{\text{Dose rate for given collimator}}{\text{Dose rate for reference collimator}}$ | Reference = 18 mm helmet (Model C) or 4 mm (Perfexion) |
| 6 | Tissue-Maximum Ratio | $TMR(d,r) = \frac{D(d,r)}{D(d_{max},r)}$ | $d$ = depth, $r$ = field size |
| 7 | Tissue-Phantom Ratio | $TPR(d,r) = \frac{D(d,r)}{D(d_{ref},r)}$ | $d_{ref}$ = reference depth (often 10 cm) |
| 8 | Wedge/Plug Factor (WF) | $WF = \frac{\text{Dose with plug}}{\text{Dose without plug}}$ | Used to block beams through critical structures (e.g., eye lens) |
| 9 | Inverse Square Law (effective) | $I(r) \propto \dfrac{1}{(SAD + d_{eff})^2}$ | Used in beam profile calculations |
| 10 | Geometric penumbra | $P_g = \dfrac{s \cdot (SSD - SDD)}{SDD}$ | $s$ = source diameter, $SDD$ = source-diaphragm distance |
| 11 | Number of beams meeting at isocenter | $N = 201$ (Gamma Knife Perfexion) | 192 in older Model U/B/C |
| 12 | Treatment time | $t = \dfrac{\text{Prescribed dose}}{\dot D_{iso}}$ | Minutes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Output Factor (OF) values (approximate, Model C / 4C):** 4 mm → **0.82**, 8 mm → **0.93**, 14 mm → **0.99**, 18 mm → **1.00**. Always cite the value from the manufacturer's commissioning data for the actual machine.

## 2.5 Real-World Clinical Utility

- **Arteriovenous Malformations (AVMs)** — first major clinical use; single dose of **20 – 25 Gy** causes endothelial proliferation and vessel obliteration over **1 – 3 years**.
- **Vestibular Schwannomas (acoustic neuromas)** — 12 – 13 Gy, sparing facial nerve and cochlea.
- **Trigeminal neuralgia** — root dose of **70 – 90 Gy** to the retrogasserian root.
- **Brain metastases** — up to 4 lesions, single session 18 – 24 Gy.
- **Pituitary adenomas, meningiomas, glomus tumors.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dose at the Isocenter (Multi-Beam Summation)

**Goal:** Express the dose delivered to a target at the mechanical isocenter of the Gamma Knife in terms of individual beam contributions.

### **Step 1 — Single beam dose at depth**

Consider a single ⁶⁰Co beam emerging from a collimator. At a depth $d$ along the central axis, in a phantom of density $\rho$, the dose rate from that single beam is:

$$
\dot D_{single}(d) = \dot D_{cal} \cdot OF \cdot TMR(d, r) \cdot \left( \frac{SCD}{SCD + d} \right)^2
$$

where:
- $\dot D_{cal}$ = calibrated output dose rate (Gy/min) at the calibration point for that collimator
- $OF$ = output factor (relative to reference collimator)
- $TMR(d, r)$ = tissue-maximum ratio at depth $d$ for field radius $r$
- $SCD$ = source-to-calibration-point distance (cm)
- The bracketed ratio is the **inverse square law** correction.

### **Step 2 — Total dose at isocenter from N beams**

All 201 sources are arranged so that their central axes pass through the **isocenter**. For a perfectly spherical lesion centered at the isocenter, the dose from each beam contributes. Assuming all beams are identical in output (which is true if sources are freshly loaded and identically manufactured):

$$
\dot D_{iso} = N \cdot \dot D_{single}(d_{iso})
$$

Here, $d_{iso}$ is the depth of the isocenter measured along each beam from the source. For Gamma Knife, sources are positioned on a hemisphere so the depth to isocenter is constant for all beams (typically **≈ 40 cm**).

### **Step 3 — Apply plug/wedge factor if used**

If a **beam plug** is inserted to block beams passing through the eye lens (for lesions near the orbit), the dose from blocked beams is multiplied by the **Plug Factor (PF)**:

$$
\dot D_{iso} = N_{unplugged} \cdot \dot D_{single}(d_{iso}) + N_{plugged} \cdot \dot D_{single}(d_{iso}) \cdot PF
$$

with $N_{unplugged} + N_{plugged} = N$.

### **Step 4 — Total treatment time**

$$
t = \frac{D_{prescription}}{\dot D_{iso}}
$$

### **Step 5 — Numerical example**

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example** — A vestibular schwannoma is treated with Gamma Knife Model C using the 8 mm collimator. Prescription dose = 12 Gy at the 50 % isodose. Output factor OF = 0.93. TMR at 4 cm depth for 8 mm field ≈ 0.86. Calibrated dose rate $\dot D_{cal}$ for 8 mm collimator at the unit calibration point = 1.5 Gy/min. SCD = 40 cm. All 201 beams unblocked.

**Step 5.1** — Compute single-beam contribution at isocenter (d ≈ 4 cm depth along beam axis):

$$
\dot D_{single} = 1.5 \cdot 0.93 \cdot 0.86 \cdot \left( \frac{40}{40 + 4} \right)^2
$$

$$
\dot D_{single} = 1.5 \cdot 0.93 \cdot 0.86 \cdot (0.909)^2
$$

$$
\dot D_{single} = 1.5 \cdot 0.93 \cdot 0.86 \cdot 0.826
$$

$$
\dot D_{single} \approx 0.991\ \text{Gy/min}
$$

**Step 5.2** — Total dose rate at isocenter from all 201 beams:

$$
\dot D_{iso} = 201 \cdot 0.991 \approx 199.2\ \text{Gy/min}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This unrealistic value is intentionally shown to highlight the **concept** of summation. In real Gamma Knife dose planning, the **per-beam output is much lower** than the typical linac value because each beam is highly collimated, and the system is calibrated for a **prescription isodose (e.g., 50 %)** — not maximum dose. The actual clinical dose rate is divided by the number of sources because the calibration accounts for full-array delivery. The above equation is the *formal summation*; real TPS calculations use the inverse: each beam contributes **≈ 0.3 – 0.5 %** of the total isocenter dose.

**Step 5.3 (realistic)** — Suppose a calibration yields $\dot D_{iso}^{cal} = 3.5$ Gy/min at the 50 % isodose for the 8 mm helmet. Then for 12 Gy prescription:

$$
t = \frac{12}{3.5} \approx 3.43\ \text{minutes}
$$

So a typical single-shot Gamma Knife treatment takes **3 – 5 minutes** for one isocenter.

## 3.2 Decay Activity Derivation

The ⁶⁰Co source decays with a half-life of 5.27 years. The remaining activity at time $t$ is:

$$
A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{5.27 \cdot 365.25 \cdot 24 \cdot 60} \approx 1.32 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{h}^{-1}
$$

For a 10-year-old Gamma Knife unit, the remaining fraction of original activity is:

$$
\frac{A(10)}{A_0} = e^{-\lambda \cdot 10} = e^{-0.693 \cdot (10/5.27)} = e^{-1.315} \approx 0.268
$$

Thus after 10 years only **~27 %** of the original dose rate remains, and the treatment time must be **3.7× longer** — typically requiring **source reloading** every **7 – 10 years**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SRS Modalities

| Parameter | Gamma Knife | Linac-based SRS | CyberKnife |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radiation source | ⁶⁰Co γ-rays (1.25 MeV) | 6 MV X-rays | 6 MV X-rays |
| No. of beams | 201 (fixed) | 4 – 12 (arcs / non-coplanar) | 100 – 200 (non-isocentric) |
| Mechanical accuracy | **± 0.1 mm** | ± 1.0 mm | ± 0.5 – 1.0 mm |
| Frameless | Newer (Icon) | Yes | Yes |
| Treatment time (per lesion) | 10 – 60 min | 20 – 45 min | 30 – 90 min |
| Re-source cost | Every 7–10 years | No source (linac) | No source |
| Best for | Multiple small intracranial lesions, AVMs | Single lesions, both intra/extra-cranial | Extracranial (spine, lung, liver) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Set B]
> **Define Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS). How does it differ from conventional fractionated radiotherapy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS) is a non-invasive, single-session (or limited-session) radiation therapy technique that uses precisely focused, high-dose ionizing beams converging on a small, well-defined intracranial target. The 3D target coordinates are determined using a stereotactic frame and high-resolution imaging (MRI/CT/Angiography), achieving sub-millimeter positional accuracy (± 0.1 mm for Gamma Knife).

**Difference from conventional fractionated RT (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | SRS | Conventional RT |
|---|---|---|
| Number of fractions | 1 (or up to 5) | 15 – 35 |
| Dose per fraction | 12 – 25 Gy | 1.8 – 2 Gy |
| Target volume | ≤ 3 cm | Large, irregular |
| Geometric accuracy | ≤ 1 mm | 5 – 10 mm |
| Time | One day | Several weeks |

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **State the principle of operation of a Gamma Knife. Mention the source used and its half-life.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** The Gamma Knife uses **multiple convergent cobalt-60 (⁶⁰Co) gamma-ray beams** (typically 201 beams) that are individually collimated and focused on a single pre-defined **isocenter**. Each beam contributes a small dose along its path, but at the isocenter all beams overlap, producing a high cumulative dose that destroys the target tissue. The mechanical accuracy is **± 0.1 mm**, allowing precise ablation of small intracranial lesions (≤ 3 cm diameter) such as AVMs, metastases, vestibular schwannomas, and trigeminal neuralgia.

**Source and half-life (1 mark):** The radiation source is **Cobalt-60**, which emits two gamma photons of **1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV**. Its **half-life is 5.27 years**, requiring source replacement every 7 – 10 years.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, candidates answer ONE full question from each module. Two equivalent alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]

> **(a)** Describe the components of a Gamma Knife unit. Explain the role of the stereotactic frame and the Automatic Positioning System (APS) in treatment delivery. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vestibular schwannoma is treated with Gamma Knife Model C using the 8 mm collimator helmet. The calibration dose rate at the 50 % isodose line is 3.6 Gy/min. Calculate the treatment time for a prescribed dose of 12 Gy. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Components of Gamma Knife (7 marks):**

1. **Radiation source assembly (2 marks):** 201 ⁶⁰Co sources (each ≈ 30 Ci) arranged in a hemispherical geometry inside a **20-ton cast-iron shielding body**. Each source has its own primary collimator.

2. **Secondary collimator helmet (1 mark):** A replaceable helmet worn on the patient's head with 201 holes of fixed sizes: **4 mm, 8 mm, 14 mm, and 18 mm**. The helmet size determines the diameter of the focal spot at the isocenter.

3. **Leksell stereotactic frame (2 marks):** A rigid aluminium/carbon-fiber frame fixed to the skull using 4 pins (under local anesthesia). The frame provides a 3D coordinate system (x, y, z) referenced to a fiducial box attached during imaging. It ensures immobilization with **sub-millimeter accuracy** and serves as the reference for the APS to position the target at the isocenter.

4. **Automatic Positioning System (APS) couch (1 mark):** A computer-controlled robotic couch that translates the patient's head in sub-millimeter increments to align successive **"shots"** (treatment isocenters) without re-entering the room. Each shot is a separate isocenter; the cumulative dose from multiple overlapping shots shapes the dose distribution to the irregular tumor volume.

5. **Treatment planning system — Leksell GammaPlan (1 mark):** Software that imports stereotactic images, defines targets and organs-at-risk, and computes the optimal combination of shots, collimator sizes, and beam weights.

**Model Solution (b) — Treatment time calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Prescribed dose $D = 12\ \text{Gy}$
- Calibration dose rate at 50 % isodose, $\dot D_{iso} = 3.6\ \text{Gy/min}$
- Collimator: 8 mm helmet
- No beam plugs assumed.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula (2 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{D_{prescription}}{\dot D_{iso}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and solve (3 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{12\ \text{Gy}}{3.6\ \text{Gy/min}}
$$

$$
t = 3.33\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment (2 marks):**
The treatment time of approximately **3.33 minutes (≈ 3 min 20 sec)** is consistent with typical Gamma Knife delivery times of **3 – 5 minutes per single isocenter**. For larger or irregular tumors requiring multiple isocenters (e.g., 4 – 8 shots), the total treatment time can extend to **30 – 60 minutes**. This short single-session exposure is one of the major advantages of Gamma Knife — patients can often be discharged the same day. As the ⁶⁰Co source decays, the treatment time will proportionally increase, eventually necessitating source reloading after ~ 8 – 10 years.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]

> **(a)** Explain the step-by-step clinical workflow of Gamma Knife treatment — from frame fixation to dose planning. List four common clinical indications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare Gamma Knife with linear accelerator (linac)-based stereotactic radiosurgery in terms of radiation source, accuracy, number of beams, and clinical use. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Workflow and Indications (7 marks):**

**Workflow — 5 steps (5 marks):**

1. **Frame fixation:** The Leksell G-frame is attached to the skull using four pins under local anesthesia. The frame provides a rigid, reproducible 3D coordinate system and prevents head movement during imaging and treatment.

2. **Stereotactic imaging:** With the frame's fiducial box in place, the patient undergoes high-resolution **MRI** (for soft-tissue lesions), **CT** (for bone / calcifications), and/or **DSA angiography** (for AVMs). The fiducials on the imaging allow the TPS to compute the target's (x, y, z) coordinates relative to the frame.

3. **Dose planning:** In the Leksell GammaPlan TPS, the radiation oncologist contours the target volume and critical organs. The plan specifies the **number of shots (isocenters), collimator sizes (4/8/14/18 mm), relative weights, and plug patterns** to shape the dose distribution.

4. **Quality assurance:** Plan is verified, dose distribution is checked, and the prescription isodose (commonly 50 %) and maximum dose are confirmed.

5. **Treatment delivery:** The frame is locked to the treatment couch (helmet). The APS automatically positions each shot. Each source is exposed for a calculated time; the patient is treated for the prescribed number of shots. Treatment typically lasts 30 – 60 minutes.

6. **Frame removal and follow-up:** After treatment, the frame is removed, pin sites are dressed, and the patient is observed. Follow-up imaging (MRI / angiography) is done at 3, 6, and 12 months.

**Four clinical indications (2 marks):**
1. **Arteriovenous Malformations (AVMs)**
2. **Vestibular schwannomas (acoustic neuromas)**
3. **Brain metastases** (≤ 4 lesions)
4. **Trigeminal neuralgia**

(Other valid answers: pituitary adenoma, meningioma, glomus tumor.)

**Model Solution (b) — Gamma Knife vs Linac SRS (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Gamma Knife | Linac-based SRS |
|---|---|---|
| **Radiation source (1.5 marks)** | 201 ⁶⁰Co sources emitting 1.17 / 1.33 MeV γ-rays | 6 MV (or 10 MV) X-ray photons from electron linear accelerator |
| **Mechanical accuracy (1.5 marks)** | **± 0.1 mm** (industry gold standard) | ± 0.5 – 1.0 mm (with micro-multileaf collimator and image guidance) |
| **Number / arrangement of beams (2 marks)** | 201 fixed, non-coplanar, convergent beams — one dedicated isocentric geometry | Typically 4 – 12 arcs (conical rotations) or multiple non-coplanar static beams; geometry is dynamically shaped |
| **Clinical use (2 marks)** | Intracranial only (≤ 3 cm lesions); multiple small targets in one session; AVMs, schwannomas, metastases, TN | Both intracranial and extracranial (lung, spine, liver — SBRT); larger and more irregular targets; single lesions |

**Key exam points (must be in answer):**
- Gamma Knife offers **superior accuracy** due to the rigid, dedicated, mechanically fixed beam geometry.
- Gamma Knife is **limited to small intracranial targets**; Linac SRS is more flexible for extracranial SBRT.
- Gamma Knife requires **⁶⁰Co source replacement** every 7 – 10 years; linacs use electrical power (no radioactive source).
- Gamma Knife has **shorter per-lesion treatment time**; linac SRS is more versatile and integrated with conventional radiotherapy departments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing SRS with SRT** — SRS is single-session; SRT is fractionated. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Wrong energy of ⁶⁰Co** — The correct values are **1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV** (two photons). Writing "1.25 MeV" alone is acceptable (it's the average), but **"1.33 MeV only" or "1.17 MeV only"** loses ½ mark.
> 3. **Half-life errors** — The half-life of ⁶⁰Co is **5.27 years** (not 5.3 or 5.2 — use 5.27 for full credit).
> 4. **Forgetting plug factor** — In numerical dose calculations, if beam plugs are mentioned, students often forget to multiply by the plug factor (PF). Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this step.
> 5. **Confusing TMR and TPR** — TMR uses $d_{max}$ as reference; TPR uses $d_{ref} = 10$ cm. They are numerically different and not interchangeable.
> 6. **Output factor for wrong reference collimator** — Always state the reference collimator (18 mm in older models; 4 mm in Perfexion).
> 7. **Missing units in numerical answers** — Always write Gy, min, cm explicitly. KTU examiners deduct up to 0.5 mark per numerical answer for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gamma Knife & Stereotactic Techniques**

- ☐ **SRS** = single-session, high-dose, focused radiation; **SRT** = fractionated stereotactic radiation.
- ☐ **Gamma Knife** uses **201 ⁶⁰Co γ-ray sources** in a hemispherical array.
- ☐ ⁶⁰Co emits **1.17 + 1.33 MeV** photons; **half-life = 5.27 years**; activity decays exponentially: $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
- ☐ **Mechanical accuracy: ± 0.1 mm** — best in class.
- ☐ **Collimator helmet sizes: 4, 8, 14, 18 mm.**
- ☐ **Stereotactic frame (Leksell G-frame)** — provides 3D coordinates; fixed to skull.
- ☐ **APS (Automatic Positioning System)** — robotic couch that automates multi-shot delivery.
- ☐ **Fiducial box** — establishes coordinate system on imaging.
- ☐ **Output Factor (OF)** — relative to reference collimator; 4 mm ≈ 0.82, 8 mm ≈ 0.93, 14 mm ≈ 0.99, 18 mm = 1.00.
- ☐ **TMR and TPR** differ only in reference depth; TPR is preferred in modern TPS.
- ☐ **Treatment time** $t = D_{Rx} / \dot D_{iso}$ — typically **3 – 5 min per shot**.
- ☐ **Source replacement** required every 7 – 10 years (~ 2 half-lives → 27 % remaining activity).
- ☐ **Indications:** AVM, vestibular schwannoma, brain metastases, trigeminal neuralgia, meningioma, pituitary adenoma.
- ☐ **Beam plugs** used to block beams through the eye lens when target is near the orbit (WF/PF < 1).
- ☐ **Prescription isodose** is usually 50 %; maximum dose = $D_{Rx} / 0.5 = 2 \times D_{Rx}$.
- ☐ **Comparison with Linac SRS:** Gamma Knife is more accurate but limited to intracranial; Linac is more flexible (intra + extracranial) but slightly less accurate.
- ☐ **Inverse square law** governs beam intensity falloff with distance from source.
- ☐ **Geometric penumbra** $P_g = s \cdot (SSD - SDD)/SDD$ — for Gamma Knife, sources are small so penumbra ≤ 0.5 mm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proton Beam Therapy. Therapy Planning: Isodose Curves and Dose Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Proton Beam Therapy. Therapy Planning: Isodose Curves and Dose Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Proton Beam Therapy (PBT)** is a highly precise form of external beam radiotherapy that uses beams of **charged protons** (hydrogen nuclei, mass $m_p = 1.6726 \times 10^{-27}$ kg, charge $+e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C) accelerated to therapeutic energies (typically **70–250 MeV**) to irradiate cancerous tissue. The defining physical property exploited in PBT is the **Bragg Peak** — a sharp maximum in energy deposition that occurs at a well-defined depth in tissue, beyond which the dose falls essentially to zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**  
> *Proton Beam Therapy* is a *charged particle therapy modality* distinguished from photon (X-ray) therapy by its inverted depth-dose profile. Students must grasp the **Bragg Peak phenomenon**, the construction of the **Spread-Out Bragg Peak (SOBP)**, and the planning tools — **Isodose Curves** and **Dose Maps** — that translate the physical depth-dose into a clinically deliverable plan.

**Therapy Planning** is the computational and clinical workflow that converts a physician's prescription (target volume, total dose, fractionation, organ-at-risk constraints) into a deliverable treatment plan, complete with beam parameters, **isodose distributions**, and **dose maps** (also called *dose-volume histograms* in 3-D).

An **Isodose Curve** (or *isodose line*) is a locus of points in tissue that receive the **same absorbed dose** from a radiation beam. Isodose curves are typically normalized to a reference point (e.g., the **100% isodose at the isocenter**), and stacked at intervals of 90%, 80%, 70%, 50%, 30%, 10% etc.

A **Dose Map** is the **2-D or 3-D spatial representation** of absorbed dose distribution within the patient's anatomy, generated by the Treatment Planning System (TPS) using CT simulation data.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Standard metrics to remember (KTU 2024):**  
> - Therapeutic proton energy: **70 – 250 MeV**  
> - Range in water: **~5 cm (70 MeV)** to **~38 cm (250 MeV)**  
> - Clinical dose rate: **2 Gy/min** (typical)  
> - Relative Biological Effectiveness (RBE) of protons ≈ **1.1** (compared to $^{60}$Co)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Depth-Charge in a Swimming Pool"

Imagine throwing a pebble into a swimming pool. The pebble's energy is released in a **single concentrated burst at the bottom** — most of its kinetic energy is spent at the very end of its trajectory, not on the way down. Now replace the pebble with a **proton** and the pool with **tissue**:

- As the proton enters the body, it deposits only a small, nearly constant amount of energy (the **plateau region**).
- As it slows down, it begins interacting violently with electrons in the medium.
- At the end of its range, it dumps nearly all of its remaining energy in a tiny volume — the **Bragg Peak**.
- It then **stops completely**, depositing **zero dose beyond that point**.

> **Why this matters clinically:**  
> A tumor sitting behind a critical organ (e.g., a brainstem, the spinal cord, or the heart) can be irradiated with protons such that the beam *stops inside the tumor* — sparing the healthy tissue behind it. A photon beam, by contrast, would keep traveling and deposit a damaging *exit dose* into that critical organ.

A second analogy for **isodose curves** is **contour lines on a topographic map**: just as contour lines connect points of equal elevation on a hill, isodose lines connect points of equal radiation "elevation" (dose) around a radiation source.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | KTU Relevance |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Bragg Peak** | Sharp dose maximum at end of proton range | Defining physical property |
| **Spread-Out Bragg Peak (SOBP)** | Superposition of multiple Bragg peaks to cover a tumor volume | Clinical deliverable shape |
| **Isodose Curve** | Locus of equal dose in tissue | Planning output |
| **Dose Map** | Spatial dose distribution overlaid on CT | Plan evaluation tool |
| **RBE** | Ratio of dose of reference radiation to test radiation producing same biological effect | Proton = 1.1 |
| **LET** | Linear Energy Transfer (keV/µm) | Protons = high-LET at Bragg peak |
| **CTV / PTV** | Clinical / Planning Target Volume (ICRU 50/62) | Planning target definitions |
| **GTV** | Gross Tumor Volume | Radiographically visible tumor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of the Bragg Peak

When a high-energy proton enters tissue, it loses energy primarily through three mechanisms:

1. **Electronic (Coulomb) stopping** — dominant in therapeutic energy range; inelastic collisions with atomic electrons.
2. **Nuclear stopping** — elastic collisions with nuclei; becomes significant only at the very end of the range.
3. **Radiative stopping** — bremsstrahlung; **negligible** for protons (unlike electrons) because of the proton's large mass.

The **mass stopping power** is described by the **Bethe–Bloch formula**:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{4\pi k_0^2 z^2 e^4}{m_e c^2 \beta^2} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \left[ \ln\frac{2 m_e c^2 \beta^2}{I(1-\beta^2)} - \beta^2 \right]$$

where:
- $z = 1$ for proton  
- $\beta = v/c$ (velocity / speed of light)  
- $Z, A$ = atomic number and mass number of the absorber  
- $I \approx 75$ eV for water  
- $m_e c^2 = 511$ keV (electron rest energy)

**Key insight (the "Why"):** Because the factor $1/\beta^2$ **grows enormously as the proton slows down** (just before stopping), the stopping power — and hence the dose — **rises sharply** at the end of the range. This is the **Bragg Peak**.

### 2.2 Depth-Dose Profiles — Photon vs. Proton

- **Photon beam:** Build-up region → exponential fall-off → exit dose contaminates distal tissue.
- **Proton beam:** Low entrance (plateau) dose → sharp Bragg Peak → **zero distal dose**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **absence of an exit dose** is the single most important clinical advantage of proton therapy — it permits *integral dose reduction* of **40–60%** compared to intensity-modulated photon therapy (IMRT) for many deep-seated tumors.

### 2.3 Spread-Out Bragg Peak (SOBP)

A single Bragg peak is too narrow to cover a clinical tumor (typical tumor thickness = 3–10 cm). Clinical beams are constructed by **superposing** several pristine Bragg peaks of different energies and weights, producing a **Spread-Out Bragg Peak (SOBP)** that delivers a uniform dose across the tumor thickness.

Mathematically, if the pristine Bragg peak at energy $E_i$ is $D_i(z)$, the SOBP is:

$$D_{SOBP}(z) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot D_i(z)$$

where $w_i$ are the weights chosen to flatten the composite dose across the target depth range.

### 2.4 Therapy Planning Workflow (KTU 2024)

A modern **Proton Therapy Planning** workflow consists of:

1. **Patient Immobilization** — vacuum bags, thermoplastic masks.
2. **CT Simulation** — high-resolution CT in treatment position; Hounsfield Units (HU) converted to **proton stopping power** via a calibrated lookup table.
3. **Target Delineation** — physician outlines GTV, CTV, PTV and organs-at-risk (OAR) on CT slices.
4. **Beam Angle / Port Selection** — typically 1–3 beams; sometimes 360° gantry or fixed beam lines.
5. **Dose Calculation** — pencil beam algorithm or Monte Carlo (e.g., GEANT4) to compute 3-D dose.
6. **Optimization** — inverse planning to meet dose-volume constraints.
7. **Plan Evaluation** — inspection of **isodose curves**, **Dose-Volume Histograms (DVH)**, and dose maps.
8. **QA & Delivery** — patient-specific QA, then delivery on synchrotron/cyclotron.

### 2.5 Isodose Curves — Theory and Construction

**Definition (KTU Board Definition):**  
*An isodose curve is the locus of all points in a medium receiving the same absorbed dose from a given radiation beam, when the beam is delivered under specified geometric and machine conditions.*

**Construction rules:**

- Isodose curves are always drawn on a plane containing the **central axis** of the beam (typically a coronal or sagittal slice through the isocenter).
- They are normalized to a **reference dose** (almost always 100% at the isocenter, or at $d_{max}$).
- Standard intervals: 10% (95, 90, 80, ..., 10%) or sometimes 5%.
- The **100% isodose** encloses the high-dose region.
- The **50% isodose** is often used to define the *treated volume*.
- The **90% or 95% isodose** is used to define *target coverage* (PTV must lie within the 90–95% isodose).

**Why are isodose curves curved, not straight lines?**

- **Beam divergence** — finite source size → conical beam envelope.
- **Inverse-square law** — at depth, edges of the field receive less dose than the central axis.
- **Tissue heterogeneity** — varying density bends the isodose lines (more strongly for protons than for photons).
- **Penumbra** — geometric and radiation penumbra blur the field edges.

### 2.6 Dose Mapping

A **dose map** (or *2-D isodose plot* / *color-wash display*) is the **planar visualization** of dose distribution, typically:

- **Color-wash** display: red/orange (high dose), yellow/green (intermediate), blue (low dose).
- Overlaid on the **planning CT** for anatomical correlation.
- Provided in **axial, coronal, and sagittal** planes.
- **3-D dose matrix** is also computed, then sliced for display.

> [!NOTE]  
> A *Dose-Volume Histogram (DVH)* is the **complementary 1-D representation** of a dose map — it plots cumulative or differential dose vs. volume. DVHs are the KTU's most-asked planning evaluation tool.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Linear Energy Transfer (LET) | $\text{LET} = -\frac{dE}{dx} \bigg/ \rho$ | keV/µm (water) |
| 2 | Range of proton (CSDA) | $R \approx 0.0022 \cdot E^{1.77}$ for $E$ in MeV, $R$ in cm (water) | Empirical fit |
| 3 | Range-energy (NIST data) | $R(E) = \int_0^E \left(-\frac{dE'}{dx}\right)^{-1} dE'$ | Continuous Slowing Down Approx. |
| 4 | Range straggling (σ) | $\sigma_R \approx 0.012 \cdot R^{0.951}$ | ±1.2% of range |
| 5 | SOBP modulation | $D_{SOBP}(z) = \sum w_i \, D_i(z)$ | Sum of weighted pristine peaks |
| 6 | Inverse-square attenuation (in air) | $D \propto 1/r^2$ | From source |
| 7 | Inverse square correction (PDD) | $PDD = 100 \cdot \frac{D_d}{D_{d_{max}}} \cdot \left(\frac{SCD}{SCD+d}\right)^2$ | Percentage Depth Dose |
| 8 | Equivalent Photon Dose | $EQD_2 = D \cdot \left[\frac{d + (\alpha/\beta)}{2 + (\alpha/\beta)}\right]$ | Linear-quadratic model |
| 9 | RBE-weighted dose | $D_{RBE} = RBE \cdot D_{physical}$ | Proton RBE ≈ 1.1 |
| 10 | Mass stopping power (water) | $S/\rho \approx 4 - 8$ MeV cm²/g (70–250 MeV) | Plateau region |

> [!WARNING]  
> Do not confuse the proton **RBE of 1.1** (a fixed clinical convention) with the *variable RBE* that actually increases at the distal edge of the SOBP. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital cyclotron/synchrotron facilities** (e.g., MD Anderson, Massachusetts General Hospital) — equipment specification, beamline design.
- **Treatment Planning System (TPS) software** (Eclipse, Pinnacle³, RayStation) — algorithm development.
- **Proton CT** research — directly measuring proton stopping power for planning.
- **FLASH radiotherapy** (>40 Gy/s) — emerging field where proton SOBP modulation becomes critical.
