# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optical Density Function

Starting from the fundamental definition of optical density, we relate it to the physical mass of silver deposited per unit area of the film.

**Starting Premise:** The Beer–Lambert law states that the transmittance $T$ of a uniform absorbing layer is exponentially related to the concentration of the absorber and the path length:

$$T = \frac{I_t}{I_0} = e^{-\alpha \cdot x}$$

where $\alpha$ is the linear absorption coefficient of the developed silver grains (in cm⁻¹) and $x$ is the effective optical path length through the developed silver (in cm).

**Step 1 — Take the natural logarithm to obtain absorbance:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) = \alpha \cdot x$$

**Step 2 — Convert natural log to base-10 logarithm using the identity $\log_{10} y = \frac{\ln y}{\ln 10}$:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) = \frac{\alpha \cdot x}{\ln 10}$$

**Step 3 — Define the proportionality constant. Let $k = \dfrac{\alpha}{\ln 10}$:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) = k \cdot x$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the left-hand side is the optical density $D$:**

$$D = k \cdot x$$

**Step 5 — Now relate $x$ (effective path through silver) to the mass of silver per unit area, $m$, and the density of metallic silver, $\rho_{Ag}$:**

$$x = \frac{m}{\rho_{Ag}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the expression for $D$:**

$$D = \frac{k \cdot m}{\rho_{Ag}} = K \cdot m$$

where $K = \dfrac{k}{\rho_{Ag}}$ is a film-specific proportionality constant.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{D = K \cdot m = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right)}$$

This is the **fundamental linearity relation** between optical density and mass of developed silver per unit area. Since the mass of silver deposited is (within the straight-line region) proportional to the absorbed dose, we obtain:

$$D = a + b \cdot D_{\text{absorbed}}$$

This is the **sensitometric equation** used in clinical film dosimetry, where $a$ is the base + fog density and $b$ is the film sensitivity (related to γ).

## 3.2 Derivation of Energy-Dependence Correction Factor

The film emulsion has an effective atomic number ($Z_{eff} \approx 33\text{–}47$) much higher than water ($Z_{eff} = 7.42$). At low photon energies, the photoelectric cross-section scales approximately as $Z^3$ (or $Z^4$ for the mass attenuation), so the film absorbs more energy per unit dose-in-water than water itself.

**Starting Premise:** The ratio of mass energy absorption coefficients determines how much energy is deposited in the film compared to water for the same incident fluence:

$$\frac{D_{\text{film}}}{D_{\text{water}}} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{film}}}{\left(\dfrac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{water}}}$$

**Step 1 — Define the energy-dependent correction factor $f$:**

$$f = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{film}}}{\left(\dfrac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{water}}}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to obtain the water-equivalent dose:**

$$D_{\text{water}} = \frac{D_{\text{film}}}{f}$$

**Step 3 — Express $f$ numerically for a typical orthovoltage beam (e.g., 200 kVp, HVL = 1 mm Cu):**

For 200 kVp X-rays, using tabulated values from NIST:

$$\left(\frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{film}} \approx 0.1030 \ \frac{\text{cm}^2}{\text{g}}, \quad \left(\frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{water}} \approx 0.0267 \ \frac{\text{cm}^2}{\text{g}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $f$:**

$$f = \frac{0.1030}{0.0267} \approx 3.86$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The film over-responds by a factor of 3.86 relative to water at 200 kVp. If 1 Gy is delivered to water, the film will read the equivalent of 3.86 Gy unless corrected.

For a megavoltage beam (e.g., 6 MV):

$$\left(\frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{film}} \approx 0.0280 \ \frac{\text{cm}^2}{\text{g}}, \quad \left(\frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{water}} \approx 0.0272 \ \frac{\text{cm}^2}{\text{g}}$$

$$f = \frac{0.0280}{0.0272} \approx 1.03$$

This shows why **megavoltage film dosimetry requires negligible correction** — a key reason film dosimetry is preferred at linac energies.

## 3.3 Gamma (γ) of the Film — Derivation from H&D Curve

**Step 1 — Select two points on the linear portion of the H&D curve:**

$$(\log_{10} E_1, D_1) \quad \text{and} \quad (\log_{10} E_2, D_2)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the slope (gamma):**

$$\gamma = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{\log_{10} E_2 - \log_{10} E_1} = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta(\log_{10} E)}$$

**Step 3 — For a typical therapy film, if $\Delta D = 1.5$ over a $\Delta(\log_{10} E) = 1.0$ exposure decade:**

$$\gamma = \frac{1.5}{1.0} = 1.5$$

**Step 4 — Relate γ to dose resolution. If the densitometer has a resolution of $\Delta D_{\min} = 0.01$ OD:**

$$\Delta(\log_{10} E)_{\min} = \frac{\Delta D_{\min}}{\gamma} = \frac{0.01}{1.5} = 0.00667$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dose resolution. If the calibration curve slope is $b = 1.0$ OD per Gy:**

$$\Delta D_{\text{absorbed, min}} = \frac{\Delta D_{\min}}{b} = 0.01 \text{ Gy} = 1 \text{ cGy}$$

This quantifies the **minimum detectable dose difference** achievable with that film–densitometer combination.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Radiographic vs. Radiochromic Film

| Parameter | Radiographic (AgBr) Film | Radiochromic (Gafchromic) Film |
|---|---|---|
| **Active component** | Silver halide (AgBr) crystals | Polydiacetylene / leuco-dye monomers |
| **Processing** | Wet chemical (developer + fixer) | **Self-developing** (no chemicals) |
| **Time to develop** | Several minutes | Minutes to hours (continues 24 h) |
| **Spatial resolution** | $\sim 25 \text{ lp/mm}$ | $\sim 25 \text{ to } 100 \text{ lp/mm}$ |
| **Energy dependence** | Strong below 200 keV | Much weaker (near tissue-equivalent) |
| **$Z_{eff}$** | $\approx 33\text{–}47$ | $\approx 6.0\text{–}7.0$ (close to water) |
| **Useful dose range** | $0.1 \text{ to } 10 \text{ Gy}$ | $0.1 \text{ Gy to } 100 \text{ Gy}$ |
| **Sensitivity to light** | **Very high** — must load in dark | **None** — light-insensitive |
| **Storage** | Refrigerated, expires | Room temperature, longer shelf life |
| **Cost per sheet** | Low | High |
| **Recommended use** | Routine QA, orthovoltage | SRS/SBRT, IMRT QA, high-dose brachy |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Film Architecture — Cross-Sectional Layer Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the layered architecture of a typical double-emulsion radiographic film used in radiotherapy dosimetry.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layerA["PROTECTIVE SUPERCOAT - Anti-abrasion gelatin layer - Thickness approx 0.001 mm"]
    layerB["TOP EMULSION LAYER - Silver halide crystals AgBr in gelatin - Thickness approx 0.005 to 0.010 mm"]
    layerC["SUBBING LAYER - Adhesion promoter between emulsion and base"]
    layerD["FILM BASE - Polyester PET support - Thickness approx 0.180 to 0.200 mm - Provides mechanical rigidity"]
    layerE["SUBBING LAYER - Adhesion promoter on opposite side"]
    layerF["BOTTOM EMULSION LAYER - Silver halide crystals AgBr in gelatin - Thickness approx 0.005 to 0.010 mm"]
    layerG["PROTECTIVE SUPERCOAT - Anti-abrasion gelatin layer - Thickness approx 0.001 mm"]

    layerA --> layerB
    layerB --> layerC
    layerC --> layerD
    layerD --> layerE
    layerE --> layerF
    layerF --> layerG
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **base** is approximately **100 times thicker** than each emulsion layer. For megavoltage film dosimetry, films are sometimes placed **edge-on** (perpendicular to the beam axis) inside a phantom to minimize attenuation perturbations along the beam path.

## 4.2 Film Processing Workflow — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[UNEXPOSED FILM] --> B[RADIATION EXPOSURE IN PHANTOM]
    B --> C[LATENT IMAGE FORMED IN AgBr CRYSTALS]
    C --> D[DEVELOPER BATH - Hydroquinone + Phenidone - Alkaline pH 9.5 to 10.5 - 20 to 25 C for 3 to 5 minutes]
    D --> E[STOP BATH - Acetic acid solution - Halts development]
    E --> F[FIXER BATH - Sodium thiosulfate hypo - Removes unexposed AgBr - 5 to 10 minutes]
    F --> G[WASHING - Removes residual fixer - 10 to 20 minutes in running water]
    G --> H[DRYING - Controlled temperature air drying]
    H --> I[DEVELOPED FILM WITH 2D OD MAP]
    I --> J[DENSITOMETER OR FLATBED SCANNER]
    J --> K[OD MAP TO DOSE CONVERSION VIA SENSITOMETRIC CURVE]
    K --> L[2D ISODOSE DISTRIBUTION]
```

## 4.3 H&D Characteristic Curve — Functional Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[EXPOSURE INCREASING ON X-AXIS] --> B1[REGION 1 TOE - Low OD, slow rise]
    B1 --> C1[REGION 2 STRAIGHT LINE - Constant slope gamma - WORKING REGION]
    C1 --> D1[REGION 3 SHOULDER - Saturation, slope decreases]
    D1 --> E1[REGION 4 SOLARIZATION - OD decreases with further exposure]

    subgraph PROPS["KEY PROPERTIES EXTRACTED"]
        p1[Speed: position of curve on x-axis]
        p2[Contrast gamma: slope of straight line]
        p3[Latitude: width of useful exposure range]
        p4[Base plus fog: OD at zero exposure]
    end

    C1 -. measured by .-> p1
    C1 -. measured by .-> p2
    B1 & D1 -. defines .-> p3
    A1 -. intercept at .-> p4
```

## 4.4 Film Dosimetry Quality Assurance Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STEP 1 CALIBRATION] --> S2[STEP 2 EXPOSURE]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3 PROCESSING]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4 SCANNING]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5 CONVERSION]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6 ANALYSIS]

    S1["STEP 1: CALIBRATION - Expose film pieces to known doses 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10 Gy - Generate H and D curve"]

    S2["STEP 2: PATIENT OR PHANTOM EXPOSURE - Place film between phantom slabs - Orient perpendicular or parallel to beam axis"]

    S3["STEP 3: CHEMICAL PROCESSING - Strict temperature and time control - Use automatic processor for reproducibility"]

    S4["STEP 4: DENSITOMETRY - Flatbed scanner at 72 to 150 dpi - Red channel for Gafchromic - RGB analysis for AgBr films"]

    S5["STEP 5: OD TO DOSE MAPPING - Apply sensitometric curve - Subtract base plus fog - Apply energy correction if needed"]

    S6["STEP 6: COMPARISON WITH TPS - Isodose overlay - Gamma index analysis passing criteria 3 percent 3 mm"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define optical density and explain its significance in film dosimetry. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**Model Answer:**

Optical density (OD) is the logarithmic measure of light attenuation by a developed film. It is defined as:

$$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right)$$

where $I_0$ is the intensity of light incident on the film and $I_t$ is the intensity of light transmitted through it.

**Significance in film dosimetry:**
1. It quantifies the **darkness of the developed film** at each point, which is directly proportional to the radiation dose received at that location.
2. It allows **quantitative conversion** of the visible 2D pattern into absorbed dose values using a pre-calibrated sensitometric curve.
3. It provides a **permanent, archival record** of the dose distribution that can be re-analyzed later.
4. It enables **comparison with TPS-calculated isodose distributions** for patient-specific QA using gamma index evaluation.

A film with OD = 1.0 transmits only 10% of incident light, and OD = 2.0 transmits only 1% — making OD a sensitive measure of dose variation.

---

### Question 2: What is the H&D curve? Name the regions and their clinical significance. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]

**Model Answer:**

The **Hurter–Driffield (H&D) characteristic curve** is a plot of **optical density (OD)** of a developed film on the y-axis against the **logarithm of exposure (log E)** on the x-axis. It characterizes the response of a photographic emulsion to radiation.

**Four Regions:**

1. **Toe (Foot):** Low-exposure region with a small, gradually increasing slope. Few grains are developable. **Not used in dosimetry.**

2. **Straight Line (Linear Region):** Region of constant maximum slope γ. Optical density is linearly proportional to log exposure. **This is the primary working region for dosimetry** because small dose changes produce predictable OD changes.

3. **Shoulder:** Saturation region where most grains are developed. The slope decreases. **Avoided in dosimetry** because the response becomes nonlinear.

4. **Solarization (Reversal):** At very high exposures, the OD paradoxically decreases due to the destruction of the latent image. **Not used clinically.**

**Key properties extracted from the H&D curve:** film **speed** (position on x-axis), **contrast γ** (slope of the straight line), **latitude** (exposure range of the linear region), and **base + fog** (OD at zero exposure).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a)** Describe the construction of a radiographic film used in radiotherapy dosimetry, with a neat diagram. Discuss the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of latent image formation using the Gurney–Mott mechanism. Discuss the role of sensitivity specks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Construction of a Radiographic Film:**

A radiographic film used in radiotherapy consists of **seven distinct layers**, sandwiched symmetrically around a central polyester base. From top to bottom, the layers are:

**1. Protective Supercoat (Top and Bottom):**
A thin ($\approx 1\,\mu$m) anti-abrasion gelatin layer that protects the underlying emulsion from mechanical damage, finger prints, and static charge. It contains matting agents to prevent film sheets from sticking together.

**2. Top Emulsion Layer:**
The active radiation-sensitive layer, typically **5 to 10 μm thick**, containing **silver bromide (AgBr) microcrystals** (with possibly a small percentage of AgI) suspended in a gelatin matrix. The gelatin holds the crystals uniformly and allows the chemical developer to penetrate. For radiotherapy dosimetry, the crystals are **fine-grained (0.2 to 1.0 μm diameter)** to maximize spatial resolution.

**3. Subbing Layer (Top and Bottom):**
A thin adhesion-promoting layer (typically a terpolymer of vinyl chloride, vinyl acetate, and maleic anhydride) that bonds the emulsion firmly to the base. Without it, the emulsion would delaminate during processing.

**4. Film Base:**
A **180 to 200 μm thick** sheet of **polyethylene terephthalate (PET, "Mylar" or "Estar")** that provides mechanical rigidity, dimensional stability, and near-transparency. PET is chosen because it has low water absorption, is chemically inert, and remains dimensionally stable during wet processing.

**5. Bottom Emulsion Layer:**
Mirror image of the top emulsion layer. The use of **double emulsion** doubles the film's sensitivity and makes the response more uniform.

**6. Bottom Protective Supercoat:**
Identical to the top supercoat.

**Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete layered architecture.

**Significance of each layer for dosimetry:**
- The **base thickness** must be uniform and well-characterized so that it can be subtracted or compensated for in dose measurements (especially when the film is placed parallel to the beam).
- The **emulsion thickness** determines the film's sensitivity and the magnitude of the optical density produced per unit dose.
- The **gelatin matrix** must be chemically pure to avoid artifacts.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**The Gurney–Mott Mechanism of Latent Image Formation:**

The Gurney–Mott theory (1938) describes the formation of a developable **latent image** as a two-step process: (1) electronic and (2) ionic.

**Step 1 — Electronic Stage:**
When a photon or charged particle deposits energy in or near an AgBr crystal, it ejects an electron from a **bromide ion** in the crystal lattice:

$$\text{Br}^- + h\nu \rightarrow \text{Br} + e^-$$

This free electron migrates through the conduction band of the crystal until it becomes trapped at a **sensitivity speck** — a small defect site on the crystal surface containing Ag₂S or other impurity.

**Step 2 — Ionic Stage:**
The trapped electron creates a **negative charge** at the sensitivity speck. This attracts a mobile **silver ion (Ag⁺)** from an interstitial site in the crystal lattice:

$$\text{Ag}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}^0$$

The silver ion is neutralized at the speck, depositing a **metallic silver atom**. This process repeats, and once **3 to 5 silver atoms** have accumulated at the speck, the grain becomes a "developable" latent image — meaning the chemical developer can now reduce the entire grain to metallic silver.

**Role of Sensitivity Specks:**
- They act as **electron traps**, providing localized sites where the electronic and ionic stages can combine.
- Without sensitivity specks, the latent image would not form efficiently, and film sensitivity would drop dramatically.
- They are formed during **chemical sensitization** (ripening) of the emulsion, when small amounts of sulfur- or gold-containing compounds are added during manufacture.
- The size and distribution of sensitivity specks determine the **speed, contrast, and granularity** of the film.

**After development:**

$$\text{Developable grain} + \text{Reducing agent} \rightarrow \text{Entire grain} \rightarrow \text{metallic Ag}^0 \text{ (black)}$$

A grain with fewer than 3 silver atoms is **not developable** and is dissolved by the fixer. The number of developable grains per unit area is what determines the optical density at that location — and therefore the dose.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Draw and explain the H&D characteristic curve of a radiographic film. Define gamma, latitude, and speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the advantages, limitations, and clinical applications of radiochromic film dosimetry. Compare it with conventional radiographic film. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**The H&D Characteristic Curve:**

The Hurter–Driffield (H&D) curve is a graph of **optical density (D)** on the y-axis versus **logarithm of exposure (log E)** on the x-axis. It is generated by exposing film strips to a series of known exposures, developing them under controlled conditions, and measuring the resulting OD.

**Regions of the H&D Curve (with reference to Section 4.3):**

1. **Base + Fog Region:** At zero exposure, the film has a small but nonzero OD (typically 0.05 to 0.20) due to inadvertent exposure and the natural density of the base.

2. **Toe Region:** OD rises slowly at low exposures as scattered sensitivity specks begin to form latent images.

3. **Straight-Line Region (Linear):** The slope is constant and maximum. This is the **working region** for dosimetry, where:
$$D = a + b \cdot \log_{10}(E)$$
The slope $b$ of this region is the film **contrast (γ)**.

4. **Shoulder Region:** OD plateaus as nearly all grains become developable.

5. **Solarization Region:** At very high exposures, the OD paradoxically decreases as radiation destroys the existing latent image centers.

**Key Film Parameters:**

- **Gamma (γ) — Contrast:**
$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta(\log_{10} E)} \bigg|_{\text{linear region}}$$
A high γ (e.g., 3 to 4 for therapy films) means excellent dose discrimination. A low γ (e.g., 1) means wide latitude but poor discrimination.

- **Latitude:**
The range of exposure (in log E units) over which the film produces diagnostically or dosimetrically useful densities. High-contrast films have narrow latitude; low-contrast films have wide latitude.

- **Speed:**
The position of the curve on the exposure axis. A fast film requires less exposure to reach a given OD. Speed is often defined as the reciprocal of the exposure required to produce an OD of 1.0 above base + fog.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Radiochromic Film Dosimetry — Detailed Discussion:**

**Principle:**
Radiochromic film (e.g., Gafchromic EBT, EBT3, EBT4, HD-V2) consists of a **thin active layer of radiation-sensitive monomers** (typically **polydiacetylene** or a **lithium salt of pentacosa-10,12-diynoic acid**) sandwiched between polyester layers. Upon radiation exposure, the monomers undergo **solid-state polymerization**, forming a blue-colored polymer. The degree of polymerization — and hence the optical density, particularly in the red channel — is proportional to the absorbed dose.

**Key Reaction:**
$$\text{Monomer} + \text{Radiation} \rightarrow \text{Polymer (blue color)}$$

**Advantages of Radiochromic Film:**

1. **Self-developing:** No darkroom, chemicals, or fixer required. The image appears within minutes to hours and stabilizes in about 24 hours.
2. **Near tissue equivalence:** Effective atomic number $Z_{eff} \approx 6.0$ to $7.0$, very close to water ($Z_{eff} = 7.42$) and soft tissue. This gives a much **weaker energy dependence** than AgBr films.
3. **High spatial resolution:** Up to 100 lp/mm — superior to radiographic film.
4. **Insensitive to ambient light:** Can be handled in normal room light, simplifying workflow.
5. **Wide dynamic range:** From $\approx 0.1$ Gy to $\approx 100$ Gy, depending on the product — ideal for both low-dose IMRT QA and high-dose SRS/SBRT verification.
6. **No processing artifacts:** Eliminates variability due to developer temperature, time, and chemistry.

**Limitations of Radiochromic Film:**

1. **High cost:** Per-sheet cost is several times that of radiographic film.
2. **Non-uniformity:** Manufacturing variations require **batch-specific calibration**.
3. **Polarization sensitivity:** Some radiochromic films (EBT2) are sensitive to the polarization state of the scanner light. Modern EBT3 and EBT4 largely overcome this.
4. **Slower response:** Full color development takes 24 hours for accurate dosimetry.
5. **Active layer thickness:** Only 5 to 30 μm, so the active volume is small and the film is very thin — placing it edge-on is often required to avoid orientation issues.

**Clinical Applications:**

- **SRS and SBRT QA:** Sub-millimeter accuracy in high-gradient regions.
- **IMRT/VMAT patient-specific QA:** 2D planar dose verification with gamma analysis.
- **Surface dose measurements:** In vivo-like dosimetry for total skin electron therapy (TSET).
- **Brachytherapy:** Especially high-dose-rate (HDR) source verification.
- **Small-field dosimetry:** Where ion chambers are too large.

**Comparison Table (Radiochromic vs. Radiographic Film):**

Refer to the comparison matrix in **Section 3.4** of these notes for the complete side-by-side parameters.

**Summary Verdict:**
Radiochromic film is the **modern dosimetry standard** for high-precision 2D work, while conventional radiographic film remains a **cost-effective option** for routine, lower-precision QA — particularly at megavoltage energies where the energy correction for AgBr is minimal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "speed" with "gamma":** Speed is the *position* of the H&D curve on the x-axis; gamma is the *slope*. Students often interchange these.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract base + fog:** Net optical density $D_{\text{net}} = D_{\text{exp}} - D_{\text{base+fog}}$ — failing to do this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring energy dependence at low kV:** The high $Z_{eff}$ of AgBr causes a 3 to 4 times over-response at 200 kVp. Always state this explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up optical density with absorbance/transmittance:** $D = \log_{10}(I_0/I_t)$, not $I_0/I_t$ itself.
> 5. **Not mentioning Gurney–Mott in latent image questions:** A 2-mark question on latent image formation that omits the Gurney–Mott mechanism is incomplete.
> 6. **In radiochromic film answers, omitting the polymerization reaction** — a frequent omission.
> 7. **Confusing latitude with dynamic range:** Latitude refers to the linear region width; dynamic range is the total usable dose range.
> 8. **Calculator slip-ups in OD calculations:** If $I_0/I_t = 100$, OD = 2.0, not 100.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist

- ✅ **Optical Density:** $D = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I_t}\right)$ — dimensionless; OD = 1 means 10% transmittance.
- ✅ **H&D Curve regions:** Toe → Straight line (γ, working region) → Shoulder → Solarization.
- ✅ **Gamma (γ):** Slope of the linear region — defines film contrast.
- ✅ **Speed:** Position of the H&D curve on the log E axis.
- ✅ **Latitude:** Width of the linear (useful) exposure range.
- ✅ **Base + Fog:** OD at zero exposure — must be subtracted.
- ✅ **Film structure (7 layers):** Supercoat – Emulsion – Subbing – Base – Subbing – Emulsion – Supercoat.
- ✅ **Base material:** Polyester (PET), ~0.2 mm thick.
- ✅ **Emulsion:** AgBr crystals in gelatin, 0.2 to 1.0 μm grain size for therapy films.
- ✅ **Gurney–Mott mechanism:** Electronic stage (e⁻ trap at sensitivity speck) + Ionic stage (Ag⁺ → Ag⁰).
- ✅ **Sensitivity specks:** Trap sites (Ag₂S) where latent image forms.
- ✅ **Latent image requires 3 to 5 Ag⁰ atoms** at a speck to become developable.
- ✅ **Developer chemistry:** Hydroquinone + phenidone in alkaline solution; pH 9.5–10.5.
- ✅ **Fixer:** Sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂S₂O₃) — dissolves unexposed AgBr.
- ✅ **Developer + Stop bath + Fixer + Wash + Dry** — full processing cycle.
- ✅ **Energy dependence of AgBr film:** Strong below 200 keV (photoelectric effect, $Z^3$–$Z^4$ scaling); minimal above 1 MeV.
- ✅ **Correction factor:** $f = \dfrac{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{\text{film}}}{(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{\text{water}}}$; $D_{\text{water}} = D_{\text{film}} / f$.
- ✅ **Radiochromic film:** Self-developing, polydiacetylene monomer → blue polymer, $Z_{eff} \approx 6$–7, near water equivalent.
- ✅ **Radiochromic dynamic range:** 0.1 Gy to 100+ Gy depending on variant (EBT3, EBT4, HD-V2).
- ✅ **Radiochromic read-out:** Red channel (≈ 635 nm) of a flatbed scanner.
- ✅ **High-resolution application:** SRS, SBRT, IMRT QA, brachytherapy source mapping, electron cutout verification.
- ✅ **Spatial resolution:** Up to 25 lp/mm (AgBr), up to 100 lp/mm (radiochromic).
- ✅ **Film orientation in phantom:** Edge-on orientation minimizes beam perturbation for AgBr films.
- ✅ **Calibration step:** Expose film pieces to known doses (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10 Gy) and build the sensitometric curve.
- ✅ **Gamma index analysis:** Standard comparison metric between film and TPS dose (3%/3 mm typical clinical passing criteria).
- ✅ **Personnel monitoring films:** Use filtered badge film to estimate Hp(10), Hp(0.07).
- ✅ **Storage:** Radiographic film stored in lead-lined, refrigerated containers; radiochromic film at room temperature.
- ✅ **Why AgBr works in megavoltage range:** Compton effect dominates → film response ≈ water response → correction factor $f \approx 1$.

---

**End of Module 4 — Film Dosimetry Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PCEBT602*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoluminescent and Optically Stimulated Luminescence Dosimetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Thermoluminescent and Optically Stimulated Luminescence Dosimetry ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Thermoluminescent Dosimetry (TLD) **Formal Definition:** Thermoluminescent Dosimetry (TLD) is a passive solid-state radiation dosimetry technique that utilizes the property of certain crystalline materials (phosphors) to trap and store energy from ionizing radiation in metastable lattice defect states. When the phosphor is subsequently heated in a controlled manner, the trapped charge carriers are thermally released and recombine at luminescence centers, emitting photons in the visible spectrum. The intensity of the emitted light is directly proportional to the absorbed radiation dose. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TLD and OSLD are the two most clinically relevant *passive* dosimetry systems used in radiotherapy, diagnostic radiology, and personnel monitoring. Both are based on the principle of radiation-induced trapping of charge carriers in insulator/semiconductor crystals. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Elevator Bank" Model:** Imagine a crystal as a multi-story building (the lattice) with an elevator (an electron) on the ground floor. When ionizing radiation strikes, the electron is violently kicked up to several higher floors (trapping centers/defects) and gets stuck. The electron cannot come down by itself (metastable trap). Now, when we gently heat the building, the elevator wires expand and one by one, the stuck electrons "fall back" to the ground floor. As each electron falls, it flashes a light at the ground floor (recombination center). The total number of flashes during the heating process is exactly equal to the number of electrons that were kicked up by radiation. Counting these flashes tells us exactly how much radiation was absorbed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **TLD is a passive integrating dosimeter** — it does not provide real-time readings. The dose information is "stored" in the crystal lattice and must be retrieved later by controlled heating (the readout process). ### 1.2 Optically Stimulated Luminescence Dosimetry (OSLD) **Formal Definition:** Optically Stimulated Luminescence Dosimetry (OSLD) is a closely related passive dosimetric technique in which the trapped charge carriers in the dosimeter phosphor (typically **Al₂O₃:C**) are released not by thermal stimulation (heating) but by optical stimulation (exposure to light of a specific wavelength, usually green or blue). The resulting luminescent emission is measured and correlated with the absorbed radiation dose. **Conceptual Analogy — The Same Elevator, But With Sunlight:** The OSLD mechanism is the same as TLD — the elevator (electron) gets kicked up and stuck on a high floor by radiation. The difference is in *how we bring it down*. Instead of heating the building, we shine a powerful light (green laser, ~532 nm) onto the crystal. The light photons knock the electron off the trap (photo-transfer) and it falls back to ground, emitting light. The brighter the emission, the higher the dose. > [!TIP] > **Why OSLD is popular in modern clinics:** Readout is much faster (seconds vs minutes for TLD), no heating is required (so the dosimeter is not destroyed by heat), and the same dosimeter can be read multiple times (with optical bleaching between reads to reset the trap population). ### 1.3 Standard Materials and Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Common TLD Phosphors (KTU-Relevant):** > - **LiF:Mg,Ti** (commercially known as TLD-100) — most widely used; nearly tissue-equivalent (Z_eff ≈ 8.2) > - **LiF:Mg,Cu,P** (TLD-100H) — higher sensitivity, lower fading > - **CaSO₄:Dy** — high sensitivity but energy-dependent > - **CaF₂:Dy** — used in environmental monitoring > > **OSLD Phosphor:** > - **Al₂O₃:C** (aluminum oxide doped with carbon) — the industry standard, e.g., Landauer nanoDot - **Effective Atomic Number (Z_eff) of TLD-100:** **8.2** (very close to soft tissue ≈ 7.4) - **Effective Atomic Number (Z_eff) of Al₂O₃:C:** **~10.2** (slightly higher than tissue, requires energy correction) - **Boltzmann constant k:** **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** - **Planck constant h:** **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle of TLD — Step-by-Step 1. **Irradiation Phase:** The TLD crystal (e.g., LiF:Mg,Ti) is exposed to ionizing radiation. Charged particles (primary/secondary electrons) traverse the crystal, creating electron-hole pairs along their tracks. 2. **Trapping:** Free electrons and holes migrate through the conduction and valence bands. They become trapped at **lattice defect sites** (introduced by dopants like Mg, Ti in LiF) — these are *metastable* states with long lifetimes at room temperature. 3. **Storage:** The trapped charge carriers are stable at room temperature (some shallow traps may fade, but main dosimetric traps are stable for months to years). 4. **Heating (Readout):** The TLD is heated at a controlled linear rate (typically 1–25 °C/s). Thermal energy excites the trapped electrons out of their traps. 5. **Recombination:** Released electrons recombine with holes at **luminescence centers** (e.g., Ti³⁺/Ti⁴⁺ complexes in LiF:Mg,Ti). 6. **Light Emission:** The recombination energy is released as a visible photon (TL emission). The light intensity vs. temperature is recorded as the **Glow Curve**. 7. **Annealing (Reset):** The crystal is then annealed (e.g., 400 °C for 1 hour + 100 °C for 2 hours for TLD-100) to empty all traps, making it ready for reuse. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Glow Curve Structure (TLD-100):** > - **Peak 1** (~80 °C) — shallow trap, fades rapidly (low-temperature peak) > - **Peak 2** (~120 °C) — low-temperature peak > - **Peak 3** (~165 °C) — intermediate peak > - **Peak 4** (~200 °C) — **MAIN DOSIMETRIC PEAK**, stable, used for dose measurement > - **Peak 5** (~220 °C) — high-temperature peak, deep trap > > Only **Peak 4** is used for routine dosimetry because of its excellent stability (low fading) and reproducibility. ### 2.2 Operational Principle of OSLD — Step-by-Step 1. **Irradiation:** Same as TLD — Al₂O₃:C crystal is exposed to radiation, populating electron traps. 2. **Optical Stimulation:** The dosimeter is exposed to green light (~532 nm) from a pulsed LED/laser. 3. **Photo-Transfer of Trapped Electrons:** Photons stimulate trapped electrons from F-centers (oxygen vacancies with trapped electrons) into the conduction band. 4. **Recombination & Luminescence:** Electrons recombine with holes at Cr³⁺ luminescence centers, emitting blue light (~420 nm). 5. **Measurement:** The blue emission is detected by a photomultiplier tube (PMT). A high-pass filter blocks the stimulating green light from reaching the detector. 6. **Bleaching (Reset):** Exposure to intense white light or UV empties remaining traps for reuse. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | TL Intensity (Randall–Wilkins 1st-order kinetics) | $I(t) = n \cdot s \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right)$ | $n$ = trapped electron conc.; $s$ = frequency factor (s⁻¹); $E$ = trap depth (eV); $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | | 2 | Glow peak temperature | $T_m = \frac{E}{k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{s \cdot k \cdot T_m^2}{\beta \cdot E}\right)}$ | $\beta$ = heating rate (K/s) | | 3 | TL signal vs. Dose (linear region) | $M = k \cdot D$ | $M$ = measured TL output (nC); $D$ = absorbed dose (Gy); $k$ = calibration factor (nC/Gy) | | 4 | Absorbed Dose from TLD reading | $D = \frac{M}{k} \cdot f_{\text{energy}} \cdot f_{\text{fading}} \cdot f_{\text{angle}} \cdot \text{ECC}$ | All $f$ = correction factors; **ECC** = Element/Energy Correction Coefficient | | 5 | Sensitivity (TLD) | $S = \frac{M}{D}$ | Units: nC/Gy or counts/Gy | | 6 | Relative Sensitivity | $S_{\text{rel}} = \frac{S_{\text{phosphor}}}{S_{\text{LiF}}}$ | Compares to standard LiF | | 7 | Energy Dependence (TLD-100) | $\left(\frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{phosphor}} / \left(\frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{\text{tissue}}$ | Ratio of mass energy absorption coefficients; for LiF it is ≈ 1.0–1.3 across diagnostic kV range | | 8 | Fading (isothermal decay) | $I(t) = I_0 \cdot \exp(-\lambda t)$ | $\lambda$ = decay constant; for TLD-100 main peak, fading < 5% in 3 months | | 9 | OSL signal vs. dose | $M_{\text{OSL}} = k' \cdot D$ | Linear in low-dose region; **supralinear** at higher doses (unlike TLD) | | 10 | Bleaching (residual) | $M_{\text{residual}} = M_0 \cdot e^{-\sigma \Phi}$ | $\sigma$ = photo-ionization cross-section; $\Phi$ = photon fluence of stimulating light | | 11 | Tissue equivalence index | $f_{\text{TE}} = \frac{(Z_{\text{eff}})_{\text{phosphor}}}{(Z_{\text{eff}})_{\text{tissue}}}$ | Should be close to 1 | | 12 | Annealing schedule (TLD-100) | 400 °C × 1 h + (80–100 °C) × 2 h | Standard NACP protocol | > [!TIP] > **Examination Tip:** The Randall–Wilkins 1st-order kinetics equation is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams for TLD. Memorize the symbols and the physical meaning of each term. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Utility - **Radiotherapy:** Patient dose verification, in-vivo dosimetry for photon and electron beams, brachytherapy source calibration checks. - **Diagnostic Radiology:** Entrance Skin Dose (ESD) measurement, CT dose index verification. - **Personnel Monitoring:** Whole-body badge dosimeters for radiation workers (often LiF or Al₂O₃:C). - **Environmental Monitoring:** Area surveys around nuclear installations. - **Space Dosimetry:** Astronaut dose monitoring (TLD/OSLD chips are routinely flown on ISS missions). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Randall–Wilkins First-Order Kinetics for TL Intensity **Statement:** Derive an expression for thermoluminescence intensity $I(t)$ as a function of temperature $T$, using the assumption of first-order retrapping kinetics. **Starting Assumptions:** - The concentration of trapped electrons at time $t$ is $n$ (cm⁻³). - The rate of escape of an electron from a trap is governed by the Arrhenius factor $p = s \cdot \exp(-E/kT)$. - First-order kinetics: every released electron recombines and contributes to TL (retrapping is negligible). - The heating rate $\beta = dT/dt$ is constant. **Step 1 — Rate of change of trapped electrons:** The number of trapped electrons decreases as they are thermally released: $$\frac{dn}{dt} = -n \cdot s \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right)$$ **Step 2 — TL intensity is defined as the rate of recombination:** Since one electron released produces one photon (first-order), the TL intensity is: $$I(t) = -\frac{dn}{dt} = n \cdot s \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate:** $$\frac{dn}{n} = -s \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right) dt$$ Integrating from $n_0$ (initial population at $t = 0$) to $n$ (population at time $t$): $$\int_{n_0}^{n} \frac{dn}{n} = -s \int_{0}^{t} \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right) dt$$ This gives: $$n(t) = n_0 \cdot \exp\left[-s \int_{0}^{t} \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right) dt\right]$$ **Step 4 — Convert time variable to temperature using $\beta = dT/dt$:** Since $dt = dT/\beta$, we substitute: $$n(T) = n_0 \cdot \exp\left[-\frac{s}{\beta} \int_{T_0}^{T} \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT'}\right) dT'\right]$$ **Step 5 — Substitute back into intensity expression:** $$\boxed{\,I(T) = n_0 \cdot s \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right) \cdot \exp\left[-\frac{s}{\beta} \int_{T_0}^{T} \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT'}\right) dT'\right]\,}$$ **Physical Interpretation of Each Term:** - The first exponential $\exp(-E/kT)$ governs the *thermal release probability* — increases with $T$. - The second exponential is the *depletion factor* — decreases as more electrons are released. - The product gives a characteristic peak (the glow peak), since intensity starts at zero, rises with heating, and then drops as traps empty. ### 3.2 Derivation II — Glow Peak Temperature Condition ($T_m$) **Step 1:** The maximum of $I(T)$ occurs when $dI/dT = 0$. Differentiating the boxed expression above: $$\frac{d}{dT}\left[\exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{s}{\beta} \int \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT'}\right) dT'\right)\right] = 0$$ **Step 2:** Differentiating the logarithm of $I$: $$\frac{d \ln I}{dT} = \frac{E}{kT^2} - \frac{s}{\beta} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right) = 0$$ **Step 3:** Rearrange: $$\frac{s}{\beta} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT_m}\right) = \frac{E}{kT_m^2}$$ **Step 4:** Take natural log and solve for $T_m$: $$\ln\left(\frac{s \cdot k \cdot T_m^2}{\beta \cdot E}\right) = \frac{E}{kT_m}$$ $$\boxed{\,T_m = \frac{E}{k \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{s \cdot k \cdot T_m^2}{\beta \cdot E}\right)}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Verification with TLD-100 Peak 4:** - $E \approx 1.35$ eV - $s \approx 2 \times 10^{12}$ s⁻¹ - $\beta = 5$ K/s - $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K Compute $T_m$: $$\frac{E}{k} = \frac{1.35}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 15{,}670 \text{ K}$$ $$\frac{s \cdot k}{\beta \cdot E} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{12})(8.617 \times 10^{-5})}{(5)(1.35)} \approx 2.55 \times 10^{6}$$ $$\ln(2.55 \times 10^{6}) \approx 14.75$$ $$T_m \approx \frac{15{,}670}{14.75} \approx 473 \text{ K} = 200 \,^\circ\text{C} \; \checkmark$$ This matches the known main dosimetric peak temperature of TLD-100. ### 3.3 Derivation III — TL Output vs. Absorbed Dose **Starting Principle:** The number of trapped electrons $n_0$ is proportional to the energy deposited $E_d$ in the crystal. $$n_0 = \eta \cdot \frac{E_d}{E_{\text{pair}}}$$ where $\eta$ is the trapping efficiency and $E_{\text{pair}}$ is the average energy required to create one electron-hole pair (~2.5 × $E_g$ in insulators, where $E_g$ is the band gap). Since absorbed dose $D = E_d / m$ (where $m$ is the mass of the phosphor), we have $E_d = D \cdot m$, so: $$n_0 = \eta \cdot \frac{D \cdot m}{E_{\text{pair}}}$$ The total TL output is the integral of the glow curve: $$M = \int I(T) \, dT = n_0 \cdot (\text{geometric/electronic factors})$$ Thus: $$M = k \cdot D$$ where $k$ is the TL sensitivity (calibration factor), confirming the linear dose-response relationship in the linear region. This is the fundamental equation used to convert a TLD reading into a dose value. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validity:** This linear relationship holds in the dose range **~10 μGy to ~1 Gy** for TLD-100. Above ~1 Gy, the response becomes **sub-linear** (saturation of traps), and at very high doses (>100 Gy), **supralinear** behavior may appear due to radiation-induced defect creation. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TLD vs. OSLD Comparison | Parameter | TLD (LiF:Mg,Ti) | OSLD (Al₂O₃:C) | |---|---|---| | Stimulation method | Heat (thermal) | Light (optical, ~532 nm) | | Readout time | Minutes (heating + cooling) | Seconds (single light pulse) | | Re-readability | No (erases on readout) | Yes (partial re-reads possible) | | Sensitivity | High | High | | Tissue equivalence (Z_eff) | Excellent (8.2 vs 7.4) | Good (~10.2) | | Energy dependence | Low | Moderate (needs correction) | | Useful dose range | 10 μGy – 10 Gy | 10 μGy – several Gy | | Fading | Low (Peak 4, <5%/3 months) | Very low (F-center is deep) | | Reusability | Yes (after annealing) | Yes (after optical bleaching) | | Cost of reader | Higher (heater + PMT) | Lower (LED/laser + PMT) | | Annealing required | Yes (high temperature) | No (optical bleach) | | Clinical use | In-vivo dosimetry, audits | In-vivo dosimetry, personnel | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TLD Readout Process — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[TLD Crystal
LiF:Mg,Ti] --> B[Irradiation
Trapped electrons + holes] B --> C[Heating Programmer
Linear heat 1-25 C per s] C --> D[Heating Planchet
Temperature T rises] D --> E[Trap Release
Electrons freed to conduction band] E --> F[Recombination at Luminescence Center
Ti3+ Ti4+ complex] F --> G[Visible Light Photon Emission
Peak wavelength 400 nm] G --> H[Photomultiplier Tube PMT
Optical detection] H --> I[Amplifier and Integrator
Signal conditioning] I --> J[Glow Curve Recorder
Intensity vs T plot] J --> K[Dose Calculation
D equals M divided by k] L[Annealing Oven
400 C 1h then 100 C 2h] --> A ``` ### 4.2 OSLD Readout Process — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[OSL Dosimeter
Al2O3 dot C doped] --> B[Irradiation Phase
Populate F centers with electrons] B --> C[Optical Stimulation Unit
Green LED 532 nm pulsed] C --> D[Photoionization
Trapped electron ejected to CB] D --> E[Recombination at Cr3+ Center
Blue emission 420 nm] E --> F[Optical Filter High Pass
Blocks 532 nm transmits 420 nm] F --> G[Photomultiplier Tube PMT
Detects OSL light] G --> H[Signal Processor
Integrates pulse intensity] H --> I[Microcontroller
Computes dose] I --> J[Display and Storage
Dose value output] K[Optical Bleaching Unit
UV or white light] --> A ``` ### 4.3 TLD Glow Curve — Characteristic Peak Structure ```mermaid graph LR A[Temperature C] --> B[Low Temp Peak 1
approx 80 C
Shallow trap] A --> C[Low Temp Peak 2
approx 120 C
Fades in days] A --> D[Intermediate Peak 3
approx 165 C
Used rarely] A --> E[Main Peak 4
approx 200 C
Dosimetric peak] A --> F[High Temp Peak 5
approx 220 C
Deep trap] E --> G[TL Intensity maximum
Used for dose calculation] ``` ### 4.4 Complete Dosimetry Workflow — TLD vs OSLD ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Radiation Source
Linac or X ray tube] --> B[Patient or Phantom] B --> C[Dosimeter Placement
On skin or in cavity] C --> D{TLD or OSLD?} D -->|TLD| E[Anneal Pre irradiation] D -->|OSLD| F[Bleach Pre irradiation] E --> G[Anneal Reader 400 C] F --> H[OSL Reader 532 nm] G --> I[Glow Curve Peak 4 area] H --> J[OSL Intensity integral] I --> K[Dose equals M divided by k] J --> K K --> L[Report to Physicist
Compare with TPS prediction] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** *Define thermoluminescent dosimetry. List any four advantages of TLD over other dosimetry systems.* **Model Answer:** **Thermoluminescent Dosimetry (TLD):** It is a passive solid-state radiation dosimetry technique in which the energy absorbed from ionizing radiation is stored in the lattice defects of a crystalline phosphor. On controlled heating, the stored energy is released as visible light, the intensity of which is proportional to the absorbed dose. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Small physical size** (typically 3 mm × 3 mm × 0.9 mm chips) — allows point-dose measurement. 2. **Wide dose range** — from ~10 μGy to several Gy. 3. **Tissue equivalence** — LiF:Mg,Ti has Z_eff ≈ 8.2, very close to soft tissue. 4. **Reusable** — can be annealed and re-used hundreds of times with proper care. 5. *(Bonus: No high-voltage supply required, suitable for in-vivo dosimetry.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** *Distinguish between TLD and OSLD on the basis of stimulation method, readout time, and re-readability.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | TLD | OSLD | |---|---|---| | Stimulation method | **Thermal (heating)** the crystal | **Optical (light, ~532 nm)** illumination | | Readout time | **Several minutes** (heating + cooling cycle) | **Seconds** (single optical pulse) | | Re-readability | **No** — readout erases the trapped charge | **Yes** — multiple non-destructive reads possible | | Annealing | Required at 400 °C | Optical bleaching only | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, explain the construction and working of a TLD reader. Describe the glow curve for TLD-100 and identify the peak used for dosimetry. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Randall–Wilkins first-order kinetics expression for thermoluminescence intensity $I(t)$ as a function of temperature. State the conditions under which it is valid. **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Construction of a TLD Reader (4 marks):** A TLD reader consists of the following major components: 1. **Heating Planchet** — A metallic (usually nickel-chrome) tray on which the TLD chip is placed. A linear heating element raises its temperature in a controlled manner (1–25 °C/s). 2. **Temperature Sensor** — A thermocouple attached to the planchet monitors the temperature in real time. 3. **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** — Detects the weak TL light emitted by the TLD chip. 4. **Optical Light Guide / Filter** — Directs the emitted light from the chip to the PMT and filters out the infrared blackbody radiation from the heater. 5. **High-Voltage Supply and Amplifier** — Provides PMT high voltage and amplifies the anode current. 6. **Signal Integrator and A/D Converter** — Integrates the PMT current over time to produce a single number proportional to total TL. 7. **Microcontroller / Computer** — Controls the heating cycle, displays the glow curve, and computes the dose using the calibration factor. **Working (2 marks):** The TLD chip, after irradiation, is placed on the planchet. The planchet is heated linearly; as temperature rises, trapped electrons are released and recombine, emitting light. The PMT converts the light into a current that is amplified, digitized, and plotted as intensity vs. temperature (glow curve). The area under the main peak (or the peak height) is converted to dose via $D = M/k$. **Glow Curve Description (1 mark):** The glow curve of TLD-100 shows 5 peaks at approximately 80 °C, 120 °C, 165 °C, 200 °C, and 220 °C. The **fourth peak (≈200 °C)** — called the main **dosimetric peak** — is used for dose measurement due to its excellent stability and reproducibility. **Model Solution for Part (b):** The full derivation is shown in Section 3.1. Key steps to present: - Define $n$ = trapped electron concentration; rate of decrease $dn/dt = -n \cdot s \cdot \exp(-E/kT)$. - Intensity $I(t) = -dn/dt = n \cdot s \cdot \exp(-E/kT)$. - Integrate: $n(t) = n_0 \exp\left[-s \int_0^t \exp(-E/kT) dt\right]$. - Convert to temperature using $dt = dT/\beta$: $n(T) = n_0 \exp\left[-\frac{s}{\beta}\int_{T_0}^T \exp(-E/kT') dT'\right]$. - Final boxed expression: $$I(T) = n_0 \cdot s \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT}\right) \cdot \exp\left[-\frac{s}{\beta}\int_{T_0}^T \exp\left(-\frac{E}{kT'}\right) dT'\right]$$ **Conditions for Validity (3 marks):** 1. **First-order kinetics** — every released electron recombines (retrapping is negligible). 2. **Constant heating rate** $\beta$ throughout the readout cycle. 3. **Single trap level** (one activation energy $E$). 4. **Luminescence center concentration** is non-rate-limiting (saturated). 5. **No thermal quenching** of luminescence within the temperature range of interest. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** What is Optically Stimulated Luminescence Dosimetry (OSLD)? Explain its principle, the material used, and the procedure for dose measurement with a neat block diagram. **(b) [7 Marks]** A TLD-100 chip is exposed to a dose of 2 Gy from a Co-60 beam. The TL reading is 540 nC. The chip is then re-annealed, exposed to an unknown dose, and gives a reading of 320 nC. Calculate the unknown dose and the sensitivity of the TLD chip. If the same chip shows a residual reading of 18 nC after the second readout, calculate the percentage residual signal. **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Definition (1 mark):** OSLD is a passive radiation dosimetry technique in which trapped charge carriers in a phosphor (Al₂O₃:C) are released by optical stimulation, producing luminescence proportional to the absorbed dose. **Material (1 mark):** The standard OSLD phosphor is **Al₂O₃:C** (aluminum oxide doped with carbon). Carbon creates **F-centers** (oxygen vacancies with two trapped electrons) which act as deep electron traps. **Principle (2 marks):** On irradiation, electrons are trapped at F-centers. During readout, green light (~532 nm) ejects the trapped electrons via photo-transfer. The electrons recombine with holes at Cr³⁺ luminescence centers, emitting blue light (~420 nm). The blue intensity is proportional to the dose. **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. Bleach the OSLD dot under UV/white light to empty residual traps. 2. Expose to radiation (during patient treatment or calibration). 3. Place in the OSL reader; the reader pulses the green LED. 4. Detect the blue emission through a high-pass filter using a PMT. 5. Integrate the signal; compute dose using the calibration factor. 6. Re-bleach for reuse. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2. **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Given:** - Calibration dose: $D_1 = 2$ Gy - Calibration TL signal: $M_1 = 540$ nC - Unknown dose TL signal: $M_2 = 320$ nC - Residual signal after second readout: $M_{\text{res}} = 18$ nC **Step 1 — Calculate TLD Sensitivity (1 mark):** Since $M = k \cdot D$ in the linear region: $$k = \frac{M_1}{D_1} = \frac{540 \text{ nC}}{2 \text{ Gy}} = 270 \text{ nC/Gy}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Unknown Dose (2 marks):** $$D_2 = \frac{M_2}{k} = \frac{320 \text{ nC}}{270 \text{ nC/Gy}} = 1.185 \text{ Gy}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Percentage Residual Signal (2 marks):** $$\% \text{ Residual} = \frac{M_{\text{res}}}{M_2} \times 100\% = \frac{18}{320} \times 100\% = 5.625\%$$ **Interpretation (1 mark):** A residual of 5.6% after readout indicates incomplete erasure of traps — this is why **annealing (for TLD)** or **optical bleaching (for OSLD)** is essential before re-use. The 5.6% residual, if uncorrected, would contribute a ~0.067 Gy background to the next measurement. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TLD/OSLD Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "Peak 4" with "Peak 5":** Many students write that Peak 5 (~220 °C) is the dosimetric peak. **Correct answer: Peak 4 (~200 °C).** Peak 5 is a high-temperature deep trap, less reliable for routine dosimetry. > 2. **Mixing up TLD and OSLD materials:** OSLD uses Al₂O₃:C; TLD uses LiF:Mg,Ti. **Do not interchange.** > 3. **Skipping the exponential integral in the derivation:** In the Randall–Wilkins derivation, the boxed expression contains a definite integral. Marks are awarded for writing this integral explicitly — do not omit it. > 4. **Forgetting the units:** Sensitivity is **nC/Gy**, dose is **Gy**, signal is **nC**. Always include units. > 5. **Using linear dose-response beyond 1 Gy:** The linear relation $M = k \cdot D$ fails for TLD-100 above ~1 Gy. State this assumption explicitly in numerical problems. > 6. **Confusing "annealing" with "readout":** Annealing is the pre-irradiation heat treatment that resets traps; readout is the post-irradiation heating that reads the dose. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **TLD Principle:** Radiation → trapped electrons in crystal defects → heating → light emission proportional to dose. - **OSLD Principle:** Same as TLD, but **light replaces heat** to release the trapped electrons. - **Main TLD Phosphor:** **LiF:Mg,Ti (TLD-100)**; Z_eff = 8.2. - **Main OSLD Phosphor:** **Al₂O₃:C**; Z_eff ≈ 10.2. - **Glow Curve:** TLD-100 has 5 peaks; **Peak 4 (~200 °C) is the dosimetric peak**. - **Randall–Wilkins Equation:** $I(T) = n_0 s \exp(-E/kT) \exp\left[-\frac{s}{\beta}\int \exp(-E/kT')dT'\right]$. - **Glow Peak Condition:** $\frac{E}{kT_m^2} = \frac{s}{\beta}\exp(-E/kT_m)$. - **Dose-Response:** Linear in 10 μGy – 1 Gy range; $D = M/k$. - **Sensitivity:** $k = M/D$ (units: nC/Gy). - **Annealing (TLD-100):** 400 °C × 1 h + 100 °C × 2 h (NACP protocol). - **Fading:** TLD-100 Peak 4 < 5% in 3 months; OSLD very low fading. - **OSLD Advantages:** Fast readout, re-readable, no annealing, no heat damage. - **OSLD Stimulation Wavelength:** ~532 nm (green); Emission: ~420 nm (blue). - **Clinical Use:** In-vivo patient dosimetry, personnel monitoring, treatment plan verification, QA audits. - **Key Energy Dependence:** TLD-100 is nearly tissue-equivalent; OSLD (Al₂O₃:C) requires an energy correction factor at diagnostic kV energies. - **Examiners love:** Numerical problems on $D = M/k$, sensitivity calculation, and the Randall–Wilkins derivation. ---

topics.title: Semiconductor Dosimeters. Radiation Protection: Permissible Doses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Semiconductor Dosimeters. Radiation Protection: Permissible Doses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SEMICONDUCTOR DOSIMETERS & RADIATION PROTECTION DOSIMETRY

## 1.1 Semiconductor Dosimeters — Formal Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semiconductor Dosimeter:** A solid-state radiation detection and measurement device that utilizes the ionization produced within the depletion region of a reverse-biased p–n junction (or similar semiconductor architecture such as MOSFET, diamond detector, or SiC detector) to quantify absorbed dose, exposure, or dose rate in radiotherapy and diagnostic radiology environments.

Semiconductor dosimeters belong to the class of **active (real-time) radiation detectors** and exploit the property that ionizing radiation creates **electron–hole pairs (EHPs)** within the sensitive volume of the semiconductor crystal. The number of charge carriers collected at the electrodes is directly proportional to the energy deposited by the incident radiation, allowing precise dosimetric measurement.

### 1.1.2 Key Physical Constants

- **Energy required to create one electron–hole pair in Silicon (Si):** $w = 3.62$ **eV** at 300 K
- **Energy required in Germanium (Ge):** $w = 2.96$ **eV**
- **Energy required in Diamond (C):** $w = 13.6$ **eV**
- **Energy required in air (gas ionisation):** $w = 33.97$ **eV (≈ 34 eV)**
- **Density of Silicon:** $\rho_{Si} = 2.33$ **g/cm³**
- **Atomic number (Z) of Si:** $Z = 14$
- **Fano Factor for Si:** $F \approx 0.115$

### 1.1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Coin-Counter in a Highway Toll Booth"

Imagine a two-lane highway (the semiconductor) with cars (charge carriers) flowing in opposite directions. A toll barrier (the depletion region) blocks both lanes. When radiation passes through, it acts like a mischievous crow pecking at cars, knocking many more cars loose than a single nudge would suggest. The barrier then opens briefly, releasing a **countable torrent of cars (electrons + holes)** that the toll operator (electronics) counts.

The key insight: radiation needs to deliver only a **tiny amount of energy** (3.62 eV in Si) compared to gas detectors (34 eV in air) to release a single "car" — so the semiconductor is roughly **9.4× more efficient** per unit energy than a traditional ion chamber.

$$\text{Relative efficiency} = \frac{w_{\text{air}}}{w_{\text{Si}}} = \frac{33.97}{3.62} \approx 9.4$$

### 1.1.4 Why Semiconductors are Central to Modern Radiotherapy

> [!NOTE]
> Modern radiotherapy (IMRT, VMAT, SRS, SBRT, proton therapy, CyberKnife) demands sub-millimetre dosimetric accuracy with high spatial resolution. Semiconductor dosimeters uniquely satisfy this requirement because of their:
> 1. **Small sensitive volume** (sub-mm³)
> 2. **High sensitivity** (≈ 18,000× greater than air-filled ion chamber per unit mass)
> 3. **Real-time readout** capability
> 4. **Mechanical robustness** (solid-state, no gas leakage)
> 5. **Compatibility with megavoltage and kilovoltage photon beams**

---

## 1.2 Radiation Protection — Permissible Doses: Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permissible Dose (Maximum Permissible Dose, MPD):** The maximum dose of ionizing radiation that a person (occupational worker or member of the public) is allowed to receive within a specified period (typically one year) without experiencing significant deterministic effects and with an acceptably low probability of stochastic effects, as recommended by the **International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP)** and adopted by the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB), India**.

The concept evolved through a series of recommendations:
- **ICRP Publication 26 (1977)**
- **ICRP Publication 60 (1990)**
- **ICRP Publication 103 (2007)** — current effective recommendation
- **AERB Safety Code (AERB/SC/Med-2)** — Indian regulatory framework

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $H_T$ | Equivalent dose in tissue $T$ |
| $H_E$ (or $E$) | Effective dose (whole-body weighted) |
| $w_T$ | Tissue weighting factor |
| $w_R$ | Radiation weighting factor |
| $D$ | Absorbed dose (Gy) |
| $Q$ | Quality factor |
| $N$ | Number of cases |

$$\boxed{H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R} \quad \text{and} \quad E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — SEMICONDUCTOR DOSIMETERS

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A semiconductor dosimeter operates through the following sequential mechanism:

**Step 1 — Depletion Region Formation**
A p–n junction diode is reverse-biased, expanding the depletion (space-charge) region. This region is devoid of free mobile carriers and acts as the **sensitive volume** for radiation detection.

**Step 2 — Ionizing Radiation Interaction**
Incoming photons (X-rays or γ-rays) or charged particles (electrons, protons) interact with the semiconductor lattice via:
- **Photoelectric effect** (dominant at low energies, $\propto Z^{4-5}$)
- **Compton scattering** (dominant in MeV range)
- **Pair production** (above 1.022 MeV)

These interactions transfer energy to bound electrons, liberating **electron–hole pairs (EHPs)**.

**Step 3 — Charge Collection**
The strong electric field ($\sim 10^5$ V/cm) within the depletion region rapidly separates the EHPs before recombination can occur, sweeping electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side.

**Step 4 — Signal Generation**
The collected charge $Q$ produces a measurable current pulse:

$$Q = \frac{E_{\text{deposited}}}{w} \cdot e$$

where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** is the elementary charge.

**Step 5 — Dose Determination**
The integrated current (or total collected charge) is related to absorbed dose $D$ by:

$$D = \frac{Q \cdot (w/e)}{m_{\text{sensitive}}}$$

where $m_{\text{sensitive}}$ is the mass of the depletion region.

### 2.2 Types of Semiconductor Dosimeters

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently examines the comparative anatomy of these devices. Memorize construction + application + limitation for each.

#### 2.2.1 p–n Junction Silicon Diode Dosimeter
- **Construction:** Encapsulated silicon diode (often p-type Si with n-type implant), coaxial cable, buildup cap (typically 0.5–1 g/cm² for megavoltage beams).
- **Operating Mode:** **Current mode** (no external bias required in pre-irradiated diodes — "zero-bias" operation).
- **Sensitivity:** $\sim 0.35$ **nC/cGy** for typical radiotherapy diodes.
- **Use:** In-vivo dosimetry, entrance dose measurement, beam profile scanning.
- **Disadvantage:** **Radiation damage** shifts sensitivity by 1–5% per kGy — requires periodic recalibration.

#### 2.2.2 MOSFET Dosimeter
- **Full Form:** **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor**
- **Construction:** n-channel MOSFET with thick SiO₂ gate oxide; floating gate traps radiation-induced charges.
- **Mechanism:** Threshold voltage $V_{th}$ shifts linearly with absorbed dose:

$$\Delta V_{th} = -\frac{(t_{ox})^2 \cdot \Delta Q_{ot}}{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A}$$

- **Sensitivity:** $\sim 1$ mV/cGy (single MOSFET) to $\sim 30$ mV/cGy (dual-MOSFET dual-bias configuration)
- **Use:** In-vivo skin dosimetry, postal dosimetry, brachytherapy, space dosimetry.
- **Advantage:** Extremely small ($\sim 0.5 \times 0.5$ mm²), permanent dose record, non-destructive readout.

#### 2.2.3 Diamond Detector
- **Type:** Natural or synthetic (CVD — Chemical Vapour Deposition) diamond crystal with metal contacts.
- **Operating Mode:** Current mode; behaves nearly as **tissue-equivalent** ($Z_{\text{eff,C}} = 6$, similar to soft tissue $Z_{\text{eff}} = 7.4$).
- **Sensitivity:** $\sim 0.36$ nC/cGy per mm³ of active volume.
- **Use:** Radiotherapy dosimetry — small field, stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS), microbeam.
- **Advantage:** Tissue equivalence → minimal energy correction.
- **Disadvantage:** High cost; polarization effects.

#### 2.2.4 CdTe / CdZnTe Compound Detectors
- **High-Z material:** $Z_{\text{eff, CdTe}} = 50$ → high photoelectric cross-section.
- **Use:** Brachytherapy seed localization, SPECT, photon counting at diagnostic energies.
- **Note:** Polarization and charge trapping effects limit routine dosimetric use in MV beams.

#### 2.2.5 Silicon Carbide (SiC) Detector
- Wide bandgap ($E_g = 3.26$ eV), low leakage current, radiation-hard.
- Emerging use in harsh radiation environments and high-temperature dosimetry.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **board-exam essentials** for semiconductor dosimetry. These relationships appear regularly in KTU 14-mark derivations.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{EHP} = E_{\text{deposited}} / w$ | Number of electron-hole pairs generated |
| 2 | $Q = N_{EHP} \cdot e$ | Total collected charge |
| 3 | $D = E_{\text{deposited}} / m$ | Absorbed dose definition (J/kg = Gy) |
| 4 | $\eta = (w_{\text{air}}/w_{\text{sc}})$ | Relative signal yield vs. air |
| 5 | $w = 2.8 E_g + 0.6$ eV (empirical) | Average EHP creation energy in semiconductors |
| 6 | $\Delta V_{th} \propto D$ | MOSFET threshold-voltage shift ∝ dose |
| 7 | $E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T$ | Effective dose (whole-body stochastic risk) |
| 8 | $H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$ | Equivalent dose in tissue $T$ |
| 9 | $\dot{H}_{T,\text{limit}}$ | Annual equivalent dose rate limit |
| 10 | $\text{Cumulative} = E_{\text{year}} \cdot N_{\text{age}-18}$ | Cumulative occupational lifetime dose |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical numerical anchor:** $w_{Si} = 3.62$ eV, $w_{Ge} = 2.96$ eV, $w_{\text{diamond}} = 13.6$ eV, $w_{\text{air}} = 33.97$ eV.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radiotherapy Clinics:** MOSFET and diode arrays are routinely used for patient-specific IMRT QA and in-vivo skin-dose monitoring.
- **Diagnostic Radiology:** Solid-state dosimeters in mammography measure mean glandular dose.
- **Space & Aviation:** MOSFET dosimeters monitor crew exposure in ISS and high-altitude flights.
- **Industrial Radiography:** SiC and diamond dosimeters used in non-destructive testing of welds.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Semiconductor-based survey meters detect contamination levels near nuclear facilities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Number of Charge Carriers Generated per Unit Absorbed Dose in Silicon

**Given:** A silicon diode dosimeter with sensitive volume $V = 1 \text{ mm}^3$ receives an absorbed dose of $D = 1$ Gy from a 6 MV photon beam.

**Step 1 — Compute the mass of the sensitive volume:**

Density of silicon: $\rho_{Si} = 2.33$ **g/cm³** $= 2.33 \times 10^{-3}$ **kg/cm³** $= 2.33 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^3$ **g/L**

$$m = \rho_{Si} \cdot V = 2.33 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times (1 \text{ mm})^3 = 2.33 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3 = 2.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g} = 2.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total deposited energy from absorbed dose:**

By definition, $D = E_{\text{deposited}} / m$, hence:

$$E_{\text{deposited}} = D \cdot m = 1 \text{ J/kg} \times 2.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg} = 2.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J}$$

Convert to electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):

$$E_{\text{deposited}} = \frac{2.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}} = 1.454 \times 10^{13} \text{ eV}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the EHP creation energy $w = 3.62$ eV/pair:**

$$N_{EHP} = \frac{E_{\text{deposited}}}{w} = \frac{1.454 \times 10^{13} \text{ eV}}{3.62 \text{ eV/pair}} = 4.016 \times 10^{12} \text{ pairs}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to collected charge $Q$:**

$$Q = N_{EHP} \cdot e = 4.016 \times 10^{12} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C} = 6.434 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C} = 0.6434 \text{ μC}$$

**Step 5 — Verification via direct formula:**

$$Q = \frac{D \cdot m \cdot e}{w} = \frac{1 \times 2.33 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{3.62} = 1.031 \times 10^{-25} \text{ C·m/kg}$$

**Correction:** Resolving proper units yields $Q \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-7}$ **C** per 1 Gy, consistent with published data for silicon diode dosimeters (typically 0.3–0.7 nC/cGy range).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Sensitivity Ratio of Silicon vs. Air Ionisation Chamber

**Given:** Compare the charge produced per unit absorbed dose in a silicon dosimeter versus a Farmer-type ion chamber (air-equivalent).

**Step 1 — Charge per unit dose in silicon per unit mass:**

For a given absorbed dose $D$, energy deposited per kg is $D \cdot 1$ J. Number of EHPs per kg:

$$N_{Si} = \frac{D \cdot 1 \text{ J/kg}}{w_{Si} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}} = \frac{D}{3.62 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{D}{5.80 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Step 2 — Charge per kg in silicon:**

$$Q_{Si}/\text{kg} = N_{Si} \cdot e = \frac{D \cdot e}{w_{Si} \cdot e} = \frac{D}{w_{Si}} \text{ (in eV units)}$$

Numerically: $Q_{Si}/\text{kg} = D / 3.62$ eV-units of charge.

**Step 3 — Same quantity in air:**

$$Q_{\text{air}}/\text{kg} = D / 33.97 \text{ eV-units}$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_{Si}}{Q_{\text{air}}} = \frac{33.97}{3.62} = 9.38$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Si/\text{air}} \approx 9.4 \text{ times more charge per unit dose per unit mass}}$$

**Step 5 — Density correction:** Silicon is $\rho_{Si}/\rho_{\text{air}} = 2.33/0.001293 = 1802$ **times denser** than air. Hence per unit **volume**, silicon yields:

$$\eta_{\text{volumetric}} = 9.4 \times 1802 \approx 16{,}940 \text{ times higher charge per cm}^3$$

This is the figure (~18,000×) commonly quoted in KTU textbooks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — MOSFET Threshold-Voltage Shift and Absorbed Dose Calibration

**Given:** A p-MOSFET dosimeter with gate oxide thickness $t_{ox} = 1$ μm and oxide area $A = 0.25$ mm² receives a dose $D$ of 1 Gy.

**Step 1 — Radiation-induced trapped charge in SiO₂:**

The oxide-trapped charge density per unit dose:

$$\Delta Q_{ot} = q \cdot f(E) \cdot D$$

where $f(E) \approx 10^{-9}$ **C/Gy** for thermal neutron-free, X-ray irradiated SiO₂ in p-MOSFET.

$$\Delta Q_{ot} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{-9} \times 1 = 1.602 \times 10^{-28} \text{ C/μm}^3$$

For volume $= A \cdot t_{ox} = 0.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 2.5 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^3$:

$$Q_{ot,\text{total}} = 1.602 \times 10^{-28} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-13} \times 10^6 \cdot \text{ m}^3 = 4.005 \times 10^{-35} \text{ C}$$

> *(Note: Numerical estimates vary; the above is illustrative. Real MOSFET sensitivity ≈ 1–3 mV/cGy depending on bias during irradiation.)*

**Step 2 — Threshold voltage shift:**

$$\Delta V_{th} = -\frac{\Delta Q_{ot}}{\varepsilon_{ox} \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A} \cdot t_{ox}$$

Substituting $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**, $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m², $t_{ox} = 10^{-6}$ m:

$$\Delta V_{th} = -\frac{\Delta Q_{ot}}{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

A typical experimental value: $\Delta V_{th}/D \approx 2$ **mV/cGy** (zero-bias) to **30 mV/cGy** (dual-bias readout).

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Cumulative Occupational Dose Over a Working Lifetime

**Given:** A radiation worker starts employment at age 18 and retires at age 65. The annual effective dose limit is 20 mSv/year (stochastic limit, averaged over 5 years).

**Step 1 — Years of service:**

$$N = 65 - 18 = 47 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative dose at the maximum permissible annual limit:**

$$E_{\text{cumulative}} = 20 \text{ mSv/year} \times 47 \text{ years} = 940 \text{ mSv} \approx 1 \text{ Sv}$$

**Step 3 — Lifetime dose limit (cumulative rule, ICRP 60):**

$$E_{\text{cumulative,max}} = (Age - 18) \times 10 \text{ mSv}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At 65 years: $E_{\text{max}} = (65-18) \times 10 = 470$ **mSv** lifetime equivalent — corresponding to 10 mSv/year averaged, not 20 mSv/year.

**Step 4 — ICRP 103 average annual limit:** $E_{\text{avg,5yr}} \leq 20$ **mSv/year**, with no single year exceeding **50 mSv**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ICRP 103 (2007) Dose Limits

| Category | Effective Dose Annual Limit | Lens of Eye ($H_T$) | Skin, Hands, Feet ($H_T$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Occupational Worker (adult)** | 20 mSv avg/yr, max 50 mSv/yr | 20 mSv/yr (averaged) | 500 mSv/yr |
| **Trainee (< 18 yrs)** | 6 mSv/yr | 20 mSv/yr | 150 mSv/yr |
| **Member of Public** | 1 mSv/yr | 15 mSv/yr | 50 mSv/yr |
| **Embryo/Fetus** | 1 mSv (declaration of pregnancy) | — | — |
| **Comforters & Caregivers** | 5 mSv per episode (with dose constraint) | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AERB (India) has largely harmonized with ICRP 103, with the occupational annual limit of **20 mSv/year averaged over 5 consecutive years** and a maximum of **30 mSv in any single year** (AERB Safety Directive).

### 3.5.1 Tissue Weighting Factors (ICRP 103)

| Tissue / Organ | $w_T$ |
|---|---|
| Bone marrow, colon, lung, stomach, breast, remainder | 0.12 each |
| Gonads | 0.08 |
| Bladder, oesophagus, liver, thyroid | 0.04 each |
| Bone surface, brain, salivary glands, skin | 0.01 each |
| **Total** | $\sum w_T = 1.00$ |

### 3.5.2 Radiation Weighting Factors (ICRP 103)

| Radiation Type | $w_R$ |
|---|---|
| Photons (X-rays, γ), electrons, muons | 1 |
| Protons (> 2 MeV) | 2 |
| Alpha particles, fission fragments, heavy ions | 20 |
| Neutrons | Continuous function of energy (2.5 to 20) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Semiconductor Dosimeter Readout Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Radiation Source: X-ray, Gamma, Electron, Proton] --> B[Semiconductor Sensitive Volume: p-n Junction / MOSFET / Diamond]
    B --> C[Depletion Region: Strong E-field, no free carriers]
    C --> D[Electron-Hole Pair Generation: EHP = E_dep / w]
    D --> E[Charge Collection: Electrons to n, Holes to p]
    E --> F[Charge Sensitive Preamplifier: CSA converts Q to V]
    F --> G[Amplifier and Pulse Shaper: Main amplifier]
    G --> H{Dose Type?}
    H -->|Pulsed Beam| I[Pulse Counter and Integrator]
    H -->|Continuous| J[Current Integrator: Charge to Dose]
    I --> K[Display: Dose Rate, cGy/min, Gy]
    J --> K
    K --> L[QA Output: Patient Record, Beam Profile, In-vivo Dose]
    M[Bias Supply: Reverse Voltage VR 0 to 150 V] --> B
    N[Temperature Compensation Circuit] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** Radiation enters the semiconductor sensitive volume, generates EHPs in the depletion region, the charge is collected and converted to voltage by the preamplifier, shaped, and routed either to pulse-counting electronics (for diagnostic photon counting) or to a current integrator (for therapy dose measurement). A bias supply reverse-biases the junction, and temperature compensation stabilises gain across patient-room thermal variations.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — MOSFET Dosimeter Cross-Sectional Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Gate Metal: Aluminum or Poly-Si] --> B[Gate Oxide SiO2: Sensitive Layer, 0.5 to 2 um]
    B --> C[Silicon Substrate: p-type bulk]
    C --> D[Source and Drain: n+ implants]
    E[Ionizing Radiation: X-ray or Gamma Photon] --> B
    B --> F[Electron-Hole Pairs in SiO2]
    F --> G[Trapped Holes in Oxide]
    G --> H[Threshold Voltage Shift ΔVth]
    H --> I[Readout: Measure ΔVth and Convert to Dose]
    D --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:** A thin SiO₂ gate oxide is the **radiation-sensitive volume**. Photons liberate electrons and holes; the holes become trapped in the oxide, shifting the MOSFET's threshold voltage. The shift $\Delta V_{th}$ is proportional to absorbed dose and is read out electrically — providing a permanent, non-destructive dose record.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Permissible Dose Framework (ICRP / AERB)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Source of Ionizing Radiation] --> B[Pathways: External, Inhalation, Ingestion]
    B --> C[Worker / Public / Patient / Embryo]
    C --> D[Dosimetry Monitoring: TLD, Film Badge, OSL, Pocket Dosimeter, MOSFET]
    D --> E[Measured Quantity: Hp d, Hp 0.07, E]
    E --> F{Compare with Limit}
    F -->|< Limit| G[Within ALARA — Continue]
    F -->|> Limit| H[Investigate, Restrict, Optimize]
    H --> I[Modify Procedures, Increase Shielding, Time-Distance-Shielding]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Annual Cumulative Record: Lifetime Dose Tracking]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This flow represents the **dose-limit cycle** mandated under ICRP 103. Routine monitoring with personal dosimeters feeds cumulative records; any value approaching the dose limit triggers optimisation, not just regulation.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Functional Map — Semiconductor vs. Other Dosimeter Classes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Radiation Detectors and Dosimeters] --> B[Gas-Filled: Ion Chamber, GM Counter, Proportional]
    A --> C[Scintillation: NaI, Plastic, Liquid]
    A --> D[Semiconductor: Si Diode, MOSFET, Diamond, CdTe, SiC]
    A --> E[Solid-State Passive: TLD, OSL, Film Badge]
    D --> D1[High Sensitivity: 9000x Air IC]
    D --> D2[Small Size: Sub-mm3]
    D --> D3[Real-Time Readout]
    D --> D4[Radiation Damage: Periodic Recalibration]
    D --> D5[Energy Dependence: Need Buildup Cap]
    E --> E1[No Power Required: Passive]
    E --> E2[Long-Term Dose Record]
    E --> E3[Re-read Possible OSL]
    E --> E4[No Real-Time Signal]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Semiconductor dosimeters occupy the **active solid-state niche** — combining small size and high sensitivity with the disadvantage of radiation-induced sensitivity drift. Passive TLD/OSL remain the regulatory standard for personal dosimetry due to legal traceability and zero power requirement.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define a semiconductor dosimeter. Mention two advantages it has over a gas-filled ionisation chamber. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A semiconductor dosimeter is a solid-state radiation detection device that uses a reverse-biased p–n junction (or similar semiconductor structure) to measure ionising radiation. The depletion region of the diode acts as the sensitive volume; incident radiation creates electron–hole pairs proportional to the energy deposited, and the resulting charge is collected and measured.

**Two advantages over a gas-filled ionisation chamber:**

1. **Higher sensitivity per unit volume** — Silicon requires only 3.62 eV to create one electron–hole pair versus 33.97 eV per ion pair in air, giving ~9.4× more charge per unit dose per unit mass and ~18,000× more per unit volume (since Si is ~1800× denser than air).
2. **Smaller sensitive volume and higher spatial resolution** — Sub-millimetre active volumes enable point-dose measurement in steep dose gradients (e.g., SRS, IMRT, brachytherapy), which an ion chamber of useful size cannot resolve.

### Question 2. State the ICRP 103 annual occupational effective dose limit. What is the cumulative dose limit? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

- **Annual occupational effective dose limit (averaged over 5 years):** $E = 20$ **mSv/year**, with the constraint that no single year exceeds **50 mSv**.
- **Cumulative lifetime limit:** $E_{\text{cum}} = (Age - 18) \times 10$ **mSv**, i.e., for a worker aged 65, the maximum cumulative effective dose is **470 mSv**.
- **Lens of the eye:** 20 mSv/year (averaged); **Skin, hands, feet:** 500 mSv/year.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

### QUESTION A — Semiconductor Dosimeter Operation & Calibration

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a silicon p–n junction diode used as a radiotherapy dosimeter. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **[7 marks]** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(b)** A silicon diode dosimeter with a sensitive volume of 1 mm³ receives an absorbed dose of 2 Gy from a 6 MV photon beam. Calculate the number of electron–hole pairs generated and the total collected charge. Given: $w_{Si} = 3.62$ **eV**, $\rho_{Si} = 2.33$ **g/cm³**, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution to (a):**
