# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Primary Barrier Thickness

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU Standard Style)
A diagnostic X-ray room has the following parameters:
- **Workload W** = 1000 mA·min / week at 100 kVp
- **Use Factor U** = 1/4 (wall behind the vertical bucky)
- **Distance d** from source to the protected point = **3 m**
- **Shielding design goal P** = 0.1 mGy/week (controlled area, staff)
- **TVL₁ = 1.7 mm Pb**, **TVLₑ = 1.5 mm Pb** at 100 kVp.
- **Conversion**: 1 mA·min at 1 m @ 100 kVp produces **0.0492 mGy** of air-kerma (standard air-kerma rate of 0.0492 mGy/mA·min·m²).

**Find the required primary-barrier thickness in mm of lead.**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert workload to air-kerma at 1 m**

$$W = 1000 \text{ mA·min/wk} \times 0.0492 \text{ mGy/mA·min·m}^2 = 49.2 \text{ mGy·m}^2/\text{wk}$$

**Step 2 — Unshielded primary air-kerma at d = 3 m, with use factor U = 1/4**

$$K_p = \frac{W \cdot U}{d^2} = \frac{49.2 \times 0.25}{3^2} = \frac{12.3}{9} = 1.367 \text{ mGy/wk}$$

**Step 3 — Required barrier transmission B**

$$B = \frac{P}{K_p} = \frac{0.1}{1.367} = 0.07315$$

**Step 4 — Number of TVLs required**

$$n_{TVL} = -\log_{10}(B) = -\log_{10}(0.07315) = 1.136$$

**Step 5 — Convert TVLs to physical lead thickness**

$$x = TVL_1 + (n_{TVL} - 1) \cdot TVL_e$$

$$x = 1.7 + (1.136 - 1) \times 1.5 = 1.7 + 0.204 = 1.904 \text{ mm Pb}$$

**Step 6 — Round up to commercially available lead sheet**

$$\boxed{x = 2.0 \text{ mm Pb (commercially available: 2 mm lead-lined gypsum board)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The final answer is **always rounded UP** to the next commercially available sheet thickness. Using 1.5 mm would result in 1.7 + 0.5·1.5 = 1.55 mm physical lead, giving a transmission slightly *higher* than allowed.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Secondary Barrier Thickness

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A side wall of the same X-ray room (above) is exposed only to:
- **Scatter from patient** (90°): scatter fraction *a* = 0.0015, $d_{patient}$ = 1 m, $d_s$ = 2 m
- **Leakage** from tube housing: tube-to-barrier distance $d_L$ = 3 m
- Occupancy T = 1 (office next door)
- Design goal P = 0.02 mGy/week (uncontrolled area, public)

**Find required secondary-barrier thickness in mm of lead. (HVL_sec = 0.6 mm Pb at 100 kVp)**

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Scatter air-kerma at the barrier**

$$K_s = \frac{W \cdot T \cdot a}{d_{patient}^{\,2} \cdot d_s^{\,2}}$$

$$K_s = \frac{1000 \times 1 \times 0.0015}{1^2 \times 2^2} = \frac{1.5}{4} = 0.375 \text{ mGy/wk}$$

**Step 2 — Leakage workload (convention W_L = W / 5)**

$$W_L = \frac{W}{5} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200 \text{ mA·min/wk}$$

**Step 3 — Leakage air-kerma at 1 m from housing = 1 mGy/h (regulatory cap).** For a 1 mA·min emission at 100 kVp → output rate at 1 m is 0.0492 mGy/mA·min. Converting leakage cap: 1 mGy/h × (X-ray-on time) gives air-kerma/week.

For a conservative 8 h/day, 5 days/week, 100% X-ray-on: $W_{L,time} = 8 \times 5 = 40$ h/wk. So

$$K_L = \frac{1 \text{ mGy/h} \times 40 \text{ h/wk}}{d_L^{\,2}} = \frac{40}{3^2} = 4.44 \text{ mGy/wk}$$

**Step 4 — Total unshielded air-kerma at the secondary barrier**

$$K_{sec} = K_s + K_L = 0.375 + 4.44 = 4.815 \text{ mGy/wk}$$

**Step 5 — Required transmission**

$$B_s = \frac{P}{K_{sec}} = \frac{0.02}{4.815} = 4.155 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6 — Number of HVLs**

$$n_{HVL} = \log_2\left(\frac{1}{B_s}\right) = \log_2(240.7) = 7.91 \text{ HVLs}$$

**Step 7 — Required lead thickness (secondary barriers use HVL, not TVL, since doses are low)**

$$x_s = n_{HVL} \cdot HVL_{sec} = 7.91 \times 0.6 = 4.75 \text{ mm Pb}$$

**Step 8 — Round up**

$$\boxed{x_s = 5.0 \text{ mm Pb (use 1/4" lead-lined plywood or 6 mm lead equivalent concrete)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Comparison

| Parameter | Primary Barrier | Secondary Barrier |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Radiation attenuated | Full primary beam | Scatter + leakage |
| Transmission equation | $B = P / (W U / d^2)$ | $B_s = P / (K_s + K_L)$ |
| Units of attenuation step | TVL (Tenth Value Layer) | HVL (Half Value Layer) |
| Typical location | Wall behind vertical bucky, floor below tube | Side walls, ceiling, doors, control-booth wall |
| Typical thickness (100 kVp, 3 m, busy room) | 1.5 – 2.5 mm Pb | 0.5 – 1.5 mm Pb |
| Standard adopted | NCRP 147 / AERB SC/MED-4 | NCRP 147 / AERB SC/MED-4 |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a primary barrier and a secondary barrier in a diagnostic X-ray room. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Primary Barrier | Secondary Barrier |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Radiation received | Direct / primary X-ray beam | Scatter + leakage only |
| Typical thickness | 1.5 – 2.5 mm Pb | 0.5 – 1.5 mm Pb |
| Attenuation step used | TVL | HVL |
| Example | Wall behind the vertical bucky (chest stand) | Side wall, control-booth wall, ceiling |

Primary barriers are always designed with **TVL** because the primary beam is intense; secondary barriers are designed with **HVL** because doses are 100–1000× lower.

*(Examiner's tip: 3-mark questions need a *definition + 2-point comparison + example*. Don't skip the example.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four advisory or regulatory agencies involved in radiation protection, and state one function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **ICRP (International Commission on Radiological Protection)** — advisory; issues dose-limit recommendations (20 mSv/yr occupational, 1 mSv/yr public).
2. **IAEA (International Atomic Energy Agency)** — advisory + operational; publishes International Basic Safety Standards (BSS).
3. **NCRP (National Council on Radiation Protection, USA)** — advisory; publishes NCRP 147 for structural shielding.
4. **AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board, India)** — regulatory; issues licences and enforces Safety Code AERB/SC/MED-4 for medical X-ray installations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **forget to use the Use Factor (U)** in primary-barrier problems — this is a 2-mark loss.
> - They write **TVL₁** and **TVLₑ** but use the same value for both — losing 1 mark.
> - In RIS/PACS questions, students confuse **DICOM** (for images) with **HL7** (for text) — a 1-mark error.
> - Always **state the design goal explicitly** (0.1 or 0.02 mGy/wk) — missing this costs a full mark in the answer key.
> - Always **round UP** the lead thickness to the next commercially available sheet — a downward rounding is treated as unsafe design (1-mark penalty).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **IHE Scheduled Workflow Profile** for a radiology department, clearly identifying the role of HIS, RIS, PACS, and the modality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diagnostic X-ray room has workload W = 1500 mA·min/week at 80 kVp, use factor U = 1/4, distance d = 2.5 m. The shielding design goal is P = 0.1 mGy/wk (controlled area). At 80 kVp, the air-kerma rate is 0.036 mGy/mA·min·m², TVL₁ = 1.3 mm Pb, TVLₑ = 1.1 mm Pb. **Calculate the required primary-barrier thickness in mm of lead.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution A(a)
The IHE Scheduled Workflow (SWF) profile is the standardised radiology workflow defined by the Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise initiative. It links four actors through HL7 and DICOM messages:

- **Order Placer (HIS)** – clinician enters a radiology order (HL7 ORM message).
- **Order Filler / Department System Scheduler (RIS)** – schedules the appointment, sends DICOM Modality Worklist (MWL) to the modality.
- **Acquisition Modality (CT/MRI/CR/DR)** – performs the study, sends DICOM C-STORE of images to PACS and DICOM MPPS (Modality Performed Procedure Step) back to RIS.
- **Image Manager / Image Archive (PACS)** – archives the DICOM images, makes them available to the Image Display (diagnostic workstation) via C-FIND / C-MOVE.
- **Report Manager (RIS)** – radiologist dictates / voice-dictates; structured report returns to HIS / EMR as HL7 ORU.

The flow is: *Order (ORM) → Schedule → MWL → Acquisition → C-STORE → PACS Archive → Radiologist Read → ORU Report → EMR*. (Diagram already shown in Section 4.4.)

#### Model Solution A(b)
**Step 1 — Workload to air-kerma at 1 m**

$$W = 1500 \times 0.036 = 54.0 \text{ mGy·m}^2/\text{wk}$$

**Step 2 — Unshielded primary air-kerma at d = 2.5 m**

$$K_p = \frac{W \cdot U}{d^2} = \frac{54.0 \times 0.25}{2.5^2} = \frac{13.5}{6.25} = 2.16 \text{ mGy/wk}$$

**Step 3 — Required transmission**

$$B = \frac{P}{K_p} = \frac{0.1}{2.16} = 0.0463$$

**Step 4 — TVLs needed**

$$n_{TVL} = -\log_{10}(0.0463) = 1.334$$

**Step 5 — Physical lead thickness**

$$x = TVL_1 + (n_{TVL} - 1) \cdot TVL_e = 1.3 + (1.334 - 1) \times 1.1 = 1.3 + 0.368 = 1.668 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Round up to standard 2 mm lead sheet.**

$$\boxed{x = 2.0 \text{ mm Pb}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **Primary Barrier**, **Secondary Barrier**, **TVL**, and **HVL**. Explain the **NCRP 147 methodology** for primary-barrier design with all relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **hierarchy of international and national (Indian) advisory and regulatory agencies** governing medical radiation protection. State the dose limits prescribed by ICRP for occupational, public, and embryo/foetus exposure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B(a)
- **Primary Barrier**: a wall that intercepts the **primary (useful) X-ray beam** directly. Designed using TVL.
- **Secondary Barrier**: a wall that receives only **scattered** radiation from the patient and **leakage** radiation from the tube housing. Designed using HVL.
- **TVL (Tenth Value Layer)**: thickness of a shielding material that attenuates the beam to **10% (1/10)** of its incident value.
- **HVL (Half Value Layer)**: thickness that attenuates the beam to **50% (1/2)** of its incident value. **TVL = 3.32 × HVL**.

**NCRP 147 Methodology** (in 5 steps):
1. Determine the **shielding design goal P** (0.1 mGy/wk controlled; 0.02 mGy/wk uncontrolled).
2. Compute **unshielded primary air-kerma** at the protected point: $K_p = W U / d^2$.
3. Compute the **required transmission**: $B = P / K_p$.
4. Compute the **number of TVLs**: $n = -\log_{10} B$.
5. Convert to thickness: $x = TVL_1 + (n - 1) TVL_e$, then round up to commercial sheet thickness.

#### Model Solution B(b)

**International Advisory Tier:**
- **UNSCEAR** — collects global radiation data.
- **ICRP** — issues dose-limit recommendations; central advisory body.
- **ICRU** — defines radiation units.
- **IAEA** — Vienna-based UN agency; co-publishes the International BSS.
- **WHO** — health-policy and cancer-screening guidelines.
- **NCRP (USA)** — publishes NCRP 49, 102, 147, 151 etc. (referenced globally).

**National Regulatory Tier (India):**
- **AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board)** — statutory regulator under the Atomic Energy Act 1962; issues safety codes (AERB/SC/MED-4, /MED-13) and licences via the e-LORA portal.
- **BARC (Bhabha Atomic Research Centre)** — supports AERB with personnel monitoring (TLD badges) and secondary-standard dosimetry.

**ICRP Dose Limits (2007 Recommendations):**
- Occupational (whole body): **20 mSv/yr** averaged over 5 years (i.e., 100 mSv in 5 yr), with **50 mSv** in any single year.
- Public (whole body): **1 mSv/yr**.
- Embryo/foetus: **1 mSv** total over the term of pregnancy.
- Lens of the eye (occupational): **20 mSv/yr** (revised from 150 mSv in 2011 statement).
- Skin, hands, feet (occupational): **500 mSv/yr**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- [ ] A **primary barrier** stops the useful beam; use **TVL** for design.
- [ ] A **secondary barrier** stops scatter + leakage; use **HVL** for design.
- [ ] Unshielded primary air-kerma: $K_p = W U / d^2$ (mGy/wk).
- [ ] Design goal: **0.1 mGy/wk** (controlled), **0.02 mGy/wk** (uncontrolled).
- [ ] Required transmission: $B = P / K$.
- [ ] $n_{TVL} = -\log_{10} B$; $n_{HVL} = \log_2 (1/B)$.
- [ ] Thickness: $x = TVL_1 + (n-1) TVL_e$, then **round up** to commercial sheet.
- [ ] TVL ≈ 3.32 × HVL.
- [ ] Leakage workload convention: **W_L = W / 5**; tube-housing leakage cap: **1 mGy/h at 1 m**.
- [ ] ICRP occupational limit: **20 mSv/yr** (5-yr avg); public: **1 mSv/yr**; foetus: **1 mSv/term**.
- [ ] **ICRP** = global advisory; **AERB** = Indian statutory regulator under the Atomic Energy Act 1962.
- [ ] **NCRP 147** is the structural-shielding bible for diagnostic X-ray rooms.
- [ ] **AERB SC/MED-4** is the Indian code; **e-LORA** is the online licensing portal.
- [ ] **RIS** = workflow/text (HL7); **PACS** = images (DICOM).
- [ ] **HL7** = textual health data; **DICOM** = imaging data; **IHE** = integration profiles.
- [ ] Standard modalities connecting to PACS: CT, MRI, CR/DR, US, mammography, PET-CT.
- [ ] Always state design goal explicitly in derivations; never forget Use Factor U.
- [ ] Always round UP the lead thickness; a thinner barrier is non-compliant and unsafe.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Imaging and Communication in Medicine (DICOM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEBT602 - PRINCIPLES OF RADIO DIAGNOSIS & RADIOTHERAPY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Digital Imaging and Communication in Medicine (DICOM) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** is a globally adopted technical standard for transmitting, storing, retrieving, processing, and displaying medical imaging information. It was originally developed by the **American College of Radiology (ACR)** in conjunction with the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)**. The current release, **DICOM 3.0**, is an open, multi-part, object-oriented standard that ensures interoperability among imaging systems produced by different manufacturers across varied healthcare environments. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight** > DICOM is mandatory reading for understanding **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)**, **Radiotherapy Treatment Planning**, and **Hospital Information Systems (HIS/RIS)** integration. KTU examiners frequently ask about DICOM file structure, SOP classes, and DICOM-RT. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you receive a parcel from a foreign country. For the recipient to open it, the sender must use a **universally agreed-upon packaging format**, include a **standardized label** (sender, receiver, contents, handling instructions), and use a **common transport method** (postal service). Without these standards, the parcel would be useless. DICOM plays exactly this role for medical images: - **The parcel** → Patient image (CT, MRI, PET, X-Ray, RT Dose) - **The packaging format** → DICOM file structure (`.dcm`) - **The standardized label** → DICOM metadata (patient name, modality, study date, pixel dimensions) - **The common transport method** → DICOM network protocol (TCP/IP) - **The recipient** → PACS archive, diagnostic workstation, treatment planning system, 3D printer > [!NOTE] > **Why DICOM matters in a clinical setting:** > A CT scanner from **Vendor A** (e.g., Siemens) needs to send images to a PACS server, a treatment planning system (TPS), and a reporting workstation — potentially from **different vendors**. DICOM guarantees all of them can read, display, and process the same image **without vendor-specific converters**. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants - **DICOM Standard Version:** **DICOM PS 3.x (2024 release)** - **Default Port for Network Communication:** **Port 104** (well-known TCP port) - **Default Character Set:** **ASCII (ISO-IR 6)** unless otherwise specified - **Preamble Size:** **128 bytes** of zeroes followed by the magic prefix **"DICM"** - **Tag Identifier Size:** **32 bits (4 bytes)** divided into Group (16 bits) + Element (16 bits) - **NEMA/ACR Standard Committee Foundation Year:** **1983 (ACR-NEMA v1.0)**; **DICOM 3.0 released in 1993** - **DICOM-RT Extension Objects:** **8 SOP classes** dedicated to radiotherapy > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology to Memorize:** > - **IOD (Information Object Definition)** → What data *is*. > - **DIMSE (DICOM Message Service Element)** → How data *is exchanged*. > - **SOP (Service-Object Pair) Class** → The *combination* of an IOD and a DIMSE service. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 DICOM Architecture – The Five-Level Hierarchy DICOM is structured into **five hierarchical abstraction levels** that map real-world clinical operations into computable objects. The KTU syllabus emphasizes Levels 1 through 3 most heavily. #### Level 1: Patient (Root Entity) - Represents the human subject undergoing examination. - Each Patient IOD contains one or more *Studies*. - Patient-level tags include: Patient Name (0010,0010), Patient ID (0010,0020), Patient Birth Date (0010,0030). #### Level 2: Study - One diagnostic event or treatment session (e.g., a CT chest scan). - Each Study contains one or more *Series*. - Study Instance UID (0020,000D) uniquely identifies the study globally. #### Level 3: Series - A collection of related images (e.g., 300 axial slices of the same protocol). - Each Series contains one or more *Images* (or RT objects). - Series Instance UID (0020,000E) is the unique identifier. #### Level 4: Image (or RT Object) - A single 2D image frame, 3D voxel volume, or RT plan/dose. - SOP Instance UID (0008,0018) is the unique identifier. #### Level 5: Frame (Sub-image) - A single 2D matrix within a multi-frame image (e.g., cine-loop, 4D CT). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Favourite Question Pattern:** > *"Explain the DICOM Information Model with a suitable diagram."* — always mention the **Real-World Object → IOD → DIMSE → SOP Class → Transfer Syntax** mapping. ### 2.2 Core DICOM Components (Deep Dive) #### 2.2.1 Information Object Definitions (IODs) An IOD is a **data abstraction** of a real-world entity. Two flavors exist: - **Normalized IOD** → contains attributes of a *single* real-world entity (e.g., a print job, a visit). - **Composite IOD** → contains attributes of *multiple* related entities (e.g., a CT Image IOD contains patient + study + series + image attributes). **This is what 95% of clinical DICOM images use.** #### 2.2.2 Service Classes Service Classes define **what operations** can be performed: | Service Class | Function | KTU Keyword | |---|---|---| | Storage | Saves image to archive | **C-STORE** | | Query/Retrieve | Find and fetch studies | **C-FIND / C-MOVE / C-GET** | | Modality Worklist (MWL) | Pre-populates patient data at scanner | **C-FIND (broad query)** | | Storage Commitment | Confirms archival | **N-ACTION** | | Print Management | Sends to DICOM printer | **N-GET / N-CREATE** | | Verification | Echo test for connectivity | **C-ECHO** | #### 2.2.3 Service-Object Pair (SOP) Classes A **SOP Class = IOD + Service Class**. It is the *fundamental unit* of DICOM negotiation. Each device declares a list of SOP Classes it supports in its **Conformance Statement**. Example: **CT Image Storage SOP Class** = CT Image IOD + Storage Service Class. #### 2.2.4 Transfer Syntaxes A Transfer Syntax defines the **bit-level encoding** of a DICOM dataset: - **Implicit VR Little Endian** (Default; VR is inferred from the dictionary) - **Explicit VR Little Endian** (VR explicitly stated; standard for network) - **Explicit VR Big Endian** (legacy) - **JPEG Baseline (Lossy)**, **JPEG Lossless**, **JPEG 2000**, **RLE** > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Confusing **Transfer Syntax** with **Syntax of the DICOM Message**. Transfer Syntax = how the *dataset* is encoded. DIMSE protocol = how the *network message* is constructed. Both are negotiated separately during Association establishment. ### 2.3 DICOM-RT (Radiotherapy Extensions) — Special Focus DICOM-RT is a **superset** of DICOM that standardizes radiotherapy-specific objects. There are **8 DICOM-RT SOP Classes** prescribed by **IEC 61217** geometry: | DICOM-RT Object | IOD | Clinical Use | |---|---|---| | **RT Image** | RT Image IOD | Digitally Reconstructed Radiograph (DRR), portal images | | **RT Plan** | RT Plan IOD | Treatment machine parameters, beam geometry, MLC positions | | **RT Dose** | RT Dose IOD | 3D dose distribution from TPS calculation | | **RT Structure Set** | RT Structure Set IOD | GTV, CTV, PTV, OAR contours | | **RT Beams Delivery Record** | RT Beams Treatment Record IOD | Linac log of actual delivered beams | | **RT Brachy Treatment Record** | RT Brachy Treatment Record IOD | HDR/LDR brachytherapy session record | | **RT Treatment Summary Record** | RT Treatment Summary Record IOD | Cumulative delivered dose over course | | **RT Ion Plan** | RT Ion Plan IOD | Proton/carbon ion therapy plan | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tagging Tip:** Examiners love to ask: *"Why is a separate RT Structure Set IOD needed if DICOM already handles images?"* — Because **contours are vector-based VOIs (Volumes of Interest)**, not pixel data, and they must be linked to a specific RT Plan and RT Dose via the **Referenced SOP Sequence (0008,1110)** UID chain. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Standards Cheat Sheet | Parameter / Term | Notation / Value | Unit | Standard Reference | |---|---|---|---| | DICOM Port Number | **104** | TCP port | RFC 3240 (registered) | | Preamble Length | **128** | Bytes | DICOM PS 3.10 | | Magic Prefix | **"DICM"** | 4 bytes ASCII | DICOM PS 3.10 | | Group Number Range | **0000–7FFF** (even) | Hexadecimal | DICOM PS 3.5 | | Private Group Range | **0009–00FF, 0109–01FF…** | Hexadecimal | DICOM PS 3.5 | | Tag Size | **(gggg,eeee)** | 32-bit (4 bytes) | DICOM PS 3.5 | | VR Size | 2 | Bytes | DICOM PS 3.5 | | Length Field (Implicit VR) | 4 | Bytes (UL) | DICOM PS 3.5 | | Length Field (Explicit VR) | 2 or 6 | Bytes (US/UL+reserved) | DICOM PS 3.5 | | Maximum String Length (LO) | 64 | Chars | DICOM PS 3.5 | | Maximum UID Length | 64 | Chars | DICOM PS 3.5 | | Network Bandwidth (Recommended) | **≥ 1 Gbps** | Ethernet | IHE-RAD Profile | | AE Title (Application Entity) | 16 | Characters | DICOM PS 3.7 | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility DICOM is the **invisible backbone** of modern medicine. In a real engineering deployment: - **PACS Vendors** (GE Centricity, Philips iSite, Fuji Synapse) use DICOM to archive **> 1 PB** of imaging data per hospital per year. - **AI Vendors** (Aidoc, Zebra Medical) parse DICOM tags to feed CNN pipelines. - **Telemedicine** (3D holographic tele-presence, NASA-NIH experiments) streams DICOM-RT Structure Sets across continents. - **TPS Systems** (Eclipse, Pinnacle, RayStation, Monaco) consume DICOM-RT inputs and produce DICOM-RT outputs. - **Hospital IT Security** must protect DICOM traffic via **TLS 1.2/1.3** (DICOM PS 3.15 Annex C) and **audit trails** per HIPAA / DISHA (India) compliance. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 DICOM File Structure — Complete Bit-Level Derivation The DICOM file is a **binary stream** with three top-level parts. The derivation below shows the exact byte-by-byte layout per **DICOM PS 3.10 (Media Storage and File Format)**. **Step 1: Preamble (128 bytes)** The 128-byte preamble is reserved for **application profile use** (e.g., to allow multi-frame TIFF previews or to detect accidental binary transfer corruption). In practice, it is filled with **null bytes (0x00)** for compliance with the standard. $$ \text{Preamble} = \big[\,b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_{128}\,\big], \quad b_i = 0\text{x}00 \;\; \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 128\} $$ **Step 2: DICM Magic Prefix (4 bytes)** The four characters **D, I, C, M** (ASCII 0x44, 0x49, 0x43, 0x4D) act as the **file signature**. Any DICOM parser first seeks to byte 129 and verifies this signature before parsing the rest. $$ \text{Prefix} = \big[\,\text{0x44},\ \text{0x49},\ \text{0x43},\ \text{0x4D}\,\big] $$ **Step 3: File Meta Information (Variable Length)** This is itself a DICOM dataset (encoded in **Explicit VR Little Endian** with the Group always equal to **0002**). It contains critical negotiation parameters: - (0002,0000) File Meta Information Group Length — UL - (0002,0001) File Meta Information Version — OB - (0002,0002) Media Storage SOP Class UID — UI - (0002,0003) Media Storage SOP Instance UID — UI - (0002,0010) Transfer Syntax UID — UI - (0002,0012) Implementation Class UID — UI - (0002,0013) Implementation Version Name — SH - (0002,0016) Source Application Entity Title — AE > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Favourite Sub-question:** > *"Why does the Meta Information always use Explicit VR Little Endian regardless of the negotiated Transfer Syntax?"* — Because the **Transfer Syntax itself is a tag inside the Meta Information** (Tag 0002,0010). Chicken-and-egg problem solved by mandating Explicit VR for the Meta block, *then* using the declared Transfer Syntax for the rest of the file. **Step 4: The Data Set (Body)** Encoded in the Transfer Syntax declared in (0002,0010). The Data Set is a sequential stream of **Data Elements** ordered by ascending (Group, Element) tag, sorted by numeric value (not lexicographic). ### 3.2 Data Element Encoding — Exhaustive Derivation A **Data Element** is the atomic unit of information in a DICOM dataset. Its structure depends on whether **Implicit VR** or **Explicit VR** is used. #### Case A: Explicit VR Transfer Syntax For most VR types (excluding OB, OD, OF, OL, OV, OW, SQ, UC, UN, UR, UT): $$ \text{DataElement}_{\text{ExplicitVR}} = \big[\,\text{Tag}_{4B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{VR}_{2B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{Length}_{2B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{ValueField}_{\text{Length bytes}}\,\big] $$ For special VRs (OB, OD, OF, OL, OV, OW, SQ, UC, UN, UR, UT) — the "encoded with 4-byte length" group: $$ \text{DataElement}_{\text{ExplicitVR,special}} = \big[\,\text{Tag}_{4B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{VR}_{2B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{0x00 0x00}_{2B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{Length}_{4B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{ValueField}_{\text{Length bytes}}\,\big] $$ #### Case B: Implicit VR Transfer Syntax $$ \text{DataElement}_{\text{ImplicitVR}} = \big[\,\text{Tag}_{4B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{Length}_{4B}\,\big\Vert\,\text{ValueField}_{\text{Length bytes}}\,\big] $$ Here, the **VR is determined by looking up the Tag in the Data Dictionary** — hence "Implicit". ### 3.3 Worked Example: Decoding a Sample Data Element Consider the following hex sequence from a CT Image header (Explicit VR Little Endian, in hex): $$ \texttt{00 10 00 10 50 4E 0A 4C 4F 48 4D 41 4E 4E 00 00 00 00 00 00} $$ **Byte-by-byte interpretation:** | Byte(s) | Hex | Field | Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | 0–1 | `00 10` | Group | 0x0010 (Patient group) | | 2–3 | `00 10` | Element | 0x0010 (Patient's Name tag) | | 4–5 | `50 4E` | VR | ASCII **"PN"** = Person Name | | 6–7 | `0A 0C` | Length | 12 bytes (0x000C) follow | | 8–19 | `4C 4F 48 4D 41 4E 4E 00 00 00 00 00` | Value | ASCII **"LOHMANN"^"^^"** (a German surname) — Person Names use the `^` separator for Family^Given^Middle^Prefix^Suffix | **Total element size:** $4_{\text{tag}} + 2_{\text{VR}} + 2_{\text{len}} + 12_{\text{value}} = 20$ bytes. The hex stream length matches perfectly. ✓ ### 3.4 DICOM Association Establishment — The TCP/IP Handshake Before any image data flows, two DICOM Application Entities (AEs) negotiate a **Presentation Context** using the **DICOM Upper Layer Protocol (PS 3.8)**. The negotiation sequence is: 1. **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ** (Request) → Requesting AE proposes up to 128 Presentation Contexts. Each PC has: - **Presentation Context ID** (1–255, odd numbers preferred) - **Abstract Syntax** (SOP Class UID) - **Transfer Syntax(es)** proposed for that Abstract Syntax 2. **A-ASSOCIATE-AC** (Accept) → Responding AE either accepts or rejects each PC individually, and may propose a single Transfer Syntax per Abstract Syntax. 3. **Data exchange** → C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, etc. are exchanged under the accepted PCs. 4. **A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP** → Graceful termination. 5. **A-ABORT** → Forced termination on error (e.g., unsupported SOP class). > [!NOTE] > **Mathematical bound on Association overhead:** > Each accepted Presentation Context occupies a slot in the PDU. The maximum number of Presentation Contexts per Association is bounded by the **Maximum Length Sub-Item** field in the **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ PDU**, with a hard limit of **127** PCs (Presentation Context IDs 1, 3, 5, …, 253). ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DICOM vs. Other Healthcare Standards | Feature | **DICOM** | **HL7 v2.x** | **HL7 FHIR** | **IHE XDS-I** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Primary Focus | Medical imaging | Hospital administrative data | Modern web-based healthcare data | Cross-enterprise document sharing | | Data Format | Binary (Tag-Value) | Pipe-and-hat text (`\|`) | JSON / XML | References to DICOM/HL7 documents | | Transport | TCP/IP (port 104) | MLLP (port 2575) | HTTP/REST | SOAP / HTTP | | Imaging Data | ✅ Native | ❌ Not designed for it | ❌ Limited (uses ImagingStudy resource) | ✅ References DICOM objects | | Use Case | CT/MRI/PET/Ultrasound storage | ADT, Orders, Results | Mobile apps, cloud | Multi-hospital image sharing | | Version of DICOM Linked | — | — | — | Wraps DICOM via WADO-URI/WADO-RS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Inference:** If asked *"How does DICOM interact with HIS/RIS?"*, the answer is **MWL (Modality Worklist)** + **MPPS (Modality Performed Procedure Step)** + **Storage Commitment**. These three SOP classes form the **IHE Scheduled Workflow (SWF) Profile**. ### 3.6 DICOM Query/Retrieve — C-FIND Walk-Through The C-FIND service uses a **3-level key matching** (Patient → Study → Series → Image). The flow is: 1. **Requester** sends C-FIND-RQ with empty *Return Key* values (wildcards `*` or `?`). 2. **Responder** sends C-FIND-RSP for each match (status `Pending`). 3. After all matches, Responder sends a final C-FIND-RSP with status `Success`. The **C-MOVE** or **C-GET** retrieve operation then uses the returned **SOP Instance UIDs** to fetch the actual pixel data. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DICOM Information Model — Hierarchical Abstraction ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef rootNode fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef midNode fill:#2e7d8f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef leafNode fill:#5fb49c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef rtNode fill:#d97e3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff A[PATIENT LEVEL
Real World Object] B[STUDY LEVEL
One Diagnostic Event] C[SERIES LEVEL
Protocol Based Grouping] D[IMAGE OR RT OBJECT LEVEL
Single 2D Frame 3D Volume or RT Plan] E[FRAME LEVEL
Sub image Matrix] A -->|has one or more| B B -->|has one or more| C C -->|has one or more| D D -->|has one or more| E F[RT IMAGE SOP CLASS]:::rtNode G[RT PLAN SOP CLASS]:::rtNode H[RT DOSE SOP CLASS]:::rtNode I[RT STRUCTURE SET SOP CLASS]:::rtNode J[CT IMAGE SOP CLASS]:::leafNode K[MR IMAGE SOP CLASS]:::leafNode L[PET IMAGE SOP CLASS]:::leafNode D --> F D --> G D --> H D --> I D --> J D --> K D --> L A:::rootNode B:::midNode C:::midNode D:::midNode E:::leafNode ``` ### 4.2 DICOM File Format — Binary Structure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef fileNode fill:#2c5f8d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef metaNode fill:#e8a838,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef dataNode fill:#4caf50,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef tagNode fill:#9c27b0,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff P1[DICOM FILE DOT DCM] P2[BYTE 1 TO 128
PREAMBLE 128 BYTES OF NULL] P3[BYTE 129 TO 132
DICM MAGIC PREFIX 4 BYTES ASCII] P4[BYTE 133 ONWARDS
FILE META INFORMATION GROUP 0002 EXPLICIT VR LITTLE ENDIAN] P5[DATA SET BODY
ENCODED PER TRANSFER SYNTAX DECLARED IN TAG 0002 0010] P6[SEQUENCE OF DATA ELEMENTS
SORTED ASCENDING BY TAG] P7[TAG 4 BYTES GROUP PLUS ELEMENT] P8[VR 2 BYTES EXPLICIT OR IMPLICIT] P9[LENGTH 2 OR 4 BYTES] P10[VALUE FIELD N BYTES PER LENGTH] P11[NEXT DATA ELEMENT UNTIL END] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7 P7 --> P8 P8 --> P9 P9 --> P10 P10 --> P11 P1:::fileNode P2:::fileNode P3:::fileNode P4:::metaNode P5:::dataNode P6:::dataNode P7:::tagNode P8:::tagNode P9:::tagNode P10:::dataNode P11:::dataNode ``` ### 4.3 DICOM Network Association — Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef aeNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef serviceNode fill:#c0504d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef sopNode fill:#f0a830,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef transportNode fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef layerNode fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff subgraph S1[SCANNER APPLICATION ENTITY CT MR PET] A1[AE TITLE LABEL E.G. CT01]:::aeNode end subgraph S2[DIMSE PROTOCOL LAYER PS 3 7] B1[C ECHO VERIFICATION]:::serviceNode B2[C STORE STORAGE]:::serviceNode B3[C FIND QUERY]:::serviceNode B4[C MOVE RETRIEVE]:::serviceNode B5[N ACTION STORAGE COMMITMENT]:::serviceNode end subgraph S3[SOP CLASSES THE NEGOTIATION UNITS] C1[CT IMAGE STORAGE 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2]:::sopNode C2[MR IMAGE STORAGE 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.4]:::sopNode C3[VERIFICATION SOP 1.2.840.10008.1.1]:::sopNode end subgraph S4[UPPER LAYER PS 3 8 TCP IP] D1[A ASSOCIATE RQ]:::transportNode D2[A ASSOCIATE AC]:::transportNode D3[P DATA TF]:::transportNode D4[A RELEASE RQ]:::transportNode D5[A ABORT]:::transportNode end subgraph S5[PHYSICAL LAYER] E1[1000BASE T ETHERNET 1 GBPS]:::layerNode E2[WIFI 802.11AC]:::layerNode E3[VPN TUNNEL TLS 1.3]:::layerNode end subgraph S6[RECEIVER PACS ARCHIVE] F1[AE TITLE LABEL E.G. PACS01]:::aeNode end A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A1 --> B3 A1 --> B4 A1 --> B5 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 B2 --> C2 B1 --> C3 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D1 C3 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 --> D4 D4 --> D5 D1 --> E1 D1 --> E2 D1 --> E3 E1 --> F1 E2 --> F1 E3 --> F1 ``` ### 4.4 DICOM-RT Workflow in a Radiotherapy Department ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef stepNode fill:#2e7d8f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef rtNode fill:#d97e3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef machineNode fill:#5b3a7d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef archiveNode fill:#3a7d5b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff ST1[STEP 1 CT SIMULATION
Patient Scanned with Markers]:::stepNode ST2[STEP 2 CT IMAGE DICOM
Sent to TPS via C STORE]:::rtNode ST3[STEP 3 CONTOURING
Physician Draws GTV CTV PTV OAR]:::stepNode ST4[STEP 4 RT STRUCTURE SET DICOM
Vector Based Contours]:::rtNode ST5[STEP 5 TREATMENT PLANNING
Planner Sets Beams MLC Dose]:::stepNode ST6[STEP 6 RT PLAN DICOM
Beam Geometry Prescription]:::rtNode ST7[STEP 6B RT DOSE DICOM
3D Dose Matrix from TPS]:::rtNode ST8[STEP 7 PLAN APPROVAL
Physicist Checks Plan]:::stepNode ST9[STEP 8 PLAN TRANSFER
Sent to LINAC via C STORE]:::rtNode ST10[STEP 9 TREATMENT DELIVERY
Linac Executes Beams]:::machineNode ST11[STEP 10 RT BEAMS DELIVERY RECORD
Linac Logs Actual MUs MLC]:::rtNode ST12[STEP 11 PACS ARCHIVE
All Objects Indexed by SOP UID]:::archiveNode ST1 --> ST2 ST2 --> ST3 ST3 --> ST4 ST4 --> ST5 ST5 --> ST6 ST5 --> ST7 ST6 --> ST8 ST7 --> ST8 ST8 --> ST9 ST9 --> ST10 ST10 --> ST11 ST11 --> ST12 ST2 --> ST12 ST4 --> ST12 ST6 --> ST12 ST7 --> ST12 ``` ### 4.5 DICOM Service Class Architecture — Functional Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef compNode fill:#4a6fa5,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef servNode fill:#e07a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff classDef outNode fill:#81b29a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff R1[IOD Information Object Definition]:::compNode R2[SCU Service Class User]:::servNode R3[SCP Service Class Provider]:::servNode R4[DIMSE Protocol]:::compNode R5[SOP Class Union]:::compNode R6[Transfer Syntax Encoding]:::compNode R7[Conformance Statement]:::outNode R8[Verification C ECHO]:::outNode R9[Storage C STORE]:::outNode R10[Query Retrieve C FIND C MOVE]:::outNode R11[Modality Worklist]:::outNode R12[Print Management]:::outNode R1 --> R5 R4 --> R5 R5 --> R2 R5 --> R3 R6 --> R5 R5 --> R7 R2 --> R8 R2 --> R9 R2 --> R10 R2 --> R11 R2 --> R12 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define DICOM. List any four major DICOM Service Classes with one-line functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is a globally adopted standard developed jointly by the **American College of Radiology (ACR)** and the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)**. It specifies the formats, protocols, and services required for the **storage, retrieval, printing, and transmission** of medical imaging information across heterogeneous equipment and software systems. **Four Major Service Classes (2 marks):** | # | Service Class | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Storage Service Class (C-STORE)** | Transfers and persistently archives images / RT objects from an SCU to an SCP. | | 2 | **Query/Retrieve Service Class (C-FIND / C-MOVE / C-GET)** | Allows an SCU to search for and fetch DICOM objects from a PACS archive. | | 3 | **Modality Worklist Service Class (C-FIND, broad query)** | Pre-populates the scanner console with scheduled patient/study data from the RIS, eliminating manual entry. | | 4 | **Print Management Service Class (N-CREATE / N-ACTION)** | Sends images to a DICOM-compliant printer for film hardcopy. | *(Alternative valid Service Classes: Verification, Storage Commitment, Procedure Step.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the purpose of the DICOM Conformance Statement. Why is it essential for inter-vendor interoperability?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **DICOM Conformance Statement** is a formal document published by every manufacturer that declares **exactly which parts of the DICOM standard a device implements**. It functions as a contractual technical specification between the device vendor and the integrating hospital. **Three essential purposes (1 mark each):** 1. **SOP Class Declaration** → Lists every SOP Class the device supports as an SCU (Service Class User) and as an SCP (Service Class Provider). 2. **Transfer Syntax Declaration** → Lists every Transfer Syntax the device can encode/decode (e.g., JPEG Lossless, Explicit VR Little Endian). 3. **IOD Conformance Statement** → Declares which optional Type 2 / Type 3 attributes the device sends or requires. **Why it is essential (built into the points above):** When integrating a Siemens CT scanner with a GE PACS, the IT engineer must read **both Conformance Statements** to find a matching pair of SOP Classes and a mutually supported Transfer Syntax. **Without a Conformance Statement, inter-vendor integration is impossible** — it is the lingua franca of DICOM negotiation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks each**, with internal sub-parts splitting **7 + 7** marks. The student may choose **one of two alternative questions**. --- ### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **DICOM Information Model** with its hierarchical levels. Explain the difference between **Composite IODs** and **Normalized IODs** with one example of each. **(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **DICOM file structure** on a storage medium as defined in **DICOM PS 3.10**. Show the role of the File Meta Information and the Transfer Syntax UID. --- #### Model Solution — Choice A **(a) DICOM Information Model & IOD Types [7 marks]** The DICOM Information Model organizes medical imaging data into a **five-level hierarchy** that mirrors real-world clinical entities (1 mark for the hierarchy diagram or description): $$ \text{Patient} \longrightarrow \text{Study} \longrightarrow \text{Series} \longrightarrow \text{Image / RT Object} \longrightarrow \text{Frame} $$ | Level | Entity | Example Tag | |---|---|---| | Patient | Person undergoing the exam | (0010,0010) Patient Name | | Study | One diagnostic event (e.g., CT Chest) | (0020,000D) Study Instance UID | | Series | One protocol acquisition group (e.g., axial slices) | (0020,000E) Series Instance UID | | Image | Single 2D slice, 3D volume, or RT plan | (0008,0018) SOP Instance UID | | Frame | Sub-image within a multi-frame object | (0028,0008) Number of Frames | **Composite vs Normalized IODs (4 marks):** | Aspect | Composite IOD | Normalized IOD | |---|---|---| | Entities Represented | Multiple related entities (Patient + Study + Series + Image) | One single real-world entity (e.g., a Print Job, a Visit) | | SOP Class Service | C-STORE based | N-GET, N-SET, N-CREATE, N-DELETE, N-ACTION, N-EVENT-REPORT | | Usage | 95% of clinical imaging (CT, MR, RT Plan) | Workflow management (e.g., Print, MPPS) | | Example | **CT Image IOD** — contains patient name, study UID, series UID, and pixel data all in one object | **Basic Film Session IOD** — represents one film print job, holds only print-related attributes | **Conclusion (2 marks):** Composite IODs dominate clinical DICOM traffic because clinicians need the **complete image-with-context** unit for diagnostic interpretation. Normalized IODs are reserved for **workflow control** operations. --- **(b) DICOM File Structure (PS 3.10) [7 marks]** A DICOM file stored on a medium (HDD, DVD, USB, PACS disk) follows the structure prescribed in **DICOM PS 3.10**: $$ \text{DICOMFile} = \text{Preamble}_{128B} \;\Vert\; \text{DICM}_{4B} \;\Vert\; \text{FileMetaInfo}_{\text{Group 0002}} \;\Vert\; \text{DataSet}_{\text{TransferSyntax declared}} $$ **Block-by-block explanation (5 marks):** 1. **Preamble (128 bytes)** → Reserved for application profile use; default is all zeroes. Allows backward-compatible inclusion of TIFF previews or proprietary signatures. 2. **DICM Magic Prefix (4 bytes)** → ASCII characters `D I C M`. Any conforming parser checks this signature **before** parsing the rest. Files lacking this prefix are not valid DICOM. 3. **File Meta Information (Group 0002)** → Encoded **always** in **Explicit VR Little Endian**, regardless of the file's main Transfer Syntax. Contains the **Transfer Syntax UID (0002,0010)** which encodes the encoding rules of the rest of the file. Also contains **Media Storage SOP Class UID (0002,0002)** identifying what type of object is in the file. 4. **Data Set** → Encoded using the Transfer Syntax declared in (0002,0010). Contains a sequence of **Data Elements** sorted by ascending tag number. **Diagram (Use the Mermaid block from Section 4.2 for reference) (2 marks).** **Critical Point for Full Marks:** The Transfer Syntax UID is declared *inside* the Meta Information, but the Meta Information itself must use Explicit VR Little Endian — this is the "**chicken-and-egg resolution**" guaranteed by the standard. --- ### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 4 — Alternative]** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain **DICOM-RT** as an extension of DICOM for radiotherapy. List the **eight DICOM-RT SOP Classes** with their clinical purpose. **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **DICOM network communication protocol** with reference to the **Upper Layer Protocol (PS 3.8)**. Explain **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ**, **A-ASSOCIATE-AC**, **P-DATA-TF**, and **A-RELEASE-RQ** PDUs. --- #### Model Solution — Choice B **(a) DICOM-RT for Radiotherapy [7 marks]** **Introduction (2 marks):** DICOM-RT is the radiotherapy-specific **superset of DICOM** that standardizes the exchange of **treatment plans, dose distributions, contours, and treatment records** between Treatment Planning Systems (TPS), Record & Verify (R&V) systems, linacs, and PACS archives. It ensures that a plan calculated in **Eclipse (Varian)** can be transferred to a **Pinnacle³ (Philips)** archive or to an ** Elekta Versa HD** linac without loss of geometric or dosimetric fidelity. **Eight DICOM-RT SOP Classes (5 marks):** | # | SOP Class | Clinical Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | **RT Image IOD** | Stores Digitally Reconstructed Radiographs (DRRs) and portal images used for patient setup verification. | | 2 | **RT Plan IOD** | Encodes beam geometry, MLC positions, monitor units, prescription, treatment machine parameters. | | 3 | **RT Dose IOD** | Stores the 3D dose distribution calculated by the TPS, including dose grid dimensions, scaling factor, and DVH-relevant metadata. | | 4 | **RT Structure Set IOD** | Contains vector-based contours of GTV, CTV, PTV, and OARs as closed ROIs. Each ROI is linked to the parent RT Plan via Referenced SOP Sequence (0008,1110). | | 5 | **RT Beams Treatment Record IOD** | Captures **actual delivered** beam parameters recorded by the linac log files — used for adaptive radiotherapy and audit. | | 6 | **RT Brachy Treatment Record IOD** | Records the dwell positions, times, and source activity for HDR/LDR brachytherapy sessions. | | 7 | **RT Treatment Summary Record IOD** | Aggregates cumulative delivered dose and fractions completed over the entire radiotherapy course. | | 8 | **RT Ion Plan IOD** | Encodes proton / carbon-ion therapy plan parameters including ion type, energy modulation, and lateral spot positions. | --- **(b) DICOM Upper Layer Protocol [7 marks]** The **DICOM Upper Layer Protocol (PS 3.8)** governs all DICOM network communication over **TCP/IP port 104**. It uses a set of **Protocol Data Units (PDUs)** to manage the session lifecycle (7 marks for describing each PDU below). **Session Lifecycle:** 1. **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ (Association Request PDU) [2 marks]** Sent by the requesting Application Entity (SCU) to propose a connection. It contains: - **Called AE Title** and **Calling AE Title** (16-char each, identifying the two endpoints). - Up to **127 Presentation Contexts**, each with a **Presentation Context ID** (odd, 1–255), an **Abstract Syntax Name** (SOP Class UID), and a list of **Proposed Transfer Syntaxes**. - Optional **User Identity Negotiation** sub-item for authentication. 2. **A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Association Accept PDU) [1 mark]** Sent by the responding AE. For each Presentation Context, it either **accepts (with one chosen Transfer Syntax)** or **rejects (with a reason code)**. Negotiation is **per-PC**, not global. 3. **P-DATA-TF (Data Transfer PDU) [2 marks]** Used to transfer DIMSE messages (C-STORE, C-FIND, etc.) and their datasets. Each PDU carries one or more **Presentation Data Values (PDVs)**, each tagged with the Presentation Context ID and a **Message Control Header** flag (bit 0 = data, bit 1 = command). Multiple PDVs can be packed in one P-DATA-TF for efficiency. 4. **A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP (Release PDUs) [1 mark]** Graceful termination. The SCU sends A-RELEASE-RQ, the SCP sends A-RELEASE-RP. No data is lost. 5. **A-ABORT PDU (mentioned for completeness) [1 mark]** Forced termination for error conditions (e.g., unsupported SOP class, network failure, application-level exception). Closes TCP socket immediately. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Do NOT confuse** *Presentation Context* (PC) with *Association*. A single Association can carry **multiple SOP Classes** on different PCs. > - **Do NOT state** that DICOM uses UDP or HTTP at the transport layer — it is **TCP only** (port 104). DICOMweb (WADO-RS, QIDO-RS, STOW-RS) is a **separate** REST-based standard. > - **Do NOT forget** that the AE Title is NOT a hostname or IP — it is a 16-character application-level identifier, *separate* from the network address. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Places Students Lose Marks on DICOM Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > 1. **Confusing Implicit VR vs Explicit VR encoding** — In Implicit VR, the VR field is **omitted**; in Explicit VR, the VR is **always present**. Length field is 4 bytes in Implicit, 2 bytes (or 2+2+4 for special VRs) in Explicit. > 2. **Forgetting the 128-byte preamble** — Many students draw a DICOM file diagram starting with "DICM" directly, missing the **128 null bytes** that precede it. > 3. **Stating that DICOM uses HTTP** — Wrong. Core DICOM uses **TCP port 104**. Only the newer **DICOMweb** (WADO-RS/STOW-RS/QIDO-RS) uses HTTP/HTTPS. > 4. **Mixing up SOP Class UID with Transfer Syntax UID** — A SOP Class UID identifies *what* the data is; a Transfer Syntax UID identifies *how* it is encoded. > 5. **Omitting the Referenced SOP Sequence (0008,1110) UID chain in DICOM-RT** — RT Structure Set, RT Plan, and RT Dose are **linked** via this UID chain. Breaking the chain = non-conformant DICOM-RT. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DICOM (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - **DICOM = ACR-NEMA standard** for medical imaging interoperability; current is **DICOM 3.0 (PS 3.1 to PS 3.22)**. - **Default TCP port = 104**. - **5-level Information Model:** Patient → Study → Series → Image → Frame. - **Two IOD types:** **Composite** (multi-entity, used in 95% of clinical imaging) vs **Normalized** (single-entity, used in workflow control). - **Three core DIMSE services:** C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE/C-GET. - **SOP Class = IOD + Service Class** — the fundamental unit of DICOM negotiation. - **File structure:** 128-byte preamble + 4-byte "DICM" + File Meta Info (Group 0002) + Data Set. - **Meta Info always uses Explicit VR Little Endian**, regardless of file's main Transfer Syntax. - **Data Element = Tag (4B) + VR (2B, implicit or explicit) + Length (2B or 4B) + Value Field**. - **Tag format:** `(gggg,eeee)` in hexadecimal; `(0010,0010)` is Patient's Name. - **Two Transfer Syntaxes to memorize:** *Explicit VR Little Endian* (1.2.840.10008.1.2.1) and *Implicit VR Little Endian* (1.2.840.10008.1.2). - **DICOM-RT has exactly 8 SOP classes** — RT Image, RT Plan, RT Dose, RT Structure Set, RT Beams Treatment Record, RT Brachy Treatment Record, RT Treatment Summary Record, RT Ion Plan. - **RT Structure Set, RT Plan, and RT Dose are interlinked** via the **Referenced SOP Sequence (0008,1110)** UID chain — never break this chain in a TPS export. - **Conformance Statement** is mandatory for every DICOM device — it is the *contract* of inter-vendor interoperability. - **DICOMweb (WADO-RS, QIDO-RS, STOW-RS)** is the modern REST/HTTP-based extension — *not* the same as classic DICOM. - **Security:** DICOM PS 3.15 mandates **TLS 1.2/1.3** for secure transport; **audit trails** required for HIPAA / DISHA compliance. - **IHE-RAD Profiles** (Scheduled Workflow, Scheduled Radiology Workflow, Radiation Therapy Workflow) layer higher-level orchestration on top of raw DICOM services. - **Modality Worklist (MWL)** eliminates manual data entry at the scanner — populated by RIS → DICOM C-FIND broad query. - **Storage Commitment (N-ACTION)** is a *promise* by the archive that it will not lose the data — essential for medico-legal safety. - **Print Management SOP Class** uses Normalized IODs (Basic Film Session, Basic Film Box, Basic Grayscale Image Box) — niche topic but occasionally asked. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy for DICOM Questions:** > 1. Always start the answer with the **full form and governing body** (ACR-NEMA). > 2. Draw a **block diagram or hierarchy** before writing text — examiners reward visual clarity. > 3. Mention the **specific Part of the DICOM Standard** (e.g., PS 3.10, PS 3.8) — it shows depth of knowledge. > 4. For DICOM-RT questions, **always draw the UID linkage** between Structure Set → Plan → Dose.
